Initially all means lie in [μ_1/2, μ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for α=1/3, the initial value of s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i is greater than α μ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine μ_1=max_i μ_i and s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'≤α μ_1. For α=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<μ^r]≥1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r−1}<μ^{r−1}]≥1/13.  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<μ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and μ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below μ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set β=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>αμ_1, then Pr[X<μ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(μ_1+s')>α/(1+α). If it stops with βμ_1≤s'≤αμ_1, write s'=γμ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least μ_1/2: with probability at least γ/(1+γ), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2−γ)μ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.

## Known Bottlenecks
  • The only missing part of the candidate classification is sufficiency for every 0 < p < 1/3.
  • Any proof must be sharp at dictator weights and must handle threshold jumps at subset sums.
  • Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.
  • Control threshold jumps/critical antichains in the strict inequality mu_p({w(A)>p})≥p for p<1/3.
  • Find a polynomial or moment-dual lower bound that exploits the Bernoulli moment structure rather than only support bounds.

  • The proposed two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the one-threshold submean lemma: for all weights w summing to 1 and all tau < p, Pr_p[sum w_i v_i > tau] >= p. — Set delta = a-b and add an independent Bernoulli(p) coordinate of weight delta. Then (1-p)Pr[X <= a] + p Pr[X <= b] = Pr[X + delta xi <= a]; after normalization the threshold is tau = a/(1+delta) < p.
  • The submean lemma is equivalent to the original Bernoulli inequality Pr_p[sum w_i v_i >= p] >= p. — The original inequality implies the submean version because {S >= p} is contained in {S > tau}. Conversely let tau increase to p and use continuity from above.
  • The two-threshold inequality holds for reciprocal parameters p = 1/k. — Use the cyclic orbit proof to get Pr[weighted sum >= 1/k] >= 1/k for the augmented normalized weights; since the reduced threshold tau is below 1/k, the target follows.
  • The two-threshold inequality holds whenever the augmented support size is at most 5. — After augmentation reduce to proving Pr[Z > tau] >= p with tau < p. If no coordinate exceeds tau, supports 4 and 5 are handled by pair-counting: all pairs win in the small-threshold cases, and for support 5 with tau >= 1/4 at least six pairs and all triples win.
  • The two-threshold inequality holds in the augmented pairwise-heavy case, where every pair of positive augmented weights has sum greater than the augmented threshold. — Then every outcome with at least two successes is good. The augmented support is greater than 1/p, and the binomial tail Pr[Bin(N,p) >= 2] is at least p for p <= 1/3.
  • The two-threshold inequality holds under the variance-slack condition p - (1-p)a - p b >= p sqrt(sum u_i^2 + (a-b)^2). — Apply Cantelli's inequality to the augmented normalized weighted Bernoulli sum; the stated condition is exactly what makes the lower-tail bound at most 1-p.
**Failed attempts:**
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
**Progress notes:** The section separation reduction is verified. The target two-threshold inequality is exactly equivalent, by adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, to the original one-threshold Bernoulli tail problem. Thus it is not an easier auxiliary lemma, though it is the right gluing form. It is proved here for reciprocal p, augmented support at most 5, pairwise-heavy augmented weights, and a Cantelli variance-slack regime. The remaining task is the full subthreshold lemma Pr_p[sum w_i v_i > tau] >= p for tau < p <= 1/3, equivalently the original inequality.

<full_solver_output task_id="t4_section_two_threshold">
## 1. Separation reduction in the section setup

Write \(q=1-p\). In the section induction, let \(G_1\subseteq G_0\subseteq 2^{[n]}\), where \(n=m-1\), and put
\[
C_i=\operatorname{conv}\{\mathbf 1_A:A\in G_i\}.
\]

A mixture with marginal \(r\ge p\) on the last coordinate has old-coordinate marginal
\[
(1-r)x_0+r x_1,\qquad x_0\in C_0,\ x_1\in C_1.
\]
Since \(C_1\subseteq C_0\), the set of all such marginals is exactly
\[
qC_0+pC_1.
\]
Indeed, if \(r\ge p\), then
\[
(1-r)x_0+r x_1
=q\left(\frac{1-r}{q}x_0+\frac{r-p}{q}x_1\right)+p x_1,
\]
and the parenthesized point lies in \(C_0\). Conversely, every \(qz_0+pz_1\) is obtained with \(r=p\).

Thus failure to find \(r\ge p\), \(x_0\in C_0\), \(x_1\in C_1\) with
\[
(1-r)x_0+r x_1\ge p\mathbf 1
\]
is equivalent to
\[
(qC_0+pC_1)\cap [p,\infty)^n=\varnothing.
\]
By strict separation, there is a nonzero vector \(c\ge0\) such that
\[
\sup_{y\in qC_0+pC_1} c\cdot y < p\sum_i c_i.
\]
Normalize \(u_i=c_i/\sum_j c_j\). Define
\[
a=\max_{A\in G_0}u(A),\qquad b=\max_{B\in G_1}u(B).
\]
Then \(0\le b\le a\), and
\[
q a+p b<p.
\]
Moreover,
\[
G_0\subseteq\{A:u(A)\le a\},\qquad
G_1\subseteq\{A:u(A)\le b\}.
\]
Therefore the proposed two-threshold inequality would indeed contradict
\[
q\mu_p(G_0)+p\mu_p(G_1)>q
\]
and close the gluing step.

---

## 2. Main reduction: the two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the original one-threshold problem

Let
\[
X=u(A)=\sum_i u_i v_i,\qquad v_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p),
\]
and set
\[
\delta=a-b\ge0.
\]
Let \(\xi\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(p)\) be independent. Then
\[
q\Pr[X\le a]+p\Pr[X\le b]
=\Pr[X+\delta \xi\le a].
\]
Now normalize the weights
\[
\widetilde u_i=\frac{u_i}{1+\delta},\qquad
\widetilde u_*=\frac{\delta}{1+\delta},
\]
and set
\[
\tau=\frac{a}{1+\delta}.
\]
The condition \(q a+p b<p\) is exactly
\[
\tau<p,
\]
because
\[
p-\tau
=\frac{p(1+\delta)-a}{1+\delta}
=\frac{p-q a-p b}{1+\delta}>0.
\]
Thus the desired inequality becomes
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_j \widetilde u_j \widetilde v_j\le \tau\right]\le q
\]
for some threshold \(\tau<p\).

So the two-threshold statement is equivalent to the following subthreshold one-threshold lemma:

> **Subthreshold lemma \(L_p\).** For all nonnegative weights \(w_j\) summing to \(1\) and all \(\tau<p\),
> \[
> \Pr_p\!\left[\sum_j w_j v_j>\tau\right]\ge p.
> \]

But \(L_p\) is itself equivalent to the original Bernoulli inequality
\[
B_p:\qquad \Pr_p\!\left[\sum_j w_j v_j\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Indeed, \(B_p\Rightarrow L_p\) because \(\{S\ge p\}\subseteq\{S>\tau\}\) whenever \(\tau<p\). Conversely, \(L_p\Rightarrow B_p\) by taking \(\tau\uparrow p\) and using continuity from above:
\[
\{S>\tau\}\downarrow \{S\ge p\}.
\]

So the proposed two-threshold inequality is not merely sufficient for the FC induction; it is essentially another formulation of the original problem.

---

## 3. Immediate consequences and proved cases

### 3.1 Reciprocal parameters \(p=1/k\)

For \(p=1/k\), the target two-threshold inequality holds.

Using the cyclic-orbit proof from t1, for any normalized weights \(w_j\),
\[
\Pr_{1/k}\!\left[\sum_j w_j v_j\ge \frac1k\right]\ge \frac1k.
\]
Since the reduced threshold \(\tau\) above satisfies \(\tau<p=1/k\),
\[
\Pr_{1/k}\!\left[\sum_j w_j v_j>\tau\right]\ge \Pr_{1/k}\!\left[\sum_j w_j v_j\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Thus the two-threshold inequality is proved for
\[
p=\frac13,\frac14,\frac15,\dots
\]

More generally, if there is a reciprocal anchor \(s=1/k\) with
\[
\tau<s\le p,
\]
then the target holds at \(p\). One uses the monotonicity fact from t2: for an increasing family \(\mathcal F\), if \(\mu_s(\mathcal F)\ge s\), then \(\mu_t(\mathcal F)\ge t\) for every \(t\ge s\).

---

### 3.2 Trivial large-coordinate case

If some original weight satisfies
\[
u_i>a,
\]
then the target inequality holds immediately, because whenever \(v_i=1\),
\[
X+\delta\xi>a.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr[X+\delta\xi>a]\ge p,
\]
equivalently
\[
q\Pr[X\le a]+p\Pr[X\le b]\le q.
\]

---

### 3.3 Augmented support at most \(5\)

Let the augmented normalized weights be
\[
\widetilde u_i=\frac{u_i}{1+\delta},\qquad \widetilde u_*=\frac{\delta}{1+\delta},
\]
and let
\[
\tau=\frac{a}{1+\delta}<p.
\]
If the augmented support has size at most \(5\), then the target inequality holds for all \(p\le1/3\).

Sketch: if some augmented weight exceeds \(\tau\), we are done. Otherwise all augmented weights are \(\le\tau\).

- For support \(4\), no pair can have total \(\le\tau\); otherwise the total weight would be at most \(3\tau<1\). Hence every outcome with at least two successes is good, and
  \[
  \Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(4,p)\ge2]\ge p
  \]
  for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\).

- For support \(5\), if \(\tau<1/4\), the same argument shows every pair is good, so \(\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(5,p)\ge2]\ge p\). If \(\tau\ge1/4\), the graph of nonwinning pairs is intersecting, hence has at most \(4\) edges; therefore at least \(6\) pairs are winning, and every triple is winning. This gives the lower bound
  \[
  6p^2(1-p)^3+\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(5,p)\ge3]
  =6p^2-8p^3+3p^4\ge p
  \]
  for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\).

So the target is proved whenever the augmented instance has support \(\le5\); equivalently, for \(a>b\), whenever the original \(u\)-support has size \(\le4\).

---

### 3.4 Pairwise-heavy augmented case

Again work with the augmented normalized weights \(\widetilde u\) and threshold \(\tau<p\). Suppose:

1. no augmented coordinate has weight \(>\tau\), and  
2. every pair of positive augmented coordinates has total weight \(>\tau\).

Then every outcome with at least two successes is good. Since all augmented weights are \(\le\tau<p\), the augmented support \(N\) satisfies \(N>1/p\), so
\[
N\ge \lfloor 1/p\rfloor+1.
\]
For \(p\le1/3\),
\[
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\ge2]\ge p.
\]
Therefore the two-threshold inequality holds.

In the original \(u,a,b\) variables, the pairwise-heavy condition says:
\[
u_i+u_j>a\quad\text{for all distinct positive }i,j,
\]
and, if \(a>b\),
\[
u_i>b\quad\text{for every positive }i.
\]

---

### 3.5 A variance-slack sufficient condition

Let
\[
\delta=a-b,\qquad
\varepsilon=p-q a-p b>0,\qquad
R=\sum_i u_i^2+\delta^2.
\]
If
\[
\varepsilon\ge p\sqrt R,
\]
then the target inequality holds.

Indeed, for the augmented normalized sum \(Y\),
\[
\mathbb E Y=p,\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\frac{pqR}{(1+\delta)^2},
\]
and the gap from the mean to the threshold is
\[
p-\tau=\frac{\varepsilon}{1+\delta}.
\]
Cantelli’s inequality gives
\[
\Pr[Y\le \tau]\le
\frac{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}{\operatorname{Var}(Y)+(p-\tau)^2}
\le q
\]
precisely when \(\varepsilon^2\ge p^2R\).

---

## 4. Boundary warning

The strict condition
\[
q a+p b<p
\]
is essential. The same inequality with \(\le\) is false.

Example: take \(p=1/3\), five equal weights \(u_i=1/5\), and
\[
a=2/5,\qquad b=1/5.
\]
Then
\[
q a+p b=\frac23\cdot\frac25+\frac13\cdot\frac15=\frac13=p,
\]
but
\[
q\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(5,1/3)\le2]
+p\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(5,1/3)\le1]
=\frac{496}{729}>\frac23.
\]
So any proof must genuinely exploit strict slack or handle boundary jumps carefully.

---

## 5. Where the direct attacks still get stuck

The cleanest structural conclusion is that the two-threshold inequality is exactly the original one-threshold Bernoulli problem after adding one extra coordinate. Therefore, a proof of the full two-threshold statement for all \(p\le1/3\) would prove the original theorem, and conversely.

Two natural direct approaches still face the same obstruction as in t1/t2:

- **Conditioning on a coordinate.** After conditioning, the no-success branch produces a normalized threshold \(\tau/(1-c)\), which can exceed \(p\), so the same induction hypothesis does not close.

- **Merging or splitting weights.** For two weights \(x\le y\), splitting versus merging changes a lower tail negatively on \(0\le s<x\) and positively on \(y\le s<x+y\). Thus no monotonic compression principle is apparent.

- **Boundary thresholds.** Continuous crossing arguments reduce failures to subset-sum boundaries, but the equality example above shows that boundary atoms can be large enough to destroy naive continuity arguments.

So the exact remaining core is still the original strict/subthreshold Bernoulli tail lemma:
\[
\Pr_p\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>\tau\right]\ge p
\qquad\text{for every }\tau<p\le1/3.
\]

## Task t3_moment_dual — Moment and polynomial-dual attack on p<1/3 sufficiency
**Proven results:**
  • For any p<r<=1-p, the cubic polynomial L_r(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)/M_3(r) is a valid lower bound for 1_{x>0} on [-p,1-p], giving Pr[X>0] >= pq((r-p)A2-(1-2p)A3)/M_3(r). — The factor x(x+p) makes the polynomial nonpositive on [-p,0], and the root at r makes it nonpositive again on [r,1-p]; scaling by its positive maximum gives the certificate.
  • For any 0<r<=s<=1-p, the quartic polynomial x(x+p)(r-x)(s-x), scaled by its positive maximum on [0,1-p], gives a valid lower-polynomial certificate with expectation mu4+(p-r-s)mu3+(rs-p(r+s))mu2. — The polynomial is nonpositive on the negative interval and is bounded above by its positive maximum on the positive interval, so its scaled expectation lower-bounds Pr[X>0].
  • A Berry-Esseen diffuse-weight criterion holds: Pr[X>0] >= 1/2 - C_BE * ((p^2+(1-p)^2)/sqrt(p(1-p))) * A3/A2^{3/2}; hence the desired bound follows whenever this error is at most 1/2-p. — Apply Berry-Esseen to the independent centered summands w_i(v_i-p), using variance p(1-p)A2 and third absolute moment sum p(1-p)(p^2+(1-p)^2)A3.
  • In the strict-reduced regime 0<w_i<p, sum w_i=1, p<1/3, the inequality Pr[sum w_i v_i>p]>p holds for support size exactly 6. — Classify losing pairs and triples. All 4-sets are winning; losing pairs/triples are bounded by elementary matching and intersecting-family arguments in four p-ranges, and the resulting binomial-polynomial lower bounds are all strictly larger than p.
**Failed attempts:**
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
**Progress notes:** The full p<1/3 sufficiency is not closed here. The main new rigorous progress is a family of polynomial-dual certificates, a Berry-Esseen diffuse case, and a complete strict proof for support size 6, pushing any strict counterexample beyond the previously handled support sizes to support at least 7.

<full_solver_output task_id="t3_moment_dual">
## t3 progress report: moment/polynomial certificates + support-six closure

Let \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and \(X=S-p\in[-p,q]\). I worked in the strict-reduced regime \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\). The full general \(p<1/3\) case is not closed here, but I obtained several reusable polynomial-dual certificates and a complete proof for support size \(6\), extending the prior support-\(\le5\) result.

---

## 1. A useful cubic lower-polynomial certificate

For \(p<r\le q\), define

\[
f_r(x)=x(x+p)(r-x).
\]

On \([-p,0]\), \(f_r(x)\le0\). On \([0,r]\), \(f_r(x)\ge0\), and on \([r,q]\), \(f_r(x)\le0\). Hence, if

\[
M_3(r)=\max_{0\le x\le r} x(x+p)(r-x),
\]

then

\[
L_r(x)=\frac{f_r(x)}{M_3(r)}
\]

satisfies

\[
L_r(x)\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\qquad\text{for all }x\in[-p,q].
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \mathbb E L_r(X)
=\frac{(r-p)\mu_2-\mu_3}{M_3(r)},
\]

where

\[
\mu_2=pqA_2,\qquad \mu_3=pq(1-2p)A_3.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\Pr[X>0]\ge
\frac{pq\big((r-p)A_2-(1-2p)A_3\big)}{M_3(r)}.
}
\]

The maximizing point is explicit:

\[
x_r=\frac{r-p+\sqrt{r^2+rp+p^2}}{3},
\qquad
M_3(r)=x_r(x_r+p)(r-x_r).
\]

Thus the cubic certificate proves the desired strict bound whenever

\[
q\big((r-p)A_2-(1-2p)A_3\big)\ge M_3(r)
\]

for some \(r\in(p,q]\).

---

## 2. A quartic two-root certificate

For \(0<r\le s\le q\), define

\[
f_{r,s}(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)(s-x).
\]

Again \(f_{r,s}(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\). On \([0,q]\), scale by

\[
M_{r,s}=\max_{0\le x\le q} f_{r,s}(x)_+.
\]

Then

\[
L_{r,s}(x)=\frac{f_{r,s}(x)}{M_{r,s}}
\]

is a valid lower polynomial:

\[
L_{r,s}(x)\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\qquad (x\in[-p,q]).
\]

Its expectation is

\[
\mathbb E f_{r,s}(X)
=
\mu_4+(p-r-s)\mu_3+\big(rs-p(r+s)\big)\mu_2.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\Pr[X>0]\ge
\frac{
\mu_4+(p-r-s)\mu_3+\big(rs-p(r+s)\big)\mu_2
}{
M_{r,s}
}.
}
\]

A particularly clean subfamily is \(s=q\), giving zeros at \(-p,0,r,q\):

\[
f_{r,q}(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)(q-x),
\]

and

\[
\mathbb E f_{r,q}(X)
=
\mu_4-(r+1-2p)\mu_3+\big(r(1-2p)-pq\big)\mu_2.
\]

After substituting the Bernoulli moments,

\[
\mu_4
=
3p^2q^2A_2^2+pq(1-6pq)A_4,
\]

the main obstruction is visible: for most of \(p\in(0,1/3)\), the coefficient \(1-6pq\) is negative, while the \(\mu_3\)-coefficient in the \(s=q\) subfamily is also negative. So this quartic ansatz gives good certificates in some parameter ranges but does not become sign-definite from the elementary constraints \(A_3\le pA_2\), \(A_4\le p^2A_2\).

---

## 3. Diffuse case via Berry–Esseen

Let

\[
\sigma^2=\operatorname{Var}(X)=pqA_2.
\]

For \(Y_i=w_i(v_i-p)\),

\[
\sum_i \mathbb E |Y_i|^3
=
pq(p^2+q^2)A_3.
\]

By Berry–Esseen, with any admissible absolute constant \(C_{\mathrm{BE}}\),

\[
\sup_x\left|
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X}{\sigma}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)
\right|
\le
C_{\mathrm{BE}}\frac{pq(p^2+q^2)A_3}{(pqA_2)^{3/2}}.
\]

Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]
\ge
\frac12
-
C_{\mathrm{BE}}
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}.
\]

Therefore the desired strict inequality follows whenever

\[
\boxed{
C_{\mathrm{BE}}
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}
\le
\frac12-p.
}
\]

In particular, since \(A_3\le \lambda A_2\) with \(\lambda=\max_i w_i\), it suffices that

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{A_2}}
\le
\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{C_{\mathrm{BE}}(p^2+q^2)}.
}
\]

This handles genuinely diffuse weight vectors.

---

## 4. Exact proof for support size \(6\)

Assume now \(n=6\), \(0<w_i<p<1/3\), and \(\sum_i w_i=1\). I prove

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the family of losing \(k\)-sets:

\[
D_k=\{A\subseteq[6]: |A|=k,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

Let

\[
e=|D_2|,\qquad h=|D_3|.
\]

Every set of size at least \(4\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so

\[
w(A)>1-2p>p.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S\le p]
=
q^6+6pq^5+e p^2q^4+h p^3q^3.
\]

We split into ranges.

### Case 1: \(p\le 1/5\)

No losing pair exists. Indeed, if \(w_i+w_j\le p\), then the remaining four weights are each \(<p\), so

\[
1=\sum_i w_i< p+4p\le1,
\]

a contradiction. Since the losing family is a down-set, no losing triple exists either. Hence

\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5=\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(6,p)\ge2].
\]

The function

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-p
\]

has derivative

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

---

### Case 2: \(1/5<p\le1/4\)

There are no two disjoint losing pairs; otherwise two losing pairs plus the remaining two weights would have total

\[
<2p+2p\le1,
\]

contradicting \(\sum_i w_i=1\). Hence the losing-pair graph is intersecting, so

\[
e\le5.
\]

Also \(h=0\), because a losing triple plus the remaining three weights would have total

\[
< p+3p\le1.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5-5p^2q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_2(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-5p^2q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_2'(p)=20pq^3-1>0
\]

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{980}{15625}>0.
\]

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

---

### Case 3: \(1/4<p<1/3\), with no losing triples

If \(h=0\), then the losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\), because three disjoint losing pairs would cover all six vertices and have total weight at most \(3p<1\). Therefore, by the matching bound for graphs on six vertices,

\[
e\le10.
\]

So

\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5-10p^2q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_3(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-10p^2q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_3'(p)=10pq^3(1+3p)-1>0
\]

on \([1/4,1/3]\), and

\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{75}{4096}>0.
\]

Thus \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

---

### Case 4: \(1/4<p<1/3\), with at least one losing triple

Fix a losing triple \(T\). First, any two losing triples must intersect in at least \(2\) points. If two losing triples were disjoint, their total weight would be at most \(2p<1\), impossible. If they intersected in exactly one point, their union would have size \(5\), and adding the remaining weight \(<p\) gives total \(<3p<1\), again impossible.

Therefore the losing triples form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on six vertices, so

\[
h\le4.
\]

Next, no losing pair can lie wholly inside \(T^c\). Such a pair, together with \(T\) and the one remaining vertex, would have total weight \(<3p<1\). Hence losing pairs are either inside \(T\) or cross between \(T\) and \(T^c\). There are at most \(3\) inside \(T\). The crossing graph is bipartite \(3\times3\), and it cannot contain a matching of size \(3\), since that would give three disjoint losing pairs of total weight at most \(3p<1\). Hence it has at most \(6\) edges. Therefore

\[
e\le 3+6=9.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S>p]
\ge
1-q^6-6pq^5-9p^2q^4-4p^3q^3.
\]

Let

\[
F_4(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-9p^2q^4-4p^3q^3-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_4'(p)=12pq^2-1>0
\]

on \([1/4,1/3]\), and

\[
F_4(1/4)=\frac{48}{4096}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Combining the four cases proves the strict statement for support size \(6\). Together with the prior support-\(\le5\) argument, any strict counterexample must have support at least \(7\).

---

## 5. Main algebraic obstructions hit

### Quadratic lower polynomials are too weak

Let \(L(x)=\alpha x+\beta x^2\). If \(L\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\) on \([-p,q]\), then the best possible positive quadratic coefficient satisfies

\[
\beta\le \frac1q.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathbb E L(X)\le \frac{\mu_2}{q}=pA_2.
\]

In the strict-reduced regime \(A_2< p\), so this gives at most \(p^2\), far below the target \(p\).

### Simple quartic majorants are not sign-definite

The natural complement majorant

\[
M_{a,b}(x)=\left(1-\frac{x}{a}\right)^2
\left(1-\frac{x}{b}\right)^2
\]

satisfies \(M_{a,b}(x)\ge \mathbf 1_{\{x<0\}}\), but

\[
\mathbb E M_{a,b}(X)
=
1+
\frac{
(a^2+b^2+4ab)\mu_2
-2(a+b)\mu_3
+\mu_4
}{a^2b^2}.
\]

To prove \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\), one would need \(\mathbb E M_{a,b}\le 1-p\). The numerator has competing signs, and after substituting the Bernoulli moments the \(A_4\)-coefficient is negative in much of \(p\in(0,1/3)\). I did not find a uniform choice of \(a,b\), even moment-dependent, that closes all cases.

---

response:
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

For \(t\in[0,1]\), define \(B(t)\) to be the following assertion: for every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative real numbers \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) satisfying \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and every family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) of independent Bernoulli\((t)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=t\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=1-t\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge t\right]\ge t.
\]
This proof establishes the following reduction lemma and consequence: if \(k\) is a positive integer, \(p\in[0,1]\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds. Consequently, if \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[0,1/3]\). This is not a complete classification of all probabilities \(p\) in the original problem; it is a conditional reduction and interval-extension consequence.

## Proof

### Reduction lemma

Fix a positive integer \(k\), a number \(p\in[0,1]\), and set \(r=kp\). Assume \(r\le1\) and assume \(B(r)\) holds. We prove \(B(p)\).

Let \(m\) be any positive integer, and let \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\). We must prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\).

Construct the following categorical coupling. For each coordinate \(i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\), choose an independent random variable
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,\ldots,k\}
\]
with
\[
\Pr[C_i=j]=p \quad (1\le j\le k),\qquad \Pr[C_i=0]=1-r.
\]
This is a valid probability distribution because \(p\ge0\), \(1-r\ge0\), and
\[
(1-r)+kp=(1-r)+r=1.
\]

For each nonzero colour \(j\in\{1,\ldots,k\}\), define the total weight of colour \(j\) by
\[
S_j:=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}.
\]
Also define
\[
T:=S_1+\cdots+S_k.
\]
Since each coordinate has exactly one colour, we have
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq 0\}}.
\]

For each \(i\), the indicator \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq0\}}\) is Bernoulli\((r)\), because
\[
\Pr[C_i\neq0]=\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[C_i=j]=kp=r.
\]
These indicators are independent, since the \(C_i\) are independent. Therefore \(T\) is a weighted sum of independent Bernoulli\((r)\) variables with weights summing to \(1\). By \(B(r)\),
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]\ge r. \tag{1}
\]

Now define events
\[
A_j:=\{S_j\ge p\},\qquad 1\le j\le k.
\]
We claim that
\[
\{T\ge r\}\subseteq A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k.
\]
Indeed, if none of the events \(A_j\) occurs, then \(S_j<p\) for every \(j\), so
\[
T=S_1+\cdots+S_k<kp=r.
\]
Thus \(T\ge r\) is impossible unless at least one \(S_j\ge p\). The non-strict threshold \(\ge\) is handled exactly by passing to the strict complement \(S_j<p\).

Using the inclusion above and the union bound,
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]
\le \Pr[A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k]
\le \sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j].
\]
For each fixed \(j\), the random variables \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Hence all \(S_j\) have the same distribution, and in particular
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j]=k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Combining this with (1) gives
\[
r\le k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Since \(k>0\) and \(r=kp\),
\[
\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \frac r k=p.
\]

Finally, \(S_1=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\), and the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Thus \(S_1\) has exactly the same distribution as \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\) for any independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Because \(m\) and the weights \(w_i\) were arbitrary, \(B(p)\) holds.

The endpoint cases are included: probabilities equal to \(0\) are allowed in the categorical distribution; if \(p=0\), then the desired lower bound is \(0\); and if \(r=1\), then colour \(0\) simply has probability \(0\).

### Consequence for \([0,1/3]\)

Assume \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\). We prove \(B(p)\) for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

If \(p=0\), then independent Bernoulli\((0)\) variables are all \(0\) almost surely, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]
Thus \(B(0)\) holds.

If \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(p)\) holds by hypothesis.

It remains to consider \(0<p<1/6\). Set
\[
a:=\frac{1}{6p}
\]
and let
\[
k:=\lceil a\rceil,
\]
the smallest integer at least \(a\). Since \(p>0\), \(k\) is a positive integer. Also \(p<1/6\), so \(a>1\). Therefore
\[
a\le k<a+1<2a.
\]
Multiplying by \(p>0\), we get
\[
\frac16=ap\le kp<2ap=\frac13.
\]
Thus \(r:=kp\) lies in \([1/6,1/3]\), so \(B(r)\) holds by hypothesis, and certainly \(r\le1\). By the reduction lemma, \(B(p)\) holds.

Hence \(B(p)\) holds for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

The proof establishes: if k is a positive integer, p in [0,1], r=kp<=1, and B(r) holds, then B(p) holds; consequently, if B(t) holds for every t in [1/6,1/3], then B(t) holds for every t in [0,1/3]. # Original Problem

# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

For \(t\in[0,1]\), define \(B(t)\) to be the following assertion: for every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative real numbers \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) satisfying \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and every family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) of independent Bernoulli\((t)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=t\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=1-t\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge t\right]\ge t.
\]
This proof establishes the following reduction lemma and consequence: if \(k\) is a positive integer, \(p\in[0,1]\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds. Consequently, if \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[0,1/3]\). This is not a complete classification of all probabilities \(p\) in the original problem; it is a conditional reduction and interval-extension consequence.

## Proof

### Reduction lemma

Fix a positive integer \(k\), a number \(p\in[0,1]\), and set \(r=kp\). Assume \(r\le1\) and assume \(B(r)\) holds. We prove \(B(p)\).

Let \(m\) be any positive integer, and let \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\). We must prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\).

Construct the following categorical coupling. For each coordinate \(i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\), choose an independent random variable
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,\ldots,k\}
\]
with
\[
\Pr[C_i=j]=p \quad (1\le j\le k),\qquad \Pr[C_i=0]=1-r.
\]
This is a valid probability distribution because \(p\ge0\), \(1-r\ge0\), and
\[
(1-r)+kp=(1-r)+r=1.
\]

For each nonzero colour \(j\in\{1,\ldots,k\}\), define the total weight of colour \(j\) by
\[
S_j:=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}.
\]
Also define
\[
T:=S_1+\cdots+S_k.
\]
Since each coordinate has exactly one colour, we have
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq 0\}}.
\]

For each \(i\), the indicator \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq0\}}\) is Bernoulli\((r)\), because
\[
\Pr[C_i\neq0]=\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[C_i=j]=kp=r.
\]
These indicators are independent, since the \(C_i\) are independent. Therefore \(T\) is a weighted sum of independent Bernoulli\((r)\) variables with weights summing to \(1\). By \(B(r)\),
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]\ge r. \tag{1}
\]

Now define events
\[
A_j:=\{S_j\ge p\},\qquad 1\le j\le k.
\]
We claim that
\[
\{T\ge r\}\subseteq A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k.
\]
Indeed, if none of the events \(A_j\) occurs, then \(S_j<p\) for every \(j\), so
\[
T=S_1+\cdots+S_k<kp=r.
\]
Thus \(T\ge r\) is impossible unless at least one \(S_j\ge p\). The non-strict threshold \(\ge\) is handled exactly by passing to the strict complement \(S_j<p\).

Using the inclusion above and the union bound,
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]
\le \Pr[A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k]
\le \sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j].
\]
For each fixed \(j\), the random variables \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Hence all \(S_j\) have the same distribution, and in particular
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j]=k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Combining this with (1) gives
\[
r\le k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Since \(k>0\) and \(r=kp\),
\[
\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \frac r k=p.
\]

Finally, \(S_1=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\), and the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Thus \(S_1\) has exactly the same distribution as \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\) for any independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Because \(m\) and the weights \(w_i\) were arbitrary, \(B(p)\) holds.

The endpoint cases are included: probabilities equal to \(0\) are allowed in the categorical distribution; if \(p=0\), then the desired lower bound is \(0\); and if \(r=1\), then colour \(0\) simply has probability \(0\).

### Consequence for \([0,1/3]\)

Assume \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\). We prove \(B(p)\) for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

If \(p=0\), then independent Bernoulli\((0)\) variables are all \(0\) almost surely, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]
Thus \(B(0)\) holds.

If \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(p)\) holds by hypothesis.

It remains to consider \(0<p<1/6\). Set
\[
a:=\frac{1}{6p}
\]
and let
\[
k:=\lceil a\rceil,
\]
the smallest integer at least \(a\). Since \(p>0\), \(k\) is a positive integer. Also \(p<1/6\), so \(a>1\). Therefore
\[
a\le k<a+1<2a.
\]
Multiplying by \(p>0\), we get
\[
\frac16=ap\le kp<2ap=\frac13.
\]
Thus \(r:=kp\) lies in \([1/6,1/3]\), so \(B(r)\) holds by hypothesis, and certainly \(r\le1\). By the reduction lemma, \(B(p)\) holds.

Hence \(B(p)\) holds for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

response:
I did **not** close the full base interval, but I found several rigorous upgrades to the moment-dual toolkit: exact 5th/6th moment formulas, two usable degree-6 certificate families, an exact SDP/Markov–Lukacs formulation for all degree-6 lower polynomials, and a new support-7 structural proof for \(p\le 1/4\).

Below \(q=1-p,\ c=pq,\ d=1-2p\), and \(\mu_j=\mathbb E X^j\).

---

## 1. Exact fifth and sixth moments

For \(X=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p)\), cumulants add and the centered Bernoulli cumulants are

\[
\kappa_2=c,\quad
\kappa_3=cd,\quad
\kappa_4=c(1-6c),
\]
\[
\kappa_5=cd(1-12c),\quad
\kappa_6=c(1-30c+120c^2).
\]

Thus for the weighted sum,

\[
\boxed{\mu_5
=
cd(1-12c)A_5+10c^2d\,A_2A_3.}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mu_6
=
c(1-30c+120c^2)A_6
+15c^2(1-6c)A_2A_4
+10c^2d^2A_3^2
+15c^3A_2^3.
}
\]

Useful constraints in the strict reduced regime include

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad
A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

with strictness in the second unless the weight vector has only one nonzero coordinate.

---

## 2. Degree-5 and degree-6 product-root certificates

Let \(R=(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), \(0<r_i\le q\), and define

\[
F_R(x)=x(x+p)\prod_{i=1}^k(r_i-x),
\qquad
M_R=\max_{0\le x\le q} F_R(x)_+.
\]

Then \(F_R(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\), so

\[
L_R(x)=\frac{F_R(x)}{M_R}
\]

satisfies \(L_R\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\). Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \frac{\mathbb E F_R(X)}{M_R}.
\]

Writing \(e_j=e_j(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), the expectation is

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)
=
\sum_{j=0}^k(-1)^j e_{k-j}\bigl(\mu_{j+2}+p\mu_{j+1}\bigr),
\]

with \(\mu_1=0\).

For \(k=3\), degree \(5\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
-\mu_5
+(e_1-p)\mu_4
+(pe_1-e_2)\mu_3
+(e_3-pe_2)\mu_2.
}
\]

For \(k=4\), degree \(6\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
\mu_6
+(p-e_1)\mu_5
+(e_2-pe_1)\mu_4
+(pe_2-e_3)\mu_3
+(e_4-pe_3)\mu_2.
}
\]

Thus any choice of roots satisfying

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)\ge pM_R
\]

certifies \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\).

A structural obstruction also appears: the leading variance coefficient is

\[
e_k-pe_{k-1}
=
e_k\left(1-p\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\right).
\]

Since \(r_i\le q\),

\[
\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\ge \frac{k}{q}.
\]

Therefore a product-root certificate of this type can have nonnegative leading \(\mu_2\)-coefficient only if

\[
p<\frac{q}{k}
\iff
p<\frac1{k+1}.
\]

So degree \(5\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/4\), and degree \(6\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/5\). They are still useful in non-diffuse/high-moment regimes, but Berry–Esseen or another non-polynomial input remains necessary for diffuse weights.

---

## 3. A new degree-6 square-complement certificate

For arbitrary positive parameters \(a,b,c\), define

\[
h(x)=\left(1-\frac xa\right)
\left(1-\frac xb\right)
\left(1-\frac xc\right)
=
1-\alpha_1x+\alpha_2x^2-\alpha_3x^3,
\]

where

\[
\alpha_1=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c,\quad
\alpha_2=\frac1{ab}+\frac1{ac}+\frac1{bc},\quad
\alpha_3=\frac1{abc}.
\]

Set

\[
L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-h(x)^2.
\]

For \(x\le0\), each factor in \(h\) is at least \(1\), so \(h(x)^2\ge1\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le0\). For \(x>0\), \(h(x)^2\ge0\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le1\). Therefore

\[
L_{a,b,c}\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\]

on \([-p,q]\). Its expectation is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)
={}&
-(\alpha_1^2+2\alpha_2)\mu_2
+2(\alpha_3+\alpha_1\alpha_2)\mu_3 \\
&-(\alpha_2^2+2\alpha_1\alpha_3)\mu_4
+2\alpha_2\alpha_3\mu_5
-\alpha_3^2\mu_6.
\end{aligned}
}
\]

Thus the explicit sufficient condition

\[
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)\ge p
\]

certifies the desired inequality.

In the equal-root subfamily \(a=b=c=t\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E L_t(X)
=
-\frac{15\mu_2}{t^2}
+\frac{20\mu_3}{t^3}
-\frac{15\mu_4}{t^4}
+\frac{6\mu_5}{t^5}
-\frac{\mu_6}{t^6}.
}
\]

This certificate is often better than product-root polynomials in skewed regimes because it does not force a zero at \(-p\); it only forces negativity on the whole negative interval.

---

## 4. Exact degree-6 SDP formulation

All degree-\(\le6\) lower-polynomial certificates can be searched exactly by Markov–Lukacs.

A polynomial \(L\) of degree at most \(6\) is valid if

\[
-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[-p,0],
\]

and

\[
1-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[0,q].
\]

Equivalently, there exist univariate SOS polynomials \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\tau_0,\tau_1\) with

\[
-L(x)=\sigma_0(x)+(x+p)(-x)\sigma_1(x),
\]

\[
1-L(x)=\tau_0(x)+x(q-x)\tau_1(x),
\]

with \(\deg\sigma_0,\deg\tau_0\le6\) and \(\deg\sigma_1,\deg\tau_1\le4\).

Maximizing

\[
\mathbb E L(X)
=
\ell_0+\ell_2\mu_2+\ell_3\mu_3+\ell_4\mu_4+\ell_5\mu_5+\ell_6\mu_6
\]

over this SDP gives the best degree-6 moment certificate for the given moment vector. Any SDP optimum \(\ge p\) proves the desired inequality for that instance.

---

## 5. New structural result: support \(7\) for \(p\le 1/4\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\). Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the losing \(k\)-sets, \(e=|D_2|\), \(h=|D_3|\).

### Case 1: \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists: a losing triple plus the four remaining weights would have total \(<p+4p\le1\), contradiction.

Also, the losing-pair graph is intersecting. If two disjoint losing pairs existed, then those pairs plus the remaining three weights would have total \(<2p+3p\le1\), contradiction. Hence \(e\le6\).

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]

Let

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{27030}{279936}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

### Case 2: \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists: a losing \(4\)-set plus the three remaining weights would have total \(<p+3p\le1\), contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<3p+p\le1\). Thus, by the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(7\) vertices,

\[
e\le12.
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting, because two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<2p+p<1\). Hence by Erdős–Ko–Rado,

\[
h\le15.
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_2'(p)
=
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Thus support \(7\) is fully handled on the lower half of the base interval, \(p\le1/4\).

---

## Main remaining obstruction

The degree-5/6 product-root families are not enough in the upper interval because their leading variance term becomes nonpositive in diffuse regimes. The square-complement degree-6 family is more flexible, but I do not yet have a sign-definite inequality from only

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

and the moment formulas. The most promising next step is to combine:

1. Berry–Esseen for small \(A_3/A_2^{3/2}\);
2. the degree-6 SDP certificate for intermediate moment vectors;
3. structural near-boundary analysis when several weights are close to \(p\).

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

<2p+2p<1.

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives

F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

    Proof sketch: Compare the bad split instance with the good merged instance. The exact difference of the two success probabilities is pq times the difference of the two residual-window probabilities.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
  • Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.
  • Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.
  • Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.
  • Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.
  • Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.
  • Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.

## Known Bottlenecks
  • The only missing part of the candidate classification is sufficiency for every 0 < p < 1/3.
  • Any proof must be sharp at dictator weights and must handle threshold jumps at subset sums.
  • Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.
  • Control threshold jumps/critical antichains in the strict inequality mu_p({w(A)>p})≥p for p<1/3.
  • Find a polynomial or moment-dual lower bound that exploits the Bernoulli moment structure rather than only support bounds.
  • Prove the weighted Bernoulli inequality on the base interval p in [1/6,1/3].
  • Rule out strict reduced counterexamples with support at least 7 in the base interval.
  • Find a higher-degree polynomial/moment certificate that exploits the Bernoulli cumulant structure in the base interval.

Two promising equivalent/stronger targets are: a fractional-cover theorem for down-sets of p-biased measure > 1-p, and the strict-threshold lemma Pr_p[w(S) > p] >= p for p < 1/3.
  Round 2: The main frontier is no longer just the one-threshold boundary inequality; a sharper two-threshold inequality naturally arises from section gluing and would imply the full FC(p) theorem. Run one solver on that exact induction/dual inequality and one solver on an orthogonal moment-polynomial proof attempt.
  Round 3: The two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the original problem, so stop treating it as an easier induction lemma. A new k-colour scaling lemma reduces the missing range p <= 1/3 to the compact base interval [1/6,1/3]. Next priority: prove the base interval, ideally by ruling out support >= 7 counterexamples or by finding a polynomial certificate valid there.

<full_solver_output task_id="t6_high_degree_moment_base">
I did **not** close the full base interval, but I found several rigorous upgrades to the moment-dual toolkit: exact 5th/6th moment formulas, two usable degree-6 certificate families, an exact SDP/Markov–Lukacs formulation for all degree-6 lower polynomials, and a new support-7 structural proof for \(p\le 1/4\).

Below \(q=1-p,\ c=pq,\ d=1-2p\), and \(\mu_j=\mathbb E X^j\).

---

## 1. Exact fifth and sixth moments

For \(X=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p)\), cumulants add and the centered Bernoulli cumulants are

\[
\kappa_2=c,\quad
\kappa_3=cd,\quad
\kappa_4=c(1-6c),
\]
\[
\kappa_5=cd(1-12c),\quad
\kappa_6=c(1-30c+120c^2).
\]

Thus for the weighted sum,

\[
\boxed{\mu_5
=
cd(1-12c)A_5+10c^2d\,A_2A_3.}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mu_6
=
c(1-30c+120c^2)A_6
+15c^2(1-6c)A_2A_4
+10c^2d^2A_3^2
+15c^3A_2^3.
}
\]

Useful constraints in the strict reduced regime include

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad
A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

with strictness in the second unless the weight vector has only one nonzero coordinate.

---

## 2. Degree-5 and degree-6 product-root certificates

Let \(R=(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), \(0<r_i\le q\), and define

\[
F_R(x)=x(x+p)\prod_{i=1}^k(r_i-x),
\qquad
M_R=\max_{0\le x\le q} F_R(x)_+.
\]

Then \(F_R(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\), so

\[
L_R(x)=\frac{F_R(x)}{M_R}
\]

satisfies \(L_R\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\). Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \frac{\mathbb E F_R(X)}{M_R}.
\]

Writing \(e_j=e_j(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), the expectation is

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)
=
\sum_{j=0}^k(-1)^j e_{k-j}\bigl(\mu_{j+2}+p\mu_{j+1}\bigr),
\]

with \(\mu_1=0\).

For \(k=3\), degree \(5\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
-\mu_5
+(e_1-p)\mu_4
+(pe_1-e_2)\mu_3
+(e_3-pe_2)\mu_2.
}
\]

For \(k=4\), degree \(6\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
\mu_6
+(p-e_1)\mu_5
+(e_2-pe_1)\mu_4
+(pe_2-e_3)\mu_3
+(e_4-pe_3)\mu_2.
}
\]

Thus any choice of roots satisfying

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)\ge pM_R
\]

certifies \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\).

A structural obstruction also appears: the leading variance coefficient is

\[
e_k-pe_{k-1}
=
e_k\left(1-p\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\right).
\]

Since \(r_i\le q\),

\[
\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\ge \frac{k}{q}.
\]

Therefore a product-root certificate of this type can have nonnegative leading \(\mu_2\)-coefficient only if

\[
p<\frac{q}{k}
\iff
p<\frac1{k+1}.
\]

So degree \(5\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/4\), and degree \(6\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/5\). They are still useful in non-diffuse/high-moment regimes, but Berry–Esseen or another non-polynomial input remains necessary for diffuse weights.

---

## 3. A new degree-6 square-complement certificate

For arbitrary positive parameters \(a,b,c\), define

\[
h(x)=\left(1-\frac xa\right)
\left(1-\frac xb\right)
\left(1-\frac xc\right)
=
1-\alpha_1x+\alpha_2x^2-\alpha_3x^3,
\]

where

\[
\alpha_1=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c,\quad
\alpha_2=\frac1{ab}+\frac1{ac}+\frac1{bc},\quad
\alpha_3=\frac1{abc}.
\]

Set

\[
L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-h(x)^2.
\]

For \(x\le0\), each factor in \(h\) is at least \(1\), so \(h(x)^2\ge1\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le0\). For \(x>0\), \(h(x)^2\ge0\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le1\). Therefore

\[
L_{a,b,c}\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\]

on \([-p,q]\). Its expectation is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)
={}&
-(\alpha_1^2+2\alpha_2)\mu_2
+2(\alpha_3+\alpha_1\alpha_2)\mu_3 \\
&-(\alpha_2^2+2\alpha_1\alpha_3)\mu_4
+2\alpha_2\alpha_3\mu_5
-\alpha_3^2\mu_6.
\end{aligned}
}
\]

Thus the explicit sufficient condition

\[
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)\ge p
\]

certifies the desired inequality.

In the equal-root subfamily \(a=b=c=t\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E L_t(X)
=
-\frac{15\mu_2}{t^2}
+\frac{20\mu_3}{t^3}
-\frac{15\mu_4}{t^4}
+\frac{6\mu_5}{t^5}
-\frac{\mu_6}{t^6}.
}
\]

This certificate is often better than product-root polynomials in skewed regimes because it does not force a zero at \(-p\); it only forces negativity on the whole negative interval.

---

## 4. Exact degree-6 SDP formulation

All degree-\(\le6\) lower-polynomial certificates can be searched exactly by Markov–Lukacs.

A polynomial \(L\) of degree at most \(6\) is valid if

\[
-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[-p,0],
\]

and

\[
1-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[0,q].
\]

Equivalently, there exist univariate SOS polynomials \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\tau_0,\tau_1\) with

\[
-L(x)=\sigma_0(x)+(x+p)(-x)\sigma_1(x),
\]

\[
1-L(x)=\tau_0(x)+x(q-x)\tau_1(x),
\]

with \(\deg\sigma_0,\deg\tau_0\le6\) and \(\deg\sigma_1,\deg\tau_1\le4\).

Maximizing

\[
\mathbb E L(X)
=
\ell_0+\ell_2\mu_2+\ell_3\mu_3+\ell_4\mu_4+\ell_5\mu_5+\ell_6\mu_6
\]

over this SDP gives the best degree-6 moment certificate for the given moment vector. Any SDP optimum \(\ge p\) proves the desired inequality for that instance.

---

## 5. New structural result: support \(7\) for \(p\le 1/4\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\). Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the losing \(k\)-sets, \(e=|D_2|\), \(h=|D_3|\).

### Case 1: \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists: a losing triple plus the four remaining weights would have total \(<p+4p\le1\), contradiction.

Also, the losing-pair graph is intersecting. If two disjoint losing pairs existed, then those pairs plus the remaining three weights would have total \(<2p+3p\le1\), contradiction. Hence \(e\le6\).

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]

Let

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{27030}{279936}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

### Case 2: \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists: a losing \(4\)-set plus the three remaining weights would have total \(<p+3p\le1\), contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<3p+p\le1\). Thus, by the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(7\) vertices,

\[
e\le12.
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting, because two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<2p+p<1\). Hence by Erdős–Ko–Rado,

\[
h\le15.
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_2'(p)
=
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Thus support \(7\) is fully handled on the lower half of the base interval, \(p\le1/4\).

---

## Main remaining obstruction

The degree-5/6 product-root families are not enough in the upper interval because their leading variance term becomes nonpositive in diffuse regimes. The square-complement degree-6 family is more flexible, but I do not yet have a sign-definite inequality from only

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

and the moment formulas. The most promising next step is to combine:

1. Berry–Esseen for small \(A_3/A_2^{3/2}\);
2. the degree-6 SDP certificate for intermediate moment vectors;
3. structural near-boundary analysis when several weights are close to \(p\).

## Task t5_bootstrap_base_combinatorial — Exploit the bootstrap reduction and attack the base interval [1/6,1/3] by combinatorial and minimal-counterexample methods.
**Proven results:**
  • If B(kp) holds for an integer k with kp <= 1, then B(p) holds. — Generate Bernoulli(kp) variables and randomly colour each success with one of k colours. Each colour class has iid Bernoulli(p) indicators, and if the total selected weight is at least kp then some colour has weight at least p.
  • In the strict reduced regime, support n=7 satisfies Pr[sum w_i v_i > p] > p for all 1/6 <= p <= 1/4. — Count losing layers. The resulting binomial-polynomial bounds exceed p.
  • In the strict reduced regime, support n=8 satisfies Pr[sum w_i v_i > p] > p for all 1/6 <= p <= 1/5. — There are no losing 4-sets; losing triples are intersecting, and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2. Erdős–Ko–Rado and Erdős–Gallai give d3 <= 21 and d2 <= 13, and the resulting lower bound is larger than p.
  • For n=7 and 1/4 <= p <= 1/3, the strict inequality holds whenever the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2. — Losing triples are intersecting, losing 4-sets are 2-intersecting, and the assumed matching bound controls losing pairs.   • For any losing k-uniform family H, its degree sequence must satisfy p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j) + (1-sp)d_(s+1) <= p|H|, where s=floor(1/p). — Sum the inequalities w(A) <= p over A in H, then minimize the weighted degree sum over weights w_i < p summing to 1 by filling the smallest degrees first.
  • In a minimal strict counterexample, every mergeable pair x <= y with x+y < p satisfies Pr[p-x<R<=p] < Pr[p-x-y<R<=p-y], where R is the residual sum. — Compare the bad split instance with the good merged instance. The exact difference of the two success probabilities is pq times the difference of the two residual-window probabilities.
