Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

## Task t6_high_degree_moment_base — Search for degree 5/6 polynomial or moment certificates valid on the base interval [1/6,1/3].
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the full base interval, but I found several rigorous upgrades to the moment-dual toolkit: exact 5th/6th moment formulas, two usable degree-6 certificate families, an exact SDP/Markov–Lukacs formulation for all degree-6 lower polynomials, and a new support-7 structural proof for \(p\le 1/4\).

Below \(q=1-p,\ c=pq,\ d=1-2p\), and \(\mu_j=\mathbb E X^j\).

---

## 1. Exact fifth and sixth moments

For \(X=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p)\), cumulants add and the centered Bernoulli cumulants are

\[
\kappa_2=c,\quad
\kappa_3=cd,\quad
\kappa_4=c(1-6c),
\]
\[
\kappa_5=cd(1-12c),\quad
\kappa_6=c(1-30c+120c^2).
\]

Thus for the weighted sum,

\[
\boxed{\mu_5
=
cd(1-12c)A_5+10c^2d\,A_2A_3.}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mu_6
=
c(1-30c+120c^2)A_6
+15c^2(1-6c)A_2A_4
+10c^2d^2A_3^2
+15c^3A_2^3.
}
\]

Useful constraints in the strict reduced regime include

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad
A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

with strictness in the second unless the weight vector has only one nonzero coordinate.

---

## 2. Degree-5 and degree-6 product-root certificates

Let \(R=(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), \(0<r_i\le q\), and define

\[
F_R(x)=x(x+p)\prod_{i=1}^k(r_i-x),
\qquad
M_R=\max_{0\le x\le q} F_R(x)_+.
\]

Then \(F_R(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\), so

\[
L_R(x)=\frac{F_R(x)}{M_R}
\]

satisfies \(L_R\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\). Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \frac{\mathbb E F_R(X)}{M_R}.
\]

Writing \(e_j=e_j(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), the expectation is

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)
=
\sum_{j=0}^k(-1)^j e_{k-j}\bigl(\mu_{j+2}+p\mu_{j+1}\bigr),
\]

with \(\mu_1=0\).

For \(k=3\), degree \(5\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
-\mu_5
+(e_1-p)\mu_4
+(pe_1-e_2)\mu_3
+(e_3-pe_2)\mu_2.
}
\]

For \(k=4\), degree \(6\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
\mu_6
+(p-e_1)\mu_5
+(e_2-pe_1)\mu_4
+(pe_2-e_3)\mu_3
+(e_4-pe_3)\mu_2.
}
\]

Thus any choice of roots satisfying

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)\ge pM_R
\]

certifies \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\).

A structural obstruction also appears: the leading variance coefficient is

\[
e_k-pe_{k-1}
=
e_k\left(1-p\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\right).
\]

Since \(r_i\le q\),

\[
\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\ge \frac{k}{q}.
\]

Therefore a product-root certificate of this type can have nonnegative leading \(\mu_2\)-coefficient only if

\[
p<\frac{q}{k}
\iff
p<\frac1{k+1}.
\]

So degree \(5\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/4\), and degree \(6\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/5\). They are still useful in non-diffuse/high-moment regimes, but Berry–Esseen or another non-polynomial input remains necessary for diffuse weights.

---

## 3. A new degree-6 square-complement certificate

For arbitrary positive parameters \(a,b,c\), define

\[
h(x)=\left(1-\frac xa\right)
\left(1-\frac xb\right)
\left(1-\frac xc\right)
=
1-\alpha_1x+\alpha_2x^2-\alpha_3x^3,
\]

where

\[
\alpha_1=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c,\quad
\alpha_2=\frac1{ab}+\frac1{ac}+\frac1{bc},\quad
\alpha_3=\frac1{abc}.
\]

Set

\[
L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-h(x)^2.
\]

For \(x\le0\), each factor in \(h\) is at least \(1\), so \(h(x)^2\ge1\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le0\). For \(x>0\), \(h(x)^2\ge0\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le1\). Therefore

\[
L_{a,b,c}\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\]

on \([-p,q]\). Its expectation is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)
={}&
-(\alpha_1^2+2\alpha_2)\mu_2
+2(\alpha_3+\alpha_1\alpha_2)\mu_3 \\
&-(\alpha_2^2+2\alpha_1\alpha_3)\mu_4
+2\alpha_2\alpha_3\mu_5
-\alpha_3^2\mu_6.
\end{aligned}
}
\]

Thus the explicit sufficient condition

\[
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)\ge p
\]

certifies the desired inequality.

In the equal-root subfamily \(a=b=c=t\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E L_t(X)
=
-\frac{15\mu_2}{t^2}
+\frac{20\mu_3}{t^3}
-\frac{15\mu_4}{t^4}
+\frac{6\mu_5}{t^5}
-\frac{\mu_6}{t^6}.
}
\]

This certificate is often better than product-root polynomials in skewed regimes because it does not force a zero at \(-p\); it only forces negativity on the whole negative interval.

---

## 4. Exact degree-6 SDP formulation

All degree-\(\le6\) lower-polynomial certificates can be searched exactly by Markov–Lukacs.

A polynomial \(L\) of degree at most \(6\) is valid if

\[
-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[-p,0],
\]

and

\[
1-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[0,q].
\]

Equivalently, there exist univariate SOS polynomials \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\tau_0,\tau_1\) with

\[
-L(x)=\sigma_0(x)+(x+p)(-x)\sigma_1(x),
\]

\[
1-L(x)=\tau_0(x)+x(q-x)\tau_1(x),
\]

with \(\deg\sigma_0,\deg\tau_0\le6\) and \(\deg\sigma_1,\deg\tau_1\le4\).

Maximizing

\[
\mathbb E L(X)
=
\ell_0+\ell_2\mu_2+\ell_3\mu_3+\ell_4\mu_4+\ell_5\mu_5+\ell_6\mu_6
\]

over this SDP gives the best degree-6 moment certificate for the given moment vector. Any SDP optimum \(\ge p\) proves the desired inequality for that instance.

---

## 5. New structural result: support \(7\) for \(p\le 1/4\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\). Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the losing \(k\)-sets, \(e=|D_2|\), \(h=|D_3|\).

### Case 1: \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists: a losing triple plus the four remaining weights would have total \(<p+4p\le1\), contradiction.

Also, the losing-pair graph is intersecting. If two disjoint losing pairs existed, then those pairs plus the remaining three weights would have total \(<2p+3p\le1\), contradiction. Hence \(e\le6\).

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]

Let

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{27030}{279936}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

### Case 2: \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists: a losing \(4\)-set plus the three remaining weights would have total \(<p+3p\le1\), contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<3p+p\le1\). Thus, by the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(7\) vertices,

\[
e\le12.
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting, because two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<2p+p<1\). Hence by Erdős–Ko–Rado,

\[
h\le15.
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_2'(p)
=
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Thus support \(7\) is fully handled on the lower half of the base interval, \(p\le1/4\).

---

## Main remaining obstruction

The degree-5/6 product-root families are not enough in the upper interval because their leading variance term becomes nonpositive in diffuse regimes. The square-complement degree-6 family is more flexible, but I do not yet have a sign-definite inequality from only

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

and the moment formulas. The most promising next step is to combine:

1. Berry–Esseen for small \(A_3/A_2^{3/2}\);
2. the degree-6 SDP certificate for intermediate moment vectors;
3. structural near-boundary analysis when several weights are close to \(p\).

## t3 progress report: moment/polynomial certificates + support-six closure

Let \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and \(X=S-p\in[-p,q]\). I worked in the strict-reduced regime \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\). The full general \(p<1/3\) case is not closed here, but I obtained several reusable polynomial-dual certificates and a complete proof for support size \(6\), extending the prior support-\(\le5\) result.

---

## 1. A useful cubic lower-polynomial certificate

For \(p<r\le q\), define

\[
f_r(x)=x(x+p)(r-x).
\]

On \([-p,0]\), \(f_r(x)\le0\). On \([0,r]\), \(f_r(x)\ge0\), and on \([r,q]\), \(f_r(x)\le0\). Hence, if

\[
M_3(r)=\max_{0\le x\le r} x(x+p)(r-x),
\]

then

\[
L_r(x)=\frac{f_r(x)}{M_3(r)}
\]

satisfies

\[
L_r(x)\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\qquad\text{for all }x\in[-p,q].
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \mathbb E L_r(X)
=\frac{(r-p)\mu_2-\mu_3}{M_3(r)},
\]

where

\[
\mu_2=pqA_2,\qquad \mu_3=pq(1-2p)A_3.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\Pr[X>0]\ge
\frac{pq\big((r-p)A_2-(1-2p)A_3\big)}{M_3(r)}.
}
\]

The maximizing point is explicit:

\[
x_r=\frac{r-p+\sqrt{r^2+rp+p^2}}{3},
\qquad
M_3(r)=x_r(x_r+p)(r-x_r).
\]

Thus the cubic certificate proves the desired strict bound whenever

\[
q\big((r-p)A_2-(1-2p)A_3\big)\ge M_3(r)
\]

for some \(r\in(p,q]\).

---

## 2. A quartic two-root certificate

For \(0<r\le s\le q\), define

\[
f_{r,s}(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)(s-x).
\]

Again \(f_{r,s}(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\). On \([0,q]\), scale by

\[
M_{r,s}=\max_{0\le x\le q} f_{r,s}(x)_+.
\]

Then

\[
L_{r,s}(x)=\frac{f_{r,s}(x)}{M_{r,s}}
\]

is a valid lower polynomial:

\[
L_{r,s}(x)\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\qquad (x\in[-p,q]).
\]

Its expectation is

\[
\mathbb E f_{r,s}(X)
=
\mu_4+(p-r-s)\mu_3+\big(rs-p(r+s)\big)\mu_2.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\Pr[X>0]\ge
\frac{
\mu_4+(p-r-s)\mu_3+\big(rs-p(r+s)\big)\mu_2
}{
M_{r,s}
}.
}
\]

A particularly clean subfamily is \(s=q\), giving zeros at \(-p,0,r,q\):

\[
f_{r,q}(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)(q-x),
\]

and

\[
\mathbb E f_{r,q}(X)
=
\mu_4-(r+1-2p)\mu_3+\big(r(1-2p)-pq\big)\mu_2.
\]

After substituting the Bernoulli moments,

\[
\mu_4
=
3p^2q^2A_2^2+pq(1-6pq)A_4,
\]

the main obstruction is visible: for most of \(p\in(0,1/3)\), the coefficient \(1-6pq\) is negative, while the \(\mu_3\)-coefficient in the \(s=q\) subfamily is also negative. So this quartic ansatz gives good certificates in some parameter ranges but does not become sign-definite from the elementary constraints \(A_3\le pA_2\), \(A_4\le p^2A_2\).

---

## 3. Diffuse case via Berry–Esseen

Let

\[
\sigma^2=\operatorname{Var}(X)=pqA_2.
\]

For \(Y_i=w_i(v_i-p)\),

\[
\sum_i \mathbb E |Y_i|^3
=
pq(p^2+q^2)A_3.
\]

By Berry–Esseen, with any admissible absolute constant \(C_{\mathrm{BE}}\),

\[
\sup_x\left|
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X}{\sigma}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)
\right|
\le
C_{\mathrm{BE}}\frac{pq(p^2+q^2)A_3}{(pqA_2)^{3/2}}.
\]

Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]
\ge
\frac12
-
C_{\mathrm{BE}}
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}.
\]

Therefore the desired strict inequality follows whenever

\[
\boxed{
C_{\mathrm{BE}}
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}
\le
\frac12-p.
}
\]

In particular, since \(A_3\le \lambda A_2\) with \(\lambda=\max_i w_i\), it suffices that

\[
\boxed{
\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{A_2}}
\le
\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{C_{\mathrm{BE}}(p^2+q^2)}.
}
\]

This handles genuinely diffuse weight vectors.

---

## 4. Exact proof for support size \(6\)

Assume now \(n=6\), \(0<w_i<p<1/3\), and \(\sum_i w_i=1\). I prove

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the family of losing \(k\)-sets:

\[
D_k=\{A\subseteq[6]: |A|=k,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

Let

\[
e=|D_2|,\qquad h=|D_3|.
\]

Every set of size at least \(4\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so

\[
w(A)>1-2p>p.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S\le p]
=
q^6+6pq^5+e p^2q^4+h p^3q^3.
\]

We split into ranges.

### Case 1: \(p\le 1/5\)

No losing pair exists. Indeed, if \(w_i+w_j\le p\), then the remaining four weights are each \(<p\), so

\[
1=\sum_i w_i< p+4p\le1,
\]

a contradiction. Since the losing family is a down-set, no losing triple exists either. Hence

\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5=\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(6,p)\ge2].
\]

The function

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-p
\]

has derivative

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

---

There are no two disjoint losing pairs; otherwise two losing pairs plus the remaining two weights would have total

<2p+2p\le1,
\]

\[
e\le5.
\]

Also \(h=0\), because a losing triple plus the remaining three weights would have total

\[
< p+3p\le1.
\]

Thus

\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5-5p^2q^4.
\]

F_2(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-5p^2q^4-p.
\]

Then

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

If \(h=0\), then the losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\), because three disjoint losing pairs would cover all six vertices and have total weight at most \(3p<1\). Therefore, by the matching bound for graphs on six vertices,

e\le10.

\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^6-6pq^5-10p^2q^4.

on \([1/4,1/3]\), and

\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{75}{4096}>0.
\]

Thus \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

---

### Case 4: \(1/4<p<1/3\), with at least one losing triple

Fix a losing triple \(T\). First, any two losing triples must intersect in at least \(2\) points. If two losing triples were disjoint, their total weight would be at most \(2p<1\), impossible. If they intersected in exactly one point, their union would have size \(5\), and adding the remaining weight \(<p\) gives total \(<3p<1\), again impossible.

Therefore the losing triples form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on six vertices, so

\[
h\le4.
\]

Next, no losing pair can lie wholly inside \(T^c\). Such a pair, together with \(T\) and the one remaining vertex, would have total weight \(<3p<1\). Hence losing pairs are either inside \(T\) or cross between \(T\) and \(T^c\). There are at most \(3\) inside \(T\). The crossing graph is bipartite \(3\times3\), and it cannot contain a matching of size \(3\), since that would give three disjoint losing pairs of total weight at most \(3p<1\). Hence it has at most \(6\) edges. Therefore

\[
e\le 3+6=9.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S>p]
\ge
1-q^6-6pq^5-9p^2q^4-4p^3q^3.
\]

Let

\[
F_4(p)=1-q^6-6pq^5-9p^2q^4-4p^3q^3-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_4'(p)=12pq^2-1>0
\]

on \([1/4,1/3]\), and

\[
F_4(1/4)=\frac{48}{4096}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Combining the four cases proves the strict statement for support size \(6\). Together with the prior support-\(\le5\) argument, any strict counterexample must have support at least \(7\).

---

## 5. Main algebraic obstructions hit

### Quadratic lower polynomials are too weak

Let \(L(x)=\alpha x+\beta x^2\). If \(L\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\) on \([-p,q]\), then the best possible positive quadratic coefficient satisfies

\[
\beta\le \frac1q.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathbb E L(X)\le \frac{\mu_2}{q}=pA_2.
\]

In the strict-reduced regime \(A_2< p\), so this gives at most \(p^2\), far below the target \(p\).

### Simple quartic majorants are not sign-definite

The natural complement majorant

\[
M_{a,b}(x)=\left(1-\frac{x}{a}\right)^2
\left(1-\frac{x}{b}\right)^2
\]

satisfies \(M_{a,b}(x)\ge \mathbf 1_{\{x<0\}}\), but

\[
\mathbb E M_{a,b}(X)
=
1+
\frac{
(a^2+b^2+4ab)\mu_2
-2(a+b)\mu_3
+\mu_4
}{a^2b^2}.
\]

To prove \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\), one would need \(\mathbb E M_{a,b}\le 1-p\). The numerator has competing signs, and after substituting the Bernoulli moments the \(A_4\)-coefficient is negative in much of \(p\in(0,1/3)\). I did not find a uniform choice of \(a,b\), even moment-dependent, that closes all cases.

---

## Task t5_bootstrap_base_combinatorial — Exploit the bootstrap reduction and attack the base interval [1/6,1/3] by combinatorial and minimal-counterexample methods.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) in this pass. I did get several clean new reductions/lemmas that shrink the possible counterexample space, especially in the lower two subintervals.

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

---

## Task t6_high_degree_moment_base — Search for degree 5/6 polynomial or moment certificates valid on the base interval [1/6,1/3].
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the full base interval, but I found several rigorous upgrades to the moment-dual toolkit: exact 5th/6th moment formulas, two usable degree-6 certificate families, an exact SDP/Markov–Lukacs formulation for all degree-6 lower polynomials, and a new support-7 structural proof for \(p\le 1/4\).

Below \(q=1-p,\ c=pq,\ d=1-2p\), and \(\mu_j=\mathbb E X^j\).

---

## 1. Exact fifth and sixth moments

For \(X=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p)\), cumulants add and the centered Bernoulli cumulants are

\[
\kappa_2=c,\quad
\kappa_3=cd,\quad
\kappa_4=c(1-6c),
\]
\[
\kappa_5=cd(1-12c),\quad
\kappa_6=c(1-30c+120c^2).
\]

Thus for the weighted sum,

\[
\boxed{\mu_5
=
cd(1-12c)A_5+10c^2d\,A_2A_3.}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mu_6
=
c(1-30c+120c^2)A_6
+15c^2(1-6c)A_2A_4
+10c^2d^2A_3^2
+15c^3A_2^3.
}
\]

Useful constraints in the strict reduced regime include

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad
A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

with strictness in the second unless the weight vector has only one nonzero coordinate.

---

## 2. Degree-5 and degree-6 product-root certificates

Let \(R=(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), \(0<r_i\le q\), and define

\[
F_R(x)=x(x+p)\prod_{i=1}^k(r_i-x),
\qquad
M_R=\max_{0\le x\le q} F_R(x)_+.
\]

Then \(F_R(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\), so

\[
L_R(x)=\frac{F_R(x)}{M_R}
\]

satisfies \(L_R\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\). Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \frac{\mathbb E F_R(X)}{M_R}.
\]

Writing \(e_j=e_j(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), the expectation is

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)
=
\sum_{j=0}^k(-1)^j e_{k-j}\bigl(\mu_{j+2}+p\mu_{j+1}\bigr),
\]

with \(\mu_1=0\).

For \(k=3\), degree \(5\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
-\mu_5
+(e_1-p)\mu_4
+(pe_1-e_2)\mu_3
+(e_3-pe_2)\mu_2.
}
\]

For \(k=4\), degree \(6\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
\mu_6
+(p-e_1)\mu_5
+(e_2-pe_1)\mu_4
+(pe_2-e_3)\mu_3
+(e_4-pe_3)\mu_2.
}
\]

Thus any choice of roots satisfying

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)\ge pM_R
\]

certifies \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\).

A structural obstruction also appears: the leading variance coefficient is

\[
e_k-pe_{k-1}
=
e_k\left(1-p\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\right).
\]

Since \(r_i\le q\),

\[
\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\ge \frac{k}{q}.
\]

Therefore a product-root certificate of this type can have nonnegative leading \(\mu_2\)-coefficient only if

\[
p<\frac{q}{k}
\iff
p<\frac1{k+1}.
\]

So degree \(5\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/4\), and degree \(6\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/5\). They are still useful in non-diffuse/high-moment regimes, but Berry–Esseen or another non-polynomial input remains necessary for diffuse weights.

---

## 3. A new degree-6 square-complement certificate

For arbitrary positive parameters \(a,b,c\), define

\[
h(x)=\left(1-\frac xa\right)
\left(1-\frac xb\right)
\left(1-\frac xc\right)
=
1-\alpha_1x+\alpha_2x^2-\alpha_3x^3,
\]

where

\[
\alpha_1=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c,\quad
\alpha_2=\frac1{ab}+\frac1{ac}+\frac1{bc},\quad
\alpha_3=\frac1{abc}.
\]

Set

\[
L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-h(x)^2.
\]

For \(x\le0\), each factor in \(h\) is at least \(1\), so \(h(x)^2\ge1\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le0\). For \(x>0\), \(h(x)^2\ge0\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le1\). Therefore

\[
L_{a,b,c}\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\]

on \([-p,q]\). Its expectation is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)
={}&
-(\alpha_1^2+2\alpha_2)\mu_2
+2(\alpha_3+\alpha_1\alpha_2)\mu_3 \\
&-(\alpha_2^2+2\alpha_1\alpha_3)\mu_4
+2\alpha_2\alpha_3\mu_5
-\alpha_3^2\mu_6.
\end{aligned}
}
\]

Thus the explicit sufficient condition

\[
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)\ge p
\]

certifies the desired inequality.

In the equal-root subfamily \(a=b=c=t\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E L_t(X)
=
-\frac{15\mu_2}{t^2}
+\frac{20\mu_3}{t^3}
-\frac{15\mu_4}{t^4}
+\frac{6\mu_5}{t^5}
-\frac{\mu_6}{t^6}.
}
\]

This certificate is often better than product-root polynomials in skewed regimes because it does not force a zero at \(-p\); it only forces negativity on the whole negative interval.

---

## 4. Exact degree-6 SDP formulation

All degree-\(\le6\) lower-polynomial certificates can be searched exactly by Markov–Lukacs.

A polynomial \(L\) of degree at most \(6\) is valid if

\[
-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[-p,0],
\]

and

\[
1-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[0,q].
\]

Equivalently, there exist univariate SOS polynomials \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\tau_0,\tau_1\) with

\[
-L(x)=\sigma_0(x)+(x+p)(-x)\sigma_1(x),
\]

\[
1-L(x)=\tau_0(x)+x(q-x)\tau_1(x),
\]

with \(\deg\sigma_0,\deg\tau_0\le6\) and \(\deg\sigma_1,\deg\tau_1\le4\).

Maximizing

\[
\mathbb E L(X)
=
\ell_0+\ell_2\mu_2+\ell_3\mu_3+\ell_4\mu_4+\ell_5\mu_5+\ell_6\mu_6
\]

over this SDP gives the best degree-6 moment certificate for the given moment vector. Any SDP optimum \(\ge p\) proves the desired inequality for that instance.

---

## 5. New structural result: support \(7\) for \(p\le 1/4\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\). Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the losing \(k\)-sets, \(e=|D_2|\), \(h=|D_3|\).

### Case 1: \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists: a losing triple plus the four remaining weights would have total \(<p+4p\le1\), contradiction.

Also, the losing-pair graph is intersecting. If two disjoint losing pairs existed, then those pairs plus the remaining three weights would have total \(<2p+3p\le1\), contradiction. Hence \(e\le6\).

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]

Let

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{27030}{279936}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

### Case 2: \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists: a losing \(4\)-set plus the three remaining weights would have total \(<p+3p\le1\), contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<3p+p\le1\). Thus, by the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(7\) vertices,

\[
e\le12.
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting, because two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<2p+p<1\). Hence by Erdős–Ko–Rado,

\[
h\le15.
\]

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]

Let

\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_2'(p)
=
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]

on \([1/5,1/4]\), and

\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]

So again \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

Thus support \(7\) is fully handled on the lower half of the base interval, \(p\le1/4\).

---

## Main remaining obstruction

The degree-5/6 product-root families are not enough in the upper interval because their leading variance term becomes nonpositive in diffuse regimes. The square-complement degree-6 family is more flexible, but I do not yet have a sign-definite inequality from only

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

and the moment formulas. The most promising next step is to combine:

1. Berry–Esseen for small \(A_3/A_2^{3/2}\);
2. the degree-6 SDP certificate for intermediate moment vectors;
3. structural near-boundary analysis when several weights are close to \(p\).

response:
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for cyclic intervals in §2.3.**  
   Let the cyclic intervals be \(I_t=\{t,t+1,t+2\}\) modulo \(N\), with \(N\ge 6\). Suppose \(\mathcal G\) is a pairwise-intersecting subfamily of these intervals. If \(\mathcal G=\varnothing\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise rotate so \(I_0\in\mathcal G\). Any interval meeting \(I_0\) must be one of
   \[
   I_{-2}, I_{-1}, I_0, I_1, I_2.
   \]
   Moreover \(I_{-2}\cap I_1=\varnothing\) and \(I_{-1}\cap I_2=\varnothing\), for all \(N\ge 6\). Hence among the four intervals \(I_{-2},I_{-1},I_1,I_2\), a pairwise-intersecting subfamily has size at most \(2\). Including \(I_0\), we get \(|\mathcal G|\le 3\). This justifies the double-counting proof of
   \[
   |\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
   \]

2. **Patch for compressed graph details in §2.2.**  
   In the low-degree lemma, if a graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices has matching number at most \(2\) and minimum degree at least \(3\), then \(|E(G)|\ge 3N/2>N-1\). By the earlier intersecting-graph bound, \(G\) cannot have all edges pairwise intersecting; hence \(G\) contains two disjoint edges \(ab\) and \(cd\).

   With \(U=\{a,b,c,d\}\) and \(W=V(G)\setminus U\), \(W\) is independent. For distinct \(w,w'\in W\), each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). If neither matched pair \(\{a,b\}\), \(\{c,d\}\) allowed \(w,w'\) to be matched to distinct endpoints, then for each pair the two vertices would have exactly the same single neighbor in that pair. This would give each of \(w,w'\) only \(2\) neighbors in \(U\), contradiction. Therefore one matched pair supplies two disjoint edges incident to \(w,w'\), and the other matched pair supplies a third disjoint edge.

   Finally, whenever the proof says that a 4-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges, this follows from the already-proved intersecting-graph bound: a graph on \(4\) vertices with no two disjoint edges has at most \(3\) edges.

After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes the following partial theorem.

Let \(0<p<1\), \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive weights with \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\). Let \(v_i\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables and \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\). For \(A\subseteq[n]\), let \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\), and call \(A\) losing if \(w(A)\le p\). Then:

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
4. For any losing \(k\)-uniform family \(\mathcal H\), with degree sequence sorted as \(d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}\), and \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]
5. In a minimal-positive-support strict counterexample to \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\), if two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and \(R\) is the residual random sum over the other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

The proof does not solve the original problem for all \(p\) or for arbitrary nonnegative weights.
</PROBLEM_SOLVED>

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[

a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.

F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.

Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

## Task t2_strict_threshold_minimal — Try to prove strict-threshold lemma for p<1/3 via threshold-family/minimal-counterexample analysis

$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Status

I did **not** close the full strict lemma in this pass. I did get a clean reduction that makes the remaining obstruction quite precise: **continuous crossings are impossible**, so any strict counterexample must already occur immediately after a subset-sum threshold is crossed. I also proved the strict lemma for support size at most 5 and recorded two useful obstructions to natural proof attempts.

---

## 1. Basic reductions

Let

\[
X=w(S)=\sum_i w_i v_i,\qquad v_i\sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p),
\]

independently, with \(w_i\ge 0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\).

If some \(w_i>p\), then

\[
\{i\in S\}\subseteq \{w(S)>p\},
\]

so

\[
\Pr[w(S)>p]\ge \Pr[i\in S]=p.
\]

Thus any **strict counterexample** must have

\[
0<w_i\le p\quad\text{for every }i.
\]

In particular, for \(p<1/3\), such a counterexample has support size at least \(4\).

---

## 2. The fixed-family crossing lemma

For an increasing family \(\mathcal F\subseteq 2^{[n]}\), write

\[
\mu_t(\mathcal F)=\sum_{A\in \mathcal F} t^{|A|}(1-t)^{n-|A|}.
\]

Let

\[
I_t(\mathcal F)=\frac{d}{dt}\mu_t(\mathcal F)
\]

be the total \(t\)-biased influence.

### Lemma 2.1: biased edge-isoperimetry at the crossing scale

For every increasing \(\mathcal F\),

\[
t I_t(\mathcal F)\ge \mu_t(\mathcal F)\log_t\mu_t(\mathcal F).
\]

In particular, if \(\mu_t(\mathcal F)=t\), then

\[
I_t(\mathcal F)\ge 1.
\]

#### Proof sketch

Induct on \(n\). Let \(\mathcal F_0,\mathcal F_1\subseteq 2^{[n-1]}\) be the sections with the last coordinate equal to \(0\) and \(1\). Since \(\mathcal F\) is increasing, \(\mathcal F_0\subseteq \mathcal F_1\). Put

\[
a=\mu_t(\mathcal F_0),\qquad b=\mu_t(\mathcal F_1),\qquad \mu=(1-t)a+tb.
\]

The derivative satisfies

\[
I_t(\mathcal F)=(1-t)I_t(\mathcal F_0)+tI_t(\mathcal F_1)+(b-a).
\]

Using induction, it remains to prove the two-point inequality

\[
(1-t)\Phi(a)+t\Phi(b)+t(b-a)\ge \Phi((1-t)a+tb),
\]

where \(\Phi(x)=x\log_t x\). By homogeneity reduce to \(b=1\), \(a=u\), \(v=t+(1-t)u\). After multiplying by \(\log t<0\), this becomes

\[
(1-t)u\log u+t(1-u)\log t-v\log v\le 0.
\]

The left side is convex in \(u\in[0,1]\) and vanishes at \(u=0,1\), hence is nonpositive. ∎

---

## 3. Continuous downward crossings are impossible

Fix weights \(w\). For \(t\in(0,1)\), define

\[
\mathcal F_t=\{S:w(S)>t\},\qquad h(t)=\mu_t(\mathcal F_t)-t.
\]

Between subset-sum values of \(w\), the family \(\mathcal F_t\) is fixed. Suppose on such an interval \(\mathcal F_t=\mathcal F\). Let

\[
r(t)=\frac{\log \mu_t(\mathcal F)}{\log t}.
\]

From Lemma 2.1,

\[
\frac{d}{dt}r(t)\le 0.
\]

Since \(t\in(0,1)\), the inequality \(\mu_t(\mathcal F)\ge t\) is equivalent to \(r(t)\le 1\). Therefore, once \(\mu_t(\mathcal F)\ge t\) holds at one point of an interval avoiding subset sums, it continues to hold to the right throughout that interval.

### Consequence

A strict counterexample cannot arise by a continuous downward crossing. If

\[
\mu_p(\{S:w(S)>p\})<p,
\]

then there is some subset-sum value \(a\le p\) such that already

\[
\mu_a(\{S:w(S)>a\})<a.
\]

Thus it is enough to prove the strict lemma at **threshold boundaries**:

\[
\boxed{\mu_p(\{S:w(S)>p\})\ge p}
\]

for those \(p<1/3\) which are subset sums of \(w\).

Equivalently, if

\[
\mathcal C_p=\{S:w(S)=p\},
\]

then the missing boundary inequality is

\[
\mu_p(\{S:w(S)\ge p\})-\mu_p(\mathcal C_p)\ge p.
\]

The derivative argument gives control of the first term but does not by itself control the jump \(\mu_p(\mathcal C_p)\).

---

## 4. Structure of boundary counterexamples

A minimal boundary counterexample must satisfy:

1. \(0<w_i\le p\) for all \(i\).
2. \(p<1/3\).
3. \(p\) is a subset sum of the weights.
4. The critical family

   \[
   \mathcal C_p=\{S:w(S)=p\}
   \]

   is an antichain.
5. For every \(A\in\mathcal C_p\),

   \[
   w(A^c)=1-p>2p.
   \]

   Hence \(A^c\) contains at least three positive-weight coordinates.

The remaining obstruction is to show that deleting the critical antichain \(\mathcal C_p\) from \(\{w(S)\ge p\}\) cannot drop the \(p\)-biased measure below \(p\).

This is exactly where the proof is still missing.

---

## 5. Strict lemma proved for support size \(\le 5\)

I did prove the strict lemma for support at most \(5\).

### Proposition 5.1

If \(p<1/3\) and \(w\) has support size at most \(5\), then

\[
\Pr_p[w(S)>p]\ge p.
\]

#### Proof

If some \(w_i>p\), we are done. So assume \(w_i\le p\) for all \(i\).

For support size \(n\le 3\), this is impossible because \(\sum_i w_i\le 3p<1\). Thus \(n=4\) or \(5\).

---

### Case \(n=4\)

If some pair \(\{i,j\}\) had

\[
w_i+w_j\le p,
\]

then the complementary pair has weight at most \(2p\), so

\[
1=\sum_i w_i\le p+2p=3p<1,
\]

a contradiction. Hence every pair has weight \(>p\). Therefore

\[
\{w(S)>p\}=\{|S|\ge 2\}.
\]

So

\[
\Pr[w(S)>p]
=1-(1-p)^4-4p(1-p)^3
=6p^2-8p^3+3p^4.
\]

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
6p^2-8p^3+3p^4-p
=p(3p^3-8p^2+6p-1)>0.
\]

Thus the strict inequality holds.

---

### Case \(n=5\)

If \(p<1/4\), the same argument shows every pair has weight \(>p\), because a nonwinning pair plus the complementary triple would have total at most

\[
p+3p=4p<1.
\]

Hence

\[
\Pr[w(S)>p]\ge \Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\ge 2]>p.
\]

If \(p\ge 1/4\), consider the graph \(G\) of nonwinning pairs:

\[
ij\in G\iff w_i+w_j\le p.
\]

Two disjoint nonwinning pairs cannot exist, because together with the fifth weight they would have total at most

\[
p+p+p=3p<1.
\]

So \(G\) is an intersecting graph on five vertices, hence has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore at least \(6\) pairs are winning.

The event \(\{w(S)>p\}\) contains:

- every selected set of size at least \(3\), and
- every selected set which is exactly a winning pair.

Thus

\[
\Pr[w(S)>p]
\ge 6p^2(1-p)^3+\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\ge 3].
\]

A direct expansion gives

\[
6p^2(1-p)^3+10p^3(1-p)^2+5p^4(1-p)+p^5
=6p^2-8p^3+3p^4.
\]

As in the \(n=4\) case, this is \(>p\) for \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\). ∎

So any strict counterexample must have support size at least \(6\).

---

## 6. Reciprocal anchor: non-strict statement at \(p=1/k\)

For completeness, here is the clean cyclic proof of the original non-strict statement when \(p=1/k\).

Let \(p=1/k\). Represent each Bernoulli variable as

\[
v_i=\mathbf 1_{\{Z_i=0\}},
\]

where \(Z_i\) is uniform on \(\mathbb Z/k\mathbb Z\). Partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits:

\[
(z_1,\dots,z_n)\mapsto (z_1+c,\dots,z_n+c).
\]

Each orbit has size \(k\). Along any orbit, each coordinate contributes \(1\) exactly once, so

\[
\sum_{c=0}^{k-1} w(S_c)=\sum_i w_i=1.
\]

Hence the average value along the orbit is \(1/k=p\). Therefore at least one point in each orbit satisfies

\[
w(S_c)\ge p.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[w(S)\ge p]\ge \frac1k=p.
\]

This proves the original non-strict statement at reciprocal parameters \(p=1/k\).

---

## 7. Why the obvious cyclic extension fails

For rational \(p=a/b\), one might try the same orbit argument with a success interval of length \(a\) in \(\mathbb Z/b\mathbb Z\). One would need every orbit to contain at least \(a\) shifts with value at least \(a/b\). That is false.

Example: take \(p=2/7\), four weights \(1/4,1/4,1/4,1/4\), and place them on \(\mathbb Z/7\mathbb Z\) at positions

\[
0,1,3,5.
\]

The length-\(2\) cyclic window sums are

\[
\frac12,\frac14,\frac14,\frac14,\frac14,\frac14,\frac14.
\]

Only one of the seven shifts is at least \(2/7\), not two. So the reciprocal cyclic proof does not extend orbitwise to general rational \(p\).

---

## 8. Exact remaining obstruction

The strict lemma for \(p<1/3\) is now reduced to the following boundary/knapsack extremal statement.

### Boundary knapsack inequality

Let \(0<p<1/3\). Let \(w_i\in(0,p]\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\). Define the down-set

\[
\mathcal D_p=\{S:w(S)\le p\}.
\]

Prove

\[
\boxed{\mu_p(\mathcal D_p)\le 1-p.}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{\mu_p(\{S:w(S)>p\})\ge p.}
\]

A plausible sharper extremal form is:

\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D_p)
\le (1-p)^m+mp(1-p)^{m-1},
\qquad m=\lceil 1/p\rceil,
\]

with extremal candidate given by \(m\) chunks of weights as close to \(p\) as possible. This sharper inequality would immediately imply the strict lemma, since

\[
1-(1-p)^m-mp(1-p)^{m-1}
=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(m,p)\ge 2]\ge p
\]

for \(m\ge 4\), i.e. for \(p<1/3\).

The main technical obstruction is proving this weighted down-set extremal inequality. Simple local merging/splitting operations are not monotone, and simple charging of critical sets to winning supersets fails: for \(p=1/4\) and four weights \(1/4\), the critical singleton mass is larger than the total strict winning mass, even though the strict inequality itself holds.

So the remaining problem is not the continuous-threshold part; it is a genuinely finite boundary extremal problem for weighted knapsack down-sets under \(p\)-biased measure.

\[
D=\{A:w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_k=D\cap {[n]\choose k}.
\]

## 1. The naive universal good-merge statement is false

The statement “for arbitrary reduced weights there is always a mergeable pair with
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]\ge \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\]
” is false.

Take \(p=0.3\), \(n=7\), and \(w_i=1/7\) for all \(i\). Every pair is mergeable, since \(2/7<p\). For any pair \(x=y=1/7\), the residual is \(R=K/7\), \(K\sim \mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\). Then
\[
(p-x,p]=(0.157\ldots,0.3]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=2\), while
\[
(p-x-y,p-y]=(0.014\ldots,0.157\ldots]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=1\). Hence
\[
\frac{\Pr[p-x<R\le p]}{\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]}
=\frac{\binom52p^2q^3}{\binom51pq^4}
=\frac{2p}{q}<1
\]
for every \(p<1/3\). Thus **all mergeable pairs are bad** in the merge sense. But this vector is not a counterexample: for \(p=0.3\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(7,0.3)\ge 3]\approx 0.353>0.3.
\]

So the missing ingredient cannot be a purely local merge statement. One must use the actual counterexample hypothesis \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) quantitatively.

---

## 2. A useful conditional good-merge lemma for the two smallest weights

Let \(a\le b\) be the two smallest weights, with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum. Define
\[
L=\{T:p-a-b<w(T)\le p-b\},
\]
\[
U=\{A:p-a<w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The merge is good exactly when \(\mu_p(U)\ge \mu_p(L)\).

For \(T\in L\), define its losing upper shadow
\[
\Gamma(T)=\{k\notin T:\ w(T)+w_k\le p\}.
\]
Because \(a,b\) are the two smallest weights, every residual weight is at least \(b\). Thus if \(T\in L\) and \(k\in \Gamma(T)\), then
\[
w(T)+w_k>p-a-b+b=p-a
\]
and also \(w(T)+w_k\le p\), so
\[
T\cup\{k\}\in U.
\]

Let \(K\) be the maximum number of lower-shadow predecessors of any \(A\in U\), i.e.
\[
K=\max_{A\in U}|\{k\in A:A\setminus\{k\}\in L\}|.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_p(U)\ge \frac{p}{qK}\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T).
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
\ge \frac{q}{p}K,
\]
then the pair \(a,b\) is a good merge.

Equivalently, in a minimal counterexample where every mergeable pair is bad, the two-smallest lower window must have unusually poor \(p\)-biased upper-shadow expansion:
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
< \frac{q}{p}K.
\]
For \(p\le 1/3\), the factor \(q/p\ge 2\), so this says the lower window must be very close to maximal losing sets. This is a concrete structural obstruction that any future proof must exploit.

---

## 3. Support \(7\), upper interval closed

I can close the remaining support-\(7\) upper-interval case, including the previous obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs.

### Proposition

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Thus no strict reduced counterexample of support \(7\) exists in the upper base interval.

### Proof

Let \(W\) be the graph of **winning pairs**:
\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Write \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=|D_2|=21-m.
\]

Because every \(w_i<p\), every set of size at least \(5\) is winning:
\[
|A|\ge 5
\implies w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p>p.
\]
So only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) matter.

#### Step 1: losing triples are intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p=3p\le 1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_3\) is intersecting, so by EKR,
\[
d_3\le 15.
\]

#### Step 2: complements of losing triples force winning-pair blockers

Let \(T\in D_3\), and let \(Q=T^c\), so \(|Q|=4\). If \(Q\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(T)+w(P_1)+w(P_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[Q]\) must meet every perfect matching of \(K_4\). A subgraph of \(K_4\) meeting all three perfect matchings contains at least three edges; its minimal blockers are a triangle or a \(3\)-edge star.

This gives the following graph-count bounds for \(d_3\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & d_3\text{ bound}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & d_3=0\\
3\le m\le 4 & d_3\le 4\\
m=5 & d_3\le 10\\
m\ge 6 & d_3\le 15
\end{array}
\]

The \(m=5\) bound is maximized by a \(5\)-edge star, which gives \(\binom53=10\) four-sets whose winning-pair graph contains a \(3\)-star.

#### Step 3: losing four-sets are \(3\)-intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_4\) and \(|A\cap B|\le 2\), then one can choose disjoint triples \(A'\subset A\), \(B'\subset B\). Since every subset of a losing set is losing, this would give two disjoint losing triples, impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is \(3\)-intersecting.

Equivalently, the complements of sets in \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
d_4\le 5.
\]
Moreover, if \(d_3\le 4\), then \(d_4\le 1\), because one losing \(4\)-set already contributes its four \(3\)-subsets to \(D_3\), and two distinct \(4\)-sets would contribute at least seven triples.

#### Step 4: pair-sum feasibility

The sum of all pair weights is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)=6.
\]
Each losing pair has weight at most \(p\), and each winning pair has weight less than \(2p\). Hence
\[
6\le (21-m)p+2mp=(21+m)p.
\]
In particular, when \(m\le 2\),
\[
p\ge \frac6{23}.
\]

#### Step 5: polynomial check

For bounds
\[
d_2\le a,\qquad d_3\le b,\qquad d_4\le c,
\]
we have
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^7+7pq^6+a p^2q^5+b p^3q^4+c p^4q^3.
\]
Writing \(r=p/q\), this is
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4).
\]
Since
\[
1-p=q=q^7(1+r)^6,
\]
it is enough to prove
\[
(1+r)^6>1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4.
\]

The four cases are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & (a,b,c) & r\text{ range} & \text{positive factor}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & (21,0,0) & r\ge 6/17 &
-1-6r+20r^2+15r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
3\le m\le 4 & (18,4,1) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-3r+16r^2+14r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m=5 & (16,10,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-r+10r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m\ge 6 & (15,15,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1+5r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5
\end{array}
\]

In each row,
\[
(1+r)^6-(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4)=r\cdot h(r),
\]
and \(h(r)\) is increasing on the stated interval. At the left endpoints the values are respectively
\[
\frac{187075}{17^5},\qquad
\frac{91}{243},\qquad
\frac{55}{243},\qquad
\frac1{243},
\]
all positive. Hence \(\mu_p(D)<1-p\), so
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]
This proves the proposition. ∎

At \(p=1/3\), the original non-strict statement \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\) is already covered by the cyclic \(p=1/k\) argument with \(k=3\). The strict \(>\) endpoint is a boundary issue and is not needed for \(B(1/3)\).

---

## 4. Consequences

Combining this with the prior support-\(7\) result for \([1/6,1/4]\), support \(7\) is now eliminated throughout the base interval \(1/6\le p<1/3\). Therefore any strict reduced counterexample with \(p<1/3\) has support at least \(8\).

The merge program remains open in arbitrary support, but the two key takeaways are:

1. **All local merge inequalities can hold in non-counterexamples**, so the counterexample condition \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) is essential.
2. In a minimal counterexample, every mergeable pair must have a lower-window family with abnormally small biased upper-shadow expansion.
