# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves a **partial theorem and auxiliary lemmas**, not the full original problem.

Let \(0<p<1\), let \(q=1-p\), let \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive real numbers satisfying
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and set
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i,
\]
and call \(A\) losing if \(w(A)\le p\). Let
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[n]:w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The losing-pair graph is the graph on vertex set \([n]\) in which \(\{i,j\}\) is an edge exactly when \(w_i+w_j\le p\).

The proof establishes the following five claims.

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

4. If \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, if \(d_i\) is the degree of \(i\) in \(\mathcal H\), and if
   \[
   d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
   \]
   is the nondecreasing rearrangement of the degrees, then, with \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]

5. Fix \(p\). Among positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\) and total weight \(1\), suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal positive support to the inequality \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\), meaning \(\Pr[S>p]<p\), and no strict counterexample with fewer positive weights exists. If two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and if \(R\) is the residual random sum over all other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

This differs from the original problem because it does not characterize all \(p\) and does not treat arbitrary nonnegative weights. It proves only the listed reduced-support and structural cases, under the strict hypotheses \(0<w_i<p\), together with two auxiliary lemmas.

## Proof

### 1. Basic notation and the layer measure

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[n]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Then \(V\) is a random subset of \([n]\), with
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]
Also,
\[
S=w(V).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]

For \(0\le j\le n\), write
\[
D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{j=0}^n d_j p^j q^{n-j}.
\]
Since \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, and of course the empty set is losing. Thus
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=n.
\]

We shall repeatedly upper-bound \(\Pr[S\le p]\). Once we prove
\[
\Pr[S\le p]<1-p=q,
\]
it follows that
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\Pr[S\le p]>p.
\]

### 2. Combinatorial estimates

We use three elementary combinatorial estimates.

#### 2.1. Intersecting graphs

If a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges.

Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, there are at most \(N-1\) edges. Otherwise, choose two edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and suppose there is an edge not containing \(a\). It must meet both \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), hence must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge \(\{a,d\}\) with \(d\notin\{b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from \(\{b,c\}\). Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges.

#### 2.2. Graphs with matching number at most \(2\)

We need the following special bounds:

- A graph on \(7\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.
- A graph on \(8\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

In the polynomial estimates below we only need the weaker bound \(12\) in the \(7\)-vertex case.

First, observe that any graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose not, so every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Let
\[
U=\{a,b,c,d\},\qquad W=V(G)\setminus U.
\]
Because the matching number is at most \(2\), \(W\) is independent. Pick two distinct vertices \(w,w'\in W\). Each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the two matched pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, the two vertices \(w,w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints of the pair; the other matched pair then supplies a third disjoint edge. This gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence some vertex has degree at most \(2\).

Next, any graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\). Indeed, otherwise it has a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\).

If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) leaves \(4\) vertices spanning at least \(10-5=5\) edges, hence containing two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), this gives a matching of size \(3\).

If \(\deg(v)=2\), let \(u\) be one of the two neighbors of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices. Removing \(u\) from those \(5\) vertices deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Any \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), again we get a matching of size \(3\).

Now consider a graph \(G\) on \(7\) vertices with at least \(12\) edges. If it had matching number at most \(2\), it would have a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Then \(G-v\) has \(6\) vertices and at least \(10\) edges. If \(G-v\) has no isolated vertex, the preceding paragraph gives a matching of size \(3\). If \(G-v\) has an isolated vertex, then its remaining \(5\) vertices must span all \(10\) possible edges, so \(G-v\) is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since \(G\) has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor inside the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\). Contradiction. Thus a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.

Finally, if a graph on \(8\) vertices had at least \(14\) edges and matching number at most \(2\), deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, which must contain a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence a \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

#### 2.3. Intersecting \(3\)-uniform families

If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

Here is a short proof. Place the \(N\) vertices around a circle. In any fixed cyclic ordering, there are \(N\) cyclic intervals of length \(3\). A pairwise intersecting subfamily of such cyclic intervals has size at most \(3\). On the other hand, a fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Since there are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings, double-counting pairs \((F,\text{cyclic ordering})\) with \(F\in\mathcal F\) appearing as a cyclic interval gives
\[
|\mathcal F|\cdot 3!(N-3)!\le 3(N-1)!.
\]
Therefore
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \frac{3(N-1)!}{6(N-3)!}
=\frac{(N-1)(N-2)}2
=\binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

### 3. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\)

We split into two subintervals.

#### 3.1. The range \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists. Indeed, if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Since weights are positive, the existence of any losing set of size at least \(3\) would imply the existence of a losing triple. Hence
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge3).
\]

The losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges. If \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\) were disjoint losing pairs, then the three remaining weights are each \(<p\), so
\[
1=w_a+w_b+w_c+w_d+\sum_{\text{remaining }i}w_i
<2p+3p\le1,
\]
again a contradiction. Thus the losing-pair graph is intersecting, and by the graph bound above,
\[
d_2\le6.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_1(p).
\]
We now check \(F_1(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\). Differentiating,
\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1.
\]
On this interval, \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^4-1
\ge 30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^4-1
=\frac{256}{125}-1
>0.
\]
Also,
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_1(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### 3.2. The range \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le\frac34<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If it had three disjoint losing pairs, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), giving
\[
1<3p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence, using the weaker convenient bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Thus
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Define
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), we have \(p\ge1/5\) and \(q\ge3/4\), so
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4
\ge
18\cdot\frac15\left(\frac34\right)^5
+
15\cdot\frac1{25}\left(\frac34\right)^4
=
\frac{2673}{2560}
>1.
\]
Therefore \(F_2'(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\). Also,
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_2(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Combining the two subintervals proves claim \(1\).

### 4. The case \(n=8\), \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If two losing triples were disjoint, then the two remaining vertices have total weight \(<2p\), so
\[
1<2p+2p\le\frac45<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus, by the intersecting \(3\)-uniform bound with \(N=8\),
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If there were three disjoint losing pairs, the two remaining weights would contribute \(<2p\), so
\[
1<3p+2p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore, by the \(8\)-vertex graph bound,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]

Consequently,
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_8(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_8'(p)=30pq^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), we have \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^6-1
\ge
30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^6-1
=
\frac{4096}{3125}-1
>0.
\]
Thus \(F_8'(p)>0\). Also,
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_8(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(2\).

### 5. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), with losing-pair matching number at most \(2\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\).

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning. Indeed, if \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p\ge p.
\]
Thus
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge5).
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,B\in D_4\) had \(|A\cap B|=1\), then \(A\cup B=[7]\), and
\[
w(A)+w(B)=w(A\cup B)+w(A\cap B)=1+w(A\cap B)>1.
\]
But \(w(A)+w(B)\le2p\le2/3\), contradiction. Hence any two losing \(4\)-sets meet in at least \(2\) points. Taking complements, the complements of the losing \(4\)-sets form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Therefore
\[
d_4\le \binom62=15.
\]

By hypothesis, the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), so, using the convenient weaker bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Hence
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Define
\[
F_3(p)
=
1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_3(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_3'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1.
\]

We verify that this derivative is positive on \([1/4,1/3]\). Put
\[
r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2
=
q^6(18r+15r^2+45r^4).
\]
Since \(q=(1+r)^{-1}\), it is enough to show
\[
18r+15r^2+45r^4>(1+r)^6.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H(r):=45r^4+15r^2+18r-(1+r)^6>0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
H(r)=-1+12r-20r^3+30r^4-6r^5-r^6.
\]
For \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\),
\[
H(r)
\ge
-1+12\cdot\frac13
-20\left(\frac12\right)^3
-6\left(\frac12\right)^5
-\left(\frac12\right)^6
=
\frac{19}{64}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_3'(p)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). Finally,
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_3(p)>0\) throughout \([1/4,1/3]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(3\).

### 6. The degree-sequence test for losing uniform families

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) be a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets. For each \(i\in[n]\), define
\[
d_i=|\{A\in\mathcal H:i\in A\}|.
\]
Let
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
\]
be the nondecreasing rearrangement of \(d_1,\dots,d_n\). Let
\[
s=\left\lfloor\frac1p\right\rfloor,
\qquad
\theta=1-sp.
\]
Because \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), we have \(n\ge s+1\), so \(d_{(s+1)}\) is defined.

Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) is losing,
\[
w(A)\le p.
\]
Summing over all \(A\in\mathcal H\),
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n d_i w_i.
\]
Relabel the coordinates so that
\[
d_1\le d_2\le\cdots\le d_n.
\]
We claim
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}\right)\\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^s d_i(w_i-p)
+
\sum_{i=s+1}^n d_iw_i
-
\theta d_{s+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(i\le s\), \(d_i\le d_{s+1}\) and \(w_i-p<0\), so
\[
d_i(w_i-p)\ge d_{s+1}(w_i-p).
\]
For \(i\ge s+1\), \(d_i\ge d_{s+1}\), so
\[
d_iw_i\ge d_{s+1}w_i.
\]
Therefore the difference above is at least
\[
d_{s+1}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^s(w_i-p)+\sum_{i=s+1}^n w_i-\theta
\right).
\]
Since \(\sum_iw_i=1\), this equals
\[
d_{s+1}(1-sp-\theta)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Combining with
\[
\sum_i d_iw_i\le p|\mathcal H|
\]
gives
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}
\le
p|\mathcal H|.
\]
This proves claim \(4\).

### 7. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

Fix \(p\). Consider positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\), total weight \(1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. A strict counterexample means a vector for which
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Assume \(w\) is such a strict counterexample with minimal positive support.

Let two weights be
\[
x\le y,\qquad x+y<p.
\]
Let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the residual random sum over all other coordinates.

Now merge the two weights \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). Since \(x+y<p\), the merged vector is still admissible and has one fewer positive coordinate. Let \(T\) denote the merged random sum:
\[
T=R+(x+y)u,
\]
where \(u\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(R\).

By minimality of the original counterexample, the merged vector is not a strict counterexample. Therefore
\[
\Pr[T>p]\ge p.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]<0.
\]

We compute this difference exactly. Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables attached to \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]
={}&q^2\Pr[R>p]
+pq\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&+pq\Pr[R>p-y]
+p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\end{aligned}
\]
For the merged variable,
\[
\Pr[T>p]
=
q\Pr[R>p]
+
p\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
Subtracting,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
={}&pq\bigl(
-\Pr[R>p]
+\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&\qquad+\Pr[R>p-y]
-\Pr[R>p-x-y]
\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(pq>0\) and the left-hand side is \(<0\), we obtain
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]
This proves claim \(5\).

- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: In §2.2, several elementary graph-matching subclaims are compressed: existence of two disjoint edges under minimum degree at least 3, the endpoint-selection step for two vertices in \(W\), and the fact that a 4-vertex graph with at least 4 edges has two disjoint edges.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for cyclic intervals in §2.3.**  
   Let the cyclic intervals be \(I_t=\{t,t+1,t+2\}\) modulo \(N\), with \(N\ge 6\). Suppose \(\mathcal G\) is a pairwise-intersecting subfamily of these intervals. If \(\mathcal G=\varnothing\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise rotate so \(I_0\in\mathcal G\). Any interval meeting \(I_0\) must be one of
   \[
   I_{-2}, I_{-1}, I_0, I_1, I_2.
   \]
   Moreover \(I_{-2}\cap I_1=\varnothing\) and \(I_{-1}\cap I_2=\varnothing\), for all \(N\ge 6\). Hence among the four intervals \(I_{-2},I_{-1},I_1,I_2\), a pairwise-intersecting subfamily has size at most \(2\). Including \(I_0\), we get \(|\mathcal G|\le 3\). This justifies the double-counting proof of
   \[
   |\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
   \]

2. **Patch for compressed graph details in §2.2.**  
   In the low-degree lemma, if a graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices has matching number at most \(2\) and minimum degree at least \(3\), then \(|E(G)|\ge 3N/2>N-1\). By the earlier intersecting-graph bound, \(G\) cannot have all edges pairwise intersecting; hence \(G\) contains two disjoint edges \(ab\) and \(cd\).

   With \(U=\{a,b,c,d\}\) and \(W=V(G)\setminus U\), \(W\) is independent. For distinct \(w,w'\in W\), each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). If neither matched pair \(\{a,b\}\), \(\{c,d\}\) allowed \(w,w'\) to be matched to distinct endpoints, then for each pair the two vertices would have exactly the same single neighbor in that pair. This would give each of \(w,w'\) only \(2\) neighbors in \(U\), contradiction. Therefore one matched pair supplies two disjoint edges incident to \(w,w'\), and the other matched pair supplies a third disjoint edge.

   Finally, whenever the proof says that a 4-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges, this follows from the already-proved intersecting-graph bound: a graph on \(4\) vertices with no two disjoint edges has at most \(3\) edges.

response:
The exact statement proved is the following partial theorem.

**Theorem.** Let \(0<p<1\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for all }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and set
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\), and call \(A\) **losing** if \(w(A)\le p\). Let the losing-pair graph have vertex set \([n]\), with edge \(\{i,j\}\) exactly when \(w_i+w_j\le p\). Then:

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
4. If \(\mathcal H\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, with degree sequence \(d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}\), and \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\), then
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]
5. Fix \(p\). Among all finite positive weight vectors with every weight \(<p\) and total weight \(1\), suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimum support to \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\). If two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and if \(R\) is the residual random sum over all other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

## Proof

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[n]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Then \(V\) is a random subset of \([n]\) with
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|},
\]
and \(S=w(V)\). Let
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[n]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad \ell_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{j=0}^n \ell_j p^j q^{n-j}.
\]
Since each \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, so
\[
\ell_0=1,\qquad \ell_1=n.
\]
Thus, to prove \(\Pr[S>p]>p\), it is enough to prove
\[
\Pr[S\le p]<q.
\]

We shall also use the simple monotonicity fact that if a losing set has size at least \(r\), then it contains a losing \(r\)-subset, because all weights are positive.

### Combinatorial estimates

We need three elementary estimates.

First, if a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges. Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, this is immediate. Otherwise choose two intersecting edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and choose an edge not containing \(a\). To meet both chosen edges, that edge must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge using a vertex outside \(\{a,b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from one of the triangle edges. Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges.

Second, we need small matching-number bounds. Let \(\nu(G)\) denote the matching number of a graph \(G\).

If \(G\) has \(N\ge6\) vertices and \(\nu(G)\le2\), then \(G\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose instead that every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Then \(e(G)\ge 3N/2>N-1\), so by the preceding paragraph \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Put \(U=\{a,b,c,d\}\) and \(W=V(G)\setminus U\). The set \(W\) is independent, because an edge inside \(W\), together with \(ab\) and \(cd\), would give a matching of size \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), choose distinct \(w,w'\in W\). All neighbors of \(w,w'\) lie in \(U\), and each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, \(w\) and \(w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints; otherwise, in each pair, both \(w\) and \(w'\) would have the same unique neighbor, giving each of them only \(2\) neighbors in \(U\), a contradiction. Therefore two disjoint edges incident to \(w,w'\), together with the other original matched pair, form a matching of size \(3\), contradiction.

Now consider a graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex. If it had no matching of size \(3\), the preceding paragraph would give a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\). If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) deletes at most \(5\) edges, leaving at least \(5\) edges on \(4\) vertices. A \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges, so together with \(uv\) we obtain a matching of size \(3\). If \(\deg(v)=2\), choose a neighbor \(u\) of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices; then removing \(u\) deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Again there are two disjoint edges, and together with \(uv\) they give a matching of size \(3\). Hence every \(6\)-vertex graph with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\).

It follows that a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges. Indeed, suppose such a graph had at least \(12\) edges. It has a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Removing \(v\) leaves a \(6\)-vertex graph with at least \(10\) edges. If that graph has no isolated vertex, it has a matching of size \(3\). If it has an isolated vertex, then the remaining \(5\) vertices span all \(10\) possible edges, so the graph is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since the original graph has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor in the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Therefore \(e(G)\le11\).

Similarly, an \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges: if it had at least \(14\) edges, deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, hence a matching of size \(3\).

Third, we need a bound for intersecting \(3\)-uniform families. If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
\]
Place the \(N\) vertices in a cyclic order. There are \(N\) cyclic intervals of length \(3\). In any fixed cyclic order, a pairwise-intersecting subfamily of those intervals has size at most \(3\): if \(I_0=\{0,1,2\}\) is included, then the only intervals meeting it are \(I_{-2},I_{-1},I_0,I_1,I_2\), and \(I_{-2}\cap I_1=\varnothing\), \(I_{-1}\cap I_2=\varnothing\). Hence among the four intervals other than \(I_0\), at most two may be chosen.

There are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings. A fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Therefore
\[
|\mathcal F|\cdot 3!(N-3)!\le 3(N-1)!,
\]
so
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \frac{3(N-1)!}{6(N-3)!}
=\binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

### 1. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\)

First suppose \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\). There is no losing triple. Indeed, if \(|A|=3\) and \(w(A)\le p\), then

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs and the remaining vertex would give

1<3p+p\le1.

Thus \(\ell_2\le12\). Consequently,
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=q-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4.
\]
Then
\[

\[

\[

\[

\[
\begin{aligned}
&\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}\right) \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^s d_i(w_i-p)
+
\sum_{i=s+1}^n d_iw_i
-
\theta d_{s+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
d_i(w_i-p)\ge d_{s+1}(w_i-p).

For \(i\ge s+1\), we have \(d_i\ge d_{s+1}\), so
\[

\[

\left(p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}\right)

\]
Using \(\sum_i w_i=1\), the expression in parentheses equals
\[

\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}.
\]

\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}

This proves assertion \(4\).

### 5. The minimal-counterexample merge inequality

Fix \(p\in(0,1)\). Suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimum support among all positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\) and total weight \(1\). Thus
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p,
\]

Let two coordinates have weights \(x\le y\) with \(x+y<p\), and let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j} w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the residual random sum over all other coordinates. Merge \(x\) and \(y\) into one weight \(x+y\). Since \(x+y<p\), the merged vector is still admissible and has one fewer positive coordinate. Let
\[
T=R+(x+y)u,
\]
where \(u\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(R\). By minimality, the merged vector is not a strict counterexample, so
\[
\Pr[T>p]\ge p.
\]
Since \(\Pr[S>p]<p\), we have
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]<0.
\]

Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables corresponding to \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
q^2\Pr[R>p]
+pq\Pr[R>p-x]
+pq\Pr[R>p-y]
+p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
For the merged vector,
\[
\Pr[T>p]
=
q\Pr[R>p]
+p\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
Subtracting,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
-\Pr[R>p]
+\Pr[R>p-x]
+\Pr[R>p-y]
-\Pr[R>p-x-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(pq>0\) and the left-hand side is negative,
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]

This proves assertion \(5\).

# Solution
The exact statement proved is the following partial theorem.

**Theorem.** Let \(0<p<1\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for all }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and set
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\), and call \(A\) **losing** if \(w(A)\le p\). Let the losing-pair graph have vertex set \([n]\), with edge \(\{i,j\}\) exactly when \(w_i+w_j\le p\). Then:

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
4. If \(\mathcal H\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, with degree sequence \(d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}\), and \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\), then
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]
5. Fix \(p\). Among all finite positive weight vectors with every weight \(<p\) and total weight \(1\), suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimum support to \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\). If two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and if \(R\) is the residual random sum over all other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

## Proof

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[n]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Then \(V\) is a random subset of \([n]\) with
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|},
\]
and \(S=w(V)\). Let
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[n]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad \ell_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{j=0}^n \ell_j p^j q^{n-j}.
\]
Since each \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, so
\[
\ell_0=1,\qquad \ell_1=n.
\]
Thus, to prove \(\Pr[S>p]>p\), it is enough to prove
\[
\Pr[S\le p]<q.
\]

We shall also use the simple monotonicity fact that if a losing set has size at least \(r\), then it contains a losing \(r\)-subset, because all weights are positive.

### Combinatorial estimates

We need three elementary estimates.

First, if a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges. Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, this is immediate. Otherwise choose two intersecting edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and choose an edge not containing \(a\). To meet both chosen edges, that edge must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge using a vertex outside \(\{a,b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from one of the triangle edges. Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges.

Second, we need small matching-number bounds. Let \(\nu(G)\) denote the matching number of a graph \(G\).

If \(G\) has \(N\ge6\) vertices and \(\nu(G)\le2\), then \(G\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose instead that every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Then \(e(G)\ge 3N/2>N-1\), so by the preceding paragraph \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Put \(U=\{a,b,c,d\}\) and \(W=V(G)\setminus U\). The set \(W\) is independent, because an edge inside \(W\), together with \(ab\) and \(cd\), would give a matching of size \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), choose distinct \(w,w'\in W\). All neighbors of \(w,w'\) lie in \(U\), and each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, \(w\) and \(w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints; otherwise, in each pair, both \(w\) and \(w'\) would have the same unique neighbor, giving each of them only \(2\) neighbors in \(U\), a contradiction. Therefore two disjoint edges incident to \(w,w'\), together with the other original matched pair, form a matching of size \(3\), contradiction.

Now consider a graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex. If it had no matching of size \(3\), the preceding paragraph would give a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\). If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) deletes at most \(5\) edges, leaving at least \(5\) edges on \(4\) vertices. A \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges, so together with \(uv\) we obtain a matching of size \(3\). If \(\deg(v)=2\), choose a neighbor \(u\) of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices; then removing \(u\) deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Again there are two disjoint edges, and together with \(uv\) they give a matching of size \(3\). Hence every \(6\)-vertex graph with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\).

It follows that a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges. Indeed, suppose such a graph had at least \(12\) edges. It has a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Removing \(v\) leaves a \(6\)-vertex graph with at least \(10\) edges. If that graph has no isolated vertex, it has a matching of size \(3\). If it has an isolated vertex, then the remaining \(5\) vertices span all \(10\) possible edges, so the graph is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since the original graph has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor in the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Therefore \(e(G)\le11\).

Similarly, an \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges: if it had at least \(14\) edges, deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, hence a matching of size \(3\).

Third, we need a bound for intersecting \(3\)-uniform families. If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

In any fixed cyclic order, a pairwise-intersecting subfamily of those intervals has size at most \(3\): if \(I_0=\{0,1,2\}\) is included, then the only intervals meeting it are \(I_{-2},I_{-1},I_0,I_1,I_2\), and \(I_{-2}\cap I_1=\varnothing\), \(I_{-1}\cap I_2=\varnothing\). There are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings. A fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Therefore
\[

\[

=\binom{N-1}{2}.

w(A)+w(B)=w(A\cup B)+w(A\cap B)=1+w(A\cap B)>1.

But \(w(A)+w(B)\le2p\le2/3\), contradiction. Thus any two losing \(4\)-sets meet in at least \(2\) points. Taking complements, the complements of losing \(4\)-sets form a pairwise-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
\ell_4\le \binom62=15.
\]

By hypothesis, the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), so \(\ell_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=q-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3.
\]
Then
\[
F_3'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1.
\]
Put \(r=p/q\). For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), we have \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2
=
q^6(18r+15r^2+45r^4).
\]
Since \(q=(1+r)^{-1}\), it is enough to show
\[
18r+15r^2+45r^4>(1+r)^6.
\]
Define
\[
H(r)=18r+15r^2+45r^4-(1+r)^6.
\]
Expanding,
\[
H(r)=-1+12r-20r^3+30r^4-6r^5-r^6.
\]
For \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\),
\[
H(r)\ge
-1+12\cdot\frac13
-20\left(\frac12\right)^3
-6\left(\frac12\right)^5
-\left(\frac12\right)^6
=
\frac{19}{64}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_3'(p)>0\). Since
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
we get \(F_3(p)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). Therefore \(\Pr[S\le p]<q\), and \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

This proves assertion \(3\).

### 4. The degree-sequence inequality

Let \(\mathcal H\) be a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets. For each \(i\in[n]\), define
\[
d_i=|\{A\in\mathcal H:i\in A\}|.
\]
Let
\[
d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
\]
be the nondecreasing rearrangement of \(d_1,\dots,d_n\), and put
\[
s=\left\lfloor\frac1p\right\rfloor,\qquad \theta=1-sp.
\]
Because every \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), we have \(np>1\), hence \(n\ge s+1\), so \(d_{(s+1)}\) is defined.

Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) is losing,
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H} w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H} w(A)=\sum_{i=1}^n d_i w_i.
\]
Relabel the coordinates so that \(d_1\le\cdots\le d_n\). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}\right) \\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^s d_i(w_i-p)
+
\sum_{i=s+1}^n d_iw_i
-
\theta d_{s+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(i\le s\), we have \(d_i\le d_{s+1}\) and \(w_i-p<0\), so
\[
d_i(w_i-p)\ge d_{s+1}(w_i-p).
\]
For \(i\ge s+1\), we have \(d_i\ge d_{s+1}\), so
\[
d_iw_i\ge d_{s+1}w_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}\right)
\ge
d_{s+1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^s(w_i-p)+\sum_{i=s+1}^n w_i-\theta\right).
\]
Using \(\sum_i w_i=1\), the expression in parentheses equals
\[
1-sp-\theta=0.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{i=1}^s d_i+\theta d_{s+1}.
\]
Combining this with \(\sum_i d_iw_i\le p|\mathcal H|\) gives
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]

This proves assertion \(4\).

### 5. The minimal-counterexample merge inequality

Fix \(p\in(0,1)\). Suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimum support among all positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\) and total weight \(1\). Thus
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p,
\]
and no admissible vector with fewer positive coordinates is a strict counterexample.

Let two coordinates have weights \(x\le y\) with \(x+y<p\), and let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j} w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the residual random sum over all other coordinates. Merge \(x\) and \(y\) into one weight \(x+y\). Since \(x+y<p\), the merged vector is still admissible and has one fewer positive coordinate. Let
\[
T=R+(x+y)u,
\]
where \(u\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(R\). By minimality, the merged vector is not a strict counterexample, so
\[
\Pr[T>p]\ge p.
\]
Since \(\Pr[S>p]<p\), we have
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]<0.
\]

Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables corresponding to \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
q^2\Pr[R>p]
+pq\Pr[R>p-x]
+pq\Pr[R>p-y]
+p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
For the merged vector,
\[
\Pr[T>p]
=
q\Pr[R>p]
+p\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
Subtracting,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
-\Pr[R>p]
+\Pr[R>p-x]
+\Pr[R>p-y]
-\Pr[R>p-x-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(pq>0\) and the left-hand side is negative,
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]

This proves assertion \(5\).

  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.4] For every α>0, define h_α:[0,1]→R by h_α(0)=−1 and h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1−t/(1+αt)) for 0<t≤1; equivalently, h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) for t>0, with the value at 0 understood by continuous extension. Then h_α is concave on [0,1].  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The extension at 0 is continuous because log(1−t/(1+αt))=−t+O(t^2). The function is smooth on (0,1), so it is enough to prove h_α''(t)≤0 there. Rather than inspect h_α'' directly, the proof multiplies by t^3 and sets η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t). Direct differentiation gives η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1−t/(1+αt)). Differentiating this expression produces the decisive factorization η'(t)=−t^2(1+3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and 0<t<1 both denominator factors are positive, and the numerator is strictly positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s↓0} η(s)=0, it follows that η(t)<0 for every t∈(0,1), hence h_α''(t)<0 on (0,1). Continuity at the endpoints then gives concavity on the whole interval. A frequently used corollary is that for any subinterval [a,b]⊆[0,1], min_{t∈[a,b]} h_α(t)=min(h_α(a),h_α(b)).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 4 (reduce support)] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with finite supports, let X=Σ_j X_j and μ=E[X], and fix δ>0. Suppose that for some index i, X_i has support {v_1,...,v_k} with k≥3. Then X_i can be replaced by a random variable X_i' that is independent of {X_j:j≠i}, has E[X_i']=E[X_i], and has support consisting of at most two values from {v_1,...,v_k}, in such a way that, writing X'=X_i'+Σ_{j≠i} X_j, one has E[X']=μ and Pr[X'<μ+δ]≤Pr[X<μ+δ].  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: For each support point v_j of X_i, let q_j be the conditional probability of the target lower-tail event when X_i is fixed to v_j: q_j=Pr[Σ_{ℓ≠i} X_ℓ+v_j<μ+δ]. A replacement distribution on the same support is specified by probabilities p_j. Preserving total mass and mean imposes the two linear constraints Σ_j p_j=1 and Σ_j p_j v_j=E[X_i], with p_j≥0. The resulting lower-tail probability is the linear objective Σ_j q_j p_j. The original distribution of X_i is feasible, so the minimum of this linear program is at most the original lower-tail probability. A basic optimal solution of an LP with two equality constraints has at most two positive p_j's (apart from degeneracy, still no more than two are needed). Taking X_i' with this basic optimal distribution gives the claim. The same LP idea is later reused in the paper with the objective Pr[sum<mean] instead of Pr[sum<mean+δ], yielding the modified reduce-support operation.
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Proposition 8] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent random variables. For each i, let μ_i=E[X_i], assume μ_1≥μ_2≥...≥μ_n, and assume X_i takes value μ_i+s_i with probability μ_i/(μ_i+s_i) and value 0 otherwise, where s_i≥0, with the evident degenerate interpretation when μ_i=s_i=0. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and s=Σ_i s_i. Then Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥s/(μ_1+s).  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: The maximum possible value of X is μ+s=Σ_i(μ_i+s_i). If some variable with s_i>0 comes up 0, the sum loses μ_i+s_i, which is strictly larger than μ_n because μ_i≥μ_n and s_i>0; hence X<μ+s−μ_n. Variables with s_i=0 are at their upper value almost surely. Therefore the event X≥μ+s−μ_n can occur only when every variable with positive surplus takes its upper value, so Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤Π_i μ_i/(μ_i+s_i). Since μ_i≤μ_1 and a/(a+t) is increasing in a for t≥0, each factor is at most μ_1/(μ_1+s_i). For fixed total surplus s, Π_i μ_1/(μ_1+s_i)≤μ_1/(μ_1+s); equivalently, combining two surpluses x,y cannot decrease the product because (μ_1/(μ_1+x))(μ_1/(μ_1+y))≤μ_1/(μ_1+x+y). Thus Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤μ_1/(μ_1+s), and taking complements gives Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥1−μ_1/(μ_1+s)=s/(μ_1+s).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 10 (stage-2 modified merge bound)] Consider the following Stage 2 process on a finite collection of independent nonconstant two-point random variables. At any time, variable X_i has mean μ_i>0 and takes values 0 and μ_i+s_i, where s_i≥0 is its surplus. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and let μ_1=max_i μ_i. Initially all means lie in [μ_1/2, μ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for α=1/3, the initial value of s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i is greater than α μ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine μ_1=max_i μ_i and s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'≤α μ_1. For α=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<μ^r]≥1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r−1}<μ^{r−1}]≥1/13.  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<μ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and μ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below μ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set β=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>αμ_1, then Pr[X<μ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(μ_1+s')>α/(1+α). If it stops with βμ_1≤s'≤αμ_1, write s'=γμ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least μ_1/2: with probability at least γ/(1+γ), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2−γ)μ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.
  • Counterexamples: for p in (1/3,1/2), m=3 and w_i=1/3 gives Pr[sum w_i v_i >= p] = p^2(3-2p) < p; for p in (1/2,1), m=2 and w_i=1/2 gives probability p^2 < p.  (from: advisor_r1)
  • Endpoint p=1/2 follows by pairing every subset A with its complement A^c under the uniform measure; at least one of w(A), w(A^c) is >= 1/2.  (from: advisor_r1)
  • Endpoint p=1/3 follows by a three-colour coupling: for each coordinate choose one of three colours uniformly, define three Bernoulli(1/3) vectors by colour indicators, and use that the three weighted sums add to 1.  (from: advisor_r1)
  • Endpoints p=0 and p=1 are trivial.  (from: advisor_r1)
  • [VERIFIED ✓] Partial classification: the inequality holds at p=0, p=1/3, p=1/2, and p=1, and fails for every p in (1/3,1/2) union (1/2,1).  (from: unknown)
    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the following partial classification.

For every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative weights \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_m\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for
\[
p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
\]

It also proves failure of the universal inequality for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
\]
using \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

It further proves failure for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
\]
using \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=\frac12\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p.
\]

The proof does not address the interval \(0<p<\frac13\).
    Verifier's is_relaxation verdict: True
    Proof sketch: For p=½ the proof pairs each random subset A with its complement, noting that w(A)+w(Aᶜ)=1 so in every pair one side has weight ≥½, and by the uniformity of A each such event has probability at least ½. For p=1/3 a three-colour coupling creates three independent Bernoulli(1/3) vectors whose weighted sums S₀+S₁+S₂=1, so by pigeonhole at least one Sᵣ≥1/3 and symmetry forces each to occur with probability ≥1/3. Sharpness is then settled by the simplest equal‐weight counterexamples (m=3 when p∈(1/3,1/2) and m=2 when p∈(1/2,1)) showing the inequality strictly fails elsewhere.

  • For a fixed increasing family F, if mu_t(F)=t then d/dt mu_t(F) >= 1; more generally t I_t(F) >= mu_t(F) log_t(mu_t(F)).  (from: t2_strict_threshold_minimal)
    Proof sketch: Proved by induction on the cube using sections and a two-point convexity inequality for Phi(x)=x log_t x.
  • A strict counterexample cannot arise by continuous downward crossing between subset-sum thresholds; any strict counterexample for p<1/3 yields a boundary counterexample at some subset-sum a<=p.  (from: t2_strict_threshold_minimal)
    Proof sketch: On intervals where F_t is fixed, the ratio log(mu_t(F))/log(t) is nonincreasing, so once mu_t(F)>=t holds it persists to the right.
  • The strict lemma holds for all weight vectors of support size at most 5 and all 0<p<1/3.  (from: t2_strict_threshold_minimal)
    Proof sketch: If all weights are <=p, then for n=4 every pair is winning; for n=5 either every pair is winning or at least six pairs are winning, giving an explicit binomial lower bound exceeding p.
  • The original non-strict statement holds for p=1/k, k>=3.  (from: t2_strict_threshold_minimal)
    Proof sketch: Represent Bernoulli(1/k) variables by uniform residues mod k and partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits; each orbit has average weighted sum 1/k, so at least one point per orbit is >=1/k.
  • For a fixed family G, G admits a probability distribution with all coordinate marginals at least p iff for every nonnegative weight vector w summing to 1 there is A in G with w(A) >= p.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: Identify distributions on G with points in conv{1_A:A in G}; separate this convex hull from [p,infinity)^m. The separating vector must be nonnegative and normalizes to the desired weight vector.
  • FC(p) is equivalent to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality for the same p.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: FC(p) applied to the bad down-set {A:w(A)<p} gives a contradiction by averaging w(A). Conversely, if the Bernoulli inequality holds and a down-set G of measure >1-p has no cover, separation gives w with G subset {A:w(A)<p}, contradicting the inequality.
  • For p=1/k, the Bernoulli inequality Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p holds for all nonnegative weights summing to 1.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: Represent Bernoulli(1/k) variables by uniform elements of Z/kZ and partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits. On every orbit the average weighted sum is 1/k, so at least one of the k points is good.
  • For 1/3 < p < 1/2, FC(p) fails on m=3 with G={A:|A|<=1}; for p>1/2 it already fails on m=2 with G={A:|A|<=1}.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: For m=3, mu_p(G)=(1-p)^2(1+2p)>1-p iff p<1/2, but any distribution on G has expected size at most 1, so cannot give all three marginals >1/3. The m=2 case is analogous for p>1/2.
  • If p<=1/3, all weights are <p, and every pair of positive weights has sum at least p, then the Bernoulli inequality holds.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: Then two successes always certify sum at least p, so the good probability is at least Pr[Bin(s,p)>=2]. For the relevant support sizes this binomial tail is at least p.
  • Any counterexample for p<=1/3 must have all weights <p, support size at least floor(1/p)+2, and some pair of weights with sum <p.  (from: t1_fractional_cover_dual)
    Proof sketch: A weight >=p gives the result immediately. If the support is at most floor(1/p)+1, then the two smallest weights already sum to at least p, reducing to the pairwise-heavy lemma.
  • Exact section-gluing dual reduction: to prove the fractional-cover theorem FC(p) by induction, it is enough to prove a two-threshold knapsack inequality. Namely, for 0<p≤1/3, weights u_i≥0 with sum_i u_i=1, and thresholds 0≤b≤a with (1-p)a+p b<p, it would suffice to show (1-p) mu_p({A:u(A)≤a}) + p mu_p({A:u(A)≤b}) ≤ 1-p.  (from: advisor_r2)

If no such triple exists, separating from the orthant gives a nonnegative vector u. Put a=max_{A in G0} u(A), b=max_{A in G1} u(A). Since b≤a, maximizing (1-r)a+r b over r∈[p,1] occurs at r=p, so separation gives (1-p)a+p b<p after normalizing sum_i u_i=1. Then G0⊂{u≤a} and G1⊂{u≤b}. The displayed two-threshold inequality would imply mu_p(G)≤1-p, contradicting the induction hypothesis setup mu_p(G)>1-p.

  • The proposed two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the one-threshold submean lemma: for all weights w summing to 1 and all tau < p, Pr_p[sum w_i v_i > tau] >= p.  (from: t4_section_two_threshold)

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
  • Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.
  • Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.
  • Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.
  • Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.
  • Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.
  • Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root polynomial certificates.: Their leading variance coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so they cannot handle diffuse weights there.
  • Crude arbitrary-support matching bounds for losing layers.: For large support, many tiny coordinates allow large matchings in losing pairs and triples.
  • Colouring with large colours plus a leftover colour.: It reduces to an upper-tail inequality for the leftover colour that is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Worst-case head threshold only: It proves the regular-tail case when alpha_k <= sigma_k, but is too crude for dominant heads because it ignores favorable head outcomes.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen in extreme one-head tails: The normal margin q barPhi(pt)-p barPhi(qt) decays in the far tail, while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not; a relative moderate-deviation bound seems needed.
  • Critical-index decay alone: Geometric decay reduces the tail mass, but it still leaves a finite head that may be too large for the existing support-7/8 layer counts without an additional robust finite-head certificate.
  • Naive universal good merge: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) have every mergeable pair bad, so local merge inequalities alone do not contradict reducedness.
  • Direct FKG/Harris comparison of the two residual windows: The upper and lower windows are differences of downsets rather than monotone events, and the equal-weight example reverses the desired comparison.
  • Crude support-7 layer counts: Bounds such as d2<=21, d3<=15, d4<=15 are too weak near p=1/4; the proof needs the winning-pair blocker tradeoff and the 3-intersection bound for losing 4-sets.

  • Any proof must be sharp at dictator weights and must handle threshold jumps at subset sums.
  • Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.
  • Control threshold jumps/critical antichains in the strict inequality mu_p({w(A)>p})≥p for p<1/3.
  • Find a polynomial or moment-dual lower bound that exploits the Bernoulli moment structure rather than only support bounds.
  • Prove the weighted Bernoulli inequality on the base interval p in [1/6,1/3].
  • Rule out strict reduced counterexamples with support at least 7 in the base interval.
  • Find a higher-degree polynomial/moment certificate that exploits the Bernoulli cumulant structure in the base interval.
  • Rule out minimal strict counterexamples with support at least 7 in p in [1/6,1/3].
  • Prove existence of a mergeable pair whose residual-window inequality goes in the non-counterexample direction.
  • Turn Berry-Esseen regularity plus finite-support layer counting into a complete large-support proof.

The main progress is an explicit finite-head/regular-tail certificate, a small-head corollary with constants, a one-dominant-atom refinement, a critical-index decay and tail-mass reduction for nonregular tails, a deterministic tail-filling finite reduction, and a new support-9 proof in the lowest base range. The remaining hard case is a large geometrically decaying head or a regular tail that first appears only after a large head, where finite-head layer-counting or relative tail estimates are still needed.

<full_solver_output task_id="t8_regular_critical_index">
Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.
