# Theorem: support \(8\) is eliminated on \([1/5,1/3)\)

Let \(q=1-p\), \(r=p/q\), and \(d_j=|D_j|\). Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j
\]

and

\[
1-p=q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]

it suffices to prove

\[
\sum_j d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

---

## 1. Range \([1/5,1/4)\)

For \(p<1/4\), no losing set of size \(5\) or more exists. Also:

- four disjoint losing pairs would have total weight \(<1\), so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\), hence by Erdős–Gallai
  \[
  d_2\le 21;
  \]
- two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total \(<4p\le1\), so \(D_3\) is intersecting, hence
  \[
  d_3\le \binom72=21;
  \]
- the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies inside \(D_3\). By Kruskal–Katona, if \(d_4\ge16\), then \(|\partial D_4|\ge23>21\). Hence
  \[
  d_4\le15.
  \]

Thus

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4).
\]

But for \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=
r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr)>0.
\]

So \(\mu_p(D)<q\).

---

## 2. Structural tools for \([1/4,1/3)\)

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and put \(m=|W|\), so

\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

### High-layer complement bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(A^c\) is a \(4\)-set of weight at least \(q\). If two such complements met in at most one vertex, their weights would sum to at most \(1+p\), contradicting \(2q>1+p\) for \(p<1/3\). Hence the complements of \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices. By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado/Frankl bound,

\[
d_4\le17.
\]

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-family, so

\[
d_5\le6.
\]

Also, every losing \(4\)-set complement must be a \(W\)-blocker: \(W\) must meet every perfect matching of that \(4\)-set. For small \(m\), direct graph checking gives

\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.
\]

### Losing-triple bound

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then the complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) must be winning; otherwise \(w(T)+w(U)+w(e)\le3p<1\). For each winning edge \(e\), there are exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence the number \(I(H)\) of disjoint pairs in \(H\) satisfies

\[
I(H)\le10m.
\]

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\), so Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]

Therefore

\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor \frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

We also use a weight-deficiency bound. For a losing pair \(ij\), set

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]

For every losing triple \(T\),

\[
\sum_{ij\subset T}\delta_{ij}=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]

Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, and

\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le ((28+m)p-7).
\]

Thus

\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

---

## 3. Range \([2/7,1/3)\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). Use

\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

So

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).
\]

A finite polynomial check over \(m=0,\dots,28\) gives

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr)>0
\]

for all \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). The worst case is \(m=0\) at \(r=2/5\), where the gap is

\[
\frac{1618}{78125}>0.
\]

Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([2/7,1/3)\).

---

## 4. Middle range \([1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\). Define the **very-high pair graph**

\[
E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.
\]

If \(T,U\in D_3\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair belongs to \(E\). Also, if \(A\in D_5\), then every pair inside \(A^c\) belongs to \(E\).

For \(p\le2/7\), one checks:

\[
|E|\le6,
\]

and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\). Hence:

- if \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_3\le31\) and \(d_5\le2\);
- if \(|E|=6\), then \(d_3\le28\) and \(d_5\le4\).

Combining this with the small-\(m\) blocker bounds for \(d_4\), the following table gives sufficient layer bounds. In each row, substituting the displayed bounds into

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+d_2r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5\bigr)
\]

gives a positive polynomial on \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\).

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & d_3 & d_4,d_5 & \text{minimum gap}\\
\hline
m=0,1,2 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4=d_5=0 &
>0
\\
m=3,4 &
\min\{6(21+m-7/r),H_m\} &
d_4\le5,\ d_5\le2 &
>0
\\
m=5 &
\min\{6(26-7/r),31\} &
d_4r^4+d_5r^5\le10r^4 &
>0.009
\\
m\ge6,\ |E|\le5 &
31 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le2 &
\frac{31}{2187}
\\
|E|=6 &
28 &
d_4\le17,\ d_5\le4 &
\frac{256}{2187}
\end{array}
\]

Therefore \(\mu_p(D)<q\) also on \([1/4,2/7]\).

Combining the three ranges proves:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No strict reduced counterexample of support }8\text{ exists for }1/5\le p<1/3.
}
\]

Together with the earlier \(n=8\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), support \(8\) is now eliminated on the entire base interval \([1/6,1/3)\).

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then

\]
for all

\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[

\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[

\[

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight

<3p+p\le1.

\[

If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

# Theorem: support \(8\) is eliminated on \([1/5,1/3)\)

Let \(q=1-p\), \(r=p/q\), and \(d_j=|D_j|\). Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j
\]

and

\[
1-p=q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]

it suffices to prove

\[
\sum_j d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

---

## 1. Range \([1/5,1/4)\)

For \(p<1/4\), no losing set of size \(5\) or more exists. Also:

- four disjoint losing pairs would have total weight \(<1\), so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\), hence by Erdős–Gallai
  \[
  d_2\le 21;
  \]
- two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total \(<4p\le1\), so \(D_3\) is intersecting, hence
  \[
  d_3\le \binom72=21;
  \]
- the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies inside \(D_3\). By Kruskal–Katona, if \(d_4\ge16\), then \(|\partial D_4|\ge23>21\). Hence
  \[
  d_4\le15.
  \]

Thus

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4).
\]

But for \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=
r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr)>0.
\]

So \(\mu_p(D)<q\).

---

## 2. Structural tools for \([1/4,1/3)\)

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and put \(m=|W|\), so

\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

### High-layer complement bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(A^c\) is a \(4\)-set of weight at least \(q\). If two such complements met in at most one vertex, their weights would sum to at most \(1+p\), contradicting \(2q>1+p\) for \(p<1/3\). Hence the complements of \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices. By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado/Frankl bound,

\[
d_4\le17.
\]

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-family, so

\[
d_5\le6.
\]

Also, every losing \(4\)-set complement must be a \(W\)-blocker: \(W\) must meet every perfect matching of that \(4\)-set. For small \(m\), direct graph checking gives

\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.
\]

### Losing-triple bound

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then the complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) must be winning; otherwise \(w(T)+w(U)+w(e)\le3p<1\). For each winning edge \(e\), there are exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence the number \(I(H)\) of disjoint pairs in \(H\) satisfies

\[
I(H)\le10m.
\]

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\), so Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]

Therefore

\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor \frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

We also use a weight-deficiency bound. For a losing pair \(ij\), set

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]

For every losing triple \(T\),

\[
\sum_{ij\subset T}\delta_{ij}=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]

Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, and

\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le ((28+m)p-7).
\]

Thus

\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

---

## 3. Range \([2/7,1/3)\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). Use

\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

So

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).

A finite polynomial check over \(m=0,\dots,28\) gives

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_m r^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr)>0

for all \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). The worst case is \(m=0\) at \(r=2/5\), where the gap is

\]

Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([2/7,1/3)\).

---

## 4. Middle range \([1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\). Define the **very-high pair graph**

E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.

If \(T,U\in D_3\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair belongs to \(E\). Also, if \(A\in D_5\), then every pair inside \(A^c\) belongs to \(E\).

For \(p\le2/7\), one checks:

and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\). Hence:

- if \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_3\le31\) and \(d_5\le2\);
- if \(|E|=6\), then \(d_3\le28\) and \(d_5\le4\).

Combining this with the small-\(m\) blocker bounds for \(d_4\), the following table gives sufficient layer bounds. In each row, substituting the displayed bounds into

\[
(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+d_2r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5\bigr)
\]

gives a positive polynomial on \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\).

\[

\\

Therefore \(\mu_p(D)<q\) also on \([1/4,2/7]\).

Combining the three ranges proves:

\text{No strict reduced counterexample of support }8\text{ exists for }1/5\le p<1/3.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],

since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[

For \(k\ge0\), write
\[

B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]

\[

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) in this pass. I did get several clean new reductions/lemmas that shrink the possible counterexample space, especially in the lower two subintervals.

Below \(q=1-p\), \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\), and in the strict reduced regime
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad D=\{A:w(A)\le p\}.
\]

---

## 1. \(k\)-colour bootstrap lemma

Let \(B(r)\) denote the non-strict assertion at parameter \(r\). If \(B(kp)\) holds for an integer \(k\) with \(kp\le1\), then \(B(p)\) holds.

**Proof.** Generate \(\eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(kp)\). Conditional on \(\eta_i=1\), give \(i\) a uniformly random colour in \([k]\). For each colour \(j\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{i\text{ has colour }j\}}\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\). Let
\[
S_j=\sum_{i:\,\text{colour }j}w_i,\qquad T=\sum_{j=1}^k S_j.
\]
If \(T\ge kp\), then some \(S_j\ge p\). Hence
\[
k\,\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \Pr[T\ge kp]\ge kp,
\]
so \(\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge p\). ∎

Thus the global problem is indeed reduced to the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. New layer-counting progress

### Proposition 2.1: support \(7\), lower two ranges

If \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]
for all
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac14.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\}\), and write \(d_j=|D_j|\).

---

### Case 1: \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

No losing triple exists: if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge3\).

Also the losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges: two disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining three vertices would have total weight
\[
<2p+3p\le1.
\]
Thus \(d_2\le6\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
So
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge 1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5.
\]
Set
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_1'(p)=30p q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
So \(F_1(p)>0\), proving the claim in this range.

---

### Case 2: \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists, because if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1.
\]
So \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1.
\]
By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<3p+p\le1.
\]
By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound, \(d_2\le11\); I use the weaker bound \(d_2\le12\). Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Let
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1>0
\]
on \([1/5,1/4]\), and
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_2(p)>0\). ∎

---

## 3. Support \(8\) in the lowest base range

### Proposition 3.1

If \(n=8\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le\frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

No losing \(4\)-set exists: if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1.
\]
Thus \(D_j=\varnothing\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting, since two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<2p+2p<1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(3\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining two vertices have total weight
\[
<3p+2p\le1.
\]
By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Let
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_8'(p)=30p q^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1>0
\]
on \([1/6,1/5]\), and
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
So \(F_8(p)>0\), proving the claim. ∎

Consequently, any strict counterexample in the lowest base range \([1/6,1/5]\) must have support at least \(9\).

---

## 4. Support \(7\), upper base range: matching obstruction isolated

### Proposition 4.1

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac13.
\]
If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### Proof

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning, because its complement has size at most \(2\), so
\[
w(A)>1-2p\ge p
\]
with strictness coming from \(w_i<p\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the last vertex have total weight
\[
<2p+p\le1.
\]
Hence
\[
d_3\le15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting: if two losing \(4\)-sets met in only one vertex, their union would be all seven vertices, and the sum of their weights would be at least \(1\), but also at most \(2p\le2/3\), impossible. Equivalently, their complements form an intersecting \(3\)-family, so
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

By assumption and Erdős–Gallai, \(d_2\le12\). Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Let
\[
F_3(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0,
\]
and a direct derivative check gives
\[
F_3'(p)=18p q^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1>0
\]
on \([1/4,1/3]\). Hence \(F_3(p)>0\). ∎

So a support-\(7\) counterexample in \([1/4,1/3]\), if one exists, must have **three disjoint losing pairs**.

---

## 5. Useful degree test for arbitrary-support layer attacks

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D_k\) be any losing \(k\)-uniform subfamily. Let \(d_i=\deg_{\mathcal H}(i)\), and sort the degrees:
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}.
\]
Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) satisfies \(w(A)\le p\),
\[
\sum_i d_i w_i=\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
Let \(s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\) and \(\theta=1-sp\). Since \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the left side is at least the LP minimum
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\theta d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Therefore every losing layer must satisfy the necessary condition
\[
\boxed{
p\sum_{j=1}^{s}d_{(j)}+\bigl(1-sp\bigr)d_{(s+1)}
\le p|\mathcal H|.
}
\]
This is especially useful in the base ranges:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
p\text{-range} & s=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\\
\hline
[1/4,1/3] & 3\text{ or }4\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/5,1/4] & 4\text{ or }5\text{ at the endpoint}\\
[1/6,1/5] & 5\text{ or }6\text{ at the endpoint}
\end{array}
\]

This degree test rules out many extremal-looking layer configurations that raw matching bounds allow. For example, in \(n=7\), an intersecting \(3\)-family of size \(15\) is a star, with degree sequence \((5,5,5,5,5,5,15)\). For \(p<1/3\), the degree test forbids all \(15\) triples from being losing.

---

## 6. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

This is a useful structural condition for any future perturbation argument.

Assume \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal support. Let \(x=w_i\le y=w_j\) with
\[
x+y<p.
\]
Let \(R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell\). Merge \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). By minimality, the merged instance is not a counterexample. Comparing the split and merged probabilities gives
\[
\Pr_{\mathrm{split}}[S>p]-\Pr_{\mathrm{merged}}[S>p]
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Big).
\]
Since the split instance is bad and the merged instance is good, necessarily
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
}
\]
Thus every mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample forces more residual mass in the lower window \((p-x-y,p-y]\) than in the upper window \((p-x,p]\). This is a strong anti-smoothing condition.

---

## 7. Current remaining obstruction

After combining the prior support-\(\le6\) work with the new lemmas above:

- In \([1/6,1/5]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(9\).
- In \([1/5,1/4]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(8\).
- In \([1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample has support at least \(7\); if support is \(7\), its losing-pair graph must contain three disjoint losing pairs.

The main unresolved piece is still an arbitrary-support layer bound that controls the tradeoff between many losing pairs and the forced scarcity of losing triples and losing higher sets.

## Task t7_merge_minimal_counterexample — Attack the minimal-counterexample merge obstruction.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Let \(q=1-p\), and for a set \(A\) write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\). I use
\[
D=\{A:w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_k=D\cap {[n]\choose k}.
\]

## 1. The naive universal good-merge statement is false

The statement “for arbitrary reduced weights there is always a mergeable pair with
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]\ge \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\]
” is false.

Take \(p=0.3\), \(n=7\), and \(w_i=1/7\) for all \(i\). Every pair is mergeable, since \(2/7<p\). For any pair \(x=y=1/7\), the residual is \(R=K/7\), \(K\sim \mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\). Then
\[
(p-x,p]=(0.157\ldots,0.3]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=2\), while
\[
(p-x-y,p-y]=(0.014\ldots,0.157\ldots]
\]
contains exactly the residual level \(K=1\). Hence
\[
\frac{\Pr[p-x<R\le p]}{\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]}
=\frac{\binom52p^2q^3}{\binom51pq^4}
=\frac{2p}{q}<1
\]
for every \(p<1/3\). Thus **all mergeable pairs are bad** in the merge sense. But this vector is not a counterexample: for \(p=0.3\),
\[
\Pr[S>p]=\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(7,0.3)\ge 3]\approx 0.353>0.3.
\]

So the missing ingredient cannot be a purely local merge statement. One must use the actual counterexample hypothesis \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) quantitatively.

---

## 2. A useful conditional good-merge lemma for the two smallest weights

Let \(a\le b\) be the two smallest weights, with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum. Define
\[
L=\{T:p-a-b<w(T)\le p-b\},
\]
\[
U=\{A:p-a<w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The merge is good exactly when \(\mu_p(U)\ge \mu_p(L)\).

For \(T\in L\), define its losing upper shadow
\[
\Gamma(T)=\{k\notin T:\ w(T)+w_k\le p\}.
\]
Because \(a,b\) are the two smallest weights, every residual weight is at least \(b\). Thus if \(T\in L\) and \(k\in \Gamma(T)\), then
\[
w(T)+w_k>p-a-b+b=p-a
\]
and also \(w(T)+w_k\le p\), so
\[
T\cup\{k\}\in U.
\]

Let \(K\) be the maximum number of lower-shadow predecessors of any \(A\in U\), i.e.
\[
K=\max_{A\in U}|\{k\in A:A\setminus\{k\}\in L\}|.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_p(U)\ge \frac{p}{qK}\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T).
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
\ge \frac{q}{p}K,
\]
then the pair \(a,b\) is a good merge.

Equivalently, in a minimal counterexample where every mergeable pair is bad, the two-smallest lower window must have unusually poor \(p\)-biased upper-shadow expansion:
\[
\frac{\sum_{T\in L}|\Gamma(T)|\,\mu_p(T)}{\mu_p(L)}
< \frac{q}{p}K.
\]
For \(p\le 1/3\), the factor \(q/p\ge 2\), so this says the lower window must be very close to maximal losing sets. This is a concrete structural obstruction that any future proof must exploit.

---

## 3. Support \(7\), upper interval closed

I can close the remaining support-\(7\) upper-interval case, including the previous obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs.

### Proposition

Let \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Thus no strict reduced counterexample of support \(7\) exists in the upper base interval.

### Proof

Let \(W\) be the graph of **winning pairs**:
\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Write \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=|D_2|=21-m.
\]

Because every \(w_i<p\), every set of size at least \(5\) is winning:
\[
|A|\ge 5
\implies w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p>p.
\]
So only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) matter.

#### Step 1: losing triples are intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p=3p\le 1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(D_3\) is intersecting, so by EKR,
\[
d_3\le 15.
\]

#### Step 2: complements of losing triples force winning-pair blockers

Let \(T\in D_3\), and let \(Q=T^c\), so \(|Q|=4\). If \(Q\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(T)+w(P_1)+w(P_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[Q]\) must meet every perfect matching of \(K_4\). A subgraph of \(K_4\) meeting all three perfect matchings contains at least three edges; its minimal blockers are a triangle or a \(3\)-edge star.

This gives the following graph-count bounds for \(d_3\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & d_3\text{ bound}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & d_3=0\\
3\le m\le 4 & d_3\le 4\\
m=5 & d_3\le 10\\
m\ge 6 & d_3\le 15
\end{array}
\]

The \(m=5\) bound is maximized by a \(5\)-edge star, which gives \(\binom53=10\) four-sets whose winning-pair graph contains a \(3\)-star.

#### Step 3: losing four-sets are \(3\)-intersecting

If \(A,B\in D_4\) and \(|A\cap B|\le 2\), then one can choose disjoint triples \(A'\subset A\), \(B'\subset B\). Since every subset of a losing set is losing, this would give two disjoint losing triples, impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is \(3\)-intersecting.

Equivalently, the complements of sets in \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Hence
\[
d_4\le 5.
\]
Moreover, if \(d_3\le 4\), then \(d_4\le 1\), because one losing \(4\)-set already contributes its four \(3\)-subsets to \(D_3\), and two distinct \(4\)-sets would contribute at least seven triples.

#### Step 4: pair-sum feasibility

The sum of all pair weights is
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)=6.
\]
Each losing pair has weight at most \(p\), and each winning pair has weight less than \(2p\). Hence
\[
6\le (21-m)p+2mp=(21+m)p.
\]
In particular, when \(m\le 2\),
\[
p\ge \frac6{23}.
\]

#### Step 5: polynomial check

For bounds
\[
d_2\le a,\qquad d_3\le b,\qquad d_4\le c,
\]
we have
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^7+7pq^6+a p^2q^5+b p^3q^4+c p^4q^3.
\]
Writing \(r=p/q\), this is
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^7(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4).
\]
Since
\[
1-p=q=q^7(1+r)^6,
\]
it is enough to prove
\[
(1+r)^6>1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4.
\]

The four cases are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\text{case} & (a,b,c) & r\text{ range} & \text{positive factor}\\
\hline
m\le 2 & (21,0,0) & r\ge 6/17 &
-1-6r+20r^2+15r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
3\le m\le 4 & (18,4,1) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-3r+16r^2+14r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m=5 & (16,10,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1-r+10r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5\\
m\ge 6 & (15,15,5) & r\ge 1/3 &
-1+5r^2+10r^3+6r^4+r^5
\end{array}
\]

In each row,
\[
(1+r)^6-(1+7r+a r^2+b r^3+c r^4)=r\cdot h(r),
\]
and \(h(r)\) is increasing on the stated interval. At the left endpoints the values are respectively
\[
\frac{187075}{17^5},\qquad
\frac{91}{243},\qquad
\frac{55}{243},\qquad
\frac1{243},
\]
all positive. Hence \(\mu_p(D)<1-p\), so
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]
This proves the proposition. ∎

At \(p=1/3\), the original non-strict statement \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\) is already covered by the cyclic \(p=1/k\) argument with \(k=3\). The strict \(>\) endpoint is a boundary issue and is not needed for \(B(1/3)\).

---

## 4. Consequences

Combining this with the prior support-\(7\) result for \([1/6,1/4]\), support \(7\) is now eliminated throughout the base interval \(1/6\le p<1/3\). Therefore any strict reduced counterexample with \(p<1/3\) has support at least \(8\).

The merge program remains open in arbitrary support, but the two key takeaways are:

1. **All local merge inequalities can hold in non-counterexamples**, so the counterexample condition \(\mu_p(D)>1-p\) is essential.
2. In a minimal counterexample, every mergeable pair must have a lower-window family with abnormally small biased upper-shadow expansion.

## Task t6_high_degree_moment_base — Search for degree 5/6 polynomial or moment certificates valid on the base interval [1/6,1/3].
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I did **not** close the full base interval, but I found several rigorous upgrades to the moment-dual toolkit: exact 5th/6th moment formulas, two usable degree-6 certificate families, an exact SDP/Markov–Lukacs formulation for all degree-6 lower polynomials, and a new support-7 structural proof for \(p\le 1/4\).

Below \(q=1-p,\ c=pq,\ d=1-2p\), and \(\mu_j=\mathbb E X^j\).

---

## 1. Exact fifth and sixth moments

For \(X=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p)\), cumulants add and the centered Bernoulli cumulants are

\[
\kappa_2=c,\quad
\kappa_3=cd,\quad
\kappa_4=c(1-6c),
\]
\[
\kappa_5=cd(1-12c),\quad
\kappa_6=c(1-30c+120c^2).
\]

Thus for the weighted sum,

\[
\boxed{\mu_5
=
cd(1-12c)A_5+10c^2d\,A_2A_3.}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mu_6
=
c(1-30c+120c^2)A_6
+15c^2(1-6c)A_2A_4
+10c^2d^2A_3^2
+15c^3A_2^3.
}
\]

Useful constraints in the strict reduced regime include

\[
A_{j+1}<pA_j,\qquad
A_3\le A_2^{3/2},
\]

with strictness in the second unless the weight vector has only one nonzero coordinate.

---

## 2. Degree-5 and degree-6 product-root certificates

Let \(R=(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), \(0<r_i\le q\), and define

\[
F_R(x)=x(x+p)\prod_{i=1}^k(r_i-x),
\qquad
M_R=\max_{0\le x\le q} F_R(x)_+.
\]

Then \(F_R(x)\le0\) on \([-p,0]\), so

\[
L_R(x)=\frac{F_R(x)}{M_R}
\]

satisfies \(L_R\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}\). Hence

\[
\Pr[X>0]\ge \frac{\mathbb E F_R(X)}{M_R}.
\]

Writing \(e_j=e_j(r_1,\dots,r_k)\), the expectation is

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)
=
\sum_{j=0}^k(-1)^j e_{k-j}\bigl(\mu_{j+2}+p\mu_{j+1}\bigr),
\]

with \(\mu_1=0\).

For \(k=3\), degree \(5\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
-\mu_5
+(e_1-p)\mu_4
+(pe_1-e_2)\mu_3
+(e_3-pe_2)\mu_2.
}
\]

For \(k=4\), degree \(6\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E F_R
=
\mu_6
+(p-e_1)\mu_5
+(e_2-pe_1)\mu_4
+(pe_2-e_3)\mu_3
+(e_4-pe_3)\mu_2.
}
\]

Thus any choice of roots satisfying

\[
\mathbb E F_R(X)\ge pM_R
\]

certifies \(\Pr[X>0]\ge p\).

A structural obstruction also appears: the leading variance coefficient is

\[
e_k-pe_{k-1}
=
e_k\left(1-p\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\right).
\]

Since \(r_i\le q\),

\[
\sum_i\frac1{r_i}\ge \frac{k}{q}.
\]

Therefore a product-root certificate of this type can have nonnegative leading \(\mu_2\)-coefficient only if

\[
p<\frac{q}{k}
\iff
p<\frac1{k+1}.
\]

So degree \(5\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/4\), and degree \(6\) product-root certificates cannot handle diffuse cases once \(p\ge1/5\). They are still useful in non-diffuse/high-moment regimes, but Berry–Esseen or another non-polynomial input remains necessary for diffuse weights.

---

## 3. A new degree-6 square-complement certificate

For arbitrary positive parameters \(a,b,c\), define

\[
h(x)=\left(1-\frac xa\right)
\left(1-\frac xb\right)
\left(1-\frac xc\right)
=
1-\alpha_1x+\alpha_2x^2-\alpha_3x^3,
\]

where

\[
\alpha_1=\frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c,\quad
\alpha_2=\frac1{ab}+\frac1{ac}+\frac1{bc},\quad
\alpha_3=\frac1{abc}.
\]

Set

\[
L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-h(x)^2.
\]

For \(x\le0\), each factor in \(h\) is at least \(1\), so \(h(x)^2\ge1\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le0\). For \(x>0\), \(h(x)^2\ge0\), hence \(L_{a,b,c}(x)\le1\). Therefore

\[
L_{a,b,c}\le \mathbf 1_{\{x>0\}}
\]

on \([-p,q]\). Its expectation is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)
={}&
-(\alpha_1^2+2\alpha_2)\mu_2
+2(\alpha_3+\alpha_1\alpha_2)\mu_3 \\
&-(\alpha_2^2+2\alpha_1\alpha_3)\mu_4
+2\alpha_2\alpha_3\mu_5
-\alpha_3^2\mu_6.
\end{aligned}
}
\]

Thus the explicit sufficient condition

\[
\mathbb E L_{a,b,c}(X)\ge p
\]

certifies the desired inequality.

In the equal-root subfamily \(a=b=c=t\),

\[
\boxed{
\mathbb E L_t(X)
=
-\frac{15\mu_2}{t^2}
+\frac{20\mu_3}{t^3}
-\frac{15\mu_4}{t^4}
+\frac{6\mu_5}{t^5}
-\frac{\mu_6}{t^6}.
}
\]

This certificate is often better than product-root polynomials in skewed regimes because it does not force a zero at \(-p\); it only forces negativity on the whole negative interval.

---

## 4. Exact degree-6 SDP formulation

All degree-\(\le6\) lower-polynomial certificates can be searched exactly by Markov–Lukacs.

A polynomial \(L\) of degree at most \(6\) is valid if

\[
-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[-p,0],
\]

and

\[
1-L(x)\ge0 \quad \text{on }[0,q].
\]

Equivalently, there exist univariate SOS polynomials \(\sigma_0,\sigma_1,\tau_0,\tau_1\) with

\[
-L(x)=\sigma_0(x)+(x+p)(-x)\sigma_1(x),
\]

\[
1-L(x)=\tau_0(x)+x(q-x)\tau_1(x),
\]

with \(\deg\sigma_0,\deg\tau_0\le6\) and \(\deg\sigma_1,\deg\tau_1\le4\).

Maximizing

\[
\mathbb E L(X)
=
\ell_0+\ell_2\mu_2+\ell_3\mu_3+\ell_4\mu_4+\ell_5\mu_5+\ell_6\mu_6
\]

over this SDP gives the best degree-6 moment certificate for the given moment vector. Any SDP optimum \(\ge p\) proves the desired inequality for that instance.

---

## 5. New structural result: support \(7\) for \(p\le 1/4\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\). Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Let \(D_k\) be the losing \(k\)-sets, \(e=|D_2|\), \(h=|D_3|\).

### Case 1: \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists: a losing triple plus the four remaining weights would have total \(<p+4p\le1\), contradiction.

Also, the losing-pair graph is intersecting. If two disjoint losing pairs existed, then those pairs plus the remaining three weights would have total \(<2p+3p\le1\), contradiction. Hence \(e\le6\).

Therefore

\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]

Let

\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]

Then

\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1>0
\]

on \([1/6,1/5]\), and

\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{27030}{279936}>0.
\]

So \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).

### Case 2: \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists: a losing \(4\)-set plus the three remaining weights would have total \(<p+3p\le1\), contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), since three disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<3p+p\le1\). Thus, by the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(7\) vertices,

\[
e\le12.
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting, because two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining vertex would have total \(<2p+p<1\). Hence by Erdős–Ko–Rado,

\[
h\le15.
\]

Therefore

\[

\[
\boxed{n=9,\quad \frac15\le p\le \frac14\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Pr[S>p]>p.}

---

## 1. Support \(9\) eliminated on \([1/5,1/4]\)

Let

\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq,
\]

and

\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]

Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^9\sum_j d_jr^j,\qquad q=q^9(1+r)^8,
\]

it suffices to show

\[
\sum_j d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]

Assume

\[
\frac15\le p\le \frac14,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\frac14\le r\le \frac13.
\]

For \(p\le1/4\), no losing set of size \(6\) or more exists: if \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so

\[
w(A^c)<3p\le q\le 1-w(A),
\]

contradiction. Thus only layers \(0,\dots,5\) matter.

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W\iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and let \(m=|W|\). Then

\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

### Losing triples

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If some pair \(e\subset C\) were losing, and \(z=C\setminus e\), then

\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence every pair of \(C\) is winning, so \(C\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Each triangle \(C\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Therefore

\[
I(H)\le 10\,\tau(W),
\]

where \(I(H)\) is the number of disjoint pairs in \(H\), and \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\).

The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\) has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{5}{28}h(h-28).
\]

Let \(T_m\) be the maximum possible number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges. Explicitly, if

\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]

then

\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]

Thus

\[
h(h-28)\le 56T_m,
\]

and we use the bound

\[
d_3\le H_m:=\max\{h\le56:\ h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]

The cap \(56\) is the Erdős matching bound for a \(3\)-graph on \(9\) vertices with no three disjoint edges.

### Losing fours

If \(A\in D_4\), let \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus the leftover vertex \(z\), then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence \(W[B]\) must meet every \(2\)-matching in \(K_5\). Equivalently, the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), hence has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore

\[
|W[B]|\ge 6.
\]

Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),

\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m,
\]

so

\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

### Losing fives

If \(A\in D_5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]

contradiction. Thus \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(|W[B]|\ge3\). Counting incidences,

\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]

so \(d_5\le7m\). Also complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(9\) vertices, hence by the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado / Ahlswede–Khachatrian bound,

\[
d_5\le21.
\]

Therefore

\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

### Polynomial certificate

For each \(m=0,\dots,36\), define

\[
P_m(r)
=
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right).
\]

The required inequality follows from

\[
P_m(r)>0
\qquad
\forall m\in\{0,\dots,36\},\quad \frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

This is a finite exact check. Here is a reproducible rational/Sturm certificate:

```python
import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")
L = sp.Rational(1, 4)
U = sp.Rational(1, 3)

def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in a graph with m edges:
    # colex/Kruskal-Katona graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56 by matching-number bound
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P = sp.expand(
        (1 + r)**8
        - (1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5)
    )
    poly = sp.Poly(P, r)

    # Exact endpoint positivity.
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, L)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, L))
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, U)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, U))

    # Exact Sturm root count on [1/4, 1/3].
    assert poly.count_roots(L, U) == 0, (m, P)

print("All 37 support-9 polynomials are positive on [1/4, 1/3].")
```

\left(\frac54\right)^8
-
\left(1+\frac94+\frac{36}{16}+\frac{28}{64}\right)
=
\frac{1505}{65536}>0.
\]

\[

\frac15\le p\le\frac14.
\]

Together with the earlier support-\(9\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), this eliminates support \(9\) on

\]

## 2. New upper-interval tool: deficiency cascade

For the remaining interval

\[
\frac14<p<\frac13,
\]

the previous \(p\le1/4\) blocker for losing \(4\)-sets no longer works, because the contradiction used \(4p\le1\). However, there is a useful general deficiency cascade.

For a losing \(k\)-set \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency

\[
\epsilon_k(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]

and put

\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\epsilon_k(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then

\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=
(k+1)p-kw(B)
=
p+k(p-w(B)).
\]

Summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, gives

\[
\boxed{
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
}
\]

For pairs, let

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j.
\]

Then

\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}.
\]

Since

\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]

and each winning pair contributes \(p-w_i-w_j>-p\), we get

\[
\boxed{
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
}
\]

Thus, in support \(9\),

\[
\boxed{
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_4+3E_4\le6E_3,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_5+4E_5\le5E_4,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_6+5E_6\le4E_5.
}
\]

This is stronger than the one-layer deficiency bounds because it forces a shared budget across \(D_3,D_4,D_5,D_6\).

---

## 3. Upper-interval high-layer bounds

For \(p<1/3\), we still have:

\[
d_5\le \min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Also, if \(A\in D_6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\) and weight at least \(q\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) we would get total weight \(<3p<1\), impossible. Hence \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\), so

\[
d_6\le \tau(W)\le T_m.
\]

The complement \(2\)-intersection bound gives

\[
d_6\le7.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
d_6\le \min\{7,T_m\}.
}
\]

The problematic layer is \(D_4\). We have the general bound

\[
d_4\le56,
\]

because losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, but this is not strong enough near \(p=2/7\).

---

## 4. Exact obstruction left by the current upper-interval bounds

Using the deficiency cascade, the blocker bounds for \(D_5,D_6\), the matching bound \(d_3\le56\), the intersecting bound \(d_4\le56\), and shadow-incidence constraints, the first relaxed obstruction occurs near

\[
r=\frac25
\qquad
\left(p=\frac27\right),
\]

with

\[
m=3.
\]

The relaxed layer profile is

\[
(d_0,d_1,d_2,d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)
=
(1,9,33,56,56,6,1).
\]

At \(r=2/5\), this gives

\[
1+9r+33r^2+56r^3+56r^4+6r^5+r^6
=
\frac{5844975}{390625},
\]

whereas

\[
(1+r)^8
=
\left(\frac75\right)^8
=
\frac{5764801}{390625}.
\]

So the relaxed certificate misses by

\[
\frac{5844975-5764801}{390625}
=
\frac{80174}{390625}.
\]

This profile is almost certainly not threshold-feasible, but the current general inequalities do not rule it out. Concretely, to close this obstruction it would suffice to prove, in the \(m=3\) neighborhood, a bound roughly of the form

\[
d_4\le47
\]

or a comparable tradeoff between \(d_3\) and \(d_4\). A natural candidate is:

> If \(D_4\subseteq\binom{[9]}4\) is an intersecting threshold family whose shadow \(D_3\) has matching number at most \(2\), and if the winning-pair graph has \(m\le3\), then \(d_4\le47\).

A sharper form, probably true, is \(d_4\le35\), but I did not complete that structural proof here.

---

## 5. Exact 8-head fallback formulation

For support \(9\), choose one coordinate as a tail, say \(b=w_9\), and write the remaining head weights as \(x_1,\dots,x_8\), with

\[
\sum_{i=1}^8 x_i=1-b.
\]

Let

\[
R=\sum_{i=1}^8 x_iv_i.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
\Pr[R>p]+p\,\Pr[p-b<R\le p].
\]

Equivalently, define two head downsets

\[
E_0=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p\},
\]

\[
E_1=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p-b\}.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
1-q\,\mu_p(E_0)-p\,\mu_p(E_1).
\]

So a support-\(9\) counterexample is exactly an \(8\)-coordinate head satisfying

\[
q\,\mu_p(E_0)+p\,\mu_p(E_1)\ge q.
\]

This is the clean finite-head fallback: it reduces the remaining support-\(9\) problem to a two-threshold weighted downset inequality on \(8\) coordinates. The known support-\(8\) theorem does not immediately apply because the head has total mass \(1-b\), not \(1\), and the Bernoulli parameter remains \(p\), but this formulation isolates precisely what an \(8\)-head certificate must prove.

## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following **partial theorem**, which is narrower than the original problem.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(w_1,\dots,w_8\) be **positive** real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i\in\{1,\dots,8\},
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_8\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and define
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i v_i.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This differs from the original problem in several ways: it treats only support size \(8\), assumes all weights are positive and strictly smaller than \(p\), proves a strict \(>\) threshold statement rather than the original non-strict \(\ge\) threshold statement, and covers only \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\).

## Proof

For \(A\subseteq[8]=\{1,\dots,8\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq,
\]
and define the bad family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[8]:w(A)\le p\}.
\]
For \(0\le j\le8\), put
\[
D_j=D\cap \binom{[8]}j,\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]

Let \(R=\{i:v_i=1\}\). Then \(R\) is a \(p\)-random subset of \([8]\), and
\[
S=w(R).
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\mu_p(D):=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{8-|A|}
=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\iff
\mu_p(D)<1-p=q.
\]
Since
\[
1+r=1+\frac pq=\frac1q,
\]
we have
\[
q=q^8(1+r)^7.
\]
Hence it is enough in every range to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

We prove this by ranges.

### 1. Range \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

Here
\[
r=\frac pq\in\left[\frac15,\frac14\right].
\]

No losing \(4\)-set exists. Indeed, if \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family: if \(T,U\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the two remaining vertices have total weight \(<2p\), so
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w([8]\setminus(T\cup U))<2p+2p\le\frac45<1,
\]
impossible. By the Erdős–Ko–Rado bound for intersecting \(3\)-families on \(8\) points,
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\): three disjoint losing pairs plus the two remaining vertices would have total weight \(<3p+2p\le1\), again impossible. By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound on \(8\) vertices,
\[
d_2\le 13.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_j d_jr^j\le 1+8r+13r^2+21r^3.
\]
Now
\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+13r^2+21r^3)
=r\bigl(-1+8r+14r^2+35r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr).
\]
The bracket is increasing for \(r>0\), and at \(r=1/5\) equals
\[
-1+\frac85+\frac{14}{25}+\frac{35}{125}
+\frac{21}{625}+\frac7{3125}+\frac1{15625}
=\frac{23061}{15625}>0.
\]
Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([1/6,1/5]\).

### 2. Range \(\frac15\le p<\frac14\)

Here
\[
r\in\left[\frac14,\frac13\right).
\]

No losing set of size \(5\) or larger exists: if \(|A|\ge5\) and \(w(A)\le p\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p<1,
\]
contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(4\), since four disjoint losing pairs would cover all vertices and have total weight at most \(4p<1\). Thus, by Erdős–Gallai,
\[
d_2\le 21.
\]

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the two remaining vertices would have total weight \(<2p+2p<1\). Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

Finally, the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies in \(D_3\): every \(3\)-subset of a losing \(4\)-set is losing. If \(d_4\ge16\), then the Kruskal–Katona theorem gives
\[
|\partial D_4|\ge \binom63+\binom32=20+3=23,
\]
because
\[
16=\binom64+\binom33.
\]
But \(|\partial D_4|\le d_3\le21\), contradiction. Hence
\[
d_4\le15.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_j d_jr^j\le 1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4.
\]
We compute
\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr).
\]
The bracket is increasing for \(r>0\), and at \(r=1/4\) equals
\[
-1+\frac{14}{16}+\frac{20}{64}
+\frac{21}{256}+\frac7{1024}+\frac1{4096}
=\frac{1133}{4096}>0.
\]
Thus \(\mu_p(D)<q\) on \([1/5,1/4)\).

### 3. Structural lemmas for \(\frac14\le p<\frac13\)

From now on assume
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Every losing set has size at most \(5\), because if \(|A|\ge6\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+2p<1.
\]

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs:
\[
ij\in W\iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]
and let \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

#### Lemma 3.1: high-layer bounds

We have
\[
d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

Proof. If \(A\in D_4\), then \(C=A^c\) is a \(4\)-set with
\[
w(C)=1-w(A)\ge q.
\]
If two such complements \(C,C'\) satisfied \(|C\cap C'|\le1\), then
\[
w(C)+w(C')=w(C\cup C')+w(C\cap C')<1+p,
\]
while
\[
w(C)+w(C')\ge2q.
\]
Since \(p<1/3\), \(2q>1+p\), contradiction. Thus the complements of losing \(4\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([8]\). The sharp \(n=8,k=4,t=2\) Frankl intersection bound gives size at most \(17\). Hence \(d_4\le17\).

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples on \([8]\). A \(2\)-intersecting triple family has size at most \(6\): either all triples share a common pair, giving at most \(6\), or otherwise the family is contained in the four triples of some \(4\)-set. Therefore \(d_5\le6\). ∎

#### Lemma 3.2: losing triples versus winning pairs

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). Then
\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor\frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

Proof. If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) satisfies
\[
w(e)=1-w(T)-w(U)\ge1-2p>p,
\]
so \(e\in W\). For each fixed pair \(e\), the other six vertices have exactly
\[
\frac12\binom63=10
\]
unordered partitions into two triples. Therefore the number \(I(H)\) of unordered disjoint pairs in \(H\) satisfies
\[
I(H)\le10m.
\]

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\). For a set \(H\) of \(h\) vertices in this graph, Hoffman’s supersaturation inequality gives
\[
I(H)\ge \frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac{h(h-21)}7\le10m,
\]
so
\[
h^2-21h-70m\le0.
\]
Solving the quadratic inequality gives the stated bound. ∎

#### Lemma 3.3: a deficiency bound for losing triples

For every \(m\),
\[
d_3\le 6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

Proof. For a losing pair \(ij\), define
\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]
If \(T=\{a,b,c\}\in D_3\), then
\[
\delta_{ab}+\delta_{ac}+\delta_{bc}
=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]
Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, so
\[
p\,d_3
\le 6\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le (28+m)p-7.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\sum_{ij}(p-w_i-w_j)=28p-7,
\]
and each winning pair contributes a negative amount \(p-w_i-w_j>-p\), because \(w_i+w_j<2p\). Therefore
\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right),
\]
using \(1/p=(1+r)/r\). ∎

#### Lemma 3.4: small winning-pair blocker bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(C=A^c\) is a \(4\)-set such that \(W[C]\) meets every perfect matching of \(C\). Indeed, otherwise \(C\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, giving
\[
w(C)\le2p<q\le w(C),
\]
contradiction.

Call such a \(4\)-set a \(W\)-blocker. For a graph \(G\) on \(8\) vertices with \(m\) edges, the number of blocker \(4\)-sets is bounded as follows:
\[
m\le2\Rightarrow 0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow \le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow \le10.
\]

Reason: on four vertices, a subgraph meets all three perfect matchings only if it contains at least three edges; with exactly three edges it must be a triangle or a \(3\)-star. Thus \(m\le2\) gives no blockers. For \(m=3,4\), if there is a triangle, all blocker \(4\)-sets containing that triangle are obtained by choosing the fourth vertex, at most \(5\) choices; if there is no triangle, blockers come from \(3\)-stars, at most \(4\). For \(m=5\), if the graph is triangle-free, blockers are \(3\)-stars and there are at most \(\binom53=10\), attained by a \(5\)-edge star. If there is one triangle, the triangle gives \(5\) blockers and the two remaining edges can create at most two additional star-only blockers. If there are two triangles, they share an edge and give at most \(5+5-1=9\) blockers. Hence the bound \(10\).

Therefore
\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.
\]

#### Lemma 3.5: very-high-pair graph for \(\frac14\le p\le\frac27\)

Assume
\[
\frac14\le p\le\frac27.
\]
Define
\[
E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.
\]
Then
\[
|E|\le6,
\]
and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\).

Proof. Put
\[
a_i=p-w_i>0,\qquad \gamma=4p-1.
\]
Then \(0\le\gamma\le1/7\), \(p=(1+\gamma)/4\),
\[
\sum_{i=1}^8 a_i=8p-1=1+2\gamma,
\]
and
\[
ij\in E\iff a_i+a_j\le\gamma.
\]
Order the \(a_i\) so that \(a_1\le\cdots\le a_8\). Let \(\ell\) be the degree of vertex \(1\) in \(E\). Since \(a_1\) is minimal, every edge of \(E\) is contained among the vertices \(1,\dots,\ell+1\).

If \(\ell\ge6\), then
\[
\sum_i a_i< a_1+6(\gamma-a_1)+p
<6\gamma+p
=6\gamma+\frac{1+\gamma}{4}
<1+2\gamma,
\]
contradiction.

If \(\ell=5\), then
\[
\sum_i a_i< a_1+5(\gamma-a_1)+2p
=5\gamma-4a_1+2p
<1+2\gamma,
\]
again contradiction.

If \(\ell=4\) and \(|E|\ge6\), then among vertices \(2,3,4,5\) there are at least two edges. For four numbers \(x_1,\dots,x_4\in[a_1,\gamma-a_1]\) with at least two pairs summing to at most \(\gamma\), their total is at most \(3\gamma-2a_1\): if the two edges are disjoint, the total is at most \(2\gamma\le3\gamma-2a_1\), while if they share a vertex \(x\), then the total is at most
\[
(\gamma-x)+(\gamma-x)+x+(\gamma-a_1)\le3\gamma-2a_1.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_i a_i\le a_1+(3\gamma-2a_1)+3p
=3\gamma-a_1+3p
<1+2\gamma,
\]
contradiction.

Therefore \(|E|\ge6\) forces \(\ell=3\). But then all edges lie among four vertices, so \(|E|\le6\), and equality means those four vertices span \(K_4\). ∎

If \(T,U\in D_3\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair lies in \(E\). Hence, if \(|E|\le5\), the Kneser supersaturation bound gives
\[
d_3\le31.
\]
Also, if \(A\in D_5\), then \(A^c\) is a triple all of whose pairs lie in \(E\); thus \(A^c\) is a triangle in \(E\). A graph with at most \(5\) edges has at most two triangles, so
\[
|E|\le5\Rightarrow d_5\le2.
\]
If \(|E|=6\), then \(E=K_4\). Any losing triple contains at most one vertex of this \(K_4\), because any two vertices in it already have weight at least \(1-2p>p\). Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom43+\binom41\binom42=4+24=28.
\]
Also, \(d_5\le4\), since \(A^c\) must be one of the four triangles of that \(K_4\).

### 4. Range \(\frac27\le p<\frac13\)

Here
\[
r\in\left[\frac25,\frac12\right).
\]
Use
\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]
Thus it suffices to prove positivity of
\[
\Gamma_m(r)
=(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_mr^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_m(r)
=-r+(m-7)r^2+(35-H_m)r^3+18r^4+15r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]

Let \(h=H_m\). Since \(h(h-21)\le70m\),
\[
2(m-7)r+3(35-h)r^2
\ge
r\left(\frac{h(h-21)}{35}-14+3(35-h)r\right).
\]
For \(21\le h\le35\), the bracket is minimized at \(r=2/5\) and is at least \(-7/20\). For \(35\le h\le56\), it is minimized at \(r=1/2\) and is at least \(-7/80\). Therefore
\[
2(m-7)r+3(35-h)r^2\ge -\frac7{40}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Gamma_m'(r)>-1-\frac7{40}+72r^3
\ge -\frac{47}{40}+\frac{576}{125}>0.
\]
So \(\Gamma_m\) is increasing on \([2/5,1/2)\).

At \(r=2/5\),
\[
\Gamma_m(2/5)
=\frac{106618+12500m-5000H_m}{78125}.
\]
For \(m=0,1,2\), directly \(H_m=21,23,26\). For \(m\ge3\),
\[
H_m\le21+\frac52m.
\]
Thus
\[
106618+12500m-5000H_m\ge1618>0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Gamma_m(r)>0
\]
throughout \([2/5,1/2)\), proving the theorem for \(p\in[2/7,1/3)\).

### 5. Range \(\frac14\le p\le\frac27\)

Here
\[
r\in\left[\frac13,\frac25\right].
\]

We split according to \(m=|W|\).

#### Small \(m\le5\)

For \(m=0,1,2\), Lemma 3.4 gives \(d_4=d_5=0\).  
For \(m=3,4\), it gives \(d_4\le5\), and also \(d_5\le2\).  
For \(m=5\), it gives \(d_4\le10\), and again \(d_5\le2\).

For \(0\le m\le5\), let
\[
B_m(r)=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right)
\]
be the deficiency bound from Lemma 3.3. Let \(h_m=H_m\). The relevant data are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
m & h_m & d_4\le c & d_5\le e & \beta_m\\
\hline
0&21&0&0&2/5\\
1&23&0&0&42/109\\
2&26&0&0&3/8\\
3&28&5&2&21/58\\
4&30&5&2&7/20\\
5&31&10&2&42/125
\end{array}
\]
Here \(\beta_m\) is defined by \(B_m(\beta_m)=h_m\).

For \(r\le\beta_m\), use \(d_3\le B_m(r)\). The gap is
\[
\Gamma^D_{m,c,e}(r)
=-r+(35+m)r^2-(91+6m)r^3+(35-c)r^4+(21-e)r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
For all rows above,
\[
(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e})''(r)
\le -10m-\frac{37856}{3125}<0
\]
on \([1/3,2/5]\), and
\[
(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e})'(1/3)\le -\frac{974}{729}<0.
\]
Thus \(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e}\) is decreasing, so its minimum on \([1/3,\beta_m]\) occurs at \(r=\beta_m\).

For \(r\ge\beta_m\), use \(d_3\le h_m\). The gap is
\[
\Gamma^H_{m,h_m,c,e}(r)
=-r+(m-7)r^2+(35-h_m)r^3+(35-c)r^4+(21-e)r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
For \(1\le m\le5\), throughout \([1/3,2/5]\),
\[
(\Gamma^H_{m,h_m,c,e})'(r)
\ge
-\frac{29}{5}
+\frac43+\frac{100}{27}+\frac{95}{81}+\frac{14}{81}+\frac7{729}
=\frac{2159}{3645}>0.
\]
Thus \(\Gamma^H\) is increasing, so its minimum on \([\beta_m,2/5]\) occurs at \(r=\beta_m\).

At the switch point, the two gaps agree. The exact values are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & \text{gap at }r=\beta_m\\
\hline
0 & \dfrac{40418}{78125}\\[1ex]
1 & \dfrac{70278389144670}{109^7}\\[1ex]
2 & \dfrac{555459}{8^7}\\[1ex]
3 & \dfrac{212387443359}{58^7}\\[1ex]
4 & \dfrac{77267603}{20^7}\\[1ex]
5 & \dfrac{238944577998}{125^7}
\end{array}
\]
All are positive. Hence the desired gap is positive for every \(m\le5\).

#### Large \(m\ge6\)

If \(|E|\le5\), then
\[
d_3\le31,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le2.
\]
Since \(m\ge6\), \(d_2=28-m\le22\). Therefore it suffices to prove positivity of
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(r)
=(1+r)^7-(1+8r+22r^2+31r^3+17r^4+2r^5).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(r)
=-r-r^2+4r^3+18r^4+19r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
Its derivative satisfies
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}'(r)
\ge -1-\frac45+\frac{12}{9}+\frac{72}{27}
=\frac{11}{5}>0,
\]
and
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(1/3)=\frac{31}{2187}>0.
\]
Thus \(\Gamma_{\le5}(r)>0\) on \([1/3,2/5]\).

If \(|E|=6\), then \(E=K_4\), and we have
\[
d_3\le28,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le4.
\]
Again \(d_2\le22\). It suffices to prove positivity of
\[
\Gamma_6(r)
=(1+r)^7-(1+8r+22r^2+28r^3+17r^4+4r^5).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_6(r)
=-r-r^2+7r^3+18r^4+17r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
Its derivative is positive on \([1/3,2/5]\), and
\[
\Gamma_6(1/3)=\frac{256}{2187}>0.
\]
Therefore \(\Gamma_6(r)>0\) on the whole interval.

This completes the proof for \(p\in[1/4,2/7]\).

Combining the four ranges
\[
\left[\frac16,\frac15\right],
\quad
\left[\frac15,\frac14\right),
\quad
\left[\frac14,\frac27\right],
\quad
\left[\frac27,\frac13\right),
\]
we obtain
\[
\mu_p(D)<q.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
The theorem is proved.

Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define \[ w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i, \] with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by \[ \mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, \] with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}. \] For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct, \[ H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing. \] Then the following statements hold. 1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\), \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] 2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] 3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\). ## Proof Let \[ V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}. \] Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ \Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}. \] Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if \[ \Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\}, \] then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since \[ \Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|} \] for every \(B\subseteq[m]\). First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A). \] Therefore \[ A\in\mathcal D \iff w(A)

1-p=q \iff w([m]\setminus A)>q \iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F. \] Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\). Using this bijection, \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D) =\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|} =\mu_q(\mathcal F), \] because \(1-q=p\). Now, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right] =\Pr[w(V)\ge p] =1-\Pr[w(V)q\right] =\Pr[w(\Xi)>q] =\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \iff 1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p \iff \mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q \iff \mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q \iff \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] This proves the equivalence. It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that \[ B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing. \] For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence \[ \#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1. \] Therefore \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j) = \sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i = \sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\} \le (r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i =r-1. \] On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\), \[ w(B_j)>q=1-p \] for every \(j\), and so \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1, \] because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\). This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting. ## Task t2_strict_threshold_minimal — Try to prove strict-threshold lemma for p<1/3 via threshold-family/minimal-counterexample analysis [unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let $v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d.
