Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Let  
\[
D_p=\{B\subseteq[n]:w(B)<p\},\qquad F_q=\{A\subseteq[n]:w(A)>q\},\qquad q=1-p.
\]
Complementation gives \(\mu_q(F_q)=\mu_p(D_p)\), so I work mostly on \(D_p\).

## 1. New complete partial theorem: support size \(\le k+3\)

### Theorem
Let \(k\ge3\), \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), \(q=1-p\). If the positive support of \(w\) has size at most \(k+3\), then
\[
\mu_q(F_q)=\mu_p(D_p)\le q.
\]
In particular, in the first high-bias case \(k=3\), \(q\in[2/3,3/4]\), no strict LTF counterexample exists on at most \(6\) positive weights.

### Proof

If some \(w_i\ge p\), then
\[
\Pr_p[w(B)\ge p]\ge \Pr_p[i\in B]=p,
\]
so \(\mu_p(D_p)\le q\). Thus, for a strict counterexample, after deleting zero weights we may assume
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i.
\]

We need one structural lemma.

### Support-excess lemma
Assume the positive support size is \(n=k+s\), and \(0<w_i<p\). If \(B_1,\dots,B_t\in D_p\) are pairwise disjoint and \(|B_j|=r_j\), then
\[
\sum_{j=1}^t (r_j-1)<s.
\]

**Proof.** Put \(U=\bigcup_j B_j\). Since each \(B_j\) is bad,
\[
w(U)<tp.
\]
Hence
\[
w(U^c)>1-tp.
\]
If \(\sum_j(r_j-1)\ge s\), then
\[
|U^c|
=k+s-\sum_j r_j
=k-t+s-\sum_j(r_j-1)
\le k-t.
\]
Therefore, because every weight is \(<p\),
\[
w(U^c)<|U^c|p\le (k-t)p.
\]
But \(kp\le1\), so
\[
1-tp\ge(k-t)p,
\]
contradicting \(w(U^c)>1-tp\). ∎

The already-known rank-two argument handles \(n\le k+2\). For completeness: when \(n=k+1\), there are no bad pairs, so
\[
\mu_p(D_p)=q^{k+1}+(k+1)pq^k=q^k(1+kp).
\]
When \(n=k+2\), the support-excess lemma says \(D_p\) has rank at most \(2\), and the bad-pair graph has matching number at most \(1\), hence at most \(n-1=k+1\) edges. Thus again
\[
\mu_p(D_p)
\le q^{k+2}+(k+2)pq^{k+1}+(k+1)p^2q^k
=q^k(1+kp).
\]

Now take the new case \(n=k+3\). The support-excess lemma gives:

* \(D_p\) has rank at most \(3\);
* there are no three disjoint bad pairs;
* no bad pair is disjoint from a bad triple.

Let \(e_2\) and \(e_3\) denote the numbers of bad pairs and bad triples. Then
\[
\mu_p(D_p)
=
q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}+e_2p^2q^{k+1}+e_3p^3q^k.
\]
Put \(\lambda=p/q\). It is enough to prove
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3\le 2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]

If \(e_3=0\), then the bad-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). By the \(r=2\) Erdős–Gallai matching bound,
\[
e_2\le \max\{10,2(k+3)-3\}=\max\{10,2k+3\}.
\]
For \(k\ge4\), this is at most \(2k+3\). For \(k=3\), since \(\lambda\ge1/3\),
\[
10\le 9+4\lambda=2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]
So the desired bound holds.

Now suppose \(e_3>0\). Let \(\mathcal H\) be the 3-uniform hypergraph of bad triples. If \(T,U\in\mathcal H\) and \(|T\cap U|\le1\), then \(U\) contains a pair disjoint from \(T\). That pair is bad because it is contained in the bad triple \(U\), contradicting the support-excess lemma. Hence \(\mathcal H\) is pairwise 2-intersecting.

A 2-intersecting 3-uniform family on \(N\ge6\) vertices has size at most \(N-2\): either all triples share a common pair, giving at most \(N-2\) choices for the third vertex, or two triples share exactly a pair but there is no global common pair; then a short check shows the whole family is contained in a fixed 4-set, giving at most \(4\le N-2\) triples. Here \(N=k+3\), so
\[
e_3\le k+1.
\]

Fix one bad triple \(T\). Every bad pair intersects \(T\). Therefore there are no bad pairs inside \(T^c\). Bad pairs are either inside \(T\), contributing at most \(3\), or cross between \(T\) and \(T^c\). The cross graph is bipartite with parts of sizes \(3\) and \(k\), and it has no matching of size \(3\), again by the support-excess lemma. By König’s theorem, it has a vertex cover of size at most \(2\), hence at most \(2k\) edges. Thus
\[
e_2\le 3+2k.
\]
Therefore
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3\le 2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]

Plugging this into the layer formula gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mu_p(D_p)
&\le q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}
+(2k+3)p^2q^{k+1}
+(k+1)p^3q^k\\
&=q^k\Big(q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3\Big).
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(q=1-p\), the bracket simplifies exactly:
\[
q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3=1+kp.
\]
Hence
\[
\mu_p(D_p)\le q^k(1+kp).
\]
Finally,
\[
q^k(1+kp)\le q
\]
is equivalent to
\[
(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp)\le1.
\]
The left-hand side is decreasing on \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), and at \(p=1/(k+1)\) the required inequality follows from the binomial estimate
\[
\left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1}\ge \frac{2k+1}{k+1}.
\]
Thus \(\mu_p(D_p)\le q\), as claimed. ∎

So the first genuinely remaining \(k=3\) case now has at least \(7\) positive weights.

---

## 2. Endpoint-to-interior differential route

Fix
\[
F=F_q=\{A:w(A)>q\},
\]
and set
\[
t_0=\frac{k-1}{k}.
\]
Frankl–Tokushige gives
\[
\mu_{t_0}(F)\le t_0.
\]
If a violation existed, then
\[
\mu_q(F)>q.
\]
For
\[
f(t)=\mu_t(F),
\]
Russo’s formula gives
\[
f'(t)=I_t(F)
=\sum_i \Pr_t\big[w(R)\le q<w(R)+w_i\big],
\]
where \(R\subseteq[n]\setminus\{i\}\) is sampled with \(t\)-biased measure. A violation would imply
\[
\int_{t_0}^q (I_t(F)-1)\,dt>0.
\]

A simple pointwise derivative bound \(I_t(F)\le1\) is false even for admissible LTFs. For \(k=3\), take \(p=0.30\), \(q=0.70\), and four weights \(w_i=1/4\). Then
\[
F_q=\{|A|\ge3\},
\]
so
\[
f(t)=4t^3(1-t)+t^4=t^3(4-3t),
\]
and
\[
f'(t)=12t^2(1-t).
\]
At \(t_0=2/3\),
\[
f'(t_0)=\frac{16}{9}>1.
\]
Thus the endpoint route needs a genuinely integrated stability statement: endpoint slack plus LTF structure must dominate the possible high influence. The support-\(\le k+3\) proof above may be viewed as a finite structural version of such a stability input.

---

## 3. Random thinning route

Let \(A\sim\mu_q\), and thin \(A\) by keeping each selected element with probability
\[
\alpha=\frac{t_0}{q}.
\]
Then \(B\sim\mu_{t_0}\). If one could prove the pointwise implication
\[
w(A)>q
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[w(B)>t_0\mid A]\ge \alpha,
\]
then
\[
\mu_{t_0}(\{w>t_0\})
\ge \alpha\,\mu_q(F_q),
\]
and since \(\mu_{t_0}(\{w>t_0\})\le t_0\), we would get
\[
\mu_q(F_q)\le \frac{t_0}{\alpha}=q.
\]

But this pointwise bound is false. For \(k=3\), take \(q=0.70\), \(t_0=2/3\), \(\alpha=20/21\), and let \(A\) contain three weights \(0.24,0.24,0.24\), so \(w(A)=0.72>q\), with each weight \(<p=0.30\). To have thinned weight \(>2/3\), all three items must survive, so
\[
\Pr[w(B)>2/3\mid A]=\alpha^3<\alpha.
\]

A more promising averaged form is the equivalent sprinkling decomposition. Generate \(A\sim\mu_q\) by first sampling \(B\sim\mu_{t_0}\), then adding each missing coordinate independently with probability
\[
\beta=\frac{q-t_0}{1-t_0}.
\]
Let
\[
G_{t_0}=\{B:w(B)>t_0\}.
\]
Then
\[
\mu_{t_0}(G_{t_0})\le t_0,
\]
and
\[
\mu_q(F_q)
\le
t_0+
\mathbb E_{B\sim\mu_{t_0}}
\left[
\mathbf 1_{\{w(B)\le t_0\}}
\Pr_\beta\big[w(B)+w(C\cap B^c)>q\mid B\big]
\right].
\]
Thus the thinning route reduces exactly to proving that the sprinkling error is at most
\[
q-t_0.
\]
The obstruction is concentration near the boundary \(w(B)=t_0\); Markov-type estimates are too weak there.

---

## 4. Spectral/Hoffman route setup

For \(\theta=q\), define the \(k\)-fold empty-intersection kernel
\[
K(X^1,\dots,X^k)
=
\prod_i\left(1-\prod_{j=1}^k X_i^j\right).
\]
If \(F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting, then
\[
\mathbb E_{\mu_\theta^{\otimes k}}
\left[
\prod_{j=1}^k \mathbf 1_F(X^j)\,K(X^1,\dots,X^k)
\right]=0.
\]

Using the \(\theta\)-biased basis
\[
\chi_i(x)=\frac{x_i-\theta}{\sqrt{\theta(1-\theta)}},
\]
one coordinate contributes
\[
1-\prod_{j=1}^k(\theta+\sigma\chi_j)
=
1-\theta^k
-
\sum_{\varnothing\neq R\subseteq[k]}
\theta^{k-|R|}\sigma^{|R|}
\prod_{j\in R}\chi_j,
\]
where \(\sigma=\sqrt{\theta(1-\theta)}\).

The LTF condition gives an additional first-level constraint:
\[
\mathbb E_\theta[\mathbf 1_F(w(X)-\theta)]>0,
\]
i.e.
\[
\sum_i w_i\,\widehat{\mathbf 1_F}(\{i\})>0.
\]
Moreover, monotonicity gives nonnegative first-level Fourier coefficients. The cap \(w_i<p\) converts this into some unweighted first-level mass. The difficulty is that the empty-intersection form contains all mixed higher-order Fourier products, not only the first level. Above \(\theta=t_0\), the usual Hoffman/Frankl–Tokushige sign pattern no longer directly controls those higher terms. So a spectral proof would need a new inequality tying the higher Fourier mass of a threshold family to its positive weighted first level.

---

## 5. Minimal-counterexample structure for \(k=3\)

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\), any strict counterexample must now satisfy:

1. after deleting zero weights, every weight obeys \(0<w_i<p\);
2. the positive support size is at least \(7\);
3. for support size \(n=7\), the bad family \(D_p\) has rank at most \(4\);
4. no four disjoint bad pairs exist;
5. no two disjoint bad triples exist;
6. no bad pair is disjoint from a bad \(4\)-set.

The new difficulty at \(n=7\) is that a bad pair may be disjoint from a bad triple; this was forbidden at \(n=6\) and was exactly what forced the bad triples to be 2-intersecting.

## Task t9_complement_kwise_ltf — Exploit the complement high-bias formulation and k-wise intersecting LTF structure.
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I’ll work in the complement formulation. Let \(p=1-q\), delete zero-weight coordinates, and write

\[
G=\{B\subseteq[n]: w(B)<p\}.
\]

Then

\[
\mu_q(F)=\Pr_{A\sim \mu_q}[w(A)>q]
       =\Pr_{B\sim \mu_p}[w(B)<p]
       =\mu_p(G).
\]

So the target is \(\mu_p(G)\le q\).

## 1. First structural point: the \(k\)-wise condition is automatic

If \(p\le 1/k\) and \(B_1,\dots,B_k\in G\), then

\[
w(B_1\cup\cdots\cup B_k)
\le \sum_{j=1}^k w(B_j)
< kp\le 1.
\]

Thus \(B_1\cup\cdots\cup B_k\ne[n]\), so the complements \(A_j=[n]\setminus B_j\in F\) have nonempty intersection. Hence every such threshold family is automatically \(k\)-wise intersecting. The real issue is therefore the weighted lower-tail inequality

\[
\Pr_{p}[w(B)<p]\le 1-p
\]

under the reduced condition \(\max_i w_i<p\).

---

## 2. A useful “support-excess” structural lemma

Let the positive support size be \(n=k+s\). For \(G=\{B:w(B)<p\}\), the following holds.

**Lemma.** If \(B_1,\dots,B_t\in G\) are pairwise disjoint and \(|B_j|=r_j\), then necessarily

\[
\sum_{j=1}^t (r_j-1)<s.
\]

In particular:

- if \(n=k+1\), then \(G\) has no bad set of size \(2\) or more;
- if \(n=k+2\), then \(G\) has no bad set of size \(3\) or more, and its bad-pair graph has matching number at most \(1\);
- if \(n=k+3\), then bad sets have size at most \(3\), there are no three disjoint bad pairs, no two disjoint bad triples, and no bad pair disjoint from a bad triple.

**Proof.** Let \(U=B_1\cup\cdots\cup B_t\). Since each \(B_j\) is bad,

\[
w(U)<tp.
\]

Thus

\[
w(U^c)>1-tp.
\]

But

\[
|U^c|=k+s-\sum_j r_j.
\]

If \(\sum_j(r_j-1)\ge s\), then

\[
|U^c|+t
= k+s-\sum_j r_j+t
= k+s-\sum_j(r_j-1)
\le k,
\]

so \(|U^c|\le k-t\). Since each coordinate has weight \(<p\),

\[
w(U^c)< |U^c|p\le (k-t)p.
\]

But \(kp\le 1\), hence

\[
1-tp\ge (k-t)p.
\]

So we get

\[
w(U^c)>1-tp\ge (k-t)p\ge |U^c|p>w(U^c),
\]

a contradiction. ∎

This lemma is the main structural gain: small support excess forces the bad family to have very low rank and strong matching restrictions.

---

## 3. Rank-two theorem: target holds for support size at most \(k+2\)

### Theorem

Let \(k\ge3\), \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_i\in(0,p)\) sum to \(1\). If the positive support size satisfies

\[
n\le k+2,
\]

then

\[
\mu_q(F)=\mu_p(G)\le q.
\]

More generally, the same conclusion holds whenever \(G\) has no bad set of size at least \(3\), and the graph of bad pairs

\[
E_2=\{\{i,j\}: w_i+w_j<p\}
\]

has matching number at most \(1\).

### Proof

Because \(\sum_i w_i=1\) and each \(w_i<p\), one has \(n>1/p\), so \(n\ge k+1\).

#### Case 1: \(n=k+1\)

For any two coordinates \(i,j\), the other \(k-1\) coordinates have total weight \(<(k-1)p\). Hence

\[
w_i+w_j>1-(k-1)p\ge p.
\]

So no pair is bad. Thus the only bad sets are \(\varnothing\) and the \(n=k+1\) singletons. Therefore

\[
\mu_p(G)
= q^{k+1}+(k+1)pq^k
= q^k(q+(k+1)p)
= q^k(1+kp).
\]

#### Case 2: \(n\ge k+2\), rank \(\le2\), bad-pair matching number \(\le1\)

The graph \(E_2\) has at most \(n-1\) edges: a graph with no two disjoint edges is either a star or a triangle, and \(n\ge5\). Therefore

\[
\mu_p(G)
\le q^n+npq^{n-1}+(n-1)p^2q^{n-2}.
\]

The bracket simplifies exactly:

\[
q^n+npq^{n-1}+(n-1)p^2q^{n-2}
= q^{n-2}\bigl(q^2+npq+(n-1)p^2\bigr)
= q^{n-2}\bigl(1+(n-2)p\bigr).
\]

The function

\[
h_n:=q^{n-2}(1+(n-2)p)
\]

is decreasing in \(n\), because

\[
h_n-h_{n+1}
= q^{n-2}(n-1)p^2>0.
\]

Hence for \(n\ge k+2\),

\[
\mu_p(G)\le h_{k+2}=q^k(1+kp).
\]

So in all cases,

\[
\mu_p(G)\le q^k(1+kp).
\]

It remains to check

\[
q^k(1+kp)\le q.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp)\le 1.
\]

Let

\[
R_k(p)=(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp).
\]

Then

\[
R_k'(p)
=(1-p)^{k-2}(1-k^2p).
\]

On \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\) and \(k\ge3\), we have \(1-k^2p<0\), so \(R_k\) is decreasing. Thus its maximum occurs at \(p=1/(k+1)\). There,

\[
R_k\!\left(\frac1{k+1}\right)
=
\left(\frac{k}{k+1}\right)^{k-1}\frac{2k+1}{k+1}.
\]

It is enough to prove

\[
\left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1}
\ge \frac{2k+1}{k+1}.
\]

By the binomial expansion,

\[
\left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1}
\ge
1+\frac{k-1}{k}
+\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2}.
\]

Subtracting \((2k+1)/(k+1)=2-1/(k+1)\), the difference is

\[
-\frac1k+\frac1{k+1}
+\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2}
=
\frac{(k-1)(k-2)(k+1)-2k}{2k^2(k+1)}\ge0
\]

for every \(k\ge3\). Hence \(q^k(1+kp)\le q\). ∎

---

## 4. Explicit \(k=3\) consequence

For \(k=3\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\). The theorem gives:

> If the positive support size is at most \(5\), then
> \[
> \Pr_p[w(B)<p]\le q.
> \]

Indeed, the bound becomes

\[
\mu_p(G)\le q^3(1+3p).
\]

Check directly:

\[
q^3(1+3p)\le q
\iff
q^2(1+3p)\le1.
\]

Since \(q=1-p\),

\[
q^2(1+3p)-1
=
(1-p)^2(1+3p)-1
=
p-5p^2+3p^3
=
p(1-5p+3p^2).
\]

On \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), the quadratic \(1-5p+3p^2\) is already negative at \(p=1/4\) and decreasing throughout the interval. Thus

\[
q^3(1+3p)\le q.
\]

So the first genuinely unresolved \(k=3\) case must have at least \(6\) positive weights.

---

## 5. The precise next obstruction

For \(k=3\), \(n=6\), the support-excess lemma says that \(G\) has rank at most \(3\). Let

\[
e_2=\#\{\{i,j\}: w_i+w_j<p\},
\]

\[
e_3=\#\{\{i,j,\ell\}: w_i+w_j+w_\ell<p\}.
\]

Then exactly

\[
\mu_p(G)
=
q^6+6pq^5+e_2p^2q^4+e_3p^3q^3.
\]

Thus the \(n=6\), \(k=3\) case reduces to proving

\[
e_2+\frac{p}{q}e_3
\le
D(p):=
\frac{q-q^6-6pq^5}{p^2q^4}.
\]

At the endpoints,

\[
D(1/4)=\frac{295}{27}\approx10.926,
\]

\[
D(1/3)=\frac{115}{8}=14.375.
\]

The automatic structural constraints give:

- no three disjoint bad pairs, so \(e_2\le10\);
- no two disjoint bad triples, so \(e_3\le10\);
- every bad triple has all three of its pairs bad;
- every bad pair intersects every bad triple;
- after sorting weights increasingly, the bad pair and bad triple layers are shifted.

These constraints are close but not by themselves sufficient at the upper endpoint: \(e_2\le10\), \(e_3\le10\) gives \(e_2+\frac12e_3\le15\), while \(14.375\) is needed. So the remaining obstruction is a small weighted enumeration problem for shifted bad-pair/bad-triple configurations on six vertices.

Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

I found a concrete finite-support advance: the \(k=3\) theorem holds through **positive support \(7\)**. Thus any strict counterexample in the high-bias interval must have at least **8** positive weights.

## Theorem: no \(k=3\) counterexample on at most 7 weights

Let \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\). If the positive support has size at most \(7\), then
\[
\mu_p(D)=\Pr_{B\sim\mu_p}[w(B)<p]\le q.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

### Proof

If some \(w_i\ge p\), then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge \Pr[v_i=1]=p.
\]
So assume \(w_i<p\) for every positive coordinate. Pad by zero weights so that there are exactly \(7\) coordinates. This does not change \(\mu_p(D)\).

Scale
\[
x_i=\frac{w_i}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
0\le x_i<1,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^7 x_i=T:=\frac1p\in[3,4],
\]
and
\[
D=\{B:x(B)<1\}.
\]

Let \(e_j\) be the number of bad \(j\)-sets:
\[
e_j=\#\{B\subseteq[7]: |B|=j,\ x(B)<1\}.
\]

There is no bad set of size at least \(5\): if \(|B|\ge5\) and \(x(B)<1\), then \(|B^c|\le2\), while
\[
x(B^c)=T-x(B)>T-1\ge2,
\]
contradicting \(x_i<1\) for each coordinate.

Thus
\[
\mu_p(D)
=
q^7+7pq^6+e_2p^2q^5+e_3p^3q^4+e_4p^4q^3.
\]
Put
\[
\lambda=\frac pq\in\left[\frac13,\frac12\right].
\]
After subtracting the empty/singleton contribution and dividing by \(p^2q^5\), it is enough to prove
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4
\le
R(\lambda),
\]
where
\[
R(\lambda)
=
\frac{q-q^7-7pq^6}{p^2q^5}
=
-\frac1\lambda+15+20\lambda+15\lambda^2+6\lambda^3+\lambda^4.
\]

Now define the **good-pair graph** \(H\) on \([7]\) by
\[
ij\in E(H)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x_i+x_j\ge1.
\]
Let
\[
g=|E(H)|=21-e_2.
\]

We need three structural facts.

### Fact 1: crude high-layer bounds

Bad triples are pairwise intersecting. Indeed, if two disjoint bad triples existed, their union would have weight \(<2\), so the remaining singleton would have weight \(>T-2\ge1\), impossible.

Hence, by the \(r=3,n=7\) Erdős–Ko–Rado bound,
\[
e_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

Bad \(4\)-sets are pairwise \(3\)-intersecting. If two bad \(4\)-sets met in at most \(2\) points, their union would have size at least \(6\) and weight \(<2\); the complement would have weight \(>T-2\ge1\), again impossible. Complements of such \(4\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples on \(7\) vertices, so
\[
e_4\le5.
\]

### Fact 2: bad triples and bad 4-sets force good pairs

If \(A\) is a bad triple and \(C=[7]\setminus A\), then
\[
x(C)>T-1\ge2.
\]
If \(H[C]\) had at most two good edges, then the bad-pair graph on \(C\) would have at least four edges, hence two disjoint bad pairs covering \(C\). Their total weight would be \(<2\), contradiction. Therefore every bad triple has complement spanning at least three good edges.

Thus
\[
e_3\le \#\{C\in\binom{[7]}4: e_H(C)\ge3\}.
\]

Similarly, if \(A\) is a bad \(4\)-set and \(C=[7]\setminus A\), then \(|C|=3\) and \(x(C)>2\). If any pair inside \(C\) were bad, that pair would have weight \(<1\), and the third vertex has weight \(<1\), giving \(x(C)<2\), contradiction. Hence \(C\) is a triangle of \(H\). Therefore
\[
e_4\le \#K_3(H).
\]

### Fact 3: for \(p\le2/7\), there are at most 15 bad pairs

Assume \(p\le2/7\), so \(T=1/p\ge7/2\). Suppose for contradiction that \(e_2\ge16\). Then the bad-pair graph \(G\) has at least \(16\) edges, so its complement \(H\) has at most \(5\) edges.

A standard fractional perfect matching criterion says that a graph \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching iff for every \(S\subseteq V(G)\), the number of isolated vertices of \(G-S\) is at most \(|S|\). Here this criterion holds: if \(G-S\) had \(r>|S|=s\) isolated vertices, then all edges among those \(r\) vertices and from them to the remaining \(7-s-r\) vertices would be missing from \(G\), hence present in \(H\). That gives at least
\[
\binom r2+r(7-s-r)>5
\]
missing edges in all possible cases, contradiction.

So \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching \((m_{ij})\) with vertex loads \(1\). Since every edge of \(G\) is a bad pair,
\[
x_i+x_j<1.
\]
Therefore
\[
T=\sum_i x_i
=
\sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij}(x_i+x_j)
<
\sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij}
=
\frac72,
\]
contradicting \(T\ge7/2\). Hence
\[
e_2\le15
\]
whenever \(p\le2/7\).

### Finish: two ranges

#### Range 1: \(p\in[1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(\lambda\in[1/3,2/5]\). By Fact 3 and the crude high-layer bounds,
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4
\le
15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2.
\]
But
\[
R(\lambda)-(15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing for \(\lambda>0\), and at \(\lambda=1/3\) it equals \(1/243>0\). So the desired bound holds throughout this range.

#### Range 2: \(p\in[2/7,1/3]\)

Here \(\lambda\in[2/5,1/2]\). Recall \(g=|E(H)|=21-e_2\).

If \(g\le2\), then no \(4\)-set spans three good edges and \(H\) has no triangle, so \(e_3=e_4=0\). Thus
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4\le21.
\]
Since \(R(\lambda)\) is increasing and \(R(2/5)>21\), this is enough.

If \(g=3\), then a \(4\)-set spanning three good edges must contain all three good edges. There are at most four such \(4\)-sets, and \(H\) has at most one triangle. Hence
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
18+4\lambda+\lambda^2
\le 20.25
<21<R(\lambda).
\]

If \(g=4\), then \(e_2=17\), \(e_3\le15\), and \(H\) has at most one triangle, so
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
17+15\lambda+\lambda^2.
\]
Now
\[
R(\lambda)-(17+15\lambda+\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+14\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-2\lambda-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing on \([2/5,1/2]\), and at \(\lambda=2/5\) it equals \(187/3125>0\). So this case is also done.

Finally, if \(g\ge5\), then \(e_2\le16\), while \(e_3\le15\) and \(e_4\le5\). Hence
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2.
\]
And
\[
R(\lambda)-(16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-\lambda-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing on \([2/5,1/2]\), and at \(\lambda=2/5\) it equals \(637/3125>0\).

Thus in every case
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4\le R(\lambda),
\]
so
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q.
\]
This proves the theorem. ∎

## Consequence

The previous support-\(\le6\) obstruction is now eliminated one step further:

> For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample to  
> \[
> \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
> \]
> must have at least **8 positive weights**.

The new \(n=7\) ingredient is genuinely LTF-specific: a bad triple’s complement has total weight \(>2\), forcing at least three good pairs in that complement. This is stronger than merely using automatic \(3\)-wise intersection.

I can close a **new support-9 range**:

\[
\boxed{n=9,\quad \frac15\le p\le \frac14\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Pr[S>p]>p.}
\]

I did **not** close the whole upper interval \([1/4,1/3)\) in this pass. I found a strong deficiency-cascade reduction for it, and the remaining obstruction is now quite explicit: it is a high \(D_4\) / low-winning-pair configuration around \(p=2/7\).

---

## 1. Support \(9\) eliminated on \([1/5,1/4]\)

Let

\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq,
\]

and

\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]

Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^9\sum_j d_jr^j,\qquad q=q^9(1+r)^8,
\]

it suffices to show

\[
\sum_j d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]

Assume

\[
\frac15\le p\le \frac14,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\frac14\le r\le \frac13.
\]

For \(p\le1/4\), no losing set of size \(6\) or more exists: if \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so

\[
w(A^c)<3p\le q\le 1-w(A),
\]

contradiction. Thus only layers \(0,\dots,5\) matter.

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W\iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and let \(m=|W|\). Then

\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

### Losing triples

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If some pair \(e\subset C\) were losing, and \(z=C\setminus e\), then

\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence every pair of \(C\) is winning, so \(C\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Each triangle \(C\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Therefore

\[
I(H)\le 10\,\tau(W),
\]

where \(I(H)\) is the number of disjoint pairs in \(H\), and \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\).

The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\) has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{5}{28}h(h-28).
\]

Let \(T_m\) be the maximum possible number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges. Explicitly, if

\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]

then

\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]

Thus

\[
h(h-28)\le 56T_m,
\]

and we use the bound

\[
d_3\le H_m:=\max\{h\le56:\ h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]

The cap \(56\) is the Erdős matching bound for a \(3\)-graph on \(9\) vertices with no three disjoint edges.

### Losing fours

If \(A\in D_4\), let \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus the leftover vertex \(z\), then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence \(W[B]\) must meet every \(2\)-matching in \(K_5\). Equivalently, the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), hence has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore

\[
|W[B]|\ge 6.
\]

Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),

\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m,
\]

so

\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

### Losing fives

If \(A\in D_5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]

contradiction. Thus \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(|W[B]|\ge3\). Counting incidences,

\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]

so \(d_5\le7m\). Also complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(9\) vertices, hence by the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado / Ahlswede–Khachatrian bound,

\[
d_5\le21.
\]

Therefore

\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

### Polynomial certificate

For each \(m=0,\dots,36\), define

\[
P_m(r)
=
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right).
\]

The required inequality follows from

\[
P_m(r)>0
\qquad
\forall m\in\{0,\dots,36\},\quad \frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

This is a finite exact check. Here is a reproducible rational/Sturm certificate:

```python
import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")
L = sp.Rational(1, 4)
U = sp.Rational(1, 3)

def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in a graph with m edges:
    # colex/Kruskal-Katona graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56 by matching-number bound
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P = sp.expand(
        (1 + r)**8
        - (1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5)
    )
    poly = sp.Poly(P, r)

    # Exact endpoint positivity.
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, L)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, L))
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, U)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, U))

    # Exact Sturm root count on [1/4, 1/3].
    assert poly.count_roots(L, U) == 0, (m, P)

print("All 37 support-9 polynomials are positive on [1/4, 1/3].")
```

For instance, the small-\(m\) endpoint gap begins with

\[
P_0(1/4)
=
\left(\frac54\right)^8
-
\left(1+\frac94+\frac{36}{16}+\frac{28}{64}\right)
=
\frac{1505}{65536}>0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mu_p(D)<q,
\]

and hence

\[
\Pr[S>p]>p
\]

for every strict reduced support-\(9\) instance with

\[
\frac15\le p\le\frac14.
\]

Together with the earlier support-\(9\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), this eliminates support \(9\) on

\[
\boxed{\frac16\le p\le\frac14.}
\]

---

## 2. New upper-interval tool: deficiency cascade

For the remaining interval

\[
\frac14<p<\frac13,
\]

the previous \(p\le1/4\) blocker for losing \(4\)-sets no longer works, because the contradiction used \(4p\le1\). However, there is a useful general deficiency cascade.

For a losing \(k\)-set \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency

\[
\epsilon_k(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]

and put

\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\epsilon_k(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then

\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=
(k+1)p-kw(B)
=
p+k(p-w(B)).
\]

Summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, gives

\[
\boxed{
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
}
\]

For pairs, let

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j.
\]

Then

\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}.
\]

Since

\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]

and each winning pair contributes \(p-w_i-w_j>-p\), we get

\[
\boxed{
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
}
\]

Thus, in support \(9\),

\[
\boxed{
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_4+3E_4\le6E_3,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_5+4E_5\le5E_4,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_6+5E_6\le4E_5.
}
\]

This is stronger than the one-layer deficiency bounds because it forces a shared budget across \(D_3,D_4,D_5,D_6\).

---

## 3. Upper-interval high-layer bounds

For \(p<1/3\), we still have:

\[
d_5\le \min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Also, if \(A\in D_6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\) and weight at least \(q\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) we would get total weight \(<3p<1\), impossible. Hence \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\), so

\[
d_6\le \tau(W)\le T_m.
\]

The complement \(2\)-intersection bound gives

\[
d_6\le7.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
d_6\le \min\{7,T_m\}.
}
\]

The problematic layer is \(D_4\). We have the general bound

\[
d_4\le56,
\]

because losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, but this is not strong enough near \(p=2/7\).

---

## 4. Exact obstruction left by the current upper-interval bounds

Using the deficiency cascade, the blocker bounds for \(D_5,D_6\), the matching bound \(d_3\le56\), the intersecting bound \(d_4\le56\), and shadow-incidence constraints, the first relaxed obstruction occurs near

\[
r=\frac25
\qquad
\left(p=\frac27\right),
\]

with

\[
m=3.
\]

The relaxed layer profile is

\[
(d_0,d_1,d_2,d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)
=
(1,9,33,56,56,6,1).
\]

At \(r=2/5\), this gives

\[
1+9r+33r^2+56r^3+56r^4+6r^5+r^6
=
\frac{5844975}{390625},
\]

whereas

\[
(1+r)^8
=
\left(\frac75\right)^8
=
\frac{5764801}{390625}.
\]

So the relaxed certificate misses by

\[
\frac{5844975-5764801}{390625}
=
\frac{80174}{390625}.
\]

This profile is almost certainly not threshold-feasible, but the current general inequalities do not rule it out. Concretely, to close this obstruction it would suffice to prove, in the \(m=3\) neighborhood, a bound roughly of the form

\[
d_4\le47
\]

or a comparable tradeoff between \(d_3\) and \(d_4\). A natural candidate is:

> If \(D_4\subseteq\binom{[9]}4\) is an intersecting threshold family whose shadow \(D_3\) has matching number at most \(2\), and if the winning-pair graph has \(m\le3\), then \(d_4\le47\).

A sharper form, probably true, is \(d_4\le35\), but I did not complete that structural proof here.

---

## 5. Exact 8-head fallback formulation

For support \(9\), choose one coordinate as a tail, say \(b=w_9\), and write the remaining head weights as \(x_1,\dots,x_8\), with

\[
\sum_{i=1}^8 x_i=1-b.
\]

Let

\[
R=\sum_{i=1}^8 x_iv_i.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
\Pr[R>p]+p\,\Pr[p-b<R\le p].
\]

Equivalently, define two head downsets

\[
E_0=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p\},
\]

\[
E_1=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p-b\}.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
1-q\,\mu_p(E_0)-p\,\mu_p(E_1).
\]

So a support-\(9\) counterexample is exactly an \(8\)-coordinate head satisfying

\[
q\,\mu_p(E_0)+p\,\mu_p(E_1)\ge q.
\]

This is the clean finite-head fallback: it reduces the remaining support-\(9\) problem to a two-threshold weighted downset inequality on \(8\) coordinates. The known support-\(8\) theorem does not immediately apply because the head has total mass \(1-b\), not \(1\), and the Bernoulli parameter remains \(p\), but this formulation isolates precisely what an \(8\)-head certificate must prove.

---

# Theorem: support \(8\) is eliminated on \([1/5,1/3)\)

Let \(q=1-p\), \(r=p/q\), and \(d_j=|D_j|\). Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j
\]

and

\[
1-p=q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]

it suffices to prove

\[
\sum_j d_j r^j<(1+r)^7.
\]

---

## 1. Range \([1/5,1/4)\)

For \(p<1/4\), no losing set of size \(5\) or more exists. Also:

- four disjoint losing pairs would have total weight \(<1\), so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\), hence by Erdős–Gallai
  \[
  d_2\le 21;
  \]
- two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices have total \(<4p\le1\), so \(D_3\) is intersecting, hence
  \[
  d_3\le \binom72=21;
  \]
- the lower shadow of \(D_4\) lies inside \(D_3\). By Kruskal–Katona, if \(d_4\ge16\), then \(|\partial D_4|\ge23>21\). Hence
  \[
  d_4\le15.
  \]

Thus

\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^8(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4).
\]

But for \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\),

\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=
r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr)>0.
\]

So \(\mu_p(D)<q\).

---

## 2. Structural tools for \([1/4,1/3)\)

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W \iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and put \(m=|W|\), so

\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

### High-layer complement bounds

If \(A\in D_4\), then \(A^c\) is a \(4\)-set of weight at least \(q\). If two such complements met in at most one vertex, their weights would sum to at most \(1+p\), contradicting \(2q>1+p\) for \(p<1/3\). Hence the complements of \(D_4\) form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices. By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado/Frankl bound,

\[
d_4\le17.
\]

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(3\)-family, so

\[
d_5\le6.
\]

Also, every losing \(4\)-set complement must be a \(W\)-blocker: \(W\) must meet every perfect matching of that \(4\)-set. For small \(m\), direct graph checking gives

\[
m\le2\Rightarrow d_4=0,\qquad
m=3,4\Rightarrow d_4\le5,\qquad
m=5\Rightarrow d_4\le10.

### Losing-triple bound

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then the complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) must be winning; otherwise \(w(T)+w(U)+w(e)\le3p<1\). \[

The Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\) has \(56\) vertices, degree \(10\), and least eigenvalue \(-6\), so Hoffman supersaturation gives

Therefore

\[

We also use a weight-deficiency bound. For a losing pair \(ij\), set

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.

For every losing triple \(T\),

\[
\sum_{ij\subset T}\delta_{ij}=3p-2w(T)\ge p.

Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, and

\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}
\le ((28+m)p-7).

Thus

\[
d_3\le 6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right)
=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).

## 3. Range \([2/7,1/3)\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). Use

d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.

# Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

# Claim Being Verified
After the listed minor fixes, the proof establishes the following: for every \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_8\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_8\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

# Solution
**Claim.** Let \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_8\) be positive real numbers with
\[
w_i<p\quad(1\le i\le 8),\qquad \sum_{i=1}^8 w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\dots,v_8\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

For \(A\subseteq[8]\), write \(w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i\). Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq,
\]
and define the bad family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[8]: w(A)\le p\}.
\]
Put \(D_j=D\cap\binom{[8]}j\) and \(d_j=|D_j|\). If \(R=\{i:v_i=1\}\), then
\[
\Pr[R=A]=p^{|A|}q^{8-|A|}
\]
and
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{8-|A|}
=q^8\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j.
\]
Also
\[
q=q^8(1+r)^7,
\]
because \(1+r=1/q\). Therefore it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^8 d_j r^j<(1+r)^7. \tag{1}
\]

We use the following standard finite extremal facts in the precise forms needed:

1. If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[8]}3\) is intersecting, then \(|\mathcal F|\le\binom72=21\).
2. If a graph on \(8\) vertices has matching number at most \(2\), then it has at most \(13\) edges; if it has matching number at most \(3\), then it has at most \(21\) edges.
3. If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[8]}4\) has \(|\mathcal F|\ge16\), then its lower shadow has size at least \(23\). Indeed, \(16=\binom64+\binom33\), so Kruskal–Katona gives shadow size at least \(\binom63+\binom32=23\).
4. If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[8]}4\) is \(2\)-intersecting, then \(|\mathcal F|\le17\). This is the \(n=8,k=4,t=2\) case of the complete intersection theorem: the relevant extremal sizes are
\[
\binom62=15,\qquad \binom43\binom41+\binom44=17,\qquad \binom64=15.
\]

---

### 1. The range \(1/6\le p\le 1/5\)

Here \(r\in[1/5,1/4]\).

There is no losing set of size at least \(4\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge4\), then \(|A^c|\le4\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Thus \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge4\).

The losing triples are intersecting. If \(T,U\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the two remaining weights have total \(<2p\), so
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w([8]\setminus(T\cup U))<p+p+2p\le\frac45,
\]
impossible. Hence \(d_3\le21\).

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). Otherwise three disjoint losing pairs together with the two remaining vertices would give
\[
1<3p+2p\le1,
\]
again impossible. Therefore \(d_2\le13\). Since \(d_0=1\) and \(d_1=8\),
\[
\sum_j d_j r^j\le 1+8r+13r^2+21r^3.
\]
Now
\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+13r^2+21r^3)
=r\bigl(-1+8r+14r^2+35r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr).
\]
The bracket is increasing for \(r>0\), and at \(r=1/5\) it equals
\[
\frac{23061}{15625}>0.
\]
Thus (1) holds for \(1/6\le p\le1/5\).

---

### 2. The range \(1/5\le p<1/4\)

Here \(r\in[1/4,1/3)\).

There is no losing set of size at least \(5\): if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p<1,
\]
a contradiction.

The losing-pair graph has no matching of size \(4\), since four disjoint losing pairs would cover all vertices and have total weight at most \(4p<1\). Hence \(d_2\le21\).

The losing triples are intersecting: two disjoint losing triples plus the remaining two vertices would have total weight \(<4p<1\). Hence \(d_3\le21\).

Also, every \(3\)-subset of a losing \(4\)-set is losing. Thus the lower shadow of \(D_4\) is contained in \(D_3\). If \(d_4\ge16\), Kruskal–Katona gives \(|\partial D_4|\ge23\), contradicting \(d_3\le21\). Therefore \(d_4\le15\).

Thus
\[
\sum_j d_j r^j\le 1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4.
\]
We compute
\[
(1+r)^7-(1+8r+21r^2+21r^3+15r^4)
=r\bigl(-1+14r^2+20r^3+21r^4+7r^5+r^6\bigr).
\]
The bracket is increasing for \(r>0\), and at \(r=1/4\) it equals
\[
\frac{1133}{4096}>0.
\]
So (1) holds for \(1/5\le p<1/4\).

---

### 3. Structural bounds for \(1/4\le p<1/3\)

Assume now \(1/4\le p<1/3\). No losing set has size at least \(6\): if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+2p<1,
\]
impossible.

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs:
\[
ij\in W\iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]
and let \(m=|W|\). Then
\[
d_2=28-m.
\]

#### Lemma 1: Bounds on \(d_4\) and \(d_5\)

We have
\[
d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]

Proof. If \(A\in D_4\), then \(C=A^c\) is a \(4\)-set with
\[
w(C)=1-w(A)\ge1-p=q.
\]
If \(C,C'\) are two such complements with \(|C\cap C'|\le1\), then
\[
w(C)+w(C')=w(C\cup C')+w(C\cap C')<1+p,
\]
while
\[
w(C)+w(C')\ge2q.
\]
Since \(p<1/3\), we have \(2q>1+p\), contradiction. Therefore the complements of losing \(4\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family in \(\binom{[8]}4\), so \(d_4\le17\).

Similarly, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples. Any \(2\)-intersecting family of triples on \([8]\) has size at most \(6\): if all triples share a fixed pair, there are at most \(6\) of them; otherwise, two triples share a pair \(P\), and a third triple not containing \(P\) forces the whole family to lie inside the four triples of a fixed \(4\)-set. Hence \(d_5\le6\). ∎

#### Lemma 2: Losing triples versus winning pairs

Let \(h=d_3\). Then
\[
h\le H_m:=\left\lfloor\frac{21+\sqrt{441+280m}}2\right\rfloor.
\]

Proof. Let \(\mathcal H=D_3\). If \(T,U\in\mathcal H\) are disjoint, then their complementary pair \(e=[8]\setminus(T\cup U)\) satisfies
\[
w(e)=1-w(T)-w(U)\ge1-2p>p,
\]
so \(e\in W\). For each fixed pair \(e\), the other six vertices have exactly
\[
\frac12\binom63=10
\]
unordered partitions into two triples. Hence the number \(I(\mathcal H)\) of unordered disjoint pairs in \(\mathcal H\) is at most \(10m\).

Now consider the Kneser graph \(KG(8,3)\), whose vertices are the \(56\) triples of \([8]\), with two triples adjacent when they are disjoint. It is \(10\)-regular and has least adjacency eigenvalue \(-6\). Let \(A\) be its adjacency matrix and \(x=1_{\mathcal H}\). Write
\[
x=\frac h{56}{\bf 1}+y,\qquad y\perp{\bf 1}.
\]
Then
\[
I(\mathcal H)=\frac12x^TAx
=\frac12\left(10\frac{h^2}{56}+y^TAy\right).
\]
Since the least eigenvalue is \(-6\),
\[
y^TAy\ge -6\,y^Ty=-6h\left(1-\frac h{56}\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
I(\mathcal H)\ge
\frac12\left(10\frac{h^2}{56}-6h\left(1-\frac h{56}\right)\right)
=\frac{h(h-21)}7.
\]
Thus
\[
\frac{h(h-21)}7\le10m,
\]
so
\[
h^2-21h-70m\le0.
\]
Solving gives the stated bound. ∎

#### Lemma 3: A second bound on \(d_3\)

For every \(m\),
\[
d_3\le 6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]

Proof. For a losing pair \(ij\), define
\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j\ge0.
\]
If \(T=\{a,b,c\}\in D_3\), then all three pairs inside \(T\) are losing, and
\[
\delta_{ab}+\delta_{ac}+\delta_{bc}
=3p-2w(T)\ge p.
\]
Each losing pair lies in at most \(6\) triples, so
\[
p\,d_3\le 6\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=28p-7.
\]
For a winning pair \(ij\in W\),
\[
-p<p-w_i-w_j<0,
\]
because \(w_i,w_j<p\). Hence
\[
\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}\le (28+m)p-7.
\]
Dividing by \(p\),
\[
d_3\le6\left(28+m-\frac7p\right).
\]
Since \(1/p=(1+r)/r\), this becomes
\[
d_3\le6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right).
\]
∎

#### Lemma 4: Small winning-pair graphs

If \(m\le2\), then \(d_4=0\). If \(m=3,4\), then \(d_4\le5\). If \(m=5\), then \(d_4\le10\).

Proof. Let \(A\in D_4\) and put \(C=A^c\). Then \(w(C)\ge q\). If \(C\) could be partitioned into two losing pairs, then
\[
w(C)\le2p<q,
\]
contradiction. Thus \(W[C]\) meets every perfect matching of \(C\). Call such a \(4\)-set a blocker.

On four vertices, a subgraph meeting all three perfect matchings contains a minimal blocker with exactly three edges, and such a minimal blocker is either a triangle or a \(3\)-star.

If \(m\le2\), no triangle or \(3\)-star exists, so there are no blockers.

If \(m=3,4\) and \(W\) contains a triangle, then every blocker contains that triangle, giving at most \(5\) possible \(4\)-sets. If \(W\) is triangle-free, then blockers come from \(3\)-stars, and there are at most \(\binom m3\le4\).

If \(m=5\) and \(W\) is triangle-free, blockers again come from \(3\)-stars, giving at most \(\binom53=10\). If \(W\) has exactly one triangle, then the \(4\)-sets containing it contribute at most \(5\) blockers, and any blocker not containing it must come from a \(3\)-star using both non-triangle edges; there are at most \(2\) such additional blockers. If \(W\) has at least two triangles, then with only \(5\) edges the two triangles share an edge and use all five edges; the triangle-containing blockers are at most \(5+5-1=9\), and every star blocker is among these. Thus \(d_4\le10\) when \(m=5\). ∎

We also need the following consequence for \(d_5\). If \(A\in D_5\) and \(C=A^c\), then \(w(C)\ge q\). For any pair \(\{i,j\}\subset C\), with third element \(k\in C\),
\[
w_i+w_j=w(C)-w_k>1-p-p=1-2p>p.
\]
Thus \(C\) spans a triangle in \(W\). Hence if \(m\le2\), then \(d_5=0\); and if \(m\le5\), then \(d_5\le2\), since a graph with at most five edges has at most two triangles.

#### Lemma 5: Very high pairs for \(1/4\le p\le2/7\)

Assume \(1/4\le p\le2/7\). Define
\[
E=\{ij:w_i+w_j\ge1-2p\}.
\]
Then
\[
|E|\le6,
\]
and if \(|E|=6\), then \(E\cong K_4\).

Proof. Put
\[
a_i=p-w_i>0,\qquad \gamma=4p-1.
\]
Then \(0\le\gamma\le1/7\), \(p=(1+\gamma)/4\), and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^8 a_i=8p-1=1+2\gamma.
\]
Moreover,
\[
ij\in E\iff a_i+a_j\le\gamma.
\]
Order the \(a_i\) so that \(a_1\le\cdots\le a_8\), and let \(\ell\) be the degree of vertex \(1\) in \(E\). If \(ij\in E\), then \(a_1+a_i\le a_i+a_j\le\gamma\) and similarly \(a_1+a_j\le\gamma\). Thus every edge of \(E\) lies among the vertices \(1,\dots,\ell+1\).

If \(\ell\ge6\), then
\[
\sum_i a_i<a_1+6(\gamma-a_1)+p<6\gamma+p<1+2\gamma,
\]
contradiction.

If \(\ell=5\), then
\[
\sum_i a_i<a_1+5(\gamma-a_1)+2p
=5\gamma-4a_1+2p<1+2\gamma,
\]
again a contradiction.

Suppose \(\ell=4\) and \(|E|\ge6\). Then among vertices \(2,3,4,5\), there are at least two \(E\)-edges. Let \(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4\) be the corresponding four \(a\)-values. Each lies in \([a_1,\gamma-a_1]\). If the two edges are disjoint, then \(x_1+x_2+x_3+x_4\le2\gamma\le3\gamma-2a_1\). If they share a vertex of value \(x\), then
\[
x_1+x_2+x_3+x_4\le x+(\gamma-x)+(\gamma-x)+(\gamma-a_1)
\le3\gamma-2a_1.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_i a_i< a_1+(3\gamma-2a_1)+3p
=3\gamma-a_1+3p<1+2\gamma,
\]
contradiction.

Therefore, if \(|E|\ge6\), we must have \(\ell=3\). Then all edges of \(E\) lie on four vertices, so \(|E|\le6\), and equality means those four vertices span a \(K_4\). ∎

Consequences of Lemma 5:

- If \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_3\le31\). Indeed, if two losing triples are disjoint, their complementary pair lies in \(E\), so the number of disjoint pairs in \(D_3\) is at most \(10|E|\le50\). By Lemma 2’s Hoffman bound,
\[
\frac{d_3(d_3-21)}7\le50,
\]
which forces \(d_3\le31\).

- If \(|E|\le5\), then \(d_5\le2\), because complements of losing \(5\)-sets are triangles in \(E\), and a graph with at most five edges has at most two triangles.

- If \(|E|=6\), then \(E=K_4\). A losing triple can contain at most one vertex of this \(K_4\), because any two vertices in it have total weight at least \(1-2p>p\). Hence
\[
d_3\le \binom43+\binom41\binom42=4+24=28.
\]
Also \(d_5\le4\), since the complement of a losing \(5\)-set must be one of the four triangles of that \(K_4\).

---

### 4. The range \(2/7\le p<1/3\)

Here \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\). By Lemmas 1 and 2,
\[
d_2=28-m,\qquad d_3\le H_m,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le6.
\]
So it suffices to prove
\[
\Gamma_m(r)>0,
\]
where
\[
\Gamma_m(r)
=(1+r)^7-\bigl(1+8r+(28-m)r^2+H_mr^3+17r^4+6r^5\bigr).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_m(r)
=-r+(m-7)r^2+(35-H_m)r^3+18r^4+15r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]

Let \(h=H_m\). Since \(h(h-21)\le70m\),
\[
2(m-7)r+3(35-h)r^2
\ge
r\left(\frac{h(h-21)}{35}-14+3(35-h)r\right).
\]
For \(21\le h\le35\), the bracket is minimized at \(r=2/5\), where it is
\[
\frac{h^2-63h+980}{35}\ge-\frac7{20}.
\]
For \(35\le h\le56\), it is minimized at \(r=1/2\), where it is
\[
\frac{2h^2-147h+2695}{70}\ge-\frac7{80}.
\]
Thus, throughout \(r\in[2/5,1/2)\),
\[
2(m-7)r+3(35-h)r^2\ge-\frac7{40}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Gamma_m'(r)>-1-\frac7{40}+72r^3
\ge -\frac{47}{40}+\frac{576}{125}>0.
\]
So \(\Gamma_m\) is increasing on \([2/5,1/2)\).

At \(r=2/5\),
\[
\Gamma_m(2/5)
=\frac{106618+12500m-5000H_m}{78125}.
\]
For \(m=0,1,2\), one has \(H_m=21,23,26\), and the numerator is positive. For \(m\ge3\),
\[
H_m\le21+\frac52m,
\]
so
\[
106618+12500m-5000H_m
\ge106618+12500m-5000\left(21+\frac52m\right)
=1618>0.
\]
Hence \(\Gamma_m(r)>0\) on \([2/5,1/2)\). Thus (1) holds for \(2/7\le p<1/3\).

---

### 5. The range \(1/4\le p\le2/7\)

Here \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\).

#### Small \(m\le5\)

For \(m=0,1,2\), Lemma 4 and the triangle argument give
\[
d_4=d_5=0.
\]
For \(m=3,4\),
\[
d_4\le5,\qquad d_5\le2.
\]
For \(m=5\),
\[
d_4\le10,\qquad d_5\le2.
\]

Let
\[
B_m(r)=6\left(21+m-\frac7r\right)
\]
be the bound from Lemma 3. The relevant data are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c}
m & H_m & c\text{ with }d_4\le c & e\text{ with }d_5\le e
& \beta_m\text{ with }B_m(\beta_m)=H_m
& \text{gap at }\beta_m \\ \hline
0&21&0&0&2/5&\dfrac{40418}{78125}\\[1ex]
1&23&0&0&42/109&\dfrac{70278389144670}{109^7}\\[1ex]
2&26&0&0&3/8&\dfrac{555459}{8^7}\\[1ex]
3&28&5&2&21/58&\dfrac{212387443359}{58^7}\\[1ex]
4&30&5&2&7/20&\dfrac{77267603}{20^7}\\[1ex]
5&31&10&2&42/125&\dfrac{238944577998}{125^7}
\end{array}
\]

All listed gaps are positive.

For \(r\le\beta_m\), use \(d_3\le B_m(r)\). The resulting gap is
\[
\Gamma^D_{m,c,e}(r)
=-r+(35+m)r^2-(91+6m)r^3+(35-c)r^4+(21-e)r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
On \(r\in[1/3,2/5]\),
\[
(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e})''(r)
\le
70+2m-\frac{546+36m}{3}
+420\frac4{25}+420\frac8{125}+210\frac{16}{625}+42\frac{32}{3125}
=-10m-\frac{37856}{3125}<0.
\]
Also
\[
(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e})'(1/3)
=-\frac{974}{729}-\frac{4m}{3}-\frac{4c}{27}-\frac{5e}{81}<0.
\]
Thus \(\Gamma^D_{m,c,e}\) is decreasing, so on \([1/3,\beta_m]\) its minimum occurs at \(r=\beta_m\).

For \(r\ge\beta_m\), use \(d_3\le H_m\). The gap is
\[
\Gamma^H_{m,H_m,c,e}(r)
=-r+(m-7)r^2+(35-H_m)r^3+(35-c)r^4+(21-e)r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
For \(m=1,\dots,5\), throughout \([1/3,2/5]\),
\[
(\Gamma^H_{m,H_m,c,e})'(r)
\ge
-\frac{29}{5}+\frac43+\frac{100}{27}
+\frac{95}{81}+\frac{14}{81}+\frac7{729}
=\frac{2159}{3645}>0.
\]
For \(m=0\), the interval \([\beta_m,2/5]\) is just the endpoint. Hence \(\Gamma^H\) is increasing on the relevant interval, and its minimum also occurs at \(r=\beta_m\). At \(r=\beta_m\), the two gaps agree because \(B_m(\beta_m)=H_m\), and the table shows that value is positive. Thus (1) holds for all \(m\le5\).

#### Large \(m\ge6\)

By Lemma 5, \(|E|\le6\).

If \(|E|\le5\), then
\[
d_3\le31,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le2.
\]
Also \(d_2=28-m\le22\). Thus it suffices to prove positivity of
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(r)
=(1+r)^7-(1+8r+22r^2+31r^3+17r^4+2r^5).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(r)
=-r-r^2+4r^3+18r^4+19r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
Its derivative satisfies
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}'(r)
\ge -1-\frac45+\frac{12}{9}+\frac{72}{27}
=\frac{11}{5}>0,
\]
and
\[
\Gamma_{\le5}(1/3)=\frac{31}{2187}>0.
\]
Therefore \(\Gamma_{\le5}(r)>0\) on \([1/3,2/5]\).

If \(|E|=6\), then \(E=K_4\), and
\[
d_3\le28,\qquad d_4\le17,\qquad d_5\le4.
\]
Again \(d_2\le22\). It suffices to prove positivity of
\[
\Gamma_6(r)
=(1+r)^7-(1+8r+22r^2+28r^3+17r^4+4r^5).
\]
Expanding,
\[
\Gamma_6(r)
=-r-r^2+7r^3+18r^4+17r^5+7r^6+r^7.
\]
Its derivative satisfies
\[
\Gamma_6'(r)
\ge -1-\frac45+\frac{21}{9}+\frac{72}{27}
=\frac{16}{5}>0,
\]
and
\[
\Gamma_6(1/3)=\frac{256}{2187}>0.
\]
Thus \(\Gamma_6(r)>0\) on \([1/3,2/5]\). Therefore (1) holds for \(1/4\le p\le2/7\).

Combining all ranges, (1) holds for every \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\). Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]<q=1-p,
\]
and therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
The claim is proved.

Verify the solution against the **Claim Being Verified**, not the Original Problem. The Claim may equal the Original Problem verbatim (a full solution) or be a deliberately weakened, special-case, or partial version (a relaxation). Either is acceptable — judge the solution on the Claim it actually makes.

A **major gap** is a critical error in the argument for the Claim Being Verified that cannot easily be fixed; if there is a major gap, the solver should likely try another approach.

A **minor gap** is a fixable mistake, omission, unclear justification, typo-level mathematical issue, or bibliographic/reference problem that can likely be repaired without changing the main strategy of the solution.

Do not penalise the solution for failing to establish more than the Claim asks for. Classify bibliographic/reference problems as minor if they are easy to correct, and major if the proof relies essentially on an invalid or unavailable reference and the issue cannot easily be repaired.

# Mandatory proof-obligation and quantitative audits

In addition to checking the solution for ordinary mathematical errors, audit the dependency structure of the proof.

For every nontrivial statement used to prove the Claim Being Verified — including lemmas, propositions, estimates, reductions, identities, cited theorems, deferred claims, and "standard" facts — determine whether it is:

2. assumed without proof;

7. insufficient to imply the step for which it is used.

If the proof relies on a statement that is unproved, assumed, not discharged, circular/equivalent to the target, or valid only under unavailable hypotheses, classify this as a major gap unless it is genuinely peripheral and easily repairable.

If the Claim Being Verified contains quantitative content — such as explicit constants, rates, error bounds, asymptotic notation, thresholds, sample complexity, probability estimates, convergence rates, dimension dependence, or parameter regimes — perform a quantitative-dependence audit:

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
Add the following finite-check details to Lemma 4.

On a fixed \(4\)-set, the three perfect matchings partition the six edges of \(K_4\). Hence a subgraph meeting all three perfect matchings must contain at least one edge from each matching, so it contains a \(3\)-edge hitting subgraph. A \(3\)-edge subgraph of \(K_4\) that contains one edge from each perfect matching is either a triangle or a \(3\)-star: the remaining \(3\)-edge type, a path \(P_4\), misses the perfect matching formed by its two non-edges.

For the case distinctions in Lemma 4:
- If \(m\le2\), no triangle or \(3\)-star exists.
- If \(m=3,4\) and \(W\) has a triangle \(T\), then any blocker either contains \(T\), or is a \(3\)-star using the extra edge and two triangle edges; such a star still has vertex set containing \(T\). Thus at most \(8-3=5\) blocker \(4\)-sets occur.
- If \(W\) is triangle-free, every blocker comes from a \(3\)-star, so there are at most \(\binom m3\) candidates.
- If \(m=5\) and there is exactly one triangle, a blocker not containing it must be a \(3\)-star using both non-triangle edges, giving at most two additional blockers.
- If \(m=5\) and there are at least two triangles, two triangles must share an edge and use all five edges; the triangle-containing \(4\)-sets are at most \(5+5-1=9\), and every \(3\)-star lies on the same four vertices, hence is already counted.

Also, the assertion that a graph with at most five edges has at most two triangles can be justified as follows: if a triangle \(T\) is fixed, only two edges remain outside \(T\); these can create at most one further triangle, since any further triangle sharing an edge with \(T\) needs exactly two such outside edges, while triangles sharing no edge with \(T\) require at least three outside edges.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
is equivalent, for the same weights, to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
With
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family.

It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.
    Verifier's is_relaxation verdict: True
    Proof sketch: By recasting the event {∑wi vi≥p} as the complement of the p-biased family D={A:w(A)<p} and then using the bijection A↦[m]\A to the q-biased family F={B:w(B)>q}, one shows exactly that Pr[∑wi vi≥p]≥p⇔μq(F)≤q. The key combinatorial lemma then proves that whenever rp≤1 no r sets in F can be disjoint: assigning each element to at most r–1 of the B₁,…,Bᵣ gives ∑j w(Bj)≤r–1, while each w(Bj)>q=1–p forces ∑j w(Bj)>r(1–p)≥r–1, a contradiction that establishes F is r-wise intersecting.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.

I found a concrete finite-support advance: the \(k=3\) theorem holds through **positive support \(7\)**. Thus any strict counterexample in the high-bias interval must have at least **8** positive weights.

## Theorem: no \(k=3\) counterexample on at most 7 weights

Let \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\). If the positive support has size at most \(7\), then
\[
\mu_p(D)=\Pr_{B\sim\mu_p}[w(B)<p]\le q.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

### Proof

If some \(w_i\ge p\), then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge \Pr[v_i=1]=p.
\]
So assume \(w_i<p\) for every positive coordinate. Pad by zero weights so that there are exactly \(7\) coordinates. This does not change \(\mu_p(D)\).

Scale
\[
x_i=\frac{w_i}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
0\le x_i<1,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^7 x_i=T:=\frac1p\in[3,4],
\]
and
\[
D=\{B:x(B)<1\}.
\]

Let \(e_j\) be the number of bad \(j\)-sets:
\[
e_j=\#\{B\subseteq[7]: |B|=j,\ x(B)<1\}.
\]

There is no bad set of size at least \(5\): if \(|B|\ge5\) and \(x(B)<1\), then \(|B^c|\le2\), while
\[
x(B^c)=T-x(B)>T-1\ge2,
\]
contradicting \(x_i<1\) for each coordinate.

Thus
\[
\mu_p(D)
=
q^7+7pq^6+e_2p^2q^5+e_3p^3q^4+e_4p^4q^3.
\]
Put
\[
\lambda=\frac pq\in\left[\frac13,\frac12\right].
\]
After subtracting the empty/singleton contribution and dividing by \(p^2q^5\), it is enough to prove
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4
\le
R(\lambda),
\]
where
\[
R(\lambda)
=
\frac{q-q^7-7pq^6}{p^2q^5}
=
-\frac1\lambda+15+20\lambda+15\lambda^2+6\lambda^3+\lambda^4.
\]

Now define the **good-pair graph** \(H\) on \([7]\) by
\[
ij\in E(H)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x_i+x_j\ge1.
\]
Let
\[
g=|E(H)|=21-e_2.
\]

We need three structural facts.

### Fact 1: crude high-layer bounds

Bad triples are pairwise intersecting. Indeed, if two disjoint bad triples existed, their union would have weight \(<2\), so the remaining singleton would have weight \(>T-2\ge1\), impossible.

Hence, by the \(r=3,n=7\) Erdős–Ko–Rado bound,
\[
e_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

Bad \(4\)-sets are pairwise \(3\)-intersecting. If two bad \(4\)-sets met in at most \(2\) points, their union would have size at least \(6\) and weight \(<2\); the complement would have weight \(>T-2\ge1\), again impossible. Complements of such \(4\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples on \(7\) vertices, so
\[
e_4\le5.
\]

### Fact 2: bad triples and bad 4-sets force good pairs

If \(A\) is a bad triple and \(C=[7]\setminus A\), then
\[
x(C)>T-1\ge2.
\]
If \(H[C]\) had at most two good edges, then the bad-pair graph on \(C\) would have at least four edges, hence two disjoint bad pairs covering \(C\). Their total weight would be \(<2\), contradiction. Therefore every bad triple has complement spanning at least three good edges.

Thus
\[
e_3\le \#\{C\in\binom{[7]}4: e_H(C)\ge3\}.
\]

Similarly, if \(A\) is a bad \(4\)-set and \(C=[7]\setminus A\), then \(|C|=3\) and \(x(C)>2\). If any pair inside \(C\) were bad, that pair would have weight \(<1\), and the third vertex has weight \(<1\), giving \(x(C)<2\), contradiction. Hence \(C\) is a triangle of \(H\). Therefore
\[
e_4\le \#K_3(H).
\]

### Fact 3: for \(p\le2/7\), there are at most 15 bad pairs

Assume \(p\le2/7\), so \(T=1/p\ge7/2\). Suppose for contradiction that \(e_2\ge16\). Then the bad-pair graph \(G\) has at least \(16\) edges, so its complement \(H\) has at most \(5\) edges.

A standard fractional perfect matching criterion says that a graph \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching iff for every \(S\subseteq V(G)\), the number of isolated vertices of \(G-S\) is at most \(|S|\). Here this criterion holds: if \(G-S\) had \(r>|S|=s\) isolated vertices, then all edges among those \(r\) vertices and from them to the remaining \(7-s-r\) vertices would be missing from \(G\), hence present in \(H\). That gives at least
\[
\binom r2+r(7-s-r)>5
\]
missing edges in all possible cases, contradiction.

So \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching \((m_{ij})\) with vertex loads \(1\). Since every edge of \(G\) is a bad pair,
\[
x_i+x_j<1.
\]
Therefore
\[
T=\sum_i x_i
=
\sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij}(x_i+x_j)
<
\sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij}
=
\frac72,
\]
contradicting \(T\ge7/2\). Hence
\[
e_2\le15
\]
whenever \(p\le2/7\).

### Finish: two ranges

#### Range 1: \(p\in[1/4,2/7]\)

Here \(\lambda\in[1/3,2/5]\). By Fact 3 and the crude high-layer bounds,
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4
\le
15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2.
\]
But
\[
R(\lambda)-(15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing for \(\lambda>0\), and at \(\lambda=1/3\) it equals \(1/243>0\). So the desired bound holds throughout this range.

#### Range 2: \(p\in[2/7,1/3]\)

Here \(\lambda\in[2/5,1/2]\). Recall \(g=|E(H)|=21-e_2\).

If \(g\le2\), then no \(4\)-set spans three good edges and \(H\) has no triangle, so \(e_3=e_4=0\). Thus
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4\le21.
\]
Since \(R(\lambda)\) is increasing and \(R(2/5)>21\), this is enough.

If \(g=3\), then a \(4\)-set spanning three good edges must contain all three good edges. There are at most four such \(4\)-sets, and \(H\) has at most one triangle. Hence
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
18+4\lambda+\lambda^2
\le 20.25
<21<R(\lambda).
\]

If \(g=4\), then \(e_2=17\), \(e_3\le15\), and \(H\) has at most one triangle, so
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
17+15\lambda+\lambda^2.
\]
Now
\[
R(\lambda)-(17+15\lambda+\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+14\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-2\lambda-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing on \([2/5,1/2]\), and at \(\lambda=2/5\) it equals \(187/3125>0\). So this case is also done.

Finally, if \(g\ge5\), then \(e_2\le16\), while \(e_3\le15\) and \(e_4\le5\). Hence
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4
\le
16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2.
\]
And
\[
R(\lambda)-(16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2)
=
\frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-\lambda-1}{\lambda}.
\]
The numerator is increasing on \([2/5,1/2]\), and at \(\lambda=2/5\) it equals \(637/3125>0\).

Thus in every case
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4\le R(\lambda),
\]
so
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q.
\]
This proves the theorem. ∎

## Consequence

The previous support-\(\le6\) obstruction is now eliminated one step further:

> For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample to  
> \[
> \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
> \]
> must have at least **8 positive weights**.

The new \(n=7\) ingredient is genuinely LTF-specific: a bad triple’s complement has total weight \(>2\), forcing at least three good pairs in that complement. This is stronger than merely using automatic \(3\)-wise intersection.

## Task t13_support9_upper_interval — Close or sharply reduce strict reduced support 9 for p in [1/4,1/3).
