Thus the argument actually proves the deterministic product estimate min_i Π_{j≤i}(1−μ_j/(σ_i+δ))≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e), and Samuels' bound transfers it to the probability.
  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.3] Define Φ:[0,1]×(0,∞)→R by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ∈(0,1], and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), with log denoting the natural logarithm. Then for every μ∈[0,1] and every ρ>0, Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ), 1/e)).  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The boundary cases are immediate: for μ=0, Φ=−1/(1+ρ)>−1=log(1/e); for μ=1, Φ=log(ρ/(1+ρ)), which is at least the logarithm of the displayed minimum. Fix 0<μ<1. First note that ∂Φ/∂ρ = 1/((1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ))>0, so Φ increases with ρ. Split at ρ0=μ/(e−1), the point where ρ/(μ+ρ)=1/e. If ρ≥ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ≥−1. By monotonicity in ρ it suffices to prove Φ(μ,ρ0)≥−1, i.e. 1−μ/(1+μ/(e−1))≥e^{−μ}. This is Lemma 3.1: for 0≤t≤1, 1−t/(1+t/(e−1))≥e^{−t}. The paper proves it by setting f(t)=(1+t/(e−1))(1−e^{−t})−t, computing f(0)=f(1)=0 and f''(t)=(e^{−t}/(e−1))(3−e−t), so f is first convex then concave and stays nonnegative. If 0<ρ≤ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ(μ,ρ)≥log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). Define g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). A direct derivative computation gives g'(ρ)=−(1−μ)((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2)/(ρ(1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ)(μ+ρ)). Since ρ≤μ/(e−1)<μ, the numerator factor ((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2) is positive, so g'<0 on (0,ρ0]. Hence g(ρ)≥g(ρ0). But g(ρ0)=Φ(μ,ρ0)+1≥0 by the previous case, completing the proof.
  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.4] For every α>0, define h_α:[0,1]→R by h_α(0)=−1 and h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1−t/(1+αt)) for 0<t≤1; equivalently, h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) for t>0, with the value at 0 understood by continuous extension. Then h_α is concave on [0,1].  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The extension at 0 is continuous because log(1−t/(1+αt))=−t+O(t^2). The function is smooth on (0,1), so it is enough to prove h_α''(t)≤0 there. Rather than inspect h_α'' directly, the proof multiplies by t^3 and sets η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t). Direct differentiation gives η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1−t/(1+αt)). Differentiating this expression produces the decisive factorization η'(t)=−t^2(1+3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and 0<t<1 both denominator factors are positive, and the numerator is strictly positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s↓0} η(s)=0, it follows that η(t)<0 for every t∈(0,1), hence h_α''(t)<0 on (0,1). Continuity at the endpoints then gives concavity on the whole interval. A frequently used corollary is that for any subinterval [a,b]⊆[0,1], min_{t∈[a,b]} h_α(t)=min(h_α(a),h_α(b)).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 4 (reduce support)] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with finite supports, let X=Σ_j X_j and μ=E[X], and fix δ>0. Suppose that for some index i, X_i has support {v_1,...,v_k} with k≥3. Then X_i can be replaced by a random variable X_i' that is independent of {X_j:j≠i}, has E[X_i']=E[X_i], and has support consisting of at most two values from {v_1,...,v_k}, in such a way that, writing X'=X_i'+Σ_{j≠i} X_j, one has E[X']=μ and Pr[X'<μ+δ]≤Pr[X<μ+δ].  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: For each support point v_j of X_i, let q_j be the conditional probability of the target lower-tail event when X_i is fixed to v_j: q_j=Pr[Σ_{ℓ≠i} X_ℓ+v_j<μ+δ]. A replacement distribution on the same support is specified by probabilities p_j. Preserving total mass and mean imposes the two linear constraints Σ_j p_j=1 and Σ_j p_j v_j=E[X_i], with p_j≥0. The resulting lower-tail probability is the linear objective Σ_j q_j p_j. The original distribution of X_i is feasible, so the minimum of this linear program is at most the original lower-tail probability. A basic optimal solution of an LP with two equality constraints has at most two positive p_j's (apart from degeneracy, still no more than two are needed). Taking X_i' with this basic optimal distribution gives the claim. The same LP idea is later reused in the paper with the objective Pr[sum<mean] instead of Pr[sum<mean+δ], yielding the modified reduce-support operation.
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Proposition 8] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent random variables. For each i, let μ_i=E[X_i], assume μ_1≥μ_2≥...≥μ_n, and assume X_i takes value μ_i+s_i with probability μ_i/(μ_i+s_i) and value 0 otherwise, where s_i≥0, with the evident degenerate interpretation when μ_i=s_i=0. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and s=Σ_i s_i. Then Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥s/(μ_1+s).  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: The maximum possible value of X is μ+s=Σ_i(μ_i+s_i). If some variable with s_i>0 comes up 0, the sum loses μ_i+s_i, which is strictly larger than μ_n because μ_i≥μ_n and s_i>0; hence X<μ+s−μ_n. Variables with s_i=0 are at their upper value almost surely. Therefore the event X≥μ+s−μ_n can occur only when every variable with positive surplus takes its upper value, so Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤Π_i μ_i/(μ_i+s_i). Since μ_i≤μ_1 and a/(a+t) is increasing in a for t≥0, each factor is at most μ_1/(μ_1+s_i). For fixed total surplus s, Π_i μ_1/(μ_1+s_i)≤μ_1/(μ_1+s); equivalently, combining two surpluses x,y cannot decrease the product because (μ_1/(μ_1+x))(μ_1/(μ_1+y))≤μ_1/(μ_1+x+y). Thus Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤μ_1/(μ_1+s), and taking complements gives Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥1−μ_1/(μ_1+s)=s/(μ_1+s).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 10 (stage-2 modified merge bound)] Consider the following Stage 2 process on a finite collection of independent nonconstant two-point random variables. At any time, variable X_i has mean μ_i>0 and takes values 0 and μ_i+s_i, where s_i≥0 is its surplus. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and let μ_1=max_i μ_i. Initially all means lie in [μ_1/2, μ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for α=1/3, the initial value of s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i is greater than α μ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine μ_1=max_i μ_i and s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'≤α μ_1. For α=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<μ^r]≥1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r−1}<μ^{r−1}]≥1/13.  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<μ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and μ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below μ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set β=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>αμ_1, then Pr[X<μ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(μ_1+s')>α/(1+α). If it stops with βμ_1≤s'≤αμ_1, write s'=γμ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least μ_1/2: with probability at least γ/(1+γ), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2−γ)μ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

  • Endpoints p=0 and p=1 are trivial.  (from: advisor_r1)

    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the following partial classification.

For every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative weights \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_m\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for
\[
p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
\]

It also proves failure of the universal inequality for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
\]
using \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

It further proves failure for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
\]
using \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=\frac12\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p.
\]

The proof does not address the interval \(0<p<\frac13\).
    Verifier's is_relaxation verdict: True
    Proof sketch: For p=½ the proof pairs each random subset A with its complement, noting that w(A)+w(Aᶜ)=1 so in every pair one side has weight ≥½, and by the uniformity of A each such event has probability at least ½. For p=1/3 a three-colour coupling creates three independent Bernoulli(1/3) vectors whose weighted sums S₀+S₁+S₂=1, so by pigeonhole at least one Sᵣ≥1/3 and symmetry forces each to occur with probability ≥1/3. Sharpness is then settled by the simplest equal‐weight counterexamples (m=3 when p∈(1/3,1/2) and m=2 when p∈(1/2,1)) showing the inequality strictly fails elsewhere.

    Proof sketch: If all weights are <=p, then for n=4 every pair is winning; for n=5 either every pair is winning or at least six pairs are winning, giving an explicit binomial lower bound exceeding p.

    Proof sketch: Represent Bernoulli(1/k) variables by uniform residues mod k and partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits; each orbit has average weighted sum 1/k, so at least one point per orbit is >=1/k.

    Proof sketch: Then two successes always certify sum at least p, so the good probability is at least Pr[Bin(s,p)>=2]. For the relevant support sizes this binomial tail is at least p.

    Proof sketch: A weight >=p gives the result immediately. If the support is at most floor(1/p)+1, then the two smallest weights already sum to at least p, reducing to the pairwise-heavy lemma.

    Proof sketch: If B_j are complements with w(B_j)<p, then w(union B_j)<kp<=1, so the union cannot cover the positive support.
  • Support-excess lemma: if n=k+s and B_1,...,B_t are disjoint bad sets of sizes r_j, then sum_j (r_j-1)<s.  (from: t9_complement_kwise_ltf)
    Proof sketch: The union has weight < tp, while its complement has too few coordinates to carry weight >1-tp under the cap w_i<p.
  • For every k>=3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the target inequality holds whenever the positive support size is at most k+2.  (from: t9_complement_kwise_ltf)

  • A pointwise cyclic/orbit interpolation of the p=1/k proof fails throughout p in (1/4,1/3).  (from: t16_global_k3_high_bias_ltf)

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
  • Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.
  • Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.
  • Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.
  • Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.
  • Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.
  • Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root polynomial certificates.: Their leading variance coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so they cannot handle diffuse weights there.
  • Crude arbitrary-support matching bounds for losing layers.: For large support, many tiny coordinates allow large matchings in losing pairs and triples.
  • Colouring with large colours plus a leftover colour.: It reduces to an upper-tail inequality for the leftover colour that is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Worst-case head threshold only: It proves the regular-tail case when alpha_k <= sigma_k, but is too crude for dominant heads because it ignores favorable head outcomes.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen in extreme one-head tails: The normal margin q barPhi(pt)-p barPhi(qt) decays in the far tail, while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not; a relative moderate-deviation bound seems needed.
  • Critical-index decay alone: Geometric decay reduces the tail mass, but it still leaves a finite head that may be too large for the existing support-7/8 layer counts without an additional robust finite-head certificate.
  • Naive universal good merge: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) have every mergeable pair bad, so local merge inequalities alone do not contradict reducedness.
  • Direct FKG/Harris comparison of the two residual windows: The upper and lower windows are differences of downsets rather than monotone events, and the equal-weight example reverses the desired comparison.
  • Crude support-7 layer counts: Bounds such as d2<=21, d3<=15, d4<=15 are too weak near p=1/4; the proof needs the winning-pair blocker tradeoff and the 3-intersection bound for losing 4-sets.
  • Purely local universal good-merge lemma.: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) make every mergeable pair locally bad even though the instance is not a counterexample.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen for a dominant head plus tiny regular tail.: The normal margin in the one-head formula decays in the far tail while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone after complementing the bad family.: General k-wise intersecting monotone families can have too-large high-biased measure; the missing ingredient must exploit the linear-threshold form F={w(A)>q} and coordinate/threshold constraints.
  • Crude independent layer bounds for n=8 above p=1/4: Bounds such as d2<=28, d3<=56, d4<=35, d5<=21 are far too weak near p=1/4 and even the improved d4<=17, d5<=6 needs a tradeoff with d2 and d3.
  • Extending support n=9 beyond [1/6,1/5]: The n=9 middle range introduces losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-2 triple families; the n=8 high-pair/blocker tradeoff did not immediately produce a clean polynomial certificate.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone: For these linear thresholds the k-wise property is automatic when p<=1/k, so it adds no extra leverage beyond the original lower-tail problem.
  • Naive moment or Cantelli bounds: The threshold is exactly the mean, so one-sided variance inequalities have zero gap and do not force Pr[S>=E S]>=p.
  • Pairwise merging of weights: The local CDF comparison changes sign depending on the outside partial sum, so merging small coordinates is not monotone without additional structure.
  • Crude layer counts for the first open k=3,n=6 case: The automatic bounds e2<=10 and e3<=10 leave a small numerical gap near p=1/3; the weighted shifted structure of the bad layers is still needed.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone on the complement family.: The property is automatic for threshold families with p<=1/k and is false as a sufficient measure bound above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint for general k-wise intersecting families.
  • Crude independent layer bounds for support 8 above p=1/4.: Bounds on d2,d3,d4,d5 separately are too weak; the successful proof needs tradeoffs involving winning pairs, blockers, high-pair graphs, and Kneser supersaturation.
  • Direct extension of the support-8 layer proof to support 9.: The n=9 middle and upper ranges introduce losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-two triple families, so the n=8 estimates do not transfer without new tradeoffs.
  • Use the p<=1/4 winning-pair blocker for D4 above p=1/4: The key contradiction becomes w(A)+w(e1)+w(e2)+w_z<4p, which no longer contradicts total mass 1 when p>1/4.

## 1. The proposed crude \(9\)-head tail bound is false

Use the non-strict convention \(S\ge p\). The strict \(S>p\) convention only makes the crude lower bound smaller at boundary points.

Take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad n=18,\qquad w_1=\cdots=w_{18}=\frac1{18}.
\]
Choose the first \(h=9\) weights as the head. Then
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_9=\frac1{18},\qquad \alpha=\frac12,\qquad \beta=\frac12,
\]
and the tail weights are all \(\le a_9\), as required.

Let \(K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(9,1/3)\) be the number of selected head coordinates. Then \(a(A)=K/18\), and
\[
p\beta=\frac16.
\]
The crude tail-filling function contributes only for \(K\ge3\). Explicitly:
\[
\psi_\beta\!\left(p-\frac K{18}\right)=
\begin{cases}
0,&K\le3,\\[1mm]
1/7,&K=4,\\[1mm]
1/4,&K=5,\\[1mm]
1,&K\ge6.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\Phi
=
\frac17\Pr[K=4]+\frac14\Pr[K=5]+\Pr[K\ge6].
\]
Using
\[
\Pr[K=4]=\frac{4032}{19683},\quad
\Pr[K=5]=\frac{2016}{19683},\quad
\Pr[K\ge6]=\frac{835}{19683},
\]
we get
\[
\Phi
=
\frac{576+504+835}{19683}
=
\frac{1915}{19683}
\approx0.0973
<
\frac13=p.
\]

So the statement “\(\Phi\ge p\) for the first \(9\) weights and an arbitrary range/mean tail” is **not true**. This is not a counterexample to the original probability inequality: in fact here
\[
S=\frac1{18}\operatorname{Bin}(18,1/3),
\]
so
\[
\Pr[S\ge1/3]=\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(18,1/3)\ge6]\approx0.588>1/3.
\]
The failure is entirely due to the crudeness of the range/mean tail replacement.

---

## 2. A stronger finite-head/tail theorem: smoothed tail filling

The right replacement for \(\psi_\beta\) is a **Gaussian-smoothed tail filling functional**.

Split the variables into:

- a head \(H=\sum_{i\le h}a_i v_i\), with \(h\le9\);
- an arbitrary buffer \(U\), total mass \(\gamma\), satisfying \(0\le U\le\gamma\) and \(\mathbb E U=p\gamma\);
- a regular remainder \(R=p\beta+X\), where
  \[
  X=\sum_{i>\ell}w_i(v_i-p),\qquad
  \sigma^2=pqB,\qquad
  \rho=\frac{C}{B^{3/2}}.
  \]

Let
\[
\alpha=\sum_{i\le h}a_i,\qquad \alpha+\gamma+\beta=1.
\]
For a head subset \(A\subseteq[h]\), write \(s_A=a(A)\) and
\[
r_A=p(\alpha+\gamma)-s_A.
\]
Given \(U=u\), the regular tail must satisfy
\[
X\ge r_A-u.
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\Pr[X\ge r_A-u]
\ge
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r_A-u}{\sigma}\right)
-
C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho,
\]
where
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]

Now define, for \(\gamma,\sigma\ge0\),
\[
\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)
=
\inf_{\substack{0\le U\le\gamma\\ \mathbb E U=p\gamma}}
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r-U}{\sigma}\right)
\right],
\]
with the convention that when \(\sigma=0\), \(\chi_{\gamma,0}=\psi_\gamma\).

Equivalently, \(\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)\) is the lower convex envelope on \([0,\gamma]\) of
\[
u\mapsto \overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r-u}{\sigma}\right)
\]
evaluated at \(u=p\gamma\). Hence it has the explicit two-point formula
\[
\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)
=
\inf_{0\le x\le p\gamma\le y\le\gamma}
\left[
\frac{y-p\gamma}{y-x}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r-x}{\sigma}\right)
+
\frac{p\gamma-x}{y-x}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r-y}{\sigma}\right)
\right],
\]
with the usual limiting interpretation when \(x=y=p\gamma\).

### Smoothed finite-head certificate

For any such split,
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[h]}
p^{|A|}q^{h-|A|}
\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r_A)
-
C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho.
}
\]

Therefore, if
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{A\subseteq[h]}
p^{|A|}q^{h-|A|}
\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}\bigl(p(\alpha+\gamma)-a(A)\bigr)
\ge
p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho,
}
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
\]

This strictly generalizes both earlier certificates:

- If \(\gamma=0\), then \(\chi_{0,\sigma}(r)=\overline\Phi(r/\sigma)\), recovering the Berry–Esseen finite-head certificate.
- If \(\sigma=0\), then \(\chi_{\gamma,0}=\psi_\gamma\), recovering the crude range/mean tail-filling bound.

---

## 3. Critical-index reduction after the first \(9\) weights

Let
\[
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\qquad C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\qquad \rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}}.
\]
Fix a regularity parameter \(\tau>0\), and set
\[
\lambda=\sqrt{1-\tau^2},\qquad c_\tau=\frac{\tau}{1-\lambda}.
\]

If the tail after \(k\) is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e. \(\rho_k>\tau\), then
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}
\]
and
\[
\sqrt{B_{k+1}}\le \lambda\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears between indices \(9\) and \(\ell-1\), then
\[
\sum_{i=10}^{\ell}w_i
\le
c_\tau\bigl(\sqrt{B_9}-\sqrt{B_\ell}\bigr).
\]
Since \(w_{10}\le a_9\), nonregularity at index \(9\) also gives
\[
\sqrt{B_9}<\frac{a_9}{\tau}.
\]
Thus the nonregular buffer mass \(\gamma=\sum_{i=10}^{\ell}w_i\) obeys the explicit bound
\[
\boxed{
\gamma
\le
c_\tau\left(\frac{a_9}{\tau}-\sqrt{B_\ell}\right).
}
\]

So the arbitrary-support problem reduces to checking the smoothed finite-head certificate over the compact finite-dimensional domain
\[
p\in[1/6,1/3],\quad
0<a_9\le\cdots\le a_1<p,\quad
\alpha=\sum_{i=1}^9a_i\le1,
\]
\[
0\le\gamma\le1-\alpha,\qquad
0\le u=\sqrt{B_\ell}\le1-\alpha-\gamma,
\]
together with the critical-index constraint
\[
\gamma\le c_\tau\left(\frac{a_9}{\tau}-u\right)_+.
\]
On this domain one must verify
\[
\sum_{A\subseteq[9]}
p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
\chi_{\gamma,\sqrt{pq}\,u}
\bigl(p(\alpha+\gamma)-a(A)\bigr)
\ge
p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\tau.
\]
If this inequality is verified for some fixed \(\tau\), then every counterexample must avoid both possibilities:

1. the first \(\tau\)-regular tail occurs after index \(9\), and the smoothed certificate applies;
2. no \(\tau\)-regular tail occurs, in which case \(u=0\) and the same framework reduces to the deterministic \(\psi\)-certificate with an additional critical-index mass bound.

The equal-\(18\) example shows why the unsmoothed \(u=0\) certificate is too weak by itself. The missing ingredient is exactly the regular or block-structured smoothing of the tail.

---

## 4. What remains

The support-\(\le9\) theorem rules out the zero-tail boundary. The crude \(9\)-head range/mean certificate fails badly, so the arbitrary-support proof cannot simply compress the tail to an interval-valued random variable with only its mean retained.

The viable finite-head route is now:

1. use the first \(9\) weights as the head;
2. use critical-index decay to split the remaining tail into a bounded nonregular buffer plus a regular remainder;
3. verify the smoothed finite-head inequality above, preferably with interval arithmetic for the one-dimensional lower convex envelope \(\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}\).

That is a concrete finite-dimensional certificate problem, and it is strictly stronger than the failed \(\psi_\beta\) test.

\]
Using the uniform Berry–Esseen inequality for independent non-identically distributed summands with constant \(C_{\rm BE}=0.56\) — the currently standard general-summand bound summarized by Pinelis/Shevtsova — gives the following. ([scixplorer.org](https://scixplorer.org/abs/2013arXiv1301.2828P/abstract?utm_source=openai))

### Proposition
If
\[
\rho\le 
\eta(p):=
\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{p(1-p)}}{0.56\bigl(p^2+(1-p)^2\bigr)},
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
\]

In particular, throughout \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),
\[
\eta(p)\ge \eta(1/3)=\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}\approx 0.25254.
\]
So any strict reduced counterexample must satisfy
\[
\boxed{\rho>0.25254}
\]
or equivalently
\[
\boxed{\frac{A_2^3}{A_3^2}<15.68.}
\]

### Proof
Let
\[
Z=S-p=\sum_i w_i(v_i-p).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Var}(Z)=pqA_2
\]
and
\[
\sum_i \mathbb E|w_i(v_i-p)|^3
=pq(p^2+q^2)A_3.
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{Z}{\sqrt{pqA_2}}\le 0\right]-\frac12\right|
\le
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr[S<p]\le \Pr[S\le p]\le
\frac12+
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\frac12-
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]
This is at least \(p\) exactly under the displayed condition on \(\rho\). ∎

So any remaining counterexample is highly non-Gaussian: it must have \(L^3/L^2\)-effective dimension \(<16\). This does **not** by itself reduce support to \(9\), since a vector with three large weights and a long tiny tail can have small effective dimension, but it sharply isolates the remaining obstruction.

---

## 2. Exact pair-merging formula

This is the most useful structural reduction I found.

Take two weights \(a\ge b>0\) with \(a+b<p\), and let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j} w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the contribution of the other coordinates. Let \(\mu_{\rm split}\) be the bad-set probability with the two separate weights \(a,b\), and let \(\mu_{\rm merge}\) be the bad-set probability after replacing them by one weight \(a+b\). Then

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big).
}
\]

### Proof
Condition on \(R\), and put \(t=p-R\). Compare the lower-tail probabilities for
\[
a\xi+b\eta<t
\]
versus
\[
(a+b)\zeta<t,
\]
where \(\xi,\eta,\zeta\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\).

The two conditional probabilities differ only in two threshold ranges:

* If \(a<t\le a+b\), i.e. \(R\in[p-a-b,p-a)\), then the split version is bad unless both \(a,b\) are selected, so its bad probability is \(1-p^2\), while the merged version is bad with probability \(q\). Difference: \(1-p^2-q=pq\).

* If \(0<t\le b\), i.e. \(R\in[p-b,p)\), then the split version is bad only if neither is selected, probability \(q^2\), while the merged version is bad with probability \(q\). Difference: \(q^2-q=-pq\).

All other ranges give equal conditional probabilities. Averaging over \(R\) proves the formula. ∎

---

## 3. Consequence: a concrete slab-stability condition would close the theorem

Assume the known finite-support result through support \(9\). Then any support-\(10\) counterexample with two smallest weights \(a\ge b\) has \(a+b\le 2/10=0.2<p\). Merging them gives a support-\(9\) instance, hence not a counterexample. Therefore the pair-merging formula forces

\[
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
>
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p)).
\]

Thus a support-\(10\) counterexample must exhibit an **adjacent-slab inversion**: the lower slab of width \(b\), sitting below the threshold by about \(a\), must have larger probability than the upper slab of the same width.

More generally, the following exact lemma would close the whole \(k=3\) interval.

### Adjacent-slab monotonicity lemma sufficient to finish
Suppose that for every strict reduced instance with support at least \(10\), if \(a\ge b\) are the two smallest weights, \(a+b<p\), and \(R\) is the sum over the other coordinates, then
\[
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
\le
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p)).
\tag{ASL}
\]
Then the desired theorem follows for all supports.

Indeed, under (ASL), merging the two smallest weights cannot decrease \(\mu_p(D)\). Starting from any counterexample of support \(n\ge10\), repeatedly merge the two smallest weights. At every stage the merged weight is still \(<p\), because the two smallest weights in an \(m\)-support instance have sum at most \(2/m\le 1/5<p\). Thus one eventually obtains a support-\(9\) counterexample, contradicting the known support-\(\le9\) result.

So the global theorem is reduced to the very explicit slab inequality (ASL), or to the slightly weaker stability form
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}+pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big)
\le q
\]
for all one-coordinate splits of support-\(9\) instances.

---

## 4. Why the naive Liggett/cyclic-orbit interpolation fails

For \(p=1/k\), the standard cyclic proof partitions the sample space into \(k\)-cycles; along each cycle the weighted sum averages to \(1/k\), so at least one point in the cycle is good. This is the proof sketched in the original convex-combination question for \(p=1/k\). ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables)) Liggett’s half-threshold theorem is the analogous sharp \(1/2\)-threshold result for \(p\ge1/2\). ([mathoverflow.net](https://mathoverflow.net/questions/278117/convex-combination-iid-bernoulli-random-variables))

A tempting interpolation is to use circle rotations with arcs of length \(p\). But this cannot work pointwise in the high-bias interval.

Take four equal weights \(1/4\) and \(p\in(1/4,1/3)\). Put four arcs of length \(p\) on the circle with starting points \(0,1/4,1/2,3/4\). The weighted coverage is at least \(p\) exactly on the double-covered set, whose measure is
\[
4p-1.
\]
Since \(p<1/3\),
\[
4p-1<p.
\]
So some diagonal orbits have good fraction \(<p\), even though the independent product measure still satisfies the desired inequality in this example. Thus the \(p=1/k\) cyclic proof cannot be extended by a pointwise orbit argument; any successful orbit method must average or couple different orbits.

---

## Current reduction summary

Given the support-\(\le9\) certificate, the remaining counterexample must simultaneously satisfy:

1. support at least \(10\);
2. strict reduced weights \(0<w_i<p\);
3. effective dimension
   \[
   A_2^3/A_3^2<15.68;
   \]
4. for every mergeable pair in a minimal-support counterexample, and in particular for the two smallest weights, the adjacent-slab inversion
   \[
   \Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
   >
   \Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
   \]
   must hold.

The most concrete next target is therefore (ASL). Proving it for the two smallest weights would collapse every counterexample down to support \(9\), where the known finite certificate rules it out.

# Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

# Claim Established (do NOT change this)
The proof establishes the restricted support-9 statement: for every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\) and \(w_i<p\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

# Current Accepted Proof
## Claim

For every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), every choice of positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with
\[
\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1,\qquad w_i<p\ \text{for all }i,
\]
and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_9\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

## Proof

Put \(q=1-p\) and \(r=p/q\). Then \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\). For \(A\subseteq[9]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
For \(j=0,\dots,9\), define
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Let \(X=\{i:v_i=1\}\). Then
\[
\Pr[X=A]=p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}=q^9r^{|A|},
\]
so
\[
\Pr[w(X)\le p]=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j.
\]
Since \(q=1/(1+r)\), we have
\[
q=q^9(1+r)^8.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]

First, \(d_0=1\) and \(d_1=9\), because \(w_i<p\) for every \(i\). Also, if \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), hence
\[
w(A^c)<3p\le 1-p,
\]
so \(w(A)=1-w(A^c)>p\). Therefore
\[
d_j=0\qquad(j\ge6).
\]

Define a graph \(W\) on vertex set \([9]\) by putting \(ij\in E(W)\) exactly when
\[
w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let \(m=|E(W)|\). Then a pair is losing precisely when it is not an edge of \(W\), so
\[
d_2=\binom92-m=36-m.
\]

We next bound \(d_3,d_4,d_5\) in terms of \(m\).

### Losing triples

Let \(H=D_3\) and \(h=|H|=d_3\). Suppose \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint triples, and let
\[
C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U),
\]
so \(|C|=3\). If \(C\) contained a losing pair \(e\), with remaining vertex \(z\in C\), then
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence every pair in \(C\) is winning, so \(C\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Let \(I(H)\) be the number of unordered disjoint pairs of triples in \(H\), and let \(\tau(W)\) be the number of triangles of \(W\). For each triangle \(C\) of \(W\), the remaining six vertices can be partitioned into two unordered triples in
\[
\frac12\binom63=10
\]
ways. Therefore
\[
I(H)\le 10\tau(W).
\]

We also need a lower bound on \(I(H)\). Consider the Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\), whose vertices are the \(3\)-subsets of \([9]\), with two vertices adjacent when the corresponding triples are disjoint. This graph has
\[
N=\binom93=84
\]
vertices, degree \(\binom63=20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). If \(x\) is the indicator vector of \(H\), then
\[
2I(H)=x^\top Ax,
\]
where \(A\) is the adjacency matrix of \(KG(9,3)\). Decomposing \(x\) into its constant part and its orthogonal part gives
\[
x^\top Ax\ge \frac{20}{84}h^2-10\left(h-\frac{h^2}{84}\right)
=\frac5{14}h^2-10h.
\]
Thus
\[
I(H)\ge \frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]

Also \(h\le56\). Indeed, if \(H\) contained three pairwise disjoint triples, those triples would partition \([9]\), and their total weight would be at most \(3p\le3/4<1\), impossible. There are \(280\) partitions of \([9]\) into three unordered triples, and each fixed triple belongs to \(10\) such partitions. If \(h>56\), the average number of \(H\)-triples in such a partition would exceed \(2\), forcing a partition containing three \(H\)-triples, contradiction.

Let \(T_m\) be the maximum possible number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges. Combining the preceding estimates,
\[
\frac5{28}h(h-28)\le I(H)\le 10T_m,
\]
so
\[
h(h-28)\le56T_m.
\]
Define
\[
H_m=\max\{h\in\{0,1,\dots,56\}:h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]
Then
\[
d_3=h\le H_m.
\]

We use the exact formula for \(T_m\). Write
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
with \(a\) maximal. Then
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
The lower bound is attained by taking a clique \(K_a\) and adding one extra vertex adjacent to exactly \(b\) vertices of the clique.

For the upper bound, argue by induction on \(m\). After deleting isolated vertices, let \(\delta\) be the minimum degree. Since \(m<\binom{a+1}{2}\), we must have \(\delta\le a-1\): otherwise the graph has at least \(\delta+1\ge a+1\) vertices and hence
\[
m\ge \frac{\delta(\delta+1)}2\ge \binom{a+1}{2},
\]
contradiction. Removing a vertex of degree \(\delta\) destroys at most \(\binom\delta2\) triangles, so by induction
\[
\tau(G)\le T_{m-\delta}+\binom\delta2.
\]
Let \(g(m)=\binom a3+\binom b2\). If \(\delta\le b\), then
\[
g(m)-g(m-\delta)-\binom\delta2=\delta(b-\delta)\ge0.
\]
If \(\delta>b\), put \(t=\delta-b\). Then
\[
m-\delta=\binom{a-1}{2}+(a-t-1),
\]
and a direct calculation gives
\[
g(m)-g(m-\delta)-\binom\delta2=t(a-\delta-1)\ge0.
\]
Thus \(T_m=g(m)\).

### Losing \(4\)-sets

The family \(D_4\) is intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p\le\frac34,
\]
impossible.

Any intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(56\). One quick proof is by cyclic orders. In any fixed cyclic order of \([9]\), the nine cyclic intervals of length \(4\) have disjointness graph a \(9\)-cycle, so an intersecting subfamily of those intervals has size at most \(4\). Counting pairs \((F,\mathcal C)\), where \(F\) is a member of the family and \(\mathcal C\) is a cyclic order in which \(F\) appears as an interval, gives
\[
|\mathcal F|\cdot 4!5!\le 4\cdot 8!,
\]
hence \(|\mathcal F|\le56\). Therefore
\[
d_4\le56.
\]

Now fix \(A\in D_4\) and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Thus the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\). A graph on \(5\) vertices with matching number at most \(1\) has at most \(4\) edges: if it contains a triangle, no additional edge is possible without creating a disjoint edge-pair; if it is triangle-free, then all edges must share a common endpoint, so it is contained in a star.

Hence \(W[B]\) contains at least \(10-4=6\) winning edges. Counting incidences \((e,B)\), where \(e\in E(W)\) and \(e\subset B\), gives
\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m.
\]
Thus
\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

### Losing \(5\)-sets

Let \(A\in D_5\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching consisting of two losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p\le\frac34,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(W[B]\) meets all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\). Since those three perfect matchings partition the edge set of \(K_4\), we have
\[
|E(W[B])|\ge3.
\]
Counting incidences again,
\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]
so
\[
d_5\le7m.
\]

We also have the independent bound \(d_5\le21\). Indeed, let
\[
\mathcal F=\{A^c:A\in D_5\}.
\]
Then \(\mathcal F\) is a family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\). If \(C,C'\in\mathcal F\) had \(|C\cap C'|\le1\), with complements \(A,A'\in D_5\), then
\[
w(A)+w(A')=1-w(C\cap C')+w(A\cap A')>1-p>2p,
\]
contradicting \(w(A)+w(A')\le2p\). Hence \(\mathcal F\) is \(2\)-intersecting.

We prove that any \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\). Let \(\Omega=\binom{[9]}4\). For \(i=0,\dots,4\), let \(A_i\) be the \(\Omega\times\Omega\) matrix with
\[
(A_i)_{ST}=1\iff |S\cap T|=4-i.
\]
The Johnson-scheme eigenvalues are as follows. On the common eigenspace indexed by \(s=0,\dots,4\), the eigenvalue of \(A_i\) is
\[
\theta_i(s)=\sum_{\ell=0}^i(-1)^\ell
\binom{s}{\ell}\binom{4-s}{i-\ell}\binom{5-s}{i-\ell},
\]
with the convention that invalid binomial coefficients are \(0\). Substitution gives
\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrrr}
 & A_0 & A_1 & A_2 & A_3 & A_4\\
\hline
s=0 & 1 & 20 & 60 & 40 & 5\\
s=1 & 1 & 11 & 6 & -14 & -4\\
s=2 & 1 & 4 & -8 & 0 & 3\\
s=3 & 1 & -1 & -3 & 5 & -2\\
s=4 & 1 & -4 & 6 & -4 & 1
\end{array}
\]
This is the standard finite-difference diagonalization of the Johnson scheme.

\[
M=20A_0-A_1-A_2+\frac52A_3-8A_4.
\]
Using the table, the eigenvalues of \(M\) are
\[
0,\ 0,\ 0,\ \frac{105}{2},\ 0.
\]
Therefore \(M\succeq0\).

Let \(x\) be the indicator vector of a \(2\)-intersecting family \(\mathcal F\), and put \(f=|\mathcal F|\). Since no two members of \(\mathcal F\) have intersection \(0\) or \(1\),
\[
x^\top A_3x=x^\top A_4x=0.
\]
Also
\[
x^\top(A_1+A_2)x=f(f-1).
\]
Hence
\[
0\le x^\top Mx=20f-f(f-1).
\]
Thus \(f\le21\). Applying this to the complement family above gives
\[
d_5\le21.
\]
Consequently,
\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

### Final polynomial estimate

For the actual value \(m=|E(W)|\), we have
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j
\le
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5.
\]
It remains to show
\[
(1+r)^8>
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\]
for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\).

Define
\[
P_m(r)=(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right).
\]
Then \(P_m(r)=rQ_m(r)\), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
Q_m(r)=&-1+(m-8)r+(56-H_m)r^2+(70-A_m)r^3\\
&+(56-B_m)r^4+28r^5+8r^6+r^7.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_m\le56\), \(A_m\le56\), and \(B_m\le21\), all coefficients after the linear term are nonnegative, with
\[
70-A_m\ge14,\qquad 56-B_m\ge35.
\]

For \(m\ge8\), we have \(Q_m'(r)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). For \(m\le7\), using the values
\[
(H_0,\dots,H_7)=(28,28,28,29,29,31,34,34),
\]
we get
\[
Q_m'(r)\ge m-8+2(56-H_m)r
\ge m-8+\frac{56-H_m}{2}>0.
\]
Thus every \(Q_m\) is increasing on \([1/4,1/3]\).

\[
4^7Q_m(1/4)=4096m+30177-1024H_m-256A_m-64B_m.
\]
For \(m\ge11\), the crude bounds \(H_m\le56\), \(A_m\le56\), \(B_m\le21\) give
\[
4^7Q_m(1/4)\ge4096m-42847\ge2209>0.
\]
For \(m=0,\dots,10\), direct substitution gives:

\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrr}
m & H_m & A_m & B_m & 4^7Q_m(1/4)\\
\hline
0&28&0&0&1505\\
1&28&5&7&3873\\
2&28&11&14&5985\\
3&29&17&21&7073\\
4&29&23&21&9633\\
5&31&29&21&10145\\
6&34&35&21&9633\\
7&34&40&21&12449\\
8&35&46&21&13985\\
9&38&52&21&13473\\
10&41&56&21&13473
\end{array}
\]
Every entry in the last column is positive. Therefore \(Q_m(1/4)>0\) for every \(m=0,\dots,36\), and since \(Q_m\) is increasing,
\[
Q_m(r)>0\qquad(r\in[1/4,1/3]).
\]
Because \(r>0\), this implies
\[
P_m(r)>0.
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]

\[
\Pr[w(X)\le p]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9d_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Taking complements,
\[
\Pr[w(X)>p]>1-q=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

\[

Below is a **large-support / regularity progress report**. I did not close every nonregular configuration, but the complementary cases are now reduced to explicit finite-head certificates plus a critical-index decay lemma, and I also get one new small-support layer-counting extension.

Throughout, reduce to the strict case
\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,\qquad p\in[1/6,1/3],
\]
since any \(w_i\ge p\) immediately gives \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Sort
\[
w_1\ge w_2\ge\cdots>0.
\]
For \(k\ge0\), write
\[
\alpha_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i,\quad
B_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^2,\quad
C_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i^3,\quad
\rho_k=\frac{C_k}{B_k^{3/2}},
\]
and
\[
\sigma_k^2=pqB_k,\qquad X_k=\sum_{i>k}w_i(v_i-p).
\]

Let
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)\le K_*:=\frac{13}{3\sqrt5}<2.
\]

---

## 1. Finite-head / regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate

For any fixed head size \(k\), condition on the head
\[
H_k=\sum_{i\le k}w_i v_i.
\]
Given a head set \(A\subseteq[k]\), the tail must satisfy
\[
X_k\ge p\alpha_k-w(A).
\]
Berry–Esseen gives
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_k}{\sigma_k}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k=:\varepsilon_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
G_k-\varepsilon_k,
}
\]
where
\[
G_k=
\sum_{A\subseteq[k]}
p^{|A|}q^{k-|A|}
\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{p\alpha_k-w(A)}{\sigma_k}\right),
\qquad \overline\Phi=1-\Phi.
\]

This is the main regular-tail certificate: if for some \(k\),
\[
G_k\ge p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_k,
\]
then \(B(p)\) holds.

---

## 2. A simple small-head corollary

Let \(C_{\rm BE}\le1\) for concreteness; otherwise replace constants by \(1/C_{\rm BE}\). Put
\[
\eta_0=\frac{1}{64C_{\rm BE}}.
\]
If for some \(k\),
\[
\rho_k\le \eta_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha_k\le \sigma_k,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

Indeed, since \(H_k\ge0\),
\[
p\alpha_k-H_k\le p\alpha_k\le p\sigma_k\le \frac{\sigma_k}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
\ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\eta_0
> \overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac1{32}.
\]
Numerically,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac13\approx0.0361>\frac1{32},
\]
so the right side is \(>p\) for every \(p\le1/3\).

Thus a genuinely regular tail after a head whose total mass is at most one tail standard deviation is enough.

---

## 3. One-dominant-head refinement

For \(k=1\), the finite-head certificate becomes especially clean. Let \(w=w_1\), \(\sigma=\sigma_1\), \(t=w/\sigma\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
\ge
q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt)
-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_1.
\]
Define
\[
M_p(t):=q\overline\Phi(pt)-p\overline\Phi(qt).
\]
Since \(q\ge p\) and \(pt\le qt\), one has \(M_p(t)>0\) for every finite \(t\). Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_1\le \frac{M_p(w_1/\sigma_1)}{C_{\rm BE}K(p)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
}
\]

This handles many “one large atom plus regular tail” cases even when \(w_1\) is much larger than \(\sigma_1\). The remaining difficulty is that \(M_p(t)\) decays in the far tail, so absolute Berry–Esseen is not enough when \(t\) is very large; one would need relative moderate-deviation estimates there.

---

## 4. Critical-index decay for nonregular tails

Fix \(\tau>0\). If a tail is not \(\tau\)-regular, i.e.
\[
\rho_k>\tau,
\]
then, because \(C_k\le w_{k+1}B_k\),
\[
w_{k+1}>\tau\sqrt{B_k}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
B_{k+1}=B_k-w_{k+1}^2<(1-\tau^2)B_k.
\]

Thus before the first \(\tau\)-regular tail, the tail variance decays geometrically:
\[
B_{k+r}\le (1-\tau^2)^r B_k.
\]

Moreover, if no \(\tau\)-regular tail appears after \(K\), then the whole remaining tail mass satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\beta_K:=\sum_{i>K}w_i
\le
\frac{\tau}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}\sqrt{B_K}
<\frac{2}{\tau}\sqrt{B_K}.
}
\]

Proof sketch: put \(u_j=\sqrt{B_j}\). Since \(u_{j+1}\le \sqrt{1-\tau^2}\,u_j\),
\[
w_{j+1}=\sqrt{u_j^2-u_{j+1}^2}
\le
\sqrt{\frac{1+\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-\tau^2}}}\,(u_j-u_{j+1}),
\]
and summing telescopes.

So a long nonregular segment forces geometric decay; either a regular tail eventually appears, or the remaining tail has small total mass and the problem becomes an explicit finite-head problem.

---

## 5. Deterministic tail-filling reduction

Suppose after a head of size \(K\), the remaining raw tail
\[
T_K=\sum_{i>K}w_i v_i
\]
has total mass \(\beta_K\). We always know
\[
0\le T_K\le \beta_K,\qquad \mathbb E T_K=p\beta_K.
\]
For any residual threshold \(r\), the sharp mean/range lower bound is
\[
\Pr[T_K\ge r]\ge \psi_{\beta_K}(r),
\]
where
\[
\psi_\beta(r)=
\begin{cases}
1, & r\le0,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{p\beta-r}{\beta-r}, & 0<r\le p\beta,\\[3mm]
0, & r>p\beta.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge
\sum_{A\subseteq[K]}
p^{|A|}q^{K-|A|}
\psi_{\beta_K}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr).
}
\]

This is useful in the no-regular-tail case: critical-index decay gives an explicit small \(\beta_K\), and the last display reduces the problem to a finite weighted layer-counting check on the head.

---

## 6. New layer-counting extension: support \(9\) in the lowest base range

I can extend the previous support-\(8\) result one step.

### Proposition

If \(n=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\frac16\le p\le \frac15,
\]
then
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

### Proof

Let \(D_j\) be the losing \(j\)-sets:
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\}.
\]

No losing set of size at least \(5\) exists, because a losing \(5\)-set plus the four remaining weights would have total
\[
< p+4p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\).

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the ninth vertex would have total
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
so the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(3\). By Erdős–Gallai,
\[
|D_2|\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all vertices and have total at most
\[
3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing triple family has matching number at most \(2\). By the Erdős matching theorem for \(3\)-graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
|D_3|\le56.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the last vertex would have total
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible, so \(D_4\) is intersecting. By Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
|D_4|\le \binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le
q^9+9pq^8+21p^2q^7+56p^3q^6+56p^4q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_9(p)=
1-q^9-9pq^8-21p^2q^7-56p^3q^6-56p^4q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_9(1/6)=\frac{238705}{10077696}>0.
\]
Also
\[
F_9'(p)=
30pq^7-21p^2q^6+112p^3q^5+280p^4q^4-1.
\]
On \([1/6,1/5]\),
\[
30pq^7-21p^2q^6
=
pq^6(30-51p)
\ge
\frac15\left(\frac45\right)^6\cdot\frac{99}{5}
>1,
\]
so \(F_9'(p)>0\). Hence \(F_9(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), proving the claim.

---

## Current state

The regular side is now quite explicit:

- If the whole vector is regular, Berry–Esseen proves \(B(p)\).
- If a regular tail remains after a small head, the small-head criterion proves \(B(p)\).
- If there is one dominant head atom and a regular tail, the one-head margin criterion often proves \(B(p)\).
- If no regular tail appears, the critical-index lemma forces geometric decay and reduces the problem to a finite-head layer-counting certificate with an explicit tail-filling function.

What remains is to close the finite-head certificates uniformly when the first regular tail occurs only after a large head, or when the no-regular-tail reduction leaves a large but geometrically decaying head. Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in the extreme-tail one-head regime; a relative moderate-deviation estimate for the regular tail looks like the next natural improvement.

I can close a **new support-9 range**:

\[
\boxed{n=9,\quad \frac15\le p\le \frac14\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Pr[S>p]>p.}
\]

I did **not** close the whole upper interval \([1/4,1/3)\) in this pass. I found a strong deficiency-cascade reduction for it, and the remaining obstruction is now quite explicit: it is a high \(D_4\) / low-winning-pair configuration around \(p=2/7\).

## 1. Support \(9\) eliminated on \([1/5,1/4]\)

Let

\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq,
\]

and

\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]

Since

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^9\sum_j d_jr^j,\qquad q=q^9(1+r)^8,
\]

it suffices to show

\[
\sum_j d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]

Assume

\[
\frac15\le p\le \frac14,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\frac14\le r\le \frac13.
\]

For \(p\le1/4\), no losing set of size \(6\) or more exists: if \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so

\[
w(A^c)<3p\le q\le 1-w(A),
\]

contradiction. Thus only layers \(0,\dots,5\) matter.

Let \(W\) be the graph of winning pairs,

\[
ij\in W\iff w_i+w_j>p,
\]

and let \(m=|W|\). Then

\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

### Losing triples

Let \(H=D_3\), \(h=d_3\). If \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If some pair \(e\subset C\) were losing, and \(z=C\setminus e\), then

\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence every pair of \(C\) is winning, so \(C\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Each triangle \(C\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the remaining \(6\) vertices into two triples. Therefore

\[
I(H)\le 10\,\tau(W),
\]

where \(I(H)\) is the number of disjoint pairs in \(H\), and \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\).

The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\) has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman supersaturation gives

\[
I(H)\ge \frac{5}{28}h(h-28).
\]

Let \(T_m\) be the maximum possible number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges. Explicitly, if

\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]

then

\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]

Thus

\[
h(h-28)\le 56T_m,
\]

and we use the bound

\[
d_3\le H_m:=\max\{h\le56:\ h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]

The cap \(56\) is the Erdős matching bound for a \(3\)-graph on \(9\) vertices with no three disjoint edges.

### Losing fours

If \(A\in D_4\), let \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus the leftover vertex \(z\), then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]

contradiction. Hence \(W[B]\) must meet every \(2\)-matching in \(K_5\). Equivalently, the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), hence has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore

\[
|W[B]|\ge 6.
\]

Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),

\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m,
\]

so

\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

### Losing fives

If \(A\in D_5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then

\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]

contradiction. Thus \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(|W[B]|\ge3\). Counting incidences,

\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]

so \(d_5\le7m\). Also complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-family on \(9\) vertices, hence by the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado / Ahlswede–Khachatrian bound,

\[
d_5\le21.
\]

Therefore

\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

### Polynomial certificate

For each \(m=0,\dots,36\), define

\[
P_m(r)
=
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right).
\]

The required inequality follows from

\[
P_m(r)>0
\qquad
\forall m\in\{0,\dots,36\},\quad \frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

This is a finite exact check. Here is a reproducible rational/Sturm certificate:

```python
import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")
L = sp.Rational(1, 4)
U = sp.Rational(1, 3)

def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in a graph with m edges:
    # colex/Kruskal-Katona graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56 by matching-number bound
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P = sp.expand(
        (1 + r)**8
        - (1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5)
    )
    poly = sp.Poly(P, r)

    # Exact endpoint positivity.
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, L)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, L))
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, U)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, U))

    # Exact Sturm root count on [1/4, 1/3].
    assert poly.count_roots(L, U) == 0, (m, P)

print("All 37 support-9 polynomials are positive on [1/4, 1/3].")
```

\frac{1505}{65536}>0.
\]

Together with the earlier support-\(9\) result on \([1/6,1/5]\), this eliminates support \(9\) on

## 2. New upper-interval tool: deficiency cascade

For the remaining interval

\[
\frac14<p<\frac13,
\]

the previous \(p\le1/4\) blocker for losing \(4\)-sets no longer works, because the contradiction used \(4p\le1\). However, there is a useful general deficiency cascade.

For a losing \(k\)-set \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency

\[
\epsilon_k(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]

and put

\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\epsilon_k(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then

\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=
(k+1)p-kw(B)
=
p+k(p-w(B)).
\]

Summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, gives

\[
\boxed{
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
}
\]

For pairs, let

\[
\delta_{ij}=p-w_i-w_j.
\]

Then

\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{ij\in D_2}\delta_{ij}.
\]

Since

\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]

and each winning pair contributes \(p-w_i-w_j>-p\), we get

\[
\boxed{
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
}
\]

Thus, in support \(9\),

\[
\boxed{
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_4+3E_4\le6E_3,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_5+4E_5\le5E_4,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
d_6+5E_6\le4E_5.
}
\]

This is stronger than the one-layer deficiency bounds because it forces a shared budget across \(D_3,D_4,D_5,D_6\).

---

## 3. Upper-interval high-layer bounds

For \(p<1/3\), we still have:

\[
d_5\le \min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Also, if \(A\in D_6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\) and weight at least \(q\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) we would get total weight \(<3p<1\), impossible. Hence \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\), so

\[
d_6\le \tau(W)\le T_m.
\]

The complement \(2\)-intersection bound gives

\[
d_6\le7.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
d_6\le \min\{7,T_m\}.
}
\]

The problematic layer is \(D_4\). We have the general bound

\[
d_4\le56,
\]

because losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, but this is not strong enough near \(p=2/7\).

## 4. Exact obstruction left by the current upper-interval bounds

## 5. Exact 8-head fallback formulation

For support \(9\), choose one coordinate as a tail, say \(b=w_9\), and write the remaining head weights as \(x_1,\dots,x_8\), with

\[
\sum_{i=1}^8 x_i=1-b.
\]

Let

\[
R=\sum_{i=1}^8 x_iv_i.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
\Pr[R>p]+p\,\Pr[p-b<R\le p].
\]

Equivalently, define two head downsets

\[
E_0=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p\},
\]

\[
E_1=\{A\subseteq[8]: x(A)\le p-b\}.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S>p]
=
1-q\,\mu_p(E_0)-p\,\mu_p(E_1).
\]

So a support-\(9\) counterexample is exactly an \(8\)-coordinate head satisfying

\[
q\,\mu_p(E_0)+p\,\mu_p(E_1)\ge q.
\]

This is the clean finite-head fallback: it reduces the remaining support-\(9\) problem to a two-threshold weighted downset inequality on \(8\) coordinates. The known support-\(8\) theorem does not immediately apply because the head has total mass \(1-b\), not \(1\), and the Bernoulli parameter remains \(p\), but this formulation isolates precisely what an \(8\)-head certificate must prove.

# 1. The key \(D_4\) improvement

Let

\[
W=\{ij:w_i+w_j>p\},\qquad m=|W|.
\]

Every losing set contains no winning pair. Hence \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of \(W\)-independent \(4\)-sets. Since \(W\) is a threshold graph, exact enumeration of threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices gives

\[
d_4\le I_m,
\]

where

\[
I_0=56,\quad I_1=50,\quad I_2=45,\quad I_3=41,\quad I_4=38,\quad I_5=36,\quad I_m=35\ \text{for }m\ge6.
\]

This alone rules out the relaxed obstruction: when \(m=3\),

\[
d_4\le41.
\]

At \(r=2/5\),

\[
1+9r+33r^2+56r^3+41r^4+6r^5+r^6
=
\frac{5694975}{390625},
\]

whereas

\[
(1+r)^8=\left(\frac75\right)^8=\frac{5764801}{390625}.
\]

So the gap is

\[
\frac{69826}{390625}>0.
\]

# 2. Weighted-degree constraint for \(D_4\)

Let

\[
\mathcal C=\{B=A^c:A\in D_4\}.

Then \(\mathcal C\subseteq\binom{[9]}5\) is \(2\)-intersecting and each \(B\in\mathcal C\) has \(w(B)\ge q\). Let \(\Delta_1\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9\) be its vertex degrees. Then

\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le
\sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le
p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4.
\]

Also, for each vertex \(i\), the link \(\mathcal C_i\) is an intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices, hence

\[
\Delta_i\le \frac12\binom84=35.

Therefore

\boxed{d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+r)\rfloor.}

\le

The following exact script enumerates all LP vertices and checks, by Sturm/root counting over rational intervals, that

\[
U_m(r)<(1+r)^8
\]

\[
m=0,\ldots,36,\qquad \frac13\le r\le\frac12.
\]

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from functools import lru_cache
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

# ---------- D4 threshold-graph enumeration ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # Creation sequence: add each new vertex as isolated or dominating.
    # This generates all threshold graphs up to labelled duplicates.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, b in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if b:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    n = 9
    sets = []
    for C in combinations(range(n), 4):
        ok = True
        for i, j in combinations(C, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                ok = False
                break
        if ok:
            mask = sum(1 << i for i in C)
            sets.append(mask)

    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    @lru_cache(None)
    def mis(P):
        if P == 0:
            return 0

        # Include isolated conflict-vertices automatically.
        iso = 0
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            if conflict[v] & P == 0:
                iso |= lb
            Q -= lb
        if iso:
            return iso.bit_count() + mis(P & ~iso)

        # Branch on a max-degree conflict vertex.
        best_v = None
        best_deg = -1
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            deg = (conflict[v] & P).bit_count()
            if deg > best_deg:
                best_deg = deg
                best_v = v
            Q -= lb

        v = best_v
        without = mis(P & ~(1 << v))
        with_v = 1 + mis(P & ~(1 << v) & ~conflict[v])
        return max(without, with_v)

    return mis((1 << N) - 1)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))


# ---------- Layer bounds ----------

def tri_max(m):
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))


# ---------- Exact Sturm positivity ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)  # r>0 on the whole interval
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    if lo == hi:
        assert sp.simplify(num.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        assert sp.simplify(den.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        return

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert poly.eval(lo) > 0 and poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # condition: A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0
    # equivalent to A*r - 56 >= 0
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # condition: A - 56/r <= 0
    # equivalent to A*r - 56 <= 0
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2


# r-intervals on which floor(35(1+r)) is bounded by a fixed integer.
breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8
cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))  # safe upper cap on this interval
        C4 = min(I[m], K)
        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # LP vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # LP vertices with one free variable and budget active.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                # free variable x = (Lambda - fixed_cost)/cost[free]
                # Need 0 <= x <= caps[free].
                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1)/r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("Exact support-9 upper-interval certificate passed.")
```

This proves the relaxed LP upper bound is strictly below \((1+r)^8\). Since every actual layer vector satisfies the LP constraints, the true layer polynomial satisfies

\[
\sum_j d_jr^j < (1+r)^8.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mu_p(D)=q^9\sum_jd_jr^j<q^9(1+r)^8=q.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\mu_p(D)>p.
\]

So the strict-reduced support-9 case is closed for

\[
\boxed{\frac14\le p<\frac13.}
\]

Combined with the lower-interval support-9 certificate from t12, this eliminates strict-reduced support \(9\) on the whole base interval \([1/6,1/3)\).
