  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Theorem 1.1] Let n≥1, let μ1≥...≥μn>0 with ∑_{i=1}^n μ_i=1, and let X1,...,Xn be independent non-negative random variables with E X_i=1 for every i. Put Z=∑_{i=1}^n μ_i X_i, M=μ1, let δ>0, and set T=1+δ. Assume that the following Samuels lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ≥ min_{1≤i≤n} Π_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k)), where the tail sum is interpreted as 0 when i=n. Then the following Feige lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The proof is local: fix one admissible choice and assume its Samuels product bound. Write σ_i=∑_{k=1}^i μ_k, so T−∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k=σ_i+δ. Choose i attaining the minimum in Samuels' bound. Introduce Φ(a,ρ)=a^{-1} log(1−a/(1+ρ)) for a>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ). For j≤i, with a_j=μ_j/σ_i and ρ=δ/σ_i, one has log(1−μ_j/(σ_i+δ))=a_j Φ(a_j,ρ). Hence log P(Z<T) is at least ∑_{j≤i} a_j Φ(a_j,ρ). The key monotonicity input is that Φ is strictly decreasing in its first variable; this follows by writing Φ(a,ρ)=(1/(1+ρ))φ(a/(1+ρ)), where φ(s)=log(1−s)/s=−∑_{k≥0}s^k/(k+1), a strictly decreasing power series on [0,1). Since a_j≤M/σ_i and ∑ a_j=1, the weighted average is at least Φ(M/σ_i,δ/σ_i). Now σ_i∈[M,1]. Put t=M/σ_i and α=δ/M; then the lower bound is at least min_{t∈[M,1]} Φ(t,αt). Lemma 3.4 says t↦Φ(t,αt) is concave, so its minimum over the interval is attained at an endpoint: min(Φ(1,δ/M), Φ(M,δ)). The first endpoint equals log(δ/(δ+M)); exponentiating gives the intermediate product lower bound min(δ/(δ+M), (1−M/(1+δ))^{1/M}). Finally Lemma 3.3 gives exp(Φ(M,δ))≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e), yielding P(Z<T)≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e). Thus the argument actually proves the deterministic product estimate min_i Π_{j≤i}(1−μ_j/(σ_i+δ))≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e), and Samuels' bound transfers it to the probability.
  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.3] Define Φ:[0,1]×(0,∞)→R by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ∈(0,1], and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), with log denoting the natural logarithm. Then for every μ∈[0,1] and every ρ>0, Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ), 1/e)).  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The boundary cases are immediate: for μ=0, Φ=−1/(1+ρ)>−1=log(1/e); for μ=1, Φ=log(ρ/(1+ρ)), which is at least the logarithm of the displayed minimum. Fix 0<μ<1. First note that ∂Φ/∂ρ = 1/((1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ))>0, so Φ increases with ρ. Split at ρ0=μ/(e−1), the point where ρ/(μ+ρ)=1/e. If ρ≥ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ≥−1. By monotonicity in ρ it suffices to prove Φ(μ,ρ0)≥−1, i.e. 1−μ/(1+μ/(e−1))≥e^{−μ}. This is Lemma 3.1: for 0≤t≤1, 1−t/(1+t/(e−1))≥e^{−t}. The paper proves it by setting f(t)=(1+t/(e−1))(1−e^{−t})−t, computing f(0)=f(1)=0 and f''(t)=(e^{−t}/(e−1))(3−e−t), so f is first convex then concave and stays nonnegative. If 0<ρ≤ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ(μ,ρ)≥log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). Define g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). A direct derivative computation gives g'(ρ)=−(1−μ)((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2)/(ρ(1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ)(μ+ρ)). Since ρ≤μ/(e−1)<μ, the numerator factor ((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2) is positive, so g'<0 on (0,ρ0]. Hence g(ρ)≥g(ρ0). But g(ρ0)=Φ(μ,ρ0)+1≥0 by the previous case, completing the proof.
  • [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.4] For every α>0, define h_α:[0,1]→R by h_α(0)=−1 and h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1−t/(1+αt)) for 0<t≤1; equivalently, h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) for t>0, with the value at 0 understood by continuous extension. Then h_α is concave on [0,1].  (from: literature_1703.05152)
    Proof sketch: The extension at 0 is continuous because log(1−t/(1+αt))=−t+O(t^2). The function is smooth on (0,1), so it is enough to prove h_α''(t)≤0 there. Rather than inspect h_α'' directly, the proof multiplies by t^3 and sets η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t). Direct differentiation gives η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1−t/(1+αt)). Differentiating this expression produces the decisive factorization η'(t)=−t^2(1+3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and 0<t<1 both denominator factors are positive, and the numerator is strictly positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s↓0} η(s)=0, it follows that η(t)<0 for every t∈(0,1), hence h_α''(t)<0 on (0,1). Continuity at the endpoints then gives concavity on the whole interval. A frequently used corollary is that for any subinterval [a,b]⊆[0,1], min_{t∈[a,b]} h_α(t)=min(h_α(a),h_α(b)).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 4 (reduce support)] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with finite supports, let X=Σ_j X_j and μ=E[X], and fix δ>0. Suppose that for some index i, X_i has support {v_1,...,v_k} with k≥3. Then X_i can be replaced by a random variable X_i' that is independent of {X_j:j≠i}, has E[X_i']=E[X_i], and has support consisting of at most two values from {v_1,...,v_k}, in such a way that, writing X'=X_i'+Σ_{j≠i} X_j, one has E[X']=μ and Pr[X'<μ+δ]≤Pr[X<μ+δ].  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: For each support point v_j of X_i, let q_j be the conditional probability of the target lower-tail event when X_i is fixed to v_j: q_j=Pr[Σ_{ℓ≠i} X_ℓ+v_j<μ+δ]. A replacement distribution on the same support is specified by probabilities p_j. Preserving total mass and mean imposes the two linear constraints Σ_j p_j=1 and Σ_j p_j v_j=E[X_i], with p_j≥0. The resulting lower-tail probability is the linear objective Σ_j q_j p_j. The original distribution of X_i is feasible, so the minimum of this linear program is at most the original lower-tail probability. A basic optimal solution of an LP with two equality constraints has at most two positive p_j's (apart from degeneracy, still no more than two are needed). Taking X_i' with this basic optimal distribution gives the claim. The same LP idea is later reused in the paper with the objective Pr[sum<mean] instead of Pr[sum<mean+δ], yielding the modified reduce-support operation.
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Proposition 8] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent random variables. For each i, let μ_i=E[X_i], assume μ_1≥μ_2≥...≥μ_n, and assume X_i takes value μ_i+s_i with probability μ_i/(μ_i+s_i) and value 0 otherwise, where s_i≥0, with the evident degenerate interpretation when μ_i=s_i=0. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and s=Σ_i s_i. Then Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥s/(μ_1+s).  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: The maximum possible value of X is μ+s=Σ_i(μ_i+s_i). If some variable with s_i>0 comes up 0, the sum loses μ_i+s_i, which is strictly larger than μ_n because μ_i≥μ_n and s_i>0; hence X<μ+s−μ_n. Variables with s_i=0 are at their upper value almost surely. Therefore the event X≥μ+s−μ_n can occur only when every variable with positive surplus takes its upper value, so Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤Π_i μ_i/(μ_i+s_i). Since μ_i≤μ_1 and a/(a+t) is increasing in a for t≥0, each factor is at most μ_1/(μ_1+s_i). For fixed total surplus s, Π_i μ_1/(μ_1+s_i)≤μ_1/(μ_1+s); equivalently, combining two surpluses x,y cannot decrease the product because (μ_1/(μ_1+x))(μ_1/(μ_1+y))≤μ_1/(μ_1+x+y). Thus Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤μ_1/(μ_1+s), and taking complements gives Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥1−μ_1/(μ_1+s)=s/(μ_1+s).
  • [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 10 (stage-2 modified merge bound)] Consider the following Stage 2 process on a finite collection of independent nonconstant two-point random variables. At any time, variable X_i has mean μ_i>0 and takes values 0 and μ_i+s_i, where s_i≥0 is its surplus. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and let μ_1=max_i μ_i. Initially all means lie in [μ_1/2, μ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for α=1/3, the initial value of s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i is greater than α μ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine μ_1=max_i μ_i and s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'≤α μ_1. For α=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<μ^r]≥1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r−1}<μ^{r−1}]≥1/13.  (from: literature_www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670)
    Proof sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<μ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and μ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below μ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set β=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>αμ_1, then Pr[X<μ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(μ_1+s')>α/(1+α). If it stops with βμ_1≤s'≤αμ_1, write s'=γμ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least μ_1/2: with probability at least γ/(1+γ), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2−γ)μ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
is equivalent, for the same weights, to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
With
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family.

It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.
    Verifier's is_relaxation verdict: True
    Proof sketch: By recasting the event {∑wi vi≥p} as the complement of the p-biased family D={A:w(A)<p} and then using the bijection A↦[m]\A to the q-biased family F={B:w(B)>q}, one shows exactly that Pr[∑wi vi≥p]≥p⇔μq(F)≤q. The key combinatorial lemma then proves that whenever rp≤1 no r sets in F can be disjoint: assigning each element to at most r–1 of the B₁,…,Bᵣ gives ∑j w(Bj)≤r–1, while each w(Bj)>q=1–p forces ∑j w(Bj)>r(1–p)≥r–1, a contradiction that establishes F is r-wise intersecting.

  • Critical-index decay after the first nine weights reduces the nonregular part of the tail to an explicit bounded buffer: if no tau-regular tail occurs before index ell, then gamma=sum_{i=10}^ell w_i <= c_tau(sqrt(B_9)-sqrt(B_ell)), and in particular gamma <= c_tau(a_9/tau - sqrt(B_ell)).  (from: t15_robust_finite_head_tail)

  • Berry–Esseen effective-dimension reduction: for p in [1/4,1/3], if rho=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^{3/2} <= 5 sqrt(2)/28, then Pr[sum w_i v_i >= p] >= p. Hence any counterexample has (sum w_i^2)^3/(sum w_i^3)^2 < 15.68.  (from: t16_global_k3_high_bias_ltf)
    Proof sketch: Apply the uniform Berry–Esseen inequality to S-p. The BE error at the mean is at most 0.56*(p^2+q^2)/sqrt(pq)*rho; if this is <=1/2-p, the normal 1/2 mass at the mean gives the desired lower bound.
  • Exact pair-merging formula: replacing two weights a>=b with a+b changes the bad probability by pq(Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) - Pr[R in [p-b,p))).  (from: t16_global_k3_high_bias_ltf)
    Proof sketch: Condition on the outside sum R and compare the two-point split variable a xi+b eta with the merged variable (a+b) zeta. The conditional difference is +pq on one slab, -pq on the adjacent upper slab, and zero elsewhere.
  • Assuming the known support <=9 theorem, the full k=3 high-bias theorem follows from the adjacent-slab monotonicity condition for the two smallest weights.  (from: t16_global_k3_high_bias_ltf)
    Proof sketch: If the lower slab never has more mass than the upper slab, merging the two smallest weights cannot decrease the bad probability. Repeated merging would turn any support >=10 counterexample into a support-9 counterexample, contradicting the finite-support result.
  • A pointwise cyclic/orbit interpolation of the p=1/k proof fails throughout p in (1/4,1/3).  (from: t16_global_k3_high_bias_ltf)

  • A crude 9-head deterministic range/mean tail replacement is false.  (from: advisor_r9)
    Proof sketch: At p=1/3 with eighteen equal weights 1/18 and the first nine weights as head, the crude tail-filling functional equals 1915/19683, far below p, although the true probability is above p.
  • A smoothed finite-head/tail certificate holds with an arbitrary buffer and a Berry-Esseen-regular remainder.  (from: advisor_r9)

Minimizing over all buffer distributions with range [0,gamma] and mean p gamma gives the lower convex envelope chi_{gamma,sigma}, yielding the stated head average certificate.
  • Critical-index decay bounds the nonregular buffer after the first nine weights.  (from: advisor_r9)

Summing this geometric decay gives gamma <= c_tau(sqrt(B_9)-sqrt(B_ell)), and hence gamma <= c_tau(a_9/tau - sqrt(B_ell)) when starting after the ninth weight.
  • For p in [1/4,1/3], Berry-Esseen rules out counterexamples with rho=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^(3/2) <= 5sqrt2/28.  (from: advisor_r9)

  • Exact pair-merging formula: replacing weights a>=b with a+b changes the bad probability by pq times the difference between a lower residual slab and an upper residual slab.  (from: advisor_r9)

    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the restricted support-9 statement: for every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\) and \(w_i<p\) for all \(i\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), one has

The combinatorial bounds on \(d_3,d_4,d_5\), the spectral bounds used for \(KG(9,3)\) and \(J(9,4)\), and the final finite polynomial check are all valid for the stated parameter range.

The heart of the argument is sharp extremal bounds on the “losing” set‐counts d_j: for j=2 via the number m of heavy pairs, for j=3 by relating disjoint triples in D_3 to triangles in the Kneser graph and applying its least‐eigenvalue bound, for j=4 by the classical bound 56 on intersecting 4-families via cyclic‐interval counting, and for j=5 by a 2-intersecting–family bound obtained from a positive semidefinite combination of Johnson‐scheme matrices.   • The effective-dimension case is closed: if rho_0=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^{3/2} <= eta(p), then Pr[S>=p]>=p; uniformly on [1/4,1/3] it suffices that rho_0<=5 sqrt(2)/28.  (from: t18_smoothed_finite_head_certificate)

(from: t18_smoothed_finite_head_certificate)
    Proof sketch: For f(u)=barPhi((r-u)/sigma), the sign of f'' is the sign of r-u, so f is convex then concave. The lower convex envelope is therefore either f itself, the endpoint chord, or f followed by a tangent-to-endpoint chord.
  • The sigma=0 critical-index range/mean certificate is false even after excluding the effective-dimension case: for p=1/3 and fourteen equal weights, the first-9-head psi certificate equals 49159/255879<1/3 while rho_0=1/sqrt(14)>5 sqrt(2)/28.  (from: t18_smoothed_finite_head_certificate)

(from: t18_smoothed_finite_head_certificate)
    Proof sketch: The regular tail has rho=1/32. If the dominant head weight is absent, the Gaussian threshold is at least 10.78 standard deviations; if it is present, the contribution is at most 1. Thus the Gaussian surplus over p is exponentially tiny, much smaller than the O(rho) BE error.
  • In support n>=10, all pairs among the bottom 4 weights are mergeable, and among the bottom 5 weights all pairs except possibly (w4,w5) are mergeable.  (from: t17_pair_merge_stability)

    Proof sketch: For a mergeable pair, the merged instance has bad probability at most q by minimality, while the original has bad probability q+epsilon; the exact merge formula gives mu-mu_merge=pq(L-U).

    Proof sketch: Conditioning on the other variables, the second discrete derivative of the threshold indicator is +1 on the lower slab and -1 on the upper slab; the pivotal covariance gives the same difference.
  • If all bottom-4 mergeable pairs are bad, then 6 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon); if all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then 9 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon <= 2/15 throughout p in [1/4,1/3] in the bottom-5 case.  (from: t17_pair_merge_stability)
    Proof sketch: Minimality makes each relevant bottom-pair level-2 Fourier coefficient at least epsilon; Parseval bounds the sum of their squares by Var(1_{S>=p})=(p-epsilon)(q+epsilon).
  • If all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then every bottom-5 coordinate has influence at least epsilon/q and the weighted influence sum satisfies sum_i w_i I_i <= 1-epsilon/p.  (from: t17_pair_merge_stability)
    Proof sketch: The covariance identity gives epsilon <= Cov(1_{A_i},v_j) <= q I_i for every coordinate with a bad partner; the weighted influence identity E[(S-p)1_G]=pq sum_i w_i I_i and S-p<=q on G give the upper bound.

    Proof sketch: For any bottom pair, the lower slab consists of residual types (K,L)=(0,2),(1,1), while the upper slab consists of (0,3),(1,2),(2,0), giving L-U=8p^2q^5(1-3p)>0. Direct binomial enumeration gives Pr(D)<0.606<q on this interval.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
  • Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.
  • Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.
  • Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.
  • Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.
  • Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.
  • Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root polynomial certificates.: Their leading variance coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so they cannot handle diffuse weights there.
  • Crude arbitrary-support matching bounds for losing layers.: For large support, many tiny coordinates allow large matchings in losing pairs and triples.
  • Colouring with large colours plus a leftover colour.: It reduces to an upper-tail inequality for the leftover colour that is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Worst-case head threshold only: It proves the regular-tail case when alpha_k <= sigma_k, but is too crude for dominant heads because it ignores favorable head outcomes.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen in extreme one-head tails: The normal margin q barPhi(pt)-p barPhi(qt) decays in the far tail, while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not; a relative moderate-deviation bound seems needed.
  • Critical-index decay alone: Geometric decay reduces the tail mass, but it still leaves a finite head that may be too large for the existing support-7/8 layer counts without an additional robust finite-head certificate.
  • Naive universal good merge: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) have every mergeable pair bad, so local merge inequalities alone do not contradict reducedness.
  • Direct FKG/Harris comparison of the two residual windows: The upper and lower windows are differences of downsets rather than monotone events, and the equal-weight example reverses the desired comparison.
  • Crude support-7 layer counts: Bounds such as d2<=21, d3<=15, d4<=15 are too weak near p=1/4; the proof needs the winning-pair blocker tradeoff and the 3-intersection bound for losing 4-sets.
  • Purely local universal good-merge lemma.: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) make every mergeable pair locally bad even though the instance is not a counterexample.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen for a dominant head plus tiny regular tail.: The normal margin in the one-head formula decays in the far tail while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone after complementing the bad family.: General k-wise intersecting monotone families can have too-large high-biased measure; the missing ingredient must exploit the linear-threshold form F={w(A)>q} and coordinate/threshold constraints.
  • Crude independent layer bounds for n=8 above p=1/4: Bounds such as d2<=28, d3<=56, d4<=35, d5<=21 are far too weak near p=1/4 and even the improved d4<=17, d5<=6 needs a tradeoff with d2 and d3.
  • Extending support n=9 beyond [1/6,1/5]: The n=9 middle range introduces losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-2 triple families; the n=8 high-pair/blocker tradeoff did not immediately produce a clean polynomial certificate.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone: For these linear thresholds the k-wise property is automatic when p<=1/k, so it adds no extra leverage beyond the original lower-tail problem.
  • Naive moment or Cantelli bounds: The threshold is exactly the mean, so one-sided variance inequalities have zero gap and do not force Pr[S>=E S]>=p.
  • Pairwise merging of weights: The local CDF comparison changes sign depending on the outside partial sum, so merging small coordinates is not monotone without additional structure.
  • Crude layer counts for the first open k=3,n=6 case: The automatic bounds e2<=10 and e3<=10 leave a small numerical gap near p=1/3; the weighted shifted structure of the bad layers is still needed.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone on the complement family.: The property is automatic for threshold families with p<=1/k and is false as a sufficient measure bound above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint for general k-wise intersecting families.
  • Crude independent layer bounds for support 8 above p=1/4.: Bounds on d2,d3,d4,d5 separately are too weak; the successful proof needs tradeoffs involving winning pairs, blockers, high-pair graphs, and Kneser supersaturation.
  • Direct extension of the support-8 layer proof to support 9.: The n=9 middle and upper ranges introduce losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-two triple families, so the n=8 estimates do not transfer without new tradeoffs.
  • Use the p<=1/4 winning-pair blocker for D4 above p=1/4: The key contradiction becomes w(A)+w(e1)+w(e2)+w_z<4p, which no longer contradicts total mass 1 when p>1/4.
  • Deficiency cascade plus crude d4<=56 on [1/4,1/3): The relaxed bounds allow a non-feasible-looking obstruction at r=2/5, m=3 with layer profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1), whose polynomial exceeds (1+r)^8 by 80174/390625.
  • Direct reduction of the 8-coordinate head to the known support-8 theorem: After removing a tail of weight b, the head weights have total mass 1-b and the relevant thresholds are p and p-b while the Bernoulli parameter remains p, so the one-threshold normalized support-8 result does not apply directly.
  • Endpoint-to-interior differential bound via Russo influence: A universal pointwise influence bound I_t(F)<=1 is false; the admissible four-equal-weights LTF F={|A|>=3} has I_{2/3}=16/9.
  • Pointwise random thinning lower bound: For k=3, q=0.70, thinning three weights 0.24,0.24,0.24 from total 0.72 requires all three to survive, giving probability alpha^3<alpha.
  • Direct Hoffman spectral extension above theta=(k-1)/k: The LTF property controls a weighted first Fourier coefficient, but the empty-intersection form above the endpoint contains uncontrolled higher-order mixed Fourier terms.
  • Extend the p≤1/4 winning-pair blocker for D4 directly to p>1/4.: The contradiction becomes a bound by 4p, which no longer contradicts total mass 1 once p>1/4.
  • Use the support-9 deficiency cascade together with only crude d4≤56 in the upper interval.: The relaxed constraints allow a non-feasible-looking obstruction near r=2/5 with layer profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1).
  • Endpoint-to-interior differential proof via a pointwise influence bound I_t(F)≤1.: The admissible LTF F={|A|≥3} on four equal weights has I_{2/3}=16/9.
  • Pointwise thinning from q to the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint.: Three weights 0.24,0.24,0.24 at q=0.70 require all three thinned elements to survive, giving probability α^3<α.
  • Direct Hoffman extension above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint using only k-wise intersection.: The LTF condition gives useful first-level Fourier information, but the empty-intersection form contains uncontrolled higher-level mixed terms.
  • Pure k-wise intersection: For p <= 1/3 the 3-wise union/intersection property is automatic for these threshold families, and by itself does not encode the mean-threshold LTF structure.
  • Direct support-8 extension of the good-pair complement count: For n=8, complements of bad triples have five vertices and weight > 2; two disjoint bad pairs plus a fifth vertex need not contradict the weight bound, so the n=7 graph-counting closure no longer works directly.
  • Crude sprinkling/Markov boundary estimate: The conditional Markov bound for sprinkling from t0=2/3 blows up near the boundary w(B)=2/3 and gives no adequate averaged control of boundary mass.
  • Weighted degree bound alone: The bound d4<=floor(35(1+r)) gives only d4<=49 at r=2/5 and does not by itself close the m=3 obstruction; the W-independent intersecting-family bound is needed.
  • Crude high-layer bounds with d4<=56: This leaves the known relaxed obstruction at m=3, r=2/5 with d4=56 and a negative polynomial gap.
  • Weighted-degree bound alone for D4 in support 9.: The bound d4<=floor(35(1+r)) still permits the old m=3, r=2/5 relaxed obstruction; the W-independent intersecting-family enumeration is essential.
  • Crude high-layer support-9 bounds with d4<=56.: These bounds leave a negative polynomial gap near the relaxed profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1).
  • Pure k-wise intersection or crude sprinkling for the k=3 high-bias LTF theorem.: The 3-wise intersection property is automatic for these threshold families and does not encode enough of the mean-threshold LTF structure; crude sprinkling estimates blow up near the boundary.
  • Use only the elementary range/mean tail function psi_beta after the first nine weights: The equal-18 example gives Phi=1915/19683<1/3, so the crude tail compression loses too much information about regular tails.
  • Induct using only the original inequality for the tail at its mean: That gives only Pr[T>=p beta]>=p, but head outcomes often require quantitative lower bounds for thresholds below the tail mean.
  • Fixed 9-head absolute Berry-Esseen alone: In far-tail or only moderately regular cases the Gaussian margin can be smaller than the uniform Berry-Esseen error; a smoothed buffer certificate or relative tail estimate is needed.
  • Pointwise cyclic/Liggett interpolation: Four equal weights with four equally spaced arcs of length p give orbit-good fraction 4p-1<p for p<1/3.
  • Berry–Esseen alone: It only forces L3/L2 effective dimension below about 16 for counterexamples, not actual support <=9; long tiny tails can coexist with small effective dimension.
  • Unconditional pairwise merging: The exact formula has two slabs with opposite signs, so merging is monotone exactly when the adjacent-slab inequality holds.
  • Use only the elementary range/mean tail function psi_beta after the first nine head weights.: The equal-18 example at p=1/3 makes the certificate much smaller than p.
  • Prove universal adjacent-slab monotonicity for arbitrary strict reduced support at least 10.: The explicit support-10 example with weights eight times 41/400 and two times 9/100 violates it.
  • Use Berry-Esseen alone to force finite support at most 9.: It only forces effective dimension below about 15.68 in the upper base interval; a long tiny tail can coexist with a few dominant weights.
  • Extend the reciprocal orbit proof pointwise to p in (1/4,1/3).: Four equal weights on four equally spaced circle arcs have orbit-good measure 4p-1, which is less than p for p<1/3.
  • Use only the sigma=0 range/mean buffer function psi_gamma under the critical-index mass bound: Fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 satisfy the effective-dimension exclusion but give a psi certificate value about 0.1921<1/3.
  • Use absolute Berry-Esseen for every gamma=0 regular tail: In the one-dominant-head far-tail regime, the Gaussian margin above p is exponentially small while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in the tail regularity parameter.
  • Treat a_9 small as automatically easy: The regular-tail counterexample has a_9=1/1536 and still defeats the absolute-BE certificate.
  • Universal bottom-5 good-pair principle: The five-0.09/five-0.11 support-10 family has every bottom-5 pair bad, so a local existence statement is false without using Pr(D)>q and minimality.
  • Parseval/Fourier mass alone: It forces an all-bottom-5-bad minimal counterexample to have small gap epsilon, at most 2/15, but does not force epsilon=0.
  • Mean-only slice certificate over the outside sum: All-bad bottom slices can be compensated by high outside-sum slices; controlling only E[R] is too weak, and one needs product-structure or additional covariance information.

## Known Bottlenecks
  • The only missing part of the candidate classification is sufficiency for every 0 < p < 1/3.
  • Any proof must be sharp at dictator weights and must handle threshold jumps at subset sums.
  • Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.
  • Control threshold jumps/critical antichains in the strict inequality mu_p({w(A)>p})≥p for p<1/3.
  • Find a polynomial or moment-dual lower bound that exploits the Bernoulli moment structure rather than only support bounds.
  • Prove the weighted Bernoulli inequality on the base interval p in [1/6,1/3].
  • Rule out strict reduced counterexamples with support at least 7 in the base interval.
  • Find a higher-degree polynomial/moment certificate that exploits the Bernoulli cumulant structure in the base interval.
  • Rule out minimal strict counterexamples with support at least 7 in p in [1/6,1/3].
  • Prove existence of a mergeable pair whose residual-window inequality goes in the non-counterexample direction.
  • Turn Berry-Esseen regularity plus finite-support layer counting into a complete large-support proof.
  • Prove or refute the proposed biased-measure theorem for k-wise intersecting monotone linear-threshold families in the range q in [(k-1)/k,k/(k+1)].

<full_solver_output task_id="t18_smoothed_finite_head_certificate">
I fixed constants and tried to push the proposed \(9\)-head critical-index certificate. The main conclusion is:

**The lower-bound formula from t15 is valid, but the hoped-for universal finite-dimensional certificate is false, even for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\).** Two different obstructions appear:
1. the \(\sigma=0\) “range/mean buffer” certificate fails even after excluding the effective-dimension case;  
2. the \(\gamma=0\) regular-tail certificate with absolute Berry–Esseen error fails in a one-dominant-head far-tail regime, even with \(a_9\) arbitrarily small.

Below are the rigorous pieces and the counterexamples.

---

## 1. Constants and a useful analytic subcase

Take the Berry–Esseen constant \(C_{\rm BE}=0.56\). For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),
\[
K(p)=\frac{p^2+(1-p)^2}{\sqrt{p(1-p)}}\le K_*=\frac{5}{2\sqrt3}.
\]

I chose
\[
\boxed{\tau=\frac1{32}}.
\]
Then
\[
C_{\rm BE}K_*\tau
=
0.56\cdot \frac{5}{2\sqrt3}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac{7}{160\sqrt3}
\approx 0.02526.
\]

Also
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-1/3\approx 0.03611,
\]
so this \(\tau\) leaves a positive margin.

The critical-index decay constant becomes especially clean:
\[
\lambda=\sqrt{1-\tau^2}=\frac{\sqrt{1023}}{32},
\qquad
c_\tau=\frac{\tau}{1-\lambda}=32+\sqrt{1023}.
\]
Thus, if the first \(\tau\)-regular tail after the first \(9\) weights begins after a nonregular buffer of mass \(\gamma\), and \(u=\sqrt{B_\ell}\), then
\[
\boxed{
\gamma\le (32+\sqrt{1023})(32a_9-u)_+.
}
\]

### Small \(\alpha+\gamma\) subcase

Let the split be
\[
S=H+U+R,
\]
where \(H\) is the first \(9\) weights, \(U\) is the nonregular buffer, and \(R=p\beta+X\) is the regular remainder with standard deviation \(\sigma\) and Berry–Esseen ratio \(\rho\le1/32\). If
\[
\boxed{\alpha+\gamma\le \sigma,}
\]
then the desired inequality holds.

Indeed, for every head and buffer outcome,
\[
p(\alpha+\gamma)-H-U\le p(\alpha+\gamma)\le p\sigma\le \frac{\sigma}{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[X\ge p(\alpha+\gamma)-H-U]
\ge
\overline\Phi(1/3)-C_{\rm BE}K_*\rho
\ge
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac{7}{160\sqrt3}
> \frac13\ge p.
\]

So this proves the regular-tail certificate in the genuinely small-head/small-buffer regime.

---

## 2. Effective-dimension case

Let
\[
A_2=\sum_i w_i^2,\qquad A_3=\sum_i w_i^3,\qquad \rho_0=\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}.
\]
Berry–Esseen at the mean gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge \Pr[S>p]\ge \frac12-C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho_0.
\]
Thus the theorem is proved whenever
\[
\rho_0\le
\eta(p):=\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{p(1-p)}}{0.56\,(p^2+(1-p)^2)}.
\]
On \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),
\[
\eta(p)\ge \eta(1/3)=\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}\approx0.25254.
\]
So any remaining counterexample must have
\[
\boxed{\rho_0>\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}.}
\]

---

## 3. Exact one-dimensional formula for \(\chi_{\gamma,\sigma}\)

This is useful for interval arithmetic, although the global certificate later fails.

For \(\sigma>0\), define
\[
f(u)=\overline\Phi\!\left(\frac{r-u}{\sigma}\right),
\qquad m=p\gamma.
\]
Then \(f''(u)\) has the sign of \(r-u\). Hence:

- If \(r\ge\gamma\), then \(f\) is convex on \([0,\gamma]\), and
  \[
  \chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)=f(m).
  \]

- If \(r\le0\), then \(f\) is concave on \([0,\gamma]\), and
  \[
  \chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)=(1-p)f(0)+p f(\gamma).
  \]

- If \(0<r<\gamma\), there is a unique tangency point \(s\in[0,r]\). If
  \[
  \frac{f(\gamma)-f(0)}{\gamma}\le f'(0),
  \]
  then \(s=0\). Otherwise \(s\in(0,r)\) is the unique solution of
  \[
  f'(s)=\frac{f(\gamma)-f(s)}{\gamma-s}.
  \]
  Then
  \[
  \chi_{\gamma,\sigma}(r)=
  \begin{cases}
  f(m),&m\le s,\\[1mm]
  \dfrac{\gamma-m}{\gamma-s}f(s)+\dfrac{m-s}{\gamma-s}f(\gamma),&m>s.
  \end{cases}
  \]

So the \(\chi\)-computation is genuinely one-dimensional and interval-arithmetic friendly.

---

## 4. The \(\sigma=0\) critical-index certificate is false

Here is an exact counterexample to the deterministic buffer certificate, not to the original theorem.

Take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad m=14,\qquad w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=\frac1{14}.
\]
Use the first \(9\) weights as head:
\[
\alpha=\frac9{14},\qquad \gamma=\frac5{14},\qquad \sigma=0.
\]
For \(\tau=1/32\), no nonempty suffix of the five remaining equal weights is \(\tau\)-regular, so the critical-index procedure reaches the end and gives precisely the \(\psi_\gamma\)-certificate.

Let \(K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(9,1/3)\). Then
\[
p\gamma=\frac5{42}.
\]
For a head outcome with \(K\) selected weights,
\[
r_K=p-\frac K{14}.
\]
The deterministic filling function gives
\[
\psi_\gamma(r_K)=
\begin{cases}
0,&K\le3,\\[1mm]
3/13,&K=4,\\[1mm]
1,&K\ge5.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore the certificate value is
\[
\Phi
=
\frac3{13}\Pr[K=4]+\Pr[K\ge5].
\]
Using
\[
\Pr[K=4]=\frac{4032}{19683},
\qquad
\Pr[K\ge5]=\frac{2851}{19683},
\]
we get
\[
\Phi
=
\frac3{13}\cdot\frac{4032}{19683}
+
\frac{2851}{19683}
=
\frac{49159}{255879}
\approx0.1921
<
\frac13.
\]

This example is **not** removed by the effective-dimension reduction, because
\[
\rho_0=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}\approx0.26726
>
\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}\approx0.25254.
\]

The original probability is fine:
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge\frac13\right]
=
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge5]
\approx0.5245>\frac13.
\]
The failure is entirely caused by compressing the five-weight buffer to only its range and mean.

So the proposed \(\sigma=0\) finite-head certificate cannot be made rigorous as stated.

---

## 5. The \(\gamma=0\) regular-tail Berry–Esseen certificate also fails

The obstruction is the far-tail, one-dominant-head regime.

Take again \(p=1/3\). Let
\[
\varepsilon=\frac1{1536},
\]
and define
\[
w_1=\frac13-8\varepsilon=\frac{21}{64},
\qquad
w_2=\cdots=w_9=\varepsilon,
\]
with \(1024\) additional tail weights, each also equal to \(\varepsilon\). Then
\[
\alpha=\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=\frac13,
\qquad
\gamma=0,
\qquad
\beta=\frac23.
\]
The regular tail has
\[
B=1024\varepsilon^2=\frac1{2304},
\qquad
\sigma=\sqrt{pqB}=\frac{\sqrt2}{144},
\qquad
\rho=\frac1{\sqrt{1024}}=\frac1{32}.
\]

Let \(G\) be the Gaussian head average:
\[
G=
\mathbb E_A\,
\overline\Phi\!\left(
\frac{p\alpha-a(A)}{\sigma}
\right).
\]
Condition on whether \(w_1\) is selected. If \(w_1\) is selected, the Gaussian tail contribution is at most \(1\). If \(w_1\) is not selected, and \(K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(8,1/3)\) counts the selected small head weights, then
\[
\frac{p\alpha-K\varepsilon}{\sigma}
=
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}
\ge
\frac{488}{32\sqrt2}
>10.78.
\]
Hence
\[
G
\le
\frac13
+
\frac23\overline\Phi(10.78)
<
\frac13+10^{-26}.
\]
But the Berry–Esseen penalty is
\[
C_{\rm BE}K(1/3)\rho
=
0.56\cdot\frac{5}{3\sqrt2}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{240\sqrt2}
\approx0.0206.
\]
Therefore
\[
G
<
p+C_{\rm BE}K(p)\rho.
\]
So the regular-tail certificate condition fails, despite the tail being \(1/32\)-regular and \(a_9=1/1536\) being extremely small.

In fact this is not a numerical accident: for any fixed \(\tau>0\), taking \(N\) tiny equal tail weights with \(N\) large gives \(\rho=N^{-1/2}\le\tau\), while the Gaussian surplus \(G-p\) is exponentially small in \(N\), much smaller than the absolute Berry–Esseen error \(O(N^{-1/2})\).

Thus the absolute Berry–Esseen certificate cannot close the \(\gamma=0\) regular-tail case uniformly.

---

## 6. What extra tail information is needed

The t15 lower bound is correct, but the proposed finite-head certificate loses too much information in two places.

### For the nonregular buffer

Range and mean are insufficient. In the equal-\(14\) example, the buffer is actually
\[
U=\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(5,1/3).
\]
When the head has \(K=3\), the required buffer value is exactly its mean:
\[
r_3=p-\frac3{14}=\frac5{42}=p\gamma.
\]
The crude \(\psi_\gamma\) bound gives zero, but the true probability is
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(5,1/3)\ge\frac5{42}\right]
=
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(5,1/3)\ge2]
=
\frac{131}{243}.
\]
So the buffer certificate needs at least one additional statistic, such as buffer variance
\[
B_{\rm buf}=\sum_{\rm buffer} w_i^2,
\]
or, better, the actual finite buffer layer distribution.

### For the regular remainder

Absolute Berry–Esseen is too crude in far-tail regimes. The one-dominant-head example requires comparing two exponentially small tail probabilities. A successful proof needs a **relative** tail estimate, e.g. a Cramér/Bahadur–Rao or saddlepoint-type bound, or an explicit tail-ratio inequality of the form
\[
q\,\Pr[X\ge x_+]\ge p\,\Pr[X\le -x_-]
\]
in the relevant asymmetric regime. The variance and \(\rho\) alone are not enough when the Gaussian margin is exponentially smaller than the BE error.

So the finite-head route should be strengthened by keeping:
1. buffer variance or finite buffer layers, not just \((\gamma,\mathbb E U)\);
2. relative large-deviation information for the regular remainder, not just absolute Berry–Esseen.

## Task t17_pair_merge_stability — Salvage pair merging by proving a stability or averaged good-merge theorem for minimal counterexamples.
**Proven results:**
  • In support n>=10, all pairs among the bottom 4 weights are mergeable, and among the bottom 5 weights all pairs except possibly (w4,w5) are mergeable. — Since 1>2w3+6w5 and w3<=w5, one gets w3+w5<1/4<=p; this dominates every bottom-4 pair and every bottom-5 pair except possibly (w4,w5).
  • In a minimal-support counterexample with gap epsilon=Pr(D)-q>0, every mergeable pair satisfies L-U>=epsilon/(pq), where L and U are the lower and upper adjacent slabs in the merge formula. — For a mergeable pair, the merged instance has bad probability at most q by minimality, while the original has bad probability q+epsilon; the exact merge formula gives mu-mu_merge=pq(L-U).
  • For a mergeable pair (i,j), pq(L-U)=Cov(1_{A_i},v_j)=Cov(1_{A_j},v_i)=the p-biased level-2 Fourier coefficient of 1_{S>=p} on {i,j}. — Conditioning on the other variables, the second discrete derivative of the threshold indicator is +1 on the lower slab and -1 on the upper slab; the pivotal covariance gives the same difference.
  • If all bottom-4 mergeable pairs are bad, then 6 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon); if all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then 9 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon <= 2/15 throughout p in [1/4,1/3] in the bottom-5 case. — Minimality makes each relevant bottom-pair level-2 Fourier coefficient at least epsilon; Parseval bounds the sum of their squares by Var(1_{S>=p})=(p-epsilon)(q+epsilon).
  • If all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then every bottom-5 coordinate has influence at least epsilon/q and the weighted influence sum satisfies sum_i w_i I_i <= 1-epsilon/p. — The covariance identity gives epsilon <= Cov(1_{A_i},v_j) <= q I_i for every coordinate with a bad partner; the weighted influence identity E[(S-p)1_G]=pq sum_i w_i I_i and S-p<=q on G give the upper bound.
  • There is a support-10 parametric local obstruction with all bottom-5 pairs bad but Pr(D)<=q: for p in (0.29,0.31], take five weights 0.09 and five weights 0.11. — For any bottom pair, the lower slab consists of residual types (K,L)=(0,2),(1,1), while the upper slab consists of (0,3),(1,2),(2,0), giving L-U=8p^2q^5(1-3p)>0. Direct binomial enumeration gives Pr(D)<0.606<q on this interval.
**Failed attempts:**
  • Universal bottom-5 good-pair principle: The five-0.09/five-0.11 support-10 family has every bottom-5 pair bad, so a local existence statement is false without using Pr(D)>q and minimality.
  • Parseval/Fourier mass alone: It forces an all-bottom-5-bad minimal counterexample to have small gap epsilon, at most 2/15, but does not force epsilon=0.
  • Mean-only slice certificate over the outside sum: All-bad bottom slices can be compensated by high outside-sum slices; controlling only E[R] is too weak, and one needs product-structure or additional covariance information.
**Progress notes:** The exact stability dichotomy is reduced to ruling out a small-gap configuration with at least nine positive bottom-pair level-2 Fourier coefficients of size >=epsilon and strong pivotal covariance constraints. A stronger local statement is false: all bottom-5 pairs can be bad in support 10, but the explicit obstruction is safely non-counterexample with Pr(D)<q.

<full_solver_output task_id="t17_pair_merge_stability">
Let the weights be sorted
\[
0<w_1\le \cdots \le w_n<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1,
\]
and write
\[
\mu=\Pr(D)=q+\varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon>0,\qquad G=D^c=\{S\ge p\},\qquad \Pr(G)=p-\varepsilon.
\]

Below are several rigorous reductions/obstructions. The main new point is that **minimality makes every bad merge quantitatively bad**, not just sign-bad.

---

## 1. Bottom-4 and almost all bottom-5 pairs are automatically mergeable

Since \(n\ge 10\),
\[
1>\,2w_3+6w_5,
\]
because \(w_1,w_2>0\), \(w_3,w_4\ge w_3\), and \(w_5,\dots,w_{10}\ge w_5\). With \(w_3\le w_5\), this implies
\[
w_3+w_5<\frac14\le p.
\]
Therefore:

* every pair among \(w_1,w_2,w_3,w_4\) is mergeable;
* every pair among \(w_1,\dots,w_5\) except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) is mergeable.

So bottom 4 gives 6 mergeable pairs, and bottom 5 gives at least 9 mergeable pairs.

---

## 2. Minimality gives a quantitative slab gap

For \(i<j\), put \(b=w_i\le a=w_j\), and let
\[
R_{ij}=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j} w_\ell v_\ell.
\]
Define
\[
L_{ij}=\Pr\bigl(R_{ij}\in[p-a-b,p-a)\bigr),\qquad
U_{ij}=\Pr\bigl(R_{ij}\in[p-b,p)\bigr).
\]
The merge formula says
\[
\mu-\mu^{(ij)}=pq\,(L_{ij}-U_{ij}),
\]
where \(\mu^{(ij)}\) is the bad probability after replacing \(w_i,w_j\) by \(w_i+w_j\).

If \((i,j)\) is mergeable, minimal support gives \(\mu^{(ij)}\le q\). Hence
\[
pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij})=\mu-\mu^{(ij)}\ge \mu-q=\varepsilon.
\]
Thus in a minimal counterexample every mergeable pair satisfies the stronger inequality
\[
\boxed{L_{ij}-U_{ij}\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{pq}.}
\]

So an all-bad bottom-5 configuration is not merely sign-bad: at least 9 slab gaps must each be of size \(\ge \varepsilon/(pq)\).

---

## 3. Pivotal/covariance/Fourier reformulation

Let
\[
A_i=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_i,p)\right\}
\]
be the event that coordinate \(i\) is pivotal for \(G=\{S\ge p\}\). For \(i<j\), one has
\[
\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=1)-\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=0)=L_{ij}-U_{ij}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]
The same identity also holds with \(i,j\) reversed:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_j},v_i)=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]

Therefore, in a minimal counterexample,
\[
\boxed{\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)\ge \varepsilon}
\]
for every mergeable bad pair.

In \(p\)-biased Fourier notation, with
\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]
the second-level coefficient of \(1_G\) satisfies
\[
\widehat{1_G}(\{i,j\})=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]
Thus every bad mergeable pair in a minimal counterexample contributes a positive level-2 Fourier coefficient of size at least \(\varepsilon\).

By Parseval,
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat{1_G}(A)^2
=\operatorname{Var}(1_G)
=(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]
Hence if all bottom-4 pairs are bad, then
\[
6\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon),
\]
and if all mergeable bottom-5 pairs are bad, then
\[
9\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Equivalently, with \(M=6\) or \(M=9\),
\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon\le
\frac{-(q-p)+\sqrt{(q-p)^2+4(M+1)pq}}{2(M+1)}.
}
\]
In particular, the all-bottom-5-bad alternative forces
\[
\varepsilon\le \frac{2}{15}\approx 0.1334
\]
throughout \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\). If \((w_4,w_5)\) is also mergeable, then \(M=10\), improving the endpoint bound to \((\sqrt{89}-1)/66\approx 0.1278\).

This does not close the desired stability statement, but it shows any all-small-pairs-bad minimal counterexample must be very close to the boundary \(\Pr(D)=q\) and must carry many large positive level-2 coefficients.

---

## 4. Influence and outside-repulsion constraints

Let
\[
I_i=\Pr(A_i)
\]
be the influence of coordinate \(i\). Since
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)\le qI_i,
\]
every bad mergeable partner of \(i\) gives
\[
I_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{q}.
\]
Thus if all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, every one of \(w_1,\dots,w_5\) has
\[
I_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{q}.
\]

Also,
\[
E[(S-p)1_G]=pq\sum_i w_i I_i.
\]
Since \(0\le S-p\le q\) on \(G\) and \(\Pr(G)=p-\varepsilon\),
\[
pq\sum_i w_iI_i\le q(p-\varepsilon),
\]
so
\[
\boxed{\sum_i w_iI_i\le 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{p}.}
\]
Combining with the bottom-5 influence lower bound gives
\[
\boxed{
\frac{\varepsilon}{q}(w_1+\cdots+w_5)
\le 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{p}.
}
\]

There is also a useful covariance-balance obstruction. For fixed \(i\), let \(H_i\) be a set of bad mergeable partners of \(i\). Since
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},R_i)
=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell \operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_\ell),
\]
where \(R_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\), and since \(R_i\in[p-w_i,p)\) on \(A_i\),
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},R_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell\notin H_i\cup\{i\}}w_\ell\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_\ell)
\le
p w_iI_i-\varepsilon\sum_{j\in H_i}w_j.
}
\]
So if many small partners of \(i\) are bad, pivotality of \(i\) must be negatively correlated with a weighted portion of the outside coordinates. This is a concrete structural obstruction any all-bad minimal counterexample must exhibit.

---

## 5. Strong local obstruction: all bottom-5 pairs can be bad in support 10

The stronger local claim “among the bottom 5 there is always a good pair” is false even with \(n=10\).

Take
\[
p\in(0.29,0.31],\qquad
w_1=\cdots=w_5=0.09,\qquad
w_6=\cdots=w_{10}=0.11.
\]
All weights are \(<p\), and the bottom five pairs are mergeable.

For a bottom pair, write
\[
R=0.11K+0.09L,
\]
where \(K\sim\mathrm{Bin}(5,p)\) counts the top weights and \(L\sim\mathrm{Bin}(3,p)\) counts the other bottom weights. The lower slab is
\[
[p-0.18,p-0.09),
\]
and the upper slab is
\[
[p-0.09,p).
\]
On this parameter interval, the lower slab contains exactly
\[
(K,L)=(0,2),(1,1),
\]
while the upper slab contains exactly
\[
(K,L)=(0,3),(1,2),(2,0).
\]
Thus
\[
L_{\rm slab}=18p^2q^6,
\]
and
\[
U_{\rm slab}=10p^2q^6+16p^3q^5.
\]
Hence
\[
L_{\rm slab}-U_{\rm slab}
=8p^2q^5(1-3p)>0
\]
for \(p<1/3\). So every bottom-5 pair is bad.

But this is not a counterexample. Indeed \(S< p\) occurs exactly when
\[
K=0,\ L\le 3;\qquad
K=1,\ L\le 2;\qquad
K=2,\ L=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu
=
q^5(1-5p^4q-p^5)
+5pq^4(q^5+5pq^4+10p^2q^3)
+10p^2q^8.
\]
For \(p\in(0.29,0.31]\), a crude interval bound gives
\[
\mu<0.606<0.69\le q.
\]
At \(p=0.3\), for example,
\[
\mu\approx 0.5162<0.7=q.
\]
So all bottom-5 pairs can be bad simultaneously, but the resulting instance is far from being a counterexample.

This shows why the counterexample hypothesis \(\mu>q\), plus minimality, is essential.

---
