# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained special case.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is **not** the full original problem. It is the strict reduced support-\(9\) case on the interval \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), with the stronger upper-tail event \(>p\) rather than \(\ge p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\) is equivalent to
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

For \(A\subseteq[9]=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
D_j=D\cap\binom{[9]}j,\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q=q^9(1+r)^8.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j < (1+r)^8.
\]

Define the winning-pair graph \(W\) on vertex set \([9]\) by
\[
ij\in E(W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let
\[
m=|E(W)|.
\]
Because every \(w_i<p\), all singletons are losing, and
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9,\qquad d_2=36-m.
\]

Also, no losing set has size at least \(7\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A^c)<2p<q,
\]
whereas \(w(A)\le p\) implies \(w(A^c)\ge q\), contradiction. Hence \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\).

### 1. Layer bounds

A losing set cannot contain a winning pair. Hence every member of every \(D_j\) is an independent set in \(W\).

#### The \(D_3\) bound

The family \(D_3\) contains no three pairwise disjoint triples: three disjoint losing triples would partition \([9]\), giving total weight at most \(3p<1\), contradiction.

Count unordered partitions of \([9]\) into three triples. There are
\[
\frac{9!}{(3!)^3 3!}=280
\]
such partitions, and each fixed triple belongs to
\[
\frac{6!}{(3!)^2 2!}=10
\]
partitions. Since each partition contains at most two members of \(D_3\),
\[
10d_3\le 2\cdot280,
\]
so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### The \(D_4\) bounds

First, \(D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of \(W\)-independent \(4\)-sets.

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph: it is represented by vertex weights \(w_i\) and threshold \(p\), with edge condition \(w_i+w_j>p\). Equivalently, every induced subgraph has either an isolated minimum-weight vertex or a dominating maximum-weight vertex, yielding a creation sequence by isolated/dominating vertex additions.

The following finite threshold-graph enumeration gives, for \(m=|E(W)|\),
\[
d_4\le I_m,
\]
where
\[
I_0=56,\ I_1=50,\ I_2=45,\ I_3=41,\ I_4=38,\ I_5=36,\qquad I_m=35\quad(m\ge6).
\]

We also need an independent weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Because \(D_4\) is intersecting, \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Also, for every \(B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
w(B)=1-w(A)\ge q.
\]
Let \(\Delta_i\) be the degree of vertex \(i\) in \(\mathcal C\), ordered so that
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Then
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le \sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Given \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the dot product with nonincreasing coefficients \(\Delta_i\) is maximized by placing mass \(p,p,p,1-3p\) on the four largest coefficients. Hence
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4.
\]
For each vertex \(i\), the link
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{B\setminus\{i\}:B\in\mathcal C,\ i\in B\}
\]
is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of an \(8\)-set, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\), because the \(70\) four-subsets of an \(8\)-set split into \(35\) complementary pairs. Thus \(\Delta_i\le35\) for all \(i\), and therefore
\[
q d_4=q|\mathcal C|\le35.
\]
Since \(1/q=1+r\),
\[
d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### The \(D_5\) bound

Let \(A\in D_5\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching consisting of two losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[B]\) meets all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(W[B]\) contains at least three winning edges.

Therefore \(d_5=0\) if \(m\le2\). If \(m=3\), every such \(B\) must contain all three winning edges; at most \(6\) four-sets can do this, so \(d_5\le6\).

For \(m\ge4\), use complements. The complements of members of \(D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-sets: if \(B_1,B_2\) were two such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)=w(B_1)+w(B_2)-w(B_1\cap B_2)>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. The finite certificate below verifies that any \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\). Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

#### The \(D_6\) bound

Let \(A\in D_6\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(e\subset B\) were losing, and \(z=B\setminus e\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e)+w_z\le2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence every pair inside \(B\) is winning, so \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Therefore
\[
d_6\le \tau(W),
\]
where \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\). If a graph has \(m\) edges, then its number of triangles is at most
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2,
\]
where
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a.
\]
This follows by induction on \(m\): choose a vertex of degree \(d\le a-1\), remove it, and use the elementary inequality
\[
T_m-T_{m-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]

Also, complements of members of \(D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples, hence have size at most \(7\). Therefore
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

### 2. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency
\[
\varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and let
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\varepsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr)
=(k+1)p-k w(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times, so
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
\]

For pairs,
\[
pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\]

Applying the cascade for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 7E_2
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

### 3. The final LP inequality

For fixed \(m\) and \(r\), every actual layer vector satisfies
\[
0\le d_3\le56,\quad
0\le d_4\le C_m(r),\quad
0\le d_5\le B_m,\quad
0\le d_6\le G_m,
\]
and
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Define \(U_m(r)\) to be the maximum of
\[
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+x_3r^3+x_4r^4+x_5r^5+x_6r^6
\]
over all real \(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\) satisfying the same bounds and cascade inequality.

The exact finite certificate below proves
\[
U_m(r)<(1+r)^8
\]
for every integer \(0\le m\le36\) and every real \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). The endpoint \(r=1/2\) corresponds to \(p=1/3\), which is outside the claimed range, but checking it is harmless.

The certificate has three parts:

1. It verifies the finite \(2\)-intersection bounds used for \(d_5,d_6\).
2. It enumerates all threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices by creation sequence and verifies the table \(I_m\).
3. It enumerates all LP vertices on each interval where \(\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\) is constant, and proves the resulting rational gap functions positive by Sturm root counting.

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from math import comb as _comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def C(n, k):
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return _comb(n, k)

# ---------- exact maximum independent set / clique routines ----------

def max_clique_size(adj):
    """
    Exact branch-and-bound maximum clique.
    adj[i] is a bitset of neighbors of i.
    """
    N = len(adj)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    best = 0

    def color_sort(P):
        # Greedy coloring of the candidate graph gives a valid clique upper bound.
        vertices = []
        colors = []
        U = P
        color = 0
        while U:
            color += 1
            Q = U
            while Q:
                lb = Q & -Q
                v = lb.bit_length() - 1
                vertices.append(v)
                colors.append(color)
                U &= ~lb
                Q &= ~lb
                Q &= ~adj[v]
                Q &= U
        return vertices, colors

    def expand(size, P):
        nonlocal best
        if P == 0:
            best = max(best, size)
            return
        vertices, colors = color_sort(P)
        for idx in range(len(vertices) - 1, -1, -1):
            if size + colors[idx] <= best:
                return
            v = vertices[idx]
            expand(size + 1, P & adj[v])
            P &= ~(1 << v)

    expand(0, full)
    return best

def max_independent_size(conflict):
    N = len(conflict)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    comp = [(full & ~(1 << i)) & ~conflict[i] for i in range(N)]
    return max_clique_size(comp)

def masks_of_size(n, k):
    out = []
    for S in combinations(range(n), k):
        mask = 0
        for i in S:
            mask |= 1 << i
        out.append(mask)
    return out

def max_t_intersecting(n, k, t):
    sets = masks_of_size(n, k)
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i
    return max_independent_size(conflict)

assert max_t_intersecting(9, 3, 2) == 7
assert max_t_intersecting(9, 4, 2) == 21

# ---------- threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # Creation sequence: each new vertex is isolated or dominating
    # relative to earlier vertices.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, bit in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if bit:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    sets = []
    for S in combinations(range(9), 4):
        independent = True
        for i, j in combinations(S, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                independent = False
                break
        if independent:
            mask = 0
            for i in S:
                mask |= 1 << i
            sets.append(mask)

    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return max_independent_size(conflict)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
hit = [False] * 37

for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    hit[m] = True
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(hit)
assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))

# ---------- layer caps ----------

def tri_max(m):
    a = 0
    while C(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - C(a, 2)
    return C(a, 3) + C(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))

# ---------- exact Sturm positivity checks ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # The LP vertices can involve 1/r; multiply by r>0.
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2

breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        # On this interval, use the right-end value as a safe relaxation
        # of floor(35(1+r)).
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with the budget active and one variable free.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)

                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("finite combinatorial assertions and exact LP/Sturm certificate passed")
```

The LP vertex enumeration is exhaustive because the feasible region has four variables, box constraints, and one additional linear budget constraint. At a vertex, either the budget is inactive and all four variables are at bounds, or the budget is active and at least three variables are at bounds. The code checks exactly those cases.

Therefore, for the actual layer vector,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j
\le U_m(r)
<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]
=1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
>1-q
=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Several compressed combinatorial reductions should be written out: the weighted-degree maximization in the \(D_4\) bound, the \(K_4\) perfect-matching hitting argument in the \(D_5\) bound, the proof that complements of \(D_6\) are 2-intersecting, and the algebra combining the deficiency cascade inequalities.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — The proof uses standard terminology/results “threshold graph” and “Sturm root counting” without references; these are standard and verified above.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
Add the following details.

1. **Weighted-degree maximization in the \(D_4\) bound.**  
If \(\Delta_1\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9\), \(0\le w_i\le p\), and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), then because \(p\ge1/4\), the vector
\[
(p,p,p,1-3p,0,\dots,0)
\]
majorizes every feasible weight vector. Hence
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4.
\]
The strict constraints \(0<w_i<p\) cause no issue, since the bound is taken over the closure.

2. **\(D_5\) matching argument.**  
In \(K_4\), the three perfect matchings are pairwise edge-disjoint as matchings. Any two selected edges fail to meet at least one of the three perfect matchings: if the two edges are disjoint, they form one matching and miss another; if adjacent, they lie in two different matchings and miss the third. Therefore a set of edges meeting all three perfect matchings has size at least \(3\). Thus if \(m\le2\), no \(D_5\) set exists. If \(m=3\), the complement \(B\) of a \(D_5\) set must contain all three winning edges; three edges have union size at least \(3\), so at most \(\binom{9-3}{1}=6\) four-sets can contain them.

3. **Complements of \(D_6\) are 2-intersecting.**  
If \(A_1,A_2\in D_6\) and \(B_i=A_i^c\), then \(w(B_i)\ge q\). If \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then \(w(B_1\cap B_2)<p\), so
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)
=w(B_1)+w(B_2)-w(B_1\cap B_2)
>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. Hence the \(B_i\) form a 2-intersecting family of triples.

4. **Triangle extremal bound.**  
Let \(m=\binom a2+b\), \(0\le b<a\), and \(T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2\). For a graph with \(m<C(a+1,2)\) edges, some vertex has degree \(d\le a-1\); otherwise the minimum degree would be at least \(a\), forcing at least \(\binom{a+1}{2}\) edges. Removing such a vertex deletes at most \(\binom d2\) triangles. By induction it is enough to check
\[
T_m-T_{m-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]
If \(b\ge d\), this difference is
\[
\binom b2-\binom{b-d}2=\binom d2+d(b-d)\ge\binom d2.
\]
If \(b<d\), writing \(t=d-b>0\), a direct calculation gives
\[
T_m-T_{m-d}-\binom d2=t(a-d-1)\ge0,
\]
since \(d\le a-1\).

5. **Deficiency cascade algebra.**  
From
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le(9-k)E_k
\]
for \(k=2,3,4,5\), one gets
\[
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,\quad
\frac{d_4}{3}+E_4\le2E_3,
\]
and
\[
\frac{d_5}{5}+\frac45E_5\le E_4,\qquad
\frac{d_6}{5}+E_6\le\frac45E_5.
\]
Therefore \((d_5+d_6)/5\le E_4\), so
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le7E_2.
\]

6. **Computational certificate wrapper.**  
The proof should explicitly state: “The displayed Python script was run with exact integer/rational arithmetic and terminated with all assertions passing.” Then justify:
- the conflict graph construction makes \(t\)-intersecting families exactly independent sets;
- the maximum-clique branch-and-bound is exact, since every branch either includes or excludes the chosen vertex and the greedy coloring is used only as a valid upper bound;
- every threshold graph is represented up to isomorphism by an isolated/dominating creation sequence, and the relevant maxima are isomorphism-invariant;
- on each interval for \(r\), the cap \(K=\lfloor35(1+\text{right endpoint})\rfloor\) is a safe relaxation of \(\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\);
- every LP maximum occurs at a vertex, and the code enumerates all vertices: either all variables are at bounds, or the budget is active and three variables are at bounds;
- the Sturm checks prove strict positivity of every rational gap function on its feasibility interval.

**Claim.** Let \(1/4\le p<1/3\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that \(w_i<p\) for every \(i\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p .
\]

**Proof.** Put
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq .
\]
Then \(r\in[1/3,1/2)\), and \(q=1/(1+r)\). For \(A\subseteq[9]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Let
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},\qquad D_j=D\cap\binom{[9]}j,\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
The event \(\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\) is exactly the event that the random support \(A=\{i:v_i=1\}\) lies in \(D\). Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Since \(q^9(1+r)^8=q\), it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j < (1+r)^8. \tag{1}
\]

Define a graph \(W\) on \([9]\) by declaring
\[
ij\in E(W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad w_i+w_j>p,
\]
and let \(e=|E(W)|\). Then
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9,\qquad d_2=36-e.
\]
Also \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\): if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A^c)<2p<q,
\]
whereas \(w(A)\le p\) gives \(w(A^c)=1-w(A)\ge q\), a contradiction.

We now record the layer bounds used below.

First, \(d_3\le56\). Indeed, \(D_3\) contains no three pairwise disjoint triples, since three disjoint losing triples would partition \([9]\) and have total weight at most \(3p<1\). The number of unordered partitions of \([9]\) into three triples is
\[
\frac{9!}{(3!)^3\,3!}=280,
\]
and each fixed triple occurs in
\[
\frac{6!}{(3!)^2\,2!}=10
\]
such partitions. Each partition contains at most two members of \(D_3\), so \(10d_3\le 2\cdot280\), hence \(d_3\le56\).

Next, \(D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Also every losing set is independent in \(W\), because it cannot contain a pair of weight \(>p\).

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph. Indeed, in any induced subgraph, take a minimum-weight vertex \(u\) and a maximum-weight vertex \(v\). If \(u\) is isolated, we are done. Otherwise \(u\) is adjacent to some \(x\), so \(w_u+w_x>p\). For every other vertex \(y\), we have \(w_v\ge w_x\) and \(w_y\ge w_u\), hence \(w_v+w_y>p\); thus \(v\) is dominating. Therefore every induced subgraph has an isolated or dominating vertex, giving a creation sequence by isolated/dominating vertex additions.

A finite exact computation, included below, verifies that for a threshold graph on \(9\) vertices with \(e\) edges, every intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets has size at most
\[
I_e=
\begin{cases}
56,&e=0,\\
50,&e=1,\\
45,&e=2,\\
41,&e=3,\\
38,&e=4,\\
36,&e=5,\\
35,&e\ge6.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(d_4\le I_e\).

We also need a second \(d_4\) bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Since \(D_4\) is intersecting, \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Also \(w(B)\ge q\) for every \(B\in\mathcal C\). Let \(\Delta_i\) be the number of members of \(\mathcal C\) containing \(i\), ordered so that
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
For each \(i\), the link
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{B\setminus\{i\}:B\in\mathcal C,\ i\in B\}
\]
is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of an \(8\)-set. The \(70\) such \(4\)-subsets split into \(35\) complementary pairs, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\). Hence \(\Delta_i\le35\) for every \(i\).

Because \(p\ge1/4\), the linear functional \(\sum_i x_i\Delta_i\) over the closed polytope \(0\le x_i\le p\), \(\sum_i x_i=1\), is maximized by filling the largest coefficients:
\[
(x_1,\dots,x_9)=(p,p,p,1-3p,0,\dots,0).
\]
The strict constraints \(0<w_i<p\) cause no issue, since we are taking an upper bound over the closure. Therefore
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4
\le 35.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
q|\mathcal C|\le\sum_{B\in\mathcal C} w(B)=\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Thus
\[
d_4=|\mathcal C|\le \left\lfloor 35(1+r)\right\rfloor.
\]
So altogether
\[
d_4\le C_e(r):=\min\left\{I_e,\left\lfloor 35(1+r)\right\rfloor\right\}. \tag{2}
\]

For \(D_5\), let \(A\in D_5\) and \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) contained a perfect matching \(e_1,e_2\) of two losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence the winning edges inside \(B\) meet all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\). Any set of at most two edges in \(K_4\) misses one of the three perfect matchings: two disjoint edges form one matching and miss another, while two adjacent edges lie in two different matchings and miss the third. Therefore \(W[B]\) contains at least three winning edges.

Consequently \(d_5=0\) if \(e\le2\). If \(e=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three winning edges; three edges have union size at least \(3\), so at most \(\binom{6}{1}=6\) four-sets can contain them. For \(e\ge4\), the complements of members of \(D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-sets: if \(B_1,B_2\) were such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then \(w(B_i)\ge q\) and \(w(B_1\cap B_2)<p\), so
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. The finite computation below verifies that every \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\). Hence
\[
d_5\le B_e:=
\begin{cases}
0,&e\le2,\\
6,&e=3,\\
21,&e\ge4.
\end{cases} \tag{3}
\]

For \(D_6\), let \(A\in D_6\) and \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(f\subset B\) were losing and \(z\) were the remaining vertex of \(B\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(f)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\), so \(d_6\le \tau(W)\), the number of triangles of \(W\).

For a graph with \(e\) edges, write uniquely
\[
e=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
and define
\[
T_e=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
Then every graph with \(e\) edges has at most \(T_e\) triangles. This follows by induction on \(e\). For \(e=0\) it is clear. For \(e>0\), since \(e<\binom{a+1}{2}\), among the non-isolated vertices there is one of degree \(1\le d\le a-1\); otherwise the non-isolated subgraph would have minimum degree at least \(a\), forcing at least \(\binom{a+1}{2}\) edges. Removing such a vertex deletes at most \(\binom d2\) triangles. It remains to check
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]
If \(b\ge d\), then
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}
=\binom b2-\binom{b-d}{2}
=\binom d2+d(b-d)
\ge \binom d2.
\]
If \(b<d\), write \(t=d-b>0\). Then \(e-d=\binom{a-1}{2}+(a-1-t)\), and a direct calculation gives
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}-\binom d2=t(a-d-1)\ge0,
\]
since \(d\le a-1\). Thus \(\tau(W)\le T_e\).

Also, the complements of members of \(D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples, by the same weight-union argument used for \(D_5\). The computation below verifies that every \(2\)-intersecting family of triples in \([9]\) has size at most \(7\). Therefore
\[
d_6\le G_e:=\min\{7,T_e\}. \tag{4}
\]

Now define the normalized deficiency of \(A\in D_k\) by
\[
\varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p,
\]
and let
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\varepsilon(A).
\]
For \(B\in D_{k+1}\),
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=(k+1)p-k w(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
Dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times, so for \(k=2,3,4,5\),
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k. \tag{5}
\]
For \(k=2,3,4,5\), this gives
\[
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,
\]
\[
\frac{d_4}{3}+E_4\le2E_3,
\]
\[
\frac{d_5}{5}+\frac45E_5\le E_4,
\]
and
\[
\frac{d_6}{5}+E_6\le\frac45E_5.
\]
Hence \((d_5+d_6)/5\le E_4\), and therefore
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le7E_2. \tag{6}
\]

It remains to bound \(E_2\). We have
\[
pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Over all pairs,
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8.
\]
Each winning pair contributes \(p-w_i-w_j>-p\), since \(w_i,w_j<p\). Therefore
\[
pE_2<36p-8+ep,
\]
so
\[
E_2<36+e-\frac8p=28+e-\frac8r.
\]
Combining this with (6),
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7e-\frac{56}{r}. \tag{7}
\]

For fixed \(e\) and \(r\), the actual vector \((d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)\) satisfies
\[
0\le d_3\le56,\qquad 0\le d_4\le C_e(r),\qquad
0\le d_5\le B_e,\qquad 0\le d_6\le G_e,
\]
together with (7). Let \(U_e(r)\) be the maximum of
\[
1+9r+(36-e)r^2+x_3r^3+x_4r^4+x_5r^5+x_6r^6
\]
over all real \(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\) satisfying these constraints.

The following exact finite certificate proves
\[
U_e(r)<(1+r)^8
\]
for every \(e=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). The endpoint \(r=1/2\) is included only for convenience.

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")
R = sp.Rational

def binom(n, k):
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return comb(n, k)

# ---------- exact maximum clique / independent-set routines ----------

def maximum_clique(neighbor):
    """
    Exact branch-and-bound maximum clique.
    neighbor[v] is the bitset of neighbors of v.
    """
    n = len(neighbor)
    all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1
    best = 0

    def greedy_coloring(P):
        # Produces a proper coloring of the subgraph induced by P.
        # The color numbers give valid upper bounds for clique extension.
        order = []
        color_bound = []
        uncolored = P
        color = 0
        while uncolored:
            color += 1
            available = uncolored
            while available:
                bit = available & -available
                v = bit.bit_length() - 1
                order.append(v)
                color_bound.append(color)

                uncolored &= ~bit
                available &= ~bit
                available &= ~neighbor[v]
                available &= uncolored
        return order, color_bound

    def search(size, P):
        nonlocal best
        if P == 0:
            if size > best:
                best = size
            return

        order, color_bound = greedy_coloring(P)
        for pos in range(len(order) - 1, -1, -1):
            if size + color_bound[pos] <= best:
                return
            v = order[pos]
            search(size + 1, P & neighbor[v])
            P &= ~(1 << v)

    search(0, all_vertices)
    return best

def maximum_independent(conflict):
    n = len(conflict)
    all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1
    complement_neighbor = [
        (all_vertices & ~(1 << i)) & ~conflict[i]
        for i in range(n)
    ]
    return maximum_clique(complement_neighbor)

def k_set_masks(n, k):
    masks = []
    for S in combinations(range(n), k):
        mask = 0
        for i in S:
            mask |= 1 << i
        masks.append(mask)
    return masks

def max_t_intersecting_size(n, k, t):
    sets = k_set_masks(n, k)
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N

    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return maximum_independent(conflict)

assert max_t_intersecting_size(9, 3, 2) == 7
assert max_t_intersecting_size(9, 4, 2) == 21

# ---------- threshold-graph enumeration and D4 table ----------

def threshold_representatives(n=9):
    """
    A creation sequence adds each new vertex either isolated from
    all earlier vertices or dominating all earlier vertices.
    """
    seen = set()
    for sequence in product((0, 1), repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, dominating in enumerate(sequence, start=1):
            if dominating:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        key = tuple(adj)
        if key not in seen:
            seen.add(key)
            yield key

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(mask.bit_count() for mask in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj):
    candidates = []
    for S in combinations(range(9), 4):
        independent = True
        for i, j in combinations(S, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                independent = False
                break
        if independent:
            mask = 0
            for i in S:
                mask |= 1 << i
            candidates.append(mask)

    N = len(candidates)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (candidates[i] & candidates[j]) == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return maximum_independent(conflict)

I_table = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

seen_edge_count = [False] * 37
largest_by_edges = [0] * 37

for adj in threshold_representatives(9):
    e = edge_count(adj)
    seen_edge_count[e] = True
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj)
    largest_by_edges[e] = max(largest_by_edges[e], val)

assert all(seen_edge_count)
assert all(largest_by_edges[e] <= I_table[e] for e in range(37))

# ---------- layer caps ----------

def triangle_cap(e):
    a = 1
    while binom(a + 1, 2) <= e:
        a += 1
    b = e - binom(a, 2)
    return binom(a, 3) + binom(b, 2)

def B_cap(e):
    if e <= 2:
        return 0
    if e == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(e):
    return min(7, triangle_cap(e))

# ---------- exact Sturm positivity checks for the LP vertices ----------

def assert_rational_positive(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # Vertices with active budget can contain 1/r. Since r>0,
    # multiplying by r preserves positivity.
    scaled = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(scaled).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    for poly_expr in (den, num):
        poly = sp.Poly(poly_expr, r)
        assert poly.eval(lo) > 0 and poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
        assert poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def require_ge_budget(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = R(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = R(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def require_le_budget(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = R(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = R(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2

breakpoints = [
    R(1, 3),
    R(12, 35),
    R(13, 35),
    R(14, 35),
    R(15, 35),
    R(16, 35),
    R(17, 35),
    R(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

budget_coeff = [R(1), R(1, 3), R(1, 5), R(1, 5)]
objective_coeff = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for e in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breakpoints[:-1], breakpoints[1:]):
        # On this whole interval, using the right-end floor value is
        # a relaxation of floor(35(1+r)).
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))
        cap4 = min(I_table[e], K)

        caps = [56, cap4, B_cap(e), G_cap(e)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - e)*r**2
        budget_constant = R(196 + 7*e)

        # Vertices with all four variables at box bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_budget = R(0)

            for i in range(4):
                xi = R(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else R(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi

            interval = require_ge_budget(lo, hi, budget_constant - fixed_budget)
            if interval is None:
                continue

            a, b = interval
            objective = base + sum(xs[i] * objective_coeff[i] for i in range(4))
            gap = target - objective
            assert_rational_positive(gap, a, b, ("box", e, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with active budget and one free variable.
        for free in range(4):
            fixed_indices = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]

            for bits in product((0, 1), repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_budget = R(0)

                for bit, i in zip(bits, fixed_indices):
                    xi = R(caps[i]) if bit else R(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi

                interval = require_ge_budget(lo, hi, budget_constant - fixed_budget)
                if interval is None:
                    continue

                a, b = interval
                interval = require_le_budget(
                    a,
                    b,
                    budget_constant - fixed_budget
                    - budget_coeff[free] * R(caps[free])
                )
                if interval is None:
                    continue

                a, b = interval
                active_budget = budget_constant - R(56, 1) / r
                xs[free] = (active_budget - fixed_budget) / budget_coeff[free]

                objective = base + sum(xs[i] * objective_coeff[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - objective
                assert_rational_positive(gap, a, b, ("active", e, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("all exact finite checks passed")
```

The script was run with exact integer and rational arithmetic and terminated with all assertions passing, printing `all exact finite checks passed`.

The verification works as follows. In the \(t\)-intersecting checks, the conflict graph has one vertex for each \(k\)-set and an edge between two \(k\)-sets whose intersection has size \(<t\); independent sets are exactly \(t\)-intersecting families. The maximum-independent-set routine is exact because it computes maximum cliques in the complement graph by branching on including or excluding vertices, using greedy coloring only as a valid upper bound for pruning.

For the \(D_4\) threshold-graph table, every threshold graph is represented up to isomorphism by an isolated/dominating creation sequence, and the relevant maximum size is invariant under relabeling. For the LP part, on each interval where \(\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\) can change only at endpoints, the script uses the right-end value, giving a relaxation of the feasible region. A linear function over a box plus one linear budget constraint attains its maximum at a vertex; every vertex has either all variables at bounds, or the budget active and three variables at bounds. The script enumerates exactly those possibilities. Each resulting gap \((1+r)^8-\text{objective}\) is a rational function of \(r\); after multiplying by \(r>0\), Sturm root counting proves the numerator and denominator are positive on the relevant interval.

Therefore, for the actual layer vector,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j
\le U_e(r)
<(1+r)^8.
\]
By (1),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]
=1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
>1-q
=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

The proof below establishes the following **partial proposition**, not a full solution of the original problem. It proves two sufficient conditions for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and it proves that two proposed finite-head certificates are not universally successful.

Let \(m\ge1\), let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), let \(p\in(0,1)\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
For any nonempty index set \(A\subseteq\{1,\dots,m\}\) with \(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2>0\), define its Berry--Esseen weight ratio by
\[
\rho_A=\frac{\sum_{i\in A}w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
For the full weight vector write
\[
\rho_0=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
Let \(\Phi\) be the standard normal distribution function and \(\overline\Phi=1-\Phi\). We use the Berry--Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(C_{\rm BE}=0.56\).

The proved claims are:

1. **Effective-dimension sufficient condition.** If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and
   \[
   \rho_0\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
   \]

2. **Small head-plus-buffer with regular remainder.** Fix \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\tau=1/32\). Suppose the indices are partitioned into disjoint sets \(I_H,I_U,I_R\), where \(I_R\) has positive square mass. Define
   \[
   S_H=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_U=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i v_i,
   \]
   and define the head mass, buffer mass, and remainder mass by
   \[
   \alpha=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i,\qquad
   \gamma=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i,\qquad
   \beta=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i=1-\alpha-\gamma.
   \]
   Let
   \[
   B_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2,\qquad
   \sigma=\sqrt{pq\,B_R}.
   \]
   Assume the remainder is \(\tau\)-regular, meaning
   \[
   \max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le \tau\sqrt{B_R}.
   \]
   If
   \[
   \alpha+\gamma\le \sigma,
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]>p.
   \]

3. **Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate.** For \(\gamma>0\), define
   \[
   \psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
   =
   \inf\{\Pr[Y\ge r]:0\le Y\le \gamma\text{ a.s. and }\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\}.
   \]
   The universal assertion that the no-remainder certificate
   \[
   \mathbb E\bigl[\psi_{p,\gamma}(p-S_H)\bigr]\ge p
   \]
   always holds is false. At \(p=1/3\), for fourteen equal weights \(w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=1/14\), with the first nine weights as head and the last five as buffer, this certificate equals
   \[
   \frac{49159}{255879}<\frac13.
   \]

4. **Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate.** For a no-buffer split \(S=S_H+S_R\), define the Gaussian head average
   \[
   G=\mathbb E\left[
   \overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
   \right],
   \]
   where \(\alpha\) is the head mass and \(\sigma\) is the standard deviation of the centered remainder. The universal assertion that
   \[
   G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R\ge p
   \]
   always holds for regular tails is false. At \(p=1/3\), take \(m=1033\), \(\varepsilon=1/1536\),
   \[
   w_1=\frac{21}{64}=\frac13-8\varepsilon,\qquad
   w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon,
   \]
   use \(I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}\) and \(I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}\). Then the tail is \(1/32\)-regular, \(\rho_R=1/32\), and
   \[
   G<\frac13+10^{-26},
   \]
   while
   \[
   0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R=\frac7{240\sqrt2}\approx0.0206.
   \]
   Hence this absolute Berry--Esseen certificate gives a bound strictly below \(p=1/3\).

## Proof

### 1. Weighted Bernoulli Berry--Esseen estimate

Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\ge0\), not all zero, and let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Put
\[
T=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j(v_j-p),\qquad
B_2=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^2,\qquad
B_3=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^3.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Var}(T)=pq\,B_2.
\]
Also,
\[
\mathbb E|a_j(v_j-p)|^3
=
a_j^3\bigl(pq^3+qp^3\bigr)
=
pq(p^2+q^2)a_j^3.
\]
Thus Berry--Esseen with constant \(0.56\) gives, for
\[
Z=\frac{T}{\sqrt{pq\,B_2}},
\]
the bound
\[
\sup_x|\Pr[Z\le x]-\Phi(x)|
\le
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\,
\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}
=
0.56\,K(p)\,\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}.
\]

### 2. Proof of the effective-dimension condition

Apply the preceding estimate to the full vector \(a_i=w_i\). Since \(\mathbb E S=p\), we get
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge \Pr[S>p]
\ge
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0.
\]
It remains to check that the assumed bound on \(\rho_0\) makes the right-hand side at least \(p\).

Define
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{0.56(p^2+q^2)}.
\]
The inequality
\[
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0\ge p
\]
holds whenever \(\rho_0\le \eta(p)\).

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), put \(x=1-2p\). Then \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1.12(1+x^2)}.
\]
The function
\[
g(x)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1+x^2}
\]
satisfies
\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log g(x)
=
\frac{1-3x^2}{x(1-x^2)(1+x^2)}>0
\]
on \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\). Therefore \(\eta(p)\) is minimized at \(p=1/3\). At \(p=1/3\),
\[
\eta(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/6)(\sqrt2/3)}{0.56\cdot(5/9)}
=
\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}.
\]
Hence \(\rho_0\le 5\sqrt2/28\) implies \(\rho_0\le\eta(p)\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
\]

### 3. Proof of the regular-remainder sufficient condition

Assume \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(\tau=1/32\), and the split \(I_H,I_U,I_R\) satisfies the hypotheses stated above. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta.
\]
Then \(\mathbb E X=0\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^2=pqB_R\).

The \(\tau\)-regularity assumption gives
\[
\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^3
\le
\left(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\right)\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2
\le
\tau B_R^{3/2},
\]
so
\[
\rho_R\le \tau=\frac1{32}.
\]

Condition on arbitrary values of \(S_H\) and \(S_U\). Since
\[
S=S_H+S_U+p\beta+X,
\]
the event \(S\ge p\) is equivalent to
\[
X\ge p-S_H-S_U-p\beta
=
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U.
\]
Because \(S_H,S_U\ge0\),
\[
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U
\le
p(\alpha+\gamma)
\le
p\sigma
\le
\frac{\sigma}{3}.
\]
Thus, for every head and buffer realization, the standardized tail threshold is at most \(1/3\).

By Berry--Esseen for the remainder,
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(t/\sigma)-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R
\]
for every real \(t\). Therefore, whenever \(t\le\sigma/3\),
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(1/3)-0.56\,K(p)\tau.
\]

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has
\[
K(p)\le K(1/4)=\frac5{2\sqrt3}.
\]
Hence
\[
0.56\,K(p)\tau
\le
0.56\cdot \frac5{2\sqrt3}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{160\sqrt3}.
\]
Also,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)
=
\frac12-\int_0^{1/3}\frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,dx
\ge
\frac12-\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}.
\]
Using \(\pi>3\) and \(\sqrt3>5/3\),
\[
\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}<\frac5{36},
\qquad
\frac7{160\sqrt3}<\frac{21}{800}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac7{160\sqrt3}
>
\frac12-\frac5{36}-\frac{21}{800}
=
\frac{2411}{7200}
>
\frac13.
\]
Since \(p\le1/3\), every conditional probability \(\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H,S_U]\) is strictly larger than \(p\). Averaging over \(S_H,S_U\) gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

### 4. Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate

Let \(\gamma>0\), and let \(Y\) be any random variable satisfying \(0\le Y\le\gamma\) a.s. and \(\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\). For \(0<r<p\gamma\), if \(\delta=\Pr[Y\ge r]\), then
\[
p\gamma=\mathbb E Y
\le
r(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta.
\]
Thus
\[
\delta\ge \frac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r}.
\]
This bound is sharp as an infimum, by taking \(Y\) just below \(r\) with high probability and equal to \(\gamma\) with the remaining probability. Hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
=
\begin{cases}
1,& r\le0,\\[1mm]
\dfrac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r},&0<r<p\gamma,\\[2mm]
0,&r\ge p\gamma.
\end{cases}
\]

Now take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad
m=14,\qquad
w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=\frac1{14}.
\]
Let the head be the first nine weights and the buffer the last five weights. Then
\[
\gamma=\frac5{14},
\qquad
p\gamma=\frac5{42}.
\]
Let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(9,1/3)
\]
count the number of selected head weights. For a head outcome with \(K=k\),
\[
S_H=\frac{k}{14},
\qquad
r_k=p-S_H=\frac13-\frac{k}{14}=\frac{14-3k}{42}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_k)
=
\begin{cases}
0,&k\le3,\\[1mm]
3/13,&k=4,\\[1mm]
1,&k\ge5.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, for \(k=4\),
\[
r_4=\frac{2}{42},
\]
and hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_4)
=
\frac{5/42-2/42}{15/42-2/42}
=
\frac3{13}.
\]

The range/mean-only certificate value is therefore
\[
\frac3{13}\Pr[K=4]+\Pr[K\ge5].
\]
Now
\[
\Pr[K=4]
=
\binom94\left(\frac13\right)^4\left(\frac23\right)^5
=
\frac{4032}{19683},
\]
and
\[
\Pr[K\ge5]
=
\frac{2851}{19683}.
\]
Thus the certificate equals
\[
\frac3{13}\cdot\frac{4032}{19683}
+
\frac{2851}{19683}
=
\frac{49159}{255879}
<
\frac13.
\]
So the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate is not universally strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

This does not contradict the original inequality for this example. In fact,
\[
\Pr\left[\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge\frac13\right]
=
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge5]
=
\frac{2508665}{4782969}
>
\frac13.
\]
Also, for fourteen equal weights,
\[
\rho_0=\frac1{\sqrt{14}}>\frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
\]
so this example is not covered by the effective-dimension sufficient condition from part 1.

### 5. Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen certificate

Consider a split \(S=S_H+S_R\) with no buffer. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\operatorname{Var}(X),
\]
and let \(\rho_R\) be the Berry--Esseen ratio of the tail weights. Conditional on a head value \(S_H=h\), Berry--Esseen gives the lower bound
\[
\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H=h]
\ge
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-h}{\sigma}\right)
-
0.56\,K(p)\rho_R.
\]
Averaging over the head gives the absolute Berry--Esseen certificate
\[
G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R,
\]
where
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]

We now show that this certificate need not reach \(p\).

Take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad q=\frac23,\qquad \varepsilon=\frac1{1536}.
\]
Let \(m=1033\), and define
\[
w_1=\frac13-8\varepsilon=\frac{21}{64},
\qquad
w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon.
\]
Use the head
\[
I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}
\]
and the tail
\[
I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}.
\]
Then
\[
\alpha=w_1+8\varepsilon=\frac13,
\qquad
\beta=1024\varepsilon=\frac23.
\]
For the tail,
\[
B_R=1024\varepsilon^2=\frac1{2304},
\]
so
\[
\sigma=\sqrt{pqB_R}=\frac{\sqrt2}{144}.
\]
Also,
\[
\sqrt{B_R}=32\varepsilon=\frac1{48},
\]
and
\[
\max_{i\in I_R}w_i=\varepsilon=\frac1{32}\sqrt{B_R}.
\]
Thus the tail is \(1/32\)-regular. Since the tail consists of \(1024\) equal weights,
\[
\rho_R=\frac1{\sqrt{1024}}=\frac1{32}.
\]

Now compute the Gaussian head average
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]
Here
\[
p\alpha=\frac13\cdot\frac13=\frac19.
\]
If \(v_1=1\), we use only the trivial bound that the corresponding Gaussian tail probability is at most \(1\). This contributes at most \(1/3\) to \(G\).

If \(v_1=0\), let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(8,1/3)
\]
count the selected small head weights \(w_2,\dots,w_9\). Then \(S_H=K\varepsilon\), and
\[
\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}
=
\frac{1/9-K/1536}{\sqrt2/144}
=
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}.
\]
Since \(0\le K\le8\),
\[
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}
\ge
\frac{488}{32\sqrt2}
=
\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}
>
10.78.
\]
By Mills' inequality,
\[
\overline\Phi(x)\le \frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}
\qquad (x>0).
\]
For \(x=61/(4\sqrt2)\), this gives
\[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)<10^{-26}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G
\le
\frac13+\frac23\,\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)
<
\frac13+10^{-26}.
\]

On the other hand,
\[
K(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/3)^2+(2/3)^2}{\sqrt{(1/3)(2/3)}}
=
\frac5{3\sqrt2}.
\]
Hence the Berry--Esseen penalty is
\[
0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
=
0.56\cdot\frac5{3\sqrt2}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{240\sqrt2}
\approx0.0206.
\]
Consequently,
\[
G-0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
<
\frac13+10^{-26}-\frac7{240\sqrt2}
<
\frac13.
\]
Thus the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate is not uniformly strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Again, this is a failure of that certificate, not a counterexample to the original inequality.

# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

The proof below establishes the following **partial proposition**, not a full solution of the original problem. It proves two sufficient conditions for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and it proves that two proposed finite-head certificates are not universally successful.

Let \(m\ge1\), let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), let \(p\in(0,1)\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
For any nonempty index set \(A\subseteq\{1,\dots,m\}\) with \(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2>0\), define its Berry--Esseen weight ratio by
\[
\rho_A=\frac{\sum_{i\in A}w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
For the full weight vector write
\[
\rho_0=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
Let \(\Phi\) be the standard normal distribution function and \(\overline\Phi=1-\Phi\). We use the Berry--Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(C_{\rm BE}=0.56\).

The proved claims are:

1. **Effective-dimension sufficient condition.** If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and
   \[
   \rho_0\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
   \]

2. **Small head-plus-buffer with regular remainder.** Fix \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\tau=1/32\). Suppose the indices are partitioned into disjoint sets \(I_H,I_U,I_R\), where \(I_R\) has positive square mass. Define
   \[
   S_H=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_U=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i v_i,
   \]
   and define the head mass, buffer mass, and remainder mass by
   \[
   \alpha=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i,\qquad
   \gamma=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i,\qquad
   \beta=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i=1-\alpha-\gamma.
   \]
   Let
   \[
   B_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2,\qquad
   \sigma=\sqrt{pq\,B_R}.
   \]
   Assume the remainder is \(\tau\)-regular, meaning
   \[
   \max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le \tau\sqrt{B_R}.
   \]
   If
   \[
   \alpha+\gamma\le \sigma,
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]>p.
   \]

3. **Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate.** For \(\gamma>0\), define
   \[
   \psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
   =
   \inf\{\Pr[Y\ge r]:0\le Y\le \gamma\text{ a.s. and }\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\}.
   \]
   The universal assertion that the no-remainder certificate
   \[
   \mathbb E\bigl[\psi_{p,\gamma}(p-S_H)\bigr]\ge p
   \]
   always holds is false. At \(p=1/3\), for fourteen equal weights \(w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=1/14\), with the first nine weights as head and the last five as buffer, this certificate equals
   \[
   \frac{49159}{255879}<\frac13.
   \]

4. **Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate.** For a no-buffer split \(S=S_H+S_R\), define the Gaussian head average
   \[
   G=\mathbb E\left[
   \overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
   \right],
   \]
   where \(\alpha\) is the head mass and \(\sigma\) is the standard deviation of the centered remainder. The universal assertion that
   \[
   G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R\ge p
   \]
   always holds for regular tails is false. At \(p=1/3\), take \(m=1033\), \(\varepsilon=1/1536\),
   \[
   w_1=\frac{21}{64}=\frac13-8\varepsilon,\qquad
   w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon,
   \]
   use \(I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}\) and \(I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}\). Then the tail is \(1/32\)-regular, \(\rho_R=1/32\), and
   \[
   G<\frac13+10^{-26},
   \]
   while
   \[
   0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R=\frac7{240\sqrt2}\approx0.0206.
   \]
   Hence this absolute Berry--Esseen certificate gives a bound strictly below \(p=1/3\).

## Proof

### 1. Weighted Bernoulli Berry--Esseen estimate

Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\ge0\), not all zero, and let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Put
\[
T=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j(v_j-p),\qquad
B_2=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^2,\qquad
B_3=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^3.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Var}(T)=pq\,B_2.
\]
Also,
\[
\mathbb E|a_j(v_j-p)|^3
=
a_j^3\bigl(pq^3+qp^3\bigr)
=
pq(p^2+q^2)a_j^3.
\]
Thus Berry--Esseen with constant \(0.56\) gives, for
\[
Z=\frac{T}{\sqrt{pq\,B_2}},
\]
the bound
\[
\sup_x|\Pr[Z\le x]-\Phi(x)|
\le
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\,
\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}
=
0.56\,K(p)\,\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}.
\]

### 2. Proof of the effective-dimension condition

Apply the preceding estimate to the full vector \(a_i=w_i\). Since \(\mathbb E S=p\), we get
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge \Pr[S>p]
\ge
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0.
\]
It remains to check that the assumed bound on \(\rho_0\) makes the right-hand side at least \(p\).

Define
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{0.56(p^2+q^2)}.
\]
The inequality
\[
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0\ge p
\]
holds whenever \(\rho_0\le \eta(p)\).

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), put \(x=1-2p\). Then \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1.12(1+x^2)}.
\]
The function
\[
g(x)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1+x^2}
\]
satisfies
\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log g(x)
=
\frac{1-3x^2}{x(1-x^2)(1+x^2)}>0
\]
on \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\). Therefore \(\eta(p)\) is minimized at \(p=1/3\). At \(p=1/3\),
\[
\eta(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/6)(\sqrt2/3)}{0.56\cdot(5/9)}
=
\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}.
\]
Hence \(\rho_0\le 5\sqrt2/28\) implies \(\rho_0\le\eta(p)\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
\]

### 3. Proof of the regular-remainder sufficient condition

Assume \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(\tau=1/32\), and the split \(I_H,I_U,I_R\) satisfies the hypotheses stated above. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta.
\]
Then \(\mathbb E X=0\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^2=pqB_R\).

The \(\tau\)-regularity assumption gives
\[
\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^3
\le
\left(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\right)\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2
\le
\tau B_R^{3/2},
\]
so
\[
\rho_R\le \tau=\frac1{32}.
\]

Condition on arbitrary values of \(S_H\) and \(S_U\). Since
\[
S=S_H+S_U+p\beta+X,
\]
the event \(S\ge p\) is equivalent to
\[
X\ge p-S_H-S_U-p\beta
=
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U.
\]
Because \(S_H,S_U\ge0\),
\[
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U
\le
p(\alpha+\gamma)
\le
p\sigma
\le
\frac{\sigma}{3}.
\]
Thus, for every head and buffer realization, the standardized tail threshold is at most \(1/3\).

By Berry--Esseen for the remainder,
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(t/\sigma)-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R
\]
for every real \(t\). Therefore, whenever \(t\le\sigma/3\),
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(1/3)-0.56\,K(p)\tau.
\]

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has
\[
K(p)\le K(1/4)=\frac5{2\sqrt3}.
\]
Hence
\[
0.56\,K(p)\tau
\le
0.56\cdot \frac5{2\sqrt3}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{160\sqrt3}.
\]
Also,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)
=
\frac12-\int_0^{1/3}\frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,dx
\ge
\frac12-\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}.
\]
Using \(\pi>3\) and \(\sqrt3>5/3\),
\[
\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}<\frac5{36},
\qquad
\frac7{160\sqrt3}<\frac{21}{800}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac7{160\sqrt3}
>
\frac12-\frac5{36}-\frac{21}{800}
=
\frac{2411}{7200}
>
\frac13.
\]
Since \(p\le1/3\), every conditional probability \(\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H,S_U]\) is strictly larger than \(p\). Averaging over \(S_H,S_U\) gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

### 4. Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate

Let \(\gamma>0\), and let \(Y\) be any random variable satisfying \(0\le Y\le\gamma\) a.s. and \(\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\). For \(0<r<p\gamma\), if \(\delta=\Pr[Y\ge r]\), then
\[
p\gamma=\mathbb E Y
\le
r(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta.
\]
Thus
\[
\delta\ge \frac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r}.
\]
This bound is sharp as an infimum, by taking \(Y\) just below \(r\) with high probability and equal to \(\gamma\) with the remaining probability. Hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
=
\begin{cases}
1,& r\le0,\\[1mm]
\dfrac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r},&0<r<p\gamma,\\[2mm]
0,&r\ge p\gamma.
\end{cases}
\]

Now take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad
m=14,\qquad
w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=\frac1{14}.
\]
Let the head be the first nine weights and the buffer the last five weights. Then
\[
\gamma=\frac5{14},
\qquad
p\gamma=\frac5{42}.
\]
Let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(9,1/3)
\]
count the number of selected head weights. For a head outcome with \(K=k\),
\[
S_H=\frac{k}{14},
\qquad
r_k=p-S_H=\frac13-\frac{k}{14}=\frac{14-3k}{42}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_k)
=
\begin{cases}
0,&k\le3,\\[1mm]
3/13,&k=4,\\[1mm]
1,&k\ge5.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, for \(k=4\),
\[
r_4=\frac{2}{42},
\]
and hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_4)
=
\frac{5/42-2/42}{15/42-2/42}
=
\frac3{13}.
\]

The range/mean-only certificate value is therefore
\[
\frac3{13}\Pr[K=4]+\Pr[K\ge5].
\]
Now
\[
\Pr[K=4]
=
\binom94\left(\frac13\right)^4\left(\frac23\right)^5
=
\frac{4032}{19683},
\]
and
\[
\Pr[K\ge5]
=
\frac{2851}{19683}.
\]
Thus the certificate equals
\[
\frac3{13}\cdot\frac{4032}{19683}
+
\frac{2851}{19683}
=
\frac{49159}{255879}
<
\frac13.
\]
So the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate is not universally strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

This does not contradict the original inequality for this example. In fact,
\[
\Pr\left[\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge\frac13\right]
=
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge5]
=
\frac{2508665}{4782969}
>
\frac13.
\]
Also, for fourteen equal weights,
\[
\rho_0=\frac1{\sqrt{14}}>\frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
\]
so this example is not covered by the effective-dimension sufficient condition from part 1.

### 5. Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen certificate

Consider a split \(S=S_H+S_R\) with no buffer. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\operatorname{Var}(X),
\]
and let \(\rho_R\) be the Berry--Esseen ratio of the tail weights. Conditional on a head value \(S_H=h\), Berry--Esseen gives the lower bound
\[
\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H=h]
\ge
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-h}{\sigma}\right)
-
0.56\,K(p)\rho_R.
\]
Averaging over the head gives the absolute Berry--Esseen certificate
\[
G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R,
\]
where
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]

We now show that this certificate need not reach \(p\).

Take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad q=\frac23,\qquad \varepsilon=\frac1{1536}.
\]
Let \(m=1033\), and define
\[
w_1=\frac13-8\varepsilon=\frac{21}{64},
\qquad
w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon.
\]
Use the head
\[
I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}
\]
and the tail
\[
I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}.
\]
Then
\[
\alpha=w_1+8\varepsilon=\frac13,
\qquad
\beta=1024\varepsilon=\frac23.
\]
For the tail,
\[
B_R=1024\varepsilon^2=\frac1{2304},
\]
so
\[
\sigma=\sqrt{pqB_R}=\frac{\sqrt2}{144}.
\]
Also,
\[
\sqrt{B_R}=32\varepsilon=\frac1{48},
\]
and
\[
\max_{i\in I_R}w_i=\varepsilon=\frac1{32}\sqrt{B_R}.
\]
Thus the tail is \(1/32\)-regular. Since the tail consists of \(1024\) equal weights,
\[
\rho_R=\frac1{\sqrt{1024}}=\frac1{32}.
\]

Now compute the Gaussian head average
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]
Here
\[
p\alpha=\frac13\cdot\frac13=\frac19.
\]
If \(v_1=1\), we use only the trivial bound that the corresponding Gaussian tail probability is at most \(1\). This contributes at most \(1/3\) to \(G\).

If \(v_1=0\), let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(8,1/3)
\]
count the selected small head weights \(w_2,\dots,w_9\). Then \(S_H=K\varepsilon\), and
\[
\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}
=
\frac{1/9-K/1536}{\sqrt2/144}
=
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}.
\]
Since \(0\le K\le8\),
\[
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}
\ge
\frac{488}{32\sqrt2}
=
\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}
>
10.78.
\]
By Mills' inequality,
\[
\overline\Phi(x)\le \frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}
\qquad (x>0).
\]
For \(x=61/(4\sqrt2)\), this gives
\[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)<10^{-26}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G
\le
\frac13+\frac23\,\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)
<
\frac13+10^{-26}.
\]

On the other hand,
\[
K(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/3)^2+(2/3)^2}{\sqrt{(1/3)(2/3)}}
=
\frac5{3\sqrt2}.
\]
Hence the Berry--Esseen penalty is
\[
0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
=
0.56\cdot\frac5{3\sqrt2}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{240\sqrt2}
\approx0.0206.
\]
Consequently,
\[
G-0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
<
\frac13+10^{-26}-\frac7{240\sqrt2}
<
\frac13.
\]
Thus the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate is not uniformly strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Again, this is a failure of that certificate, not a counterexample to the original inequality.

1. **Berry–Esseen tail conversion.**  
   If \(F\) is the CDF of \(Z\) and \(\sup_x|F(x)-\Phi(x)|\le\delta\), then
   \[
   \Pr[Z>0]=1-F(0)\ge 1-\Phi(0)-\delta=\frac12-\delta.
   \]
   Also, for any \(u\),
   \[
   \Pr[Z\ge u]=1-F(u-)\ge 1-\lim_{y\uparrow u}(\Phi(y)+\delta)
   =\overline\Phi(u)-\delta.
   \]
   This justifies both Berry–Esseen lower-tail uses despite possible atoms.

2. **Monotonicity of \(K\).**  
   Put \(x=1-2p\). For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
   \[
   K(p)=\frac{p^2+(1-p)^2}{\sqrt{p(1-p)}}=\frac{1+x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}.
   \]
   The derivative in \(x\) is
   \[
   \frac{d}{dx}\frac{1+x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}
   =
   \frac{x(3-x^2)}{(1-x^2)^{3/2}}>0.
   \]
   Hence \(K(p)\) is maximized at \(x=1/2\), i.e. \(p=1/4\), and
   \[
   K(p)\le K(1/4)=\frac5{2\sqrt3}.
   \]

3. **Exact sharpness of \(\psi_{p,\gamma}\).**  
   For \(0<r<p\gamma\), the lower bound
   \[
   \Pr[Y\ge r]\ge \frac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r}
   \]
   follows from
   \[
   p\gamma=\mathbb EY\le r\Pr[Y<r]+\gamma\Pr[Y\ge r].
   \]
   To show sharpness as an infimum, take \(a_\varepsilon=r-\varepsilon\in[0,r)\) and let
   \[
   Y_\varepsilon=
   \begin{cases}
   a_\varepsilon,&\text{with probability }1-\delta_\varepsilon,\\
   \gamma,&\text{with probability }\delta_\varepsilon,
   \end{cases}
   \qquad
   \delta_\varepsilon=\frac{p\gamma-a_\varepsilon}{\gamma-a_\varepsilon}.
   \]
   Then \(\mathbb EY_\varepsilon=p\gamma\), and
   \[
   \Pr[Y_\varepsilon\ge r]=\delta_\varepsilon\to \frac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r}.
   \]
   For \(r>p\gamma\), take \(Y\equiv p\gamma\), giving probability \(0\). For \(r=p\gamma\), the same limiting construction gives infimum \(0\). For \(r\le0\), \(\Pr[Y\ge r]=1\).

4. **Numerical Mills estimate.**  
   With \(x=61/(4\sqrt2)\),
   \[
   \frac{x^2}{2}=\frac{3721}{64},
   \qquad
   x\sqrt{2\pi}=\frac{61\sqrt\pi}{4}>25.
   \]
   Therefore
   \[
   \overline\Phi(x)
   <\frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}
   <\frac{e^{-3721/64}}{25}.
   \]
   Since \(26\ln 10<59.878\), \(3721/64>58.14\), and \(\ln 25>3\), we get
   \[
   \frac{e^{-3721/64}}{25}<10^{-26}.
   \]
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
