response:
**Claim.** Let \(1/4\le p<1/3\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers with \(w_i<p\) for every \(i\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p .
\]

Write \([9]=\{1,\dots,9\}\), and for \(A\subseteq[9]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Also, \(\binom Xk\) denotes the family of \(k\)-subsets of \(X\), and a family is \(t\)-intersecting if any two of its members meet in at least \(t\) points.

## Finite verification lemma

For \(0\le e\le 36\), write uniquely
\[
e=\binom a2+b,\qquad a\ge1,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
and define
\[
T_e=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
Also define
\[
I_e=
\begin{cases}
56,&e=0,\\
50,&e=1,\\
45,&e=2,\\
41,&e=3,\\
38,&e=4,\\
36,&e=5,\\
35,&6\le e\le36,
\end{cases}
\]
\[
B_e=
\begin{cases}
0,&e\le2,\\
6,&e=3,\\
21,&e\ge4,
\end{cases}
\qquad
G_e=\min\{7,T_e\}.
\]
For \(\rho\in[1/3,1/2]\), put
\[
L_e(\rho)=\min\{I_e,\lfloor 35(1+\rho)\rfloor\}.
\]

A threshold graph is a graph obtainable from the one-vertex graph by repeatedly adding either an isolated vertex or a vertex adjacent to every earlier vertex.

The following finite facts will be used.

1. Every \(2\)-intersecting family of \(3\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(7\), and every \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\).

2. If \(H\) is a threshold graph on \(9\) vertices with \(e\) edges, then every intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets of \(H\) has size at most \(I_e\).

3. For every \(e\in\{0,\dots,36\}\), every \(\rho\in[1/3,1/2]\), and every real quadruple \((x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6)\) satisfying
\[
0\le x_3\le56,\qquad
0\le x_4\le L_e(\rho),\qquad
0\le x_5\le B_e,\qquad
0\le x_6\le G_e,
\]
and
\[
x_3+\frac{x_4}{3}+\frac{x_5+x_6}{5}
\le 196+7e-\frac{56}{\rho},
\]
one has
\[
1+9\rho+(36-e)\rho^2+x_3\rho^3+x_4\rho^4+x_5\rho^5+x_6\rho^6
<(1+\rho)^8.
\]

The exact verification of this lemma is included after the main argument.

## Proof of the claim

Set
\[
q=1-p,\qquad \rho=\frac pq.
\]
Since \(1/4\le p<1/3\), we have
\[
\frac13\le \rho<\frac12,
\qquad
q=\frac1{1+\rho}.
\]

For \(A\subseteq[9]\), define
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\qquad
\mathcal D_j=\mathcal D\cap\binom{[9]}j,
\qquad
d_j=|\mathcal D_j|.
\]
The event \(\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\) is exactly the event that the random support \(\{i:v_i=1\}\) belongs to \(\mathcal D\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j.
\]
Since \(q^9(1+\rho)^8=q\), it suffices to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j<(1+\rho)^8.
\]

Define a graph \(W\) on \([9]\) by
\[
ij\in E(W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let \(e=|E(W)|\). Then
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9,\qquad d_2=\binom92-e=36-e.
\]
Also \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\). Indeed, if \(A\in\mathcal D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A^c)<2p<1-p=q,
\]
whereas \(w(A)\le p\) gives \(w(A^c)=1-w(A)\ge q\), contradiction.

We now bound \(d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6\).

First,
\[
d_3\le56.
\]
Indeed, \(\mathcal D_3\) cannot contain three pairwise disjoint triples, since three disjoint triples partition \([9]\), and their total weight would be at most \(3p<1\). The number of partitions of \([9]\) into three unordered triples is
\[
\frac{9!}{(3!)^3 3!}=280,
\]
and each fixed triple appears in
\[
\frac{6!}{(3!)^2 2!}=10
\]
such partitions. Since every partition contains at most two members of \(\mathcal D_3\), we get \(10d_3\le 2\cdot280\), hence \(d_3\le56\).

Next, \(\mathcal D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in\mathcal D_4\) were disjoint and \(z\) were the remaining point, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Also every member of \(\mathcal D\) is independent in \(W\), because a set containing an edge \(ij\in E(W)\) has weight \(>p\).

The graph \(W\) is threshold. To see this, consider any induced subgraph. Choose a vertex \(u\) of minimum weight and a vertex \(v\) of maximum weight inside that induced subgraph. If \(u\) is isolated, we are done. Otherwise \(u\) is adjacent to some \(x\), so \(w_u+w_x>p\). For every other vertex \(y\), we have \(w_v\ge w_x\) and \(w_y\ge w_u\), hence
\[
w_v+w_y\ge w_x+w_u>p.
\]
Thus \(v\) is dominating. Hence every nonempty induced subgraph has an isolated or dominating vertex. By the standard recursive characterization of threshold graphs, this implies that \(W\) is threshold: remove such a vertex, apply induction to the remaining induced subgraph, and add the removed vertex back last as isolated or dominating.

By the finite verification lemma, every intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in a threshold graph with \(e\) edges has size at most \(I_e\). Since \(\mathcal D_4\) is such a family,
\[
d_4\le I_e.
\]

We need a second bound for \(d_4\). Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in\mathcal D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Because \(\mathcal D_4\) is intersecting, \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Also, for every \(B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
w(B)=1-w(B^c)\ge 1-p=q.
\]
For \(i\in[9]\), let
\[
\Delta_i=|\{B\in\mathcal C:i\in B\}|,
\]
ordered so that
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
For each \(i\), the link
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{B\setminus\{i\}:B\in\mathcal C,\ i\in B\}
\]
is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of an \(8\)-set. The \(70\) such \(4\)-subsets split into \(35\) complementary pairs, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\). Thus
\[
\Delta_i\le35
\]
for every \(i\).

Now maximize \(\sum_i x_i\Delta_i\) over the closed polytope
\[
0\le x_i\le p,\qquad \sum_i x_i=1.
\]
Since the \(\Delta_i\) are decreasing, the maximum is obtained by putting as much mass as possible on the largest coefficients. Because \(p\ge1/4\) and \(p<1/3\), this gives
\[
(x_1,\dots,x_9)=(p,p,p,1-3p,0,\dots,0).
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4
\le 35.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le \sum_{B\in\mathcal C} w(B)
=\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Hence
\[
d_4=|\mathcal C|\le \frac{35}{q}=35(1+\rho),
\]
and since \(d_4\) is an integer,
\[
d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+\rho)\rfloor.
\]
Combining the two \(d_4\)-bounds gives
\[
d_4\le L_e(\rho).
\]

For \(d_5\), take \(A\in\mathcal D_5\) and write \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint nonedges \(e_1,e_2\) of \(W\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le 3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus the edge set of \(W[B]\) meets every perfect matching of \(K_4\). Any set of at most two edges in \(K_4\) misses some perfect matching, so \(W[B]\) contains at least three edges.

Therefore \(d_5=0\) if \(e\le2\). If \(e=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three edges of \(W\); those three edges have union size at least \(3\), so there are at most \(\binom61=6\) possible \(4\)-sets \(B\).

Finally, if \(e\ge4\), the complements of members of \(\mathcal D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\). Indeed, if \(B_1,B_2\) were two such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then \(w(B_i)\ge q\) and \(w(B_1\cap B_2)<p\), giving
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)
=w(B_1)+w(B_2)-w(B_1\cap B_2)
>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. By the finite verification lemma, such a family has size at most \(21\). Hence
\[
d_5\le B_e.
\]

For \(d_6\), take \(A\in\mathcal D_6\) and write \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(f\subset B\) were a nonedge of \(W\), and \(z\) were the remaining element of \(B\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(f)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus \(B\) spans a triangle in \(W\), so \(d_6\) is at most the number of triangles of \(W\).

We claim that every graph with \(e\) edges has at most \(T_e\) triangles. This is the usual extremal triangle bound, and we give the short induction proof. For \(e=0\) it is clear. Suppose \(e>0\), and write \(e=\binom a2+b\), \(0\le b<a\). Since \(e<\binom{a+1}{2}\), the subgraph induced by the non-isolated vertices has some vertex of degree \(1\le d\le a-1\); otherwise its minimum degree would be at least \(a\), forcing at least \(\binom{a+1}{2}\) edges. Remove such a vertex. This deletes at most \(\binom d2\) triangles. By induction, the remaining graph has at most \(T_{e-d}\) triangles. Thus it is enough to check
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]
If \(b\ge d\), then
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}
=\binom b2-\binom{b-d}2
=\binom d2+d(b-d)
\ge \binom d2.
\]
If \(b<d\), write \(t=d-b>0\). Then
\[
e-d=\binom{a-1}{2}+(a-1-t),
\]
and a direct expansion gives
\[
T_e-T_{e-d}-\binom d2=t(a-d-1)\ge0,
\]
because \(d\le a-1\). Hence the triangle bound holds.

Thus \(d_6\le T_e\). Also, the complements of members of \(\mathcal D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples by the same union argument used for \(d_5\). By the finite verification lemma, such a family has size at most \(7\). Therefore
\[
d_6\le G_e=\min\{7,T_e\}.
\]

We now derive one more constraint using weight deficiencies. For \(A\in\mathcal D_k\), define
\[
\varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}{p},
\qquad
E_k=\sum_{A\in\mathcal D_k}\varepsilon(A).
\]
If \(B\in\mathcal D_{k+1}\), then
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=(k+1)p-k\,w(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
Dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in\mathcal D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in\mathcal D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times. Therefore, for \(k=2,3,4,5\),
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
\]
In particular,
\[
d_3+2E_3\le7E_2,
\]
\[
\frac{d_4}{3}+E_4\le2E_3,
\]
\[
\frac{d_5}{5}+\frac45E_5\le E_4,
\]
and
\[
\frac{d_6}{5}+E_6\le\frac45E_5.
\]
The last two inequalities imply
\[
\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le E_4.
\]
Combining with the first two gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le7E_2.
\]

It remains to bound \(E_2\). We have
\[
pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Over all pairs,
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]
because each \(w_i\) appears in exactly \(8\) pair sums. Let
\[
S=\sum_{ij\in E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
For every edge \(ij\in E(W)\), we have \(w_i+w_j>p\), and since \(w_i,w_j<p\), also \(w_i+w_j<2p\). Hence
\[
p-w_i-w_j>-p.
\]
Thus \(S\ge -ep\). Consequently
\[
pE_2=(36p-8)-S\le36p-8+ep.
\]
Dividing by \(p\) gives
\[
E_2\le 36+e-\frac8p
=28+e-\frac8\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7e-\frac{56}{\rho}.
\]

We have shown that the actual quadruple \((d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)\) satisfies
\[
0\le d_3\le56,\qquad 0\le d_4\le L_e(\rho),\qquad 0\le d_5\le B_e,\qquad 0\le d_6\le G_e,
\]
and
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7e-\frac{56}{\rho}.
\]
By the finite verification lemma,
\[
1+9\rho+(36-e)\rho^2+d_3\rho^3+d_4\rho^4+d_5\rho^5+d_6\rho^6
<(1+\rho)^8.
\]
Since \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\), this is exactly
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j<(1+\rho)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j
<q^9(1+\rho)^8
=q.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]
=1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
>1-q=p.
\]
In particular,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p
\]
under the same hypotheses.

It remains only to supply the finite verification lemma.

## Exact verification of the finite lemma

The following Python/SymPy script performs the finite checks using exact integer and rational arithmetic. The maximum-clique routine is exact; greedy coloring is used only as a valid upper bound for pruning. Sturm root-counting is used for the one-variable rational inequalities in the LP verification. The Sturm checks apply to explicit rational-coefficient univariate polynomials with rational endpoints; endpoint positivity is checked separately.

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from math import comb

rho = sp.symbols("rho")
Q = sp.Rational

def choose(n, k):
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return comb(n, k)

# ---------- exact maximum clique / independent set ----------

def maximum_clique(adj):
    """
    adj[v] is a bitset of neighbors of v.
    Returns the exact maximum clique size.
    """
    n = len(adj)
    all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1
    best = 0

    def greedy_coloring(P):
        """
        Greedily color the subgraph induced by P.
        The returned color numbers give valid upper bounds
        for clique extension in the standard branch-and-bound search.
        """
        order = []
        color_bound = []
        uncolored = P
        color = 0

        while uncolored:
            color += 1
            available = uncolored
            while available:
                bit = available & -available
                v = bit.bit_length() - 1

                order.append(v)
                color_bound.append(color)

                uncolored &= ~bit
                available &= ~bit
                available &= ~adj[v]
                available &= uncolored

        return order, color_bound

    def search(size, P):
        nonlocal best

        if P == 0:
            if size > best:
                best = size
            return

        order, color_bound = greedy_coloring(P)

        for pos in range(len(order) - 1, -1, -1):
            if size + color_bound[pos] <= best:
                return

            v = order[pos]
            search(size + 1, P & adj[v])
            P &= ~(1 << v)

    search(0, all_vertices)
    return best

def maximum_independent(conflict):
    """
    conflict[v] is a bitset of vertices incompatible with v.
    Returns the exact maximum independent set size.
    """
    n = len(conflict)
    all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1
    complement_adj = [
        ((all_vertices ^ (1 << i)) & ~conflict[i])
        for i in range(n)
    ]
    return maximum_clique(complement_adj)

def k_set_masks(n, k):
    ans = []
    for S in combinations(range(n), k):
        mask = 0
        for i in S:
            mask |= 1 << i
        ans.append(mask)
    return ans

def max_t_intersecting(n, k, t):
    """
    Maximum size of a t-intersecting family of k-subsets of [n].
    """
    sets = k_set_masks(n, k)
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N

    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return maximum_independent(conflict)

assert max_t_intersecting(9, 3, 2) == 7
assert max_t_intersecting(9, 4, 2) == 21

# ---------- threshold graphs and the I_e table ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    """
    Creation sequence: each new vertex is added either isolated
    from all previous vertices or dominating all previous vertices.
    """
    seen = set()

    for seq in product((0, 1), repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n

        for v, dominating in enumerate(seq, start=1):
            if dominating:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v

        key = tuple(adj)
        if key not in seen:
            seen.add(key)
            yield key

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(mask.bit_count() for mask in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj):
    candidates = []

    for S in combinations(range(9), 4):
        ok = True
        for i, j in combinations(S, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                ok = False
                break

        if ok:
            mask = 0
            for i in S:
                mask |= 1 << i
            candidates.append(mask)

    N = len(candidates)
    conflict = [0] * N

    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (candidates[i] & candidates[j]) == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return maximum_independent(conflict)

Icap = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

seen_edge_count = [False] * 37
largest = [0] * 37

for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    e = edge_count(adj)
    seen_edge_count[e] = True
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj)
    largest[e] = max(largest[e], val)

assert all(seen_edge_count)
assert all(largest[e] <= Icap[e] for e in range(37))

# ---------- caps B_e and G_e ----------

def triangle_cap(e):
    a = 1
    while choose(a + 1, 2) <= e:
        a += 1
    b = e - choose(a, 2)
    return choose(a, 3) + choose(b, 2)

def B_cap(e):
    if e <= 2:
        return 0
    if e == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(e):
    return min(7, triangle_cap(e))

# ---------- exact Sturm checks for the LP inequalities ----------

def positive_rational_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    """
    Certify expr > 0 on [lo, hi].
    Since rho > 0, multiplying by rho preserves positivity.
    """
    scaled = sp.together(expr * rho)
    num, den = scaled.as_numer_denom()

    for poly_expr in (sp.expand(num), sp.expand(den)):
        poly = sp.Poly(poly_expr, rho)
        assert poly.eval(lo) > 0 and poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
        if lo < hi:
            assert poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def interval_ge_budget(lo, hi, A):
    """
    Return the subinterval on which A - 56/rho >= 0.
    """
    A = Q(A)

    if A <= 0:
        return None

    root = Q(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)

    if lo2 > hi:
        return None

    return lo2, hi

def interval_le_budget(lo, hi, A):
    """
    Return the subinterval on which A - 56/rho <= 0.
    """
    A = Q(A)

    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi

    root = Q(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)

    if lo > hi2:
        return None

    return lo, hi2

breaks = [
    Q(1, 3),
    Q(12, 35),
    Q(13, 35),
    Q(14, 35),
    Q(15, 35),
    Q(16, 35),
    Q(17, 35),
    Q(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + rho) ** 8

budget_coeff = [Q(1), Q(1, 3), Q(1, 5), Q(1, 5)]
objective_coeff = [rho**3, rho**4, rho**5, rho**6]

for e in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        # On this interval, using the right endpoint value of the floor
        # only enlarges the feasible region.
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))
        cap4 = min(Icap[e], K)

        caps = [56, cap4, B_cap(e), G_cap(e)]
        base = 1 + 9 * rho + (36 - e) * rho**2
        budget_constant = Q(196 + 7 * e)

        # Vertices with all four coordinates at box bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            fixed_budget = Q(0)
            objective = base

            for i in range(4):
                xi = Q(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else Q(0)
                fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi
                objective += objective_coeff[i] * xi

            interval = interval_ge_budget(lo, hi, budget_constant - fixed_budget)
            if interval is None:
                continue

            a, b = interval
            gap = target - objective
            positive_rational_on(gap, a, b, ("box", e, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with active budget and three coordinates at box bounds.
        for free in range(4):
            fixed_indices = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]

            for bits in product((0, 1), repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_budget = Q(0)

                for bit, i in zip(bits, fixed_indices):
                    xi = Q(caps[i]) if bit else Q(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi

                interval = interval_ge_budget(
                    lo,
                    hi,
                    budget_constant - fixed_budget
                )
                if interval is None:
                    continue

                a, b = interval

                interval = interval_le_budget(
                    a,
                    b,
                    budget_constant
                    - fixed_budget
                    - budget_coeff[free] * Q(caps[free])
                )
                if interval is None:
                    continue

                a, b = interval

                active_budget = budget_constant - Q(56, 1) / rho
                xs[free] = (active_budget - fixed_budget) / budget_coeff[free]

                objective = base + sum(
                    xs[i] * objective_coeff[i] for i in range(4)
                )
                gap = target - objective

                positive_rational_on(
                    gap,
                    a,
                    b,
                    ("active", e, lo, hi, free, bits)
                )

print("all finite checks passed")
```

The assertions prove the finite lemma as follows. The \(t\)-intersecting computations build the conflict graph whose vertices are \(k\)-sets and whose edges join pairs with intersection size \(<t\); independent sets are exactly \(t\)-intersecting families. The threshold-graph computation enumerates all isolated/dominating creation sequences on \(9\) vertices, and the relevant optimum is invariant under relabeling. Finally, the LP verification checks every vertex of the relaxed feasible polytope on each interval where \(\lfloor35(1+\rho)\rfloor\) can change. For each resulting rational gap, the script clears denominators by the positive factor \(\rho\), separates numerator and denominator, checks endpoint positivity, and uses exact Sturm root-counting through `count_roots` to verify that no zero occurs in the interval. Hence the sign is positive throughout each interval. Thus the finite lemma holds, completing the proof.

Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.
  • [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.  (from: literature_1011.2803)
    Proof sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

\]
is equivalent, for the same weights, to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
With
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Orbit proof for rational p=a/b: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.
  • Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.
  • Local merging of small weights: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.
  • Section induction: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.
  • Naive stronger cover for G_0: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.
  • Orbit-wise rational-p extension: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.
  • Pairwise smoothing or merging weights: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.
  • Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.
  • Direct coordinate induction: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.
  • Weight merging/compression: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.
  • Boundary continuity: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.
  • Quadratic lower polynomial: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.
  • Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.
  • Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.
  • Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.
  • Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.
  • Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.
  • Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.
  • Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.
  • Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.
  • Universal degree-5/6 product-root polynomial certificates.: Their leading variance coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so they cannot handle diffuse weights there.
  • Crude arbitrary-support matching bounds for losing layers.: For large support, many tiny coordinates allow large matchings in losing pairs and triples.
  • Colouring with large colours plus a leftover colour.: It reduces to an upper-tail inequality for the leftover colour that is false for diffuse equal weights.
  • Worst-case head threshold only: It proves the regular-tail case when alpha_k <= sigma_k, but is too crude for dominant heads because it ignores favorable head outcomes.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen in extreme one-head tails: The normal margin q barPhi(pt)-p barPhi(qt) decays in the far tail, while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not; a relative moderate-deviation bound seems needed.
  • Critical-index decay alone: Geometric decay reduces the tail mass, but it still leaves a finite head that may be too large for the existing support-7/8 layer counts without an additional robust finite-head certificate.
  • Naive universal good merge: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) have every mergeable pair bad, so local merge inequalities alone do not contradict reducedness.
  • Direct FKG/Harris comparison of the two residual windows: The upper and lower windows are differences of downsets rather than monotone events, and the equal-weight example reverses the desired comparison.
  • Crude support-7 layer counts: Bounds such as d2<=21, d3<=15, d4<=15 are too weak near p=1/4; the proof needs the winning-pair blocker tradeoff and the 3-intersection bound for losing 4-sets.
  • Purely local universal good-merge lemma.: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) make every mergeable pair locally bad even though the instance is not a counterexample.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen for a dominant head plus tiny regular tail.: The normal margin in the one-head formula decays in the far tail while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not.
  • Use k-wise intersection alone after complementing the bad family.: General k-wise intersecting monotone families can have too-large high-biased measure; the missing ingredient must exploit the linear-threshold form F={w(A)>q} and coordinate/threshold constraints.
  • Crude independent layer bounds for n=8 above p=1/4: Bounds such as d2<=28, d3<=56, d4<=35, d5<=21 are far too weak near p=1/4 and even the improved d4<=17, d5<=6 needs a tradeoff with d2 and d3.

  • Use k-wise intersection alone: For these linear thresholds the k-wise property is automatic when p<=1/k, so it adds no extra leverage beyond the original lower-tail problem.
  • Naive moment or Cantelli bounds: The threshold is exactly the mean, so one-sided variance inequalities have zero gap and do not force Pr[S>=E S]>=p.
  • Pairwise merging of weights: The local CDF comparison changes sign depending on the outside partial sum, so merging small coordinates is not monotone without additional structure.
  • Crude layer counts for the first open k=3,n=6 case: The automatic bounds e2<=10 and e3<=10 leave a small numerical gap near p=1/3; the weighted shifted structure of the bad layers is still needed.

  • Direct Hoffman extension above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint using only k-wise intersection.: The LTF condition gives useful first-level Fourier information, but the empty-intersection form contains uncontrolled higher-level mixed terms.
  • Pure k-wise intersection: For p <= 1/3 the 3-wise union/intersection property is automatic for these threshold families, and by itself does not encode the mean-threshold LTF structure.
  • Direct support-8 extension of the good-pair complement count: For n=8, complements of bad triples have five vertices and weight > 2; two disjoint bad pairs plus a fifth vertex need not contradict the weight bound, so the n=7 graph-counting closure no longer works directly.
  • Crude sprinkling/Markov boundary estimate: The conditional Markov bound for sprinkling from t0=2/3 blows up near the boundary w(B)=2/3 and gives no adequate averaged control of boundary mass.
  • Weighted degree bound alone: The bound d4<=floor(35(1+r)) gives only d4<=49 at r=2/5 and does not by itself close the m=3 obstruction; the W-independent intersecting-family bound is needed.
  • Crude high-layer bounds with d4<=56: This leaves the known relaxed obstruction at m=3, r=2/5 with d4=56 and a negative polynomial gap.
  • Weighted-degree bound alone for D4 in support 9.: The bound d4<=floor(35(1+r)) still permits the old m=3, r=2/5 relaxed obstruction; the W-independent intersecting-family enumeration is essential.
  • Crude high-layer support-9 bounds with d4<=56.: These bounds leave a negative polynomial gap near the relaxed profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1).
  • Pure k-wise intersection or crude sprinkling for the k=3 high-bias LTF theorem.: The 3-wise intersection property is automatic for these threshold families and does not encode enough of the mean-threshold LTF structure; crude sprinkling estimates blow up near the boundary.
  • Use only the elementary range/mean tail function psi_beta after the first nine weights: The equal-18 example gives Phi=1915/19683<1/3, so the crude tail compression loses too much information about regular tails.
  • Induct using only the original inequality for the tail at its mean: That gives only Pr[T>=p beta]>=p, but head outcomes often require quantitative lower bounds for thresholds below the tail mean.
  • Fixed 9-head absolute Berry-Esseen alone: In far-tail or only moderately regular cases the Gaussian margin can be smaller than the uniform Berry-Esseen error; a smoothed buffer certificate or relative tail estimate is needed.
  • Pointwise cyclic/Liggett interpolation: Four equal weights with four equally spaced arcs of length p give orbit-good fraction 4p-1<p for p<1/3.
  • Berry–Esseen alone: It only forces L3/L2 effective dimension below about 16 for counterexamples, not actual support <=9; long tiny tails can coexist with small effective dimension.
  • Unconditional pairwise merging: The exact formula has two slabs with opposite signs, so merging is monotone exactly when the adjacent-slab inequality holds.
  • Use only the elementary range/mean tail function psi_beta after the first nine head weights.: The equal-18 example at p=1/3 makes the certificate much smaller than p.
  • Prove universal adjacent-slab monotonicity for arbitrary strict reduced support at least 10.: The explicit support-10 example with weights eight times 41/400 and two times 9/100 violates it.
  • Use Berry-Esseen alone to force finite support at most 9.: It only forces effective dimension below about 15.68 in the upper base interval; a long tiny tail can coexist with a few dominant weights.
  • Extend the reciprocal orbit proof pointwise to p in (1/4,1/3).: Four equal weights on four equally spaced circle arcs have orbit-good measure 4p-1, which is less than p for p<1/3.
  • Use only the sigma=0 range/mean buffer function psi_gamma under the critical-index mass bound: Fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 satisfy the effective-dimension exclusion but give a psi certificate value about 0.1921<1/3.
  • Use absolute Berry-Esseen for every gamma=0 regular tail: In the one-dominant-head far-tail regime, the Gaussian margin above p is exponentially small while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in the tail regularity parameter.
  • Treat a_9 small as automatically easy: The regular-tail counterexample has a_9=1/1536 and still defeats the absolute-BE certificate.
  • Universal bottom-5 good-pair principle: The five-0.09/five-0.11 support-10 family has every bottom-5 pair bad, so a local existence statement is false without using Pr(D)>q and minimality.
  • Parseval/Fourier mass alone: It forces an all-bottom-5-bad minimal counterexample to have small gap epsilon, at most 2/15, but does not force epsilon=0.
  • Mean-only slice certificate over the outside sum: All-bad bottom slices can be compensated by high outside-sum slices; controlling only E[R] is too weak, and one needs product-structure or additional covariance information.
  • Universal bottom-five good-pair principle.: A support-10 family with five weights 0.09 and five weights 0.11 has all bottom-five pairs locally bad for p around 0.3, although it is not a counterexample.
  • Range-and-mean compression of the nonregular critical-index buffer.: For fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 the resulting certificate is about 0.192, far below 1/3.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen for every regular tail after a finite head.: In one-dominant-head far-tail cases the normal margin is exponentially tiny while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in the regularity parameter.
  • Single-flip injection from {T<p-a} to {T>=p}: A lower-tail outcome may require flipping several small zero-coordinates to cross p, losing more than the allowed p/q measure factor.
  • Monotonicity under splitting tail weights: Splitting can decrease the functional qP[T>=p]-pP[T<p-a]; for example at p=0.3,a=0.1, splitting a 0.4 tail atom into 0.2+0.2 lowers the margin, though it stays positive.
  • Absolute Berry-Esseen alone in far tails: The Gaussian relative margin q barPhi(pa/sigma)-p barPhi(qa/sigma) can be exponentially small when a/sigma is large, while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in regularity.
  • Pointwise interval/orbit proof for non-reciprocal p: For p<1/3, disjoint active intervals can have no upper-level points but a positive lower-level gap; the reciprocal coloring argument does not extend pointwise without an additional counting idea.
  • Bottom-pair Parseval only: Using only the six bottom-four or nine bottom-five coefficients gives epsilon bounds around 0.1, far too weak.
  • Weighted influence identity alone: It yields epsilon<=p/n after the all-pair upgrade, but does not force epsilon=0.
  • Smallest-coordinate antichain alone: It gives a sharp O(n^{-3/2}) gap reduction but still permits arbitrarily small positive epsilon.

  • Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.

  • Close strict reduced support 9 for p in [1/4,1/3).
  • Prove a sharp structural/tradeoff bound for the losing 4-layer D4 when the winning-pair graph is sparse.
  • Prove the k=3 complement high-bias LTF theorem μ_q({w(A)>q})≤q for q in [2/3,3/4].

  • Develop a relative, not absolute, tail comparison for weighted Bernoulli sums around asymmetric deviations from the mean.
  • Convert the verified finite-support theorems up to support 9 into a robust arbitrary-support reduction.

      verifier_problem_solved: The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality
\[

is equivalent, for the same weights, to

With
\[

\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family.

It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.

Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that \[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\] ## Problem Solved The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define \[ w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i, \] with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by \[ \mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, \] with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define \[ \mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)q\}. \] For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct, \[ H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing. \] Then the following statements hold. 1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\), \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] 2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] 3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\). ## Proof Let \[ V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}. \] Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ \Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}. \] Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if \[ \Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\}, \] then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since \[ \Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|} \] for every \(B\subseteq[m]\). First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\), \[ w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A). \] Therefore \[ A\in\mathcal D \iff w(A)

1-p=q \iff w([m]\setminus A)>q \iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F. \] Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\). Using this bijection, \[ \mu_p(\mathcal D) =\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|} =\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|} =\mu_q(\mathcal F), \] because \(1-q=p\). Now, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right] =\Pr[w(V)\ge p] =1-\Pr[w(V)q\right] =\Pr[w(\Xi)>q] =\mu_q(\mathcal F). \] Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p \iff 1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p \iff \mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q \iff \mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q \iff \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q. \] This proves the equivalence. It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that \[ B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing. \] For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence \[ \#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1. \] Therefore \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j) = \sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i = \sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\} \le (r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i =r-1. \] On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\), \[ w(B_j)>q=1-p \] for every \(j\), and so \[ \sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1, \] because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\). This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting. ## Task writeup_r7_1377dd — writeup (partial): For every integer k≥3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality holds whenever the weight vector has at most k+3 positive coordinates. Otherwise, \[ w(U^c)\le |U^c|p\le (k-t)p\le 1-tp, \] using $kp\le1$. This contradicts $w(U^c)>1-tp$. Hence \[ \sum_j(r_j-1)0$. Consider the $3$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal H$ whose edges are the bad triples. If $T,U\in\mathcal H$ and $|T\cap U|\le1$, then $U$ contains a pair disjoint from $T$. Since $D$ is downward closed, that pair is also bad, contradicting the fact that no bad pair is disjoint from a bad triple. Therefore $\mathcal H$ is pairwise $2$-intersecting. We use the following elementary bound: if $\mathcal H$ is a pairwise $2$-intersecting family of triples on an $N$-element ground set with $N\ge6$, then \[ |\mathcal H|\le N-2. \] Indeed, if all triples contain a common pair, there are at most $N-2$ choices for the third vertex. Otherwise, choose two triples \[ T=\{a,b,c\},\qquad U=\{a,b,d\} \] with intersection $\{a,b\}$, and choose a triple $V$ not containing both $a,b$. To meet both $T$ and $U$ in at least two points, $V$ must be either $\{a,c,d\}$ or $\{b,c,d\}$. Then every triple containing $\{a,b\}$ must have third vertex $c$ or $d$, and every triple not containing $\{a,b\}$ is contained in $\{a,b,c,d\}$. Hence the whole family is contained in a fixed $4$-set, so it has at most $\binom43=4\le N-2$ triples because $N\ge6$. Here $N=k+3\ge6$, so \[ e_3\le k+1. \] Fix one bad triple $T$. Every bad pair intersects $T$. Hence bad pairs are either contained inside $T$, contributing at most $3$, or are cross pairs with one vertex in $T$ and one vertex in $T^c$. The cross pairs form a bipartite graph with parts of sizes $3$ and $k$. This bipartite graph has no matching of size $3$, because such a matching would be three disjoint bad pairs. We claim it has at most $2k$ edges. If every vertex in the $k$-side has degree at most $2$, this is immediate. Otherwise, some vertex on the $k$-side has degree $3$, adjacent to all three vertices of $T$. Removing that vertex, the remaining bipartite graph has no matching of size $2$, for such a matching together with the removed vertex would form a matching of size $3$. A bipartite graph with no matching of size $2$ is a star, so it has at most $\max\{3,k-1\}$ edges. Thus the total number of cross edges is at most \[ 3+\max\{3,k-1\}\le2k \] for $k\ge3$. Therefore \[ e_2\le3+2k. \] Combining this with $e_3\le k+1$ gives \[ e_2+\lambda e_3 \le 2k+3+(k+1)\lambda. \] Now compute $\mu_p(D)$ layer by layer. Since every bad set has size at most $3$, \[ \mu_p(D) = q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}+e_2p^2q^{k+1}+e_3p^3q^k. \] Using the bound on $e_2+\lambda e_3$, \[ e_2p^2q^{k+1}+e_3p^3q^k =p^2q^{k+1}\left(e_2+\frac pq e_3\right) \le p^2q^{k+1}\left(2k+3+(k+1)\frac pq\right). \] Hence \[ \mu_p(D) \le q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}+(2k+3)p^2q^{k+1}+(k+1)p^3q^k. \] Factoring out $q^k$, \[ \mu_p(D) \le q^k\bigl(q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3\bigr). \] The bracket simplifies as follows: \[ \begin{aligned} &q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3\\ &=(q^3+3pq^2+3p^2q+p^3)+k(pq^2+2p^2q+p^3)\\ &=(p+q)^3+kp(q^2+2pq+p^2)\\ &=1+kp(p+q)^2\\ &=1+kp. \end{aligned} \] Thus, in all three possible cases $n=k+1,k+2,k+3$, we have shown \[ \mu_p(D)\le q^k(1+kp). \] It remains to prove \[ q^k(1+kp)\le q. \] Since $q>0$, this is equivalent to \[ (1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp)\le1. \] Define \[ g(p)=(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp). \] Then \[ g'(p) =-(k-1)(1-p)^{k-2}(1+kp)+k(1-p)^{k-1} =(1-p)^{k-2}(1-k^2p). \] For $p\ge1/(k+1)$ and $k\ge3$, \[ k^2p\ge \frac{k^2}{k+1}>1, \] so $g'(p)<0$. A standard fractional perfect matching criterion says that a graph \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching iff for every \(S\subseteq V(G)\), the number of isolated vertices of \(G-S\) is at most \(|S|\). Here this criterion holds: if \(G-S\) had \(r>|S|=s\) isolated vertices, then all edges among those \(r\) vertices and from them to the remaining \(7-s-r\) vertices would be missing from \(G\), hence present in \(H\). That gives at least \[ \binom r2+r(7-s-r)>5 \] missing edges in all possible cases, contradiction. So \(G\) has a fractional perfect matching \((m_{ij})\) with vertex loads \(1\). Since every edge of \(G\) is a bad pair, \[ x_i+x_j<1. \] Therefore \[ T=\sum_i x_i = \sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij}(x_i+x_j) < \sum_{ij\in E(G)}m_{ij} = \frac72, \] contradicting \(T\ge7/2\). Hence \[ e_2\le15 \] whenever \(p\le2/7\). ### Finish: two ranges #### Range 1: \(p\in[1/4,2/7]\) Here \(\lambda\in[1/3,2/5]\). By Fact 3 and the crude high-layer bounds, \[ e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4 \le 15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2. \] But \[ R(\lambda)-(15+15\lambda+5\lambda^2) = \frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-1}{\lambda}. \] The numerator is increasing for \(\lambda>0\), and at \(\lambda=1/3\) it equals \(1/243>0\). So the desired bound holds throughout this range. #### Range 2: \(p\in[2/7,1/3]\) Here \(\lambda\in[2/5,1/2]\). Recall \(g=|E(H)|=21-e_2\). If \(g\le2\), then no \(4\)-set spans three good edges and \(H\) has no triangle, so \(e_3=e_4=0\). Thus \[ e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4\le21. \] Since \(R(\lambda)\) is increasing and \(R(2/5)>21\), this is enough. If \(g=3\), then a \(4\)-set spanning three good edges must contain all three good edges. There are at most four such \(4\)-sets, and \(H\) has at most one triangle. Hence \[ e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4 \le 18+4\lambda+\lambda^2 \le 20.25 <210\). So this case is also done. Finally, if \(g\ge5\), then \(e_2\le16\), while \(e_3\le15\) and \(e_4\le5\). Hence \[ e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2e_4 \le 16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2. \] And \[ R(\lambda)-(16+15\lambda+5\lambda^2) = \frac{\lambda^5+6\lambda^4+10\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-\lambda-1}{\lambda}. \] The numerator is increasing on \([2/5,1/2]\), and at \(\lambda=2/5\) it equals \(637/3125>0\). Thus in every case \[ e_2+\lambda e_3+\lambda^2 e_4\le R(\lambda), \] so \[ \mu_p(D)\le q. \] This proves the theorem. ∎ ## Consequence The previous support-\(\le6\) obstruction is now eliminated one step further: > For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), any strict counterexample to > \[ > \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p > \] > must have at least **8 positive weights**. Indeed, if \(A\in\mathcal D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so \[ w(A^c)<2p<1-p=q, \] whereas \(w(A)\le p\) gives \(w(A^c)=1-w(A)\ge q\), contradiction. We now bound \(d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6\). First, \[ d_3\le56. \] Indeed, \(\mathcal D_3\) cannot contain three pairwise disjoint triples, since three disjoint triples partition \([9]\), and their total weight would be at most \(3p<1\). The number of partitions of \([9]\) into three unordered triples is \[ \frac{9!}{(3!)^3 3!}=280, \] and each fixed triple appears in \[ \frac{6!}{(3!)^2 2!}=10 \] such partitions. Since every partition contains at most two members of \(\mathcal D_3\), we get \(10d_3\le 2\cdot280\), hence \(d_3\le56\). Next, \(\mathcal D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in\mathcal D_4\) were disjoint and \(z\) were the remaining point, then \[ 1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1, \] impossible. Also every member of \(\mathcal D\) is independent in \(W\), because a set containing an edge \(ij\in E(W)\) has weight \(>p\). The graph \(W\) is threshold. To see this, consider any induced subgraph. Choose a vertex \(u\) of minimum weight and a vertex \(v\) of maximum weight inside that induced subgraph. If \(u\) is isolated, we are done. Otherwise \(u\) is adjacent to some \(x\), so \(w_u+w_x>p\). For every other vertex \(y\), we have \(w_v\ge w_x\) and \(w_y\ge w_u\), hence \[ w_v+w_y\ge w_x+w_u>p. \] Thus \(v\) is dominating. Hence every nonempty induced subgraph has an isolated or dominating vertex. By the standard recursive characterization of threshold graphs, this implies that \(W\) is threshold: remove such a vertex, apply induction to the remaining induced subgraph, and add the removed vertex back last as isolated or dominating. By the finite verification lemma, every intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in a threshold graph with \(e\) edges has size at most \(I_e\). Since \(\mathcal D_4\) is such a family, \[ d_4\le I_e. \] We need a second bound for \(d_4\). The \(70\) such \(4\)-subsets split into \(35\) complementary pairs, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\). Thus \[ \Delta_i\le35 \] for every \(i\). Now maximize \(\sum_i x_i\Delta_i\) over the closed polytope \[ 0\le x_i\le p,\qquad \sum_i x_i=1. \] Since the \(\Delta_i\) are decreasing, the maximum is obtained by putting as much mass as possible on the largest coefficients. Because \(p\ge1/4\) and \(p<1/3\), this gives \[ (x_1,\dots,x_9)=(p,p,p,1-3p,0,\dots,0). \] Therefore \[ \sum_i w_i\Delta_i \le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4 \le 35. \] On the other hand, \[ q|\mathcal C| \le \sum_{B\in\mathcal C} w(B) =\sum_i w_i\Delta_i. \] Hence \[ d_4=|\mathcal C|\le \frac{35}{q}=35(1+\rho), \] and since \(d_4\) is an integer, \[ d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+\rho)\rfloor. \] Combining the two \(d_4\)-bounds gives \[ d_4\le L_e(\rho). \] For \(d_5\), take \(A\in\mathcal D_5\) and write \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint nonedges \(e_1,e_2\) of \(W\), then \[ 1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le 3p<1, \] impossible. Thus the edge set of \(W[B]\) meets every perfect matching of \(K_4\). Any set of at most two edges in \(K_4\) misses some perfect matching, so \(W[B]\) contains at least three edges. Therefore \(d_5=0\) if \(e\le2\). If \(e=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three edges of \(W\); those three edges have union size at least \(3\), so there are at most \(\binom61=6\) possible \(4\)-sets \(B\). Finally, if \(e\ge4\), the complements of members of \(\mathcal D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\). Indeed, if \(B_1,B_2\) were two such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then \(w(B_i)\ge q\) and \(w(B_1\cap B_2)2q-p=2-3p>1, \] impossible. By the finite verification lemma, such a family has size at most \(21\). Hence \[ d_5\le B_e. \] For \(d_6\), take \(A\in\mathcal D_6\) and write \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(f\subset B\) were a nonedge of \(W\), and \(z\) were the remaining element of \(B\), then \[ 1=w(A)+w(f)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1, \] impossible. Thus \(B\) spans a triangle in \(W\), so \(d_6\) is at most the number of triangles of \(W\). We claim that every graph with \(e\) edges has at most \(T_e\) triangles. This is the usual extremal triangle bound, and we give the short induction proof. For \(e=0\) it is clear. Suppose \(e>0\), and write \(e=\binom a2+b\), \(0\le b0\). Then \[ e-d=\binom{a-1}{2}+(a-1-t), \] and a direct expansion gives \[ T_e-T_{e-d}-\binom d2=t(a-d-1)\ge0, \] because \(d\le a-1\). Hence the triangle bound holds. Thus \(d_6\le T_e\). Also, the complements of members of \(\mathcal D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples by the same union argument used for \(d_5\). By the finite verification lemma, such a family has size at most \(7\). Therefore \[ d_6\le G_e=\min\{7,T_e\}. \] We now derive one more constraint using weight deficiencies. For \(A\in\mathcal D_k\), define \[ \varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}{p}, \qquad E_k=\sum_{A\in\mathcal D_k}\varepsilon(A). \] If \(B\in\mathcal D_{k+1}\), then \[ \sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A)) =(k+1)p-k\,w(B) =p+k(p-w(B)). \] Dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in\mathcal D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in\mathcal D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times. Therefore, for \(k=2,3,4,5\), \[ d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k. \] In particular, \[ d_3+2E_3\le7E_2, \] \[ \frac{d_4}{3}+E_4\le2E_3, \] \[ \frac{d_5}{5}+\frac45E_5\le E_4, \] and \[ \frac{d_6}{5}+E_6\le\frac45E_5. \] The last two inequalities imply \[ \frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le E_4. \] Combining with the first two gives \[ d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}\le7E_2. \] It remains to bound \(E_2\). We have \[ pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j). \] Over all pairs, \[ \sum_{ip\), and since \(w_i,w_j-p. \] Thus \(S\ge -ep\). Consequently \[ pE_2=(36p-8)-S\le36p-8+ep. \] Dividing by \(p\) gives \[ E_2\le 36+e-\frac8p =28+e-\frac8\rho. \] Therefore \[ d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5} \le 196+7e-\frac{56}{\rho}. \] We have shown that the actual quadruple \((d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)\) satisfies \[ 0\le d_3\le56,\qquad 0\le d_4\le L_e(\rho),\qquad 0\le d_5\le B_e,\qquad 0\le d_6\le G_e, \] and \[ d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5} \le 196+7e-\frac{56}{\rho}. \] By the finite verification lemma, \[ 1+9\rho+(36-e)\rho^2+d_3\rho^3+d_4\rho^4+d_5\rho^5+d_6\rho^6 <(1+\rho)^8. \] Since \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\), this is exactly \[ \sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j<(1+\rho)^8. \] Hence \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right] =q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j\rho^j p\right] =1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right] >1-q=p. \] In particular, \[ \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p \] under the same hypotheses. It remains only to supply the finite verification lemma. ## Exact verification of the finite lemma The following Python/SymPy script performs the finite checks using exact integer and rational arithmetic. The maximum-clique routine is exact; greedy coloring is used only as a valid upper bound for pruning. Sturm root-counting is used for the one-variable rational inequalities in the LP verification. The Sturm checks apply to explicit rational-coefficient univariate polynomials with rational endpoints; endpoint positivity is checked separately. ```python import sympy as sp from itertools import combinations, product from math import comb rho = sp.symbols("rho") Q = sp.Rational def choose(n, k): if k < 0 or k > n: return 0 return comb(n, k) # ---------- exact maximum clique / independent set ---------- def maximum_clique(adj): """ adj[v] is a bitset of neighbors of v. Returns the exact maximum clique size. """ n = len(adj) all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1 best = 0 def greedy_coloring(P): """ Greedily color the subgraph induced by P. The returned color numbers give valid upper bounds for clique extension in the standard branch-and-bound search. """ order = [] color_bound = [] uncolored = P color = 0 while uncolored: color += 1 available = uncolored while available: bit = available & -available v = bit.bit_length() - 1 order.append(v) color_bound.append(color) uncolored &= ~bit available &= ~bit available &= ~adj[v] available &= uncolored return order, color_bound def search(size, P): nonlocal best if P == 0: if size > best: best = size return order, color_bound = greedy_coloring(P) for pos in range(len(order) - 1, -1, -1): if size + color_bound[pos] <= best: return v = order[pos] search(size + 1, P & adj[v]) P &= ~(1 << v) search(0, all_vertices) return best def maximum_independent(conflict): """ conflict[v] is a bitset of vertices incompatible with v. Returns the exact maximum independent set size. """ n = len(conflict) all_vertices = (1 << n) - 1 complement_adj = [ ((all_vertices ^ (1 << i)) & ~conflict[i]) for i in range(n) ] return maximum_clique(complement_adj) def k_set_masks(n, k): ans = [] for S in combinations(range(n), k): mask = 0 for i in S: mask |= 1 << i ans.append(mask) return ans def max_t_intersecting(n, k, t): """ Maximum size of a t-intersecting family of k-subsets of [n]. """ sets = k_set_masks(n, k) N = len(sets) conflict = [0] * N for i in range(N): for j in range(i + 1, N): if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t: conflict[i] |= 1 << j conflict[j] |= 1 << i return maximum_independent(conflict) assert max_t_intersecting(9, 3, 2) == 7 assert max_t_intersecting(9, 4, 2) == 21 # ---------- threshold graphs and the I_e table ---------- def threshold_graphs(n=9): """ Creation sequence: each new vertex is added either isolated from all previous vertices or dominating all previous vertices. """ seen = set() for seq in product((0, 1), repeat=n - 1): adj = [0] * n for v, dominating in enumerate(seq, start=1): if dominating: for u in range(v): adj[v] |= 1 << u adj[u] |= 1 << v key = tuple(adj) if key not in seen: seen.add(key) yield key def edge_count(adj): return sum(mask.bit_count() for mask in adj) // 2 def max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj): candidates = [] for S in combinations(range(9), 4): ok = True for i, j in combinations(S, 2): if (adj[i] >> j) & 1: ok = False break if ok: mask = 0 for i in S: mask |= 1 << i candidates.append(mask) N = len(candidates) conflict = [0] * N for i in range(N): for j in range(i + 1, N): if (candidates[i] & candidates[j]) == 0: conflict[i] |= 1 << j conflict[j] |= 1 << i return maximum_independent(conflict) Icap = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31 seen_edge_count = [False] * 37 largest = [0] * 37 for adj in threshold_graphs(9): e = edge_count(adj) seen_edge_count[e] = True val = max_intersecting_independent_4_family(adj) largest[e] = max(largest[e], val) assert all(seen_edge_count) assert all(largest[e] <= Icap[e] for e in range(37)) # ---------- caps B_e and G_e ---------- def triangle_cap(e): a = 1 while choose(a + 1, 2) <= e: a += 1 b = e - choose(a, 2) return choose(a, 3) + choose(b, 2) def B_cap(e): if e <= 2: return 0 if e == 3: return 6 return 21 def G_cap(e): return min(7, triangle_cap(e)) # ---------- exact Sturm checks for the LP inequalities ---------- def positive_rational_on(expr, lo, hi, tag): """ Certify expr > 0 on [lo, hi]. Since rho > 0, multiplying by rho preserves positivity. """ scaled = sp.together(expr * rho) num, den = scaled.as_numer_denom() for poly_expr in (sp.expand(num), sp.expand(den)): poly = sp.Poly(poly_expr, rho) assert poly.eval(lo) > 0 and poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag if lo < hi: assert poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag def interval_ge_budget(lo, hi, A): """ Return the subinterval on which A - 56/rho >= 0. """ A = Q(A) if A <= 0: return None root = Q(56, 1) / A lo2 = max(lo, root) if lo2 > hi: return None return lo2, hi def interval_le_budget(lo, hi, A): """ Return the subinterval on which A - 56/rho <= 0. """ A = Q(A) if A <= 0: return lo, hi root = Q(56, 1) / A hi2 = min(hi, root) if lo > hi2: return None return lo, hi2 breaks = [ Q(1, 3), Q(12, 35), Q(13, 35), Q(14, 35), Q(15, 35), Q(16, 35), Q(17, 35), Q(1, 2), ] target = (1 + rho) ** 8 budget_coeff = [Q(1), Q(1, 3), Q(1, 5), Q(1, 5)] objective_coeff = [rho**3, rho**4, rho**5, rho**6] for e in range(37): for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]): # On this interval, using the right endpoint value of the floor # only enlarges the feasible region. K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi))) cap4 = min(Icap[e], K) caps = [56, cap4, B_cap(e), G_cap(e)] base = 1 + 9 * rho + (36 - e) * rho**2 budget_constant = Q(196 + 7 * e) # Vertices with all four coordinates at box bounds. for mask in range(16): fixed_budget = Q(0) objective = base for i in range(4): xi = Q(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else Q(0) fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi objective += objective_coeff[i] * xi interval = interval_ge_budget(lo, hi, budget_constant - fixed_budget) if interval is None: continue a, b = interval gap = target - objective positive_rational_on(gap, a, b, ("box", e, lo, hi, mask)) # Vertices with active budget and three coordinates at box bounds. for free in range(4): fixed_indices = [i for i in range(4) if i != free] for bits in product((0, 1), repeat=3): xs = [None] * 4 fixed_budget = Q(0) for bit, i in zip(bits, fixed_indices): xi = Q(caps[i]) if bit else Q(0) xs[i] = xi fixed_budget += budget_coeff[i] * xi interval = interval_ge_budget( lo, hi, budget_constant - fixed_budget ) if interval is None: continue a, b = interval interval = interval_le_budget( a, b, budget_constant - fixed_budget - budget_coeff[free] * Q(caps[free]) ) if interval is None: continue a, b = interval active_budget = budget_constant - Q(56, 1) / rho xs[free] = (active_budget - fixed_budget) / budget_coeff[free] objective = base + sum( xs[i] * objective_coeff[i] for i in range(4) ) gap = target - objective positive_rational_on( gap, a, b, ("active", e, lo, hi, free, bits) ) print("all finite checks passed") ``` The assertions prove the finite lemma as follows. The exact statement proved is the following partial proposition. **Proposition.** Assume the Berry–Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(0.56\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge 0\), \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Put \[ S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad q=1-p,\qquad \kappa(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}. \] For any index set \(A\) with \(B_A:=\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2>0\), define \[ \rho_A=\frac{\sum_{i\in A}w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}. \] For the full vector write \(\rho_0=\rho_{\{1,\dots,m\}}\). Then: 1. If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \[ \rho_0\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28}, \] then \[ \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p. \] 2. Let \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and suppose the indices are partitioned into \(I_H,I_U,I_R\), with \(B_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2>0\). Define \[ S_H=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i v_i,\quad S_U=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i v_i,\quad S_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i v_i, \] and \[ \alpha=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i,\qquad \gamma=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i,\qquad \beta=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i. \] Let \[ \sigma=\sqrt{pq\,B_R}. \] If \[ \max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le \frac1{32}\sqrt{B_R} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \sigma, \] then \[ \Pr[S\ge p]>p. \] 3. The range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate is not universally sufficient. Namely, for \[ \psi_{p,\gamma}(r)= \inf\{\Pr[Y\ge r]:0\le Y\le \gamma \text{ a.s.},\ \mathbb EY=p\gamma\}, \] at \(p=1/3\), with fourteen equal weights \(w_i=1/14\), head \(I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}\), and buffer \(I_U=\{10,\dots,14\}\), one has \[ \mathbb E\bigl[\psi_{p,\gamma}(p-S_H)\bigr] = \frac{49159}{255879} < \frac13. \] 4. The no-buffer absolute Berry–Esseen Gaussian certificate is not universally sufficient even for a \(1/32\)-regular tail. At \(p=1/3\), take \(m=1033\), \(\varepsilon=1/1536\), \[ w_1=\frac{21}{64}=\frac13-8\varepsilon,\qquad w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon, \] with head \(I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}\) and tail \(I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}\). Then the tail is \(1/32\)-regular, \(\rho_R=1/32\), and for \[ G=\mathbb E\left[ \overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right) \right], \] one has \[ G<\frac13+10^{-26}, \] while \[ 0.56\,\kappa(1/3)\rho_R=\frac7{240\sqrt2}. \] Hence \[ G-0.56\,\kappa(1/3)\rho_R<\frac13. \] Here \(\Phi\) is the standard normal distribution function and \(\overline\Phi=1-\Phi\). --- ### 1. Weighted Bernoulli Berry–Esseen estimate Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\ge 0\), not all zero, and let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\). Put \[ T=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j(v_j-p),\qquad B_2=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^2,\qquad B_3=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^3. \] Then \[ \operatorname{Var}(T)=pq\,B_2. \] Also, \[ \mathbb E|a_j(v_j-p)|^3 = a_j^3\bigl(pq^3+qp^3\bigr) = pq(p^2+q^2)a_j^3. \] Thus, for \[ Z=\frac{T}{\sqrt{pq\,B_2}}, \] Berry–Esseen gives \[ \sup_x|\Pr[Z\le x]-\Phi(x)| \le 0.56\,\kappa(p)\,\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}. \] Write this error bound as \(\delta\). If \(F\) is the CDF of \(Z\), then for every real \(u\), \[ \Pr[Z\ge u] = 1-F(u-) \ge 1-\lim_{y\uparrow u}(\Phi(y)+\delta) = \overline\Phi(u)-\delta. \] This form explicitly handles possible atoms in the Bernoulli sum. --- ### 2. Proof of the effective-dimension sufficient condition Apply the preceding estimate to the full vector \(a_i=w_i\). Since \(\mathbb ES=p\), \[ Z=\frac{S-p}{\sqrt{pq\sum_i w_i^2}}. \] Therefore \[ \Pr[S\ge p]\ge \frac12-0.56\,\kappa(p)\rho_0. \] It is enough to show that, for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \[ 0.56\,\kappa(p)\cdot \frac{5\sqrt2}{28}\le \frac12-p. \] Equivalently, define \[ \eta(p)=\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{0.56(p^2+q^2)}. \] We need \(\eta(p)\ge 5\sqrt2/28\). Set \(x=1-2p\). Then \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), \[ p=\frac{1-x}{2},\qquad q=\frac{1+x}{2}, \] and \[ \eta(p)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1.12(1+x^2)}. \] Let \[ g(x)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1+x^2}. \] Then \[ \frac{d}{dx}\log g(x) = \frac{1-3x^2}{x(1-x^2)(1+x^2)}. \] For \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), this derivative is positive. Hence \(g\), and therefore \(\eta\), is minimized at \(x=1/3\), i.e. at \(p=1/3\). At \(p=1/3\), \[ \eta(1/3) = \frac{(1/6)(\sqrt2/3)}{0.56\cdot(5/9)} = \frac{5\sqrt2}{28}. \] Thus \(\rho_0\le 5\sqrt2/28\) implies \[ \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p. \] --- ### 3. Proof of the small-head-plus-buffer regular-remainder condition Assume \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\). Let \[ X=S_R-p\beta. \] Then \(\mathbb EX=0\) and \[ \operatorname{Var}(X)=pq\,B_R=\sigma^2. \] The \(1/32\)-regularity assumption gives \[ \sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^3 \le \left(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\right)\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2 \le \frac1{32}B_R^{3/2}. \] Hence \[ \rho_R\le \frac1{32}. \] Condition on arbitrary values of \(S_H\) and \(S_U\). Since \[ S=S_H+S_U+p\beta+X, \] the event \(S\ge p\) is equivalent to \[ X\ge p-S_H-S_U-p\beta = p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U. \] Because \(S_H,S_U\ge 0\), \[ p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U \le p(\alpha+\gamma) \le p\sigma \le \frac{\sigma}{3}. \] Thus the standardized tail threshold is at most \(1/3\). By the Berry–Esseen tail estimate, \[ \Pr[X\ge t]\ge \overline\Phi(t/\sigma)-0.56\,\kappa(p)\rho_R. \] Therefore, conditionally on any head and buffer realization, \[ \Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H,S_U] \ge \overline\Phi(1/3)-0.56\,\kappa(p)\frac1{32}. \] It remains to lower-bound the right-hand side. \[

Moreover, the only unresolved part is sharply reduced: by the bootstrap lemma it is enough to prove the interval \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), and in that interval any strict reduced counterexample must have at least \(10\) positive weights and satisfy strong pair-merge/Fourier constraints.
