## 1. Global skeleton

Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every nonnegative weight vector \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

From the verified endpoint/counterexample result:

- \(B(p)\) holds for
  \[
  p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
  \]
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=3\), \(w_i=1/3\).
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=2\), \(w_i=1/2\).

Thus the only missing interval is

\[
0<p<\frac13.
\]

The verified bootstrap lemma says:

> If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

Consequently, it is enough to prove \(B(p)\) only on the base interval

\[
\boxed{p\in[1/6,1/3].}
\]

Indeed, any \(0<p<1/6\) can be multiplied by a suitable integer \(k\) to land in \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. Standard reductions inside the base interval

Fix \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), put \(q=1-p\), and suppose a strict counterexample exists.

Delete zero weights. If some positive weight satisfies \(w_i\ge p\), then

\[
\left\{v_i=1\right\}\subseteq\left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]

so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Therefore every strict counterexample may be assumed **reduced**:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1.
\]

Let

\[
S=\sum_i w_iv_i,\qquad D=\{S<p\}.
\]

A strict counterexample is exactly

\[
\Pr[D]>q.
\]

Using the verified support-\(9\) strict theorem on \([1/6,1/3)\), plus the elementary splitting argument below, one obtains:

\[
\boxed{\text{Any strict reduced counterexample in }[1/6,1/3)\text{ has at least }10\text{ positive weights.}}
\]

The splitting argument is: if a strict counterexample has fewer than \(9\) positive weights, split one weight \(c\) into \(c-\delta\) and \(\delta\). The new sum differs from the old one by at most \(\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\), for sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) the new bad probability remains \(>q\). Repeating gives a strict reduced counterexample with exactly \(9\) positive weights, contradicting the verified support-\(9\) theorem.

---

## 3. The exact one-coordinate tail-ratio reduction

A very clean way to close the whole problem would be the following lemma.

### Tail-ratio lemma needed

Let \(0<a<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), and let

\[
T=\sum_j x_j\eta_j,
\]

where \(\eta_j\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\), \(x_j\ge0\), and

\[
\sum_j x_j=1-a.
\]

If one could prove

\[
\boxed{
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a],
}
\tag{TR}
\]

then \(B(p)\) would follow immediately.

Indeed, condition on one coordinate of weight \(a\):

\[
S=av+T.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]

Subtracting \(p\),

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]

Thus (TR) is exactly the missing inequality after conditioning on one coordinate.

The lemma is known/proved in several cases:

- If \(p=1/k\), a cyclic \(k\)-coloring proof gives (TR).
- If the tail has some weight \(x_j\ge p\), then \(\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\), while \(T<p-a\) forces \(\eta_j=0\), so (TR) follows.
- If the tail has support at most \(3\), (TR) follows by a direct case analysis.

But I do not have a proof of (TR) in arbitrary support for non-reciprocal \(p<1/3\).

---

## 4. Reciprocal probabilities \(p=1/k\)

For completeness, here is the short verified proof for \(p=1/k\).

Let \(p=1/k\). Color each coordinate independently and uniformly with one of \(k\) colors. For a fixed color \(r\), the indicator that coordinate \(i\) has color \(r\) is Bernoulli\((1/k)\). Let

\[
S_r=\sum_{i:\,C_i=r}w_i.
\]

For every coloring,

\[
S_1+\cdots+S_k=1.
\]

Hence at least one \(S_r\) is at least \(1/k\). By symmetry,

\[
1
\le
\sum_{r=1}^k \Pr[S_r\ge1/k]
=
k\Pr[S_1\ge1/k],
\]

so

\[
\Pr[S_1\ge1/k]\ge\frac1k.
\]

Thus \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\).

---

## 5. Strong constraints on any remaining minimal counterexample

Suppose \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\) and choose a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support \(n\). By the support reduction above,

\[
n\ge10.
\]

Write

\[
\Pr[S<p]=q+\varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon>0,
\]

and let

\[
G=\{S\ge p\},\qquad F=1_G.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]

For \(p\)-biased Fourier characters

\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]

one obtains the following consequences.

### All-pair merge constraint

For any pair \(i<j\), merge \(w_i,w_j\) into one weight \(w_i+w_j\). If \(w_i+w_j<p\), minimality says the merged instance is not a strict counterexample. If \(w_i+w_j\ge p\), the merged coordinate alone gives success probability at least \(p\). Hence in all cases the merged bad probability is at most \(q\).

The exact merge formula gives

\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge \varepsilon
\qquad\text{for every }i<j.
\]

Thus any minimal counterexample has a complete graph of positive level-\(2\) Fourier coefficients.

### Influence lower bounds

Let

\[
A_i=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_i,p)\right\},
\qquad
I_i=\Pr[A_i].
\]

Then

\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]

Also \(A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G\), so

\[
pI_i\le p-\varepsilon.
\]

Combining,

\[
w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p}
\qquad\text{for every }i,
\]

and therefore

\[
\varepsilon\le \frac pn.
\]

### Parseval obstruction

Parseval gives

\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Using the first- and second-level lower bounds,

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon^2
\left[
\frac qp\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2
+
\binom n2
\right]
\le
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
}
\]

Since the convex function \(\bigl((1-x)/x\bigr)^2\) is minimized under \(\sum_iw_i=1\) at equal weights,

\[
\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2\ge n(n-1)^2.
\]

For \(p\le1/3\), this yields the quantitative bound

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon
<
\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
}
\]

In particular, for \(n=10\),

\[
\varepsilon<0.0118.
\]

### Smallest-coordinate antichain bound

Let \(w_1\) be the smallest weight. The pivotal family

\[
A_1
=
\left\{B\subseteq\{2,\dots,n\}:w(B)\in[p-w_1,p)\right\}
\]

is an antichain, because adding any coordinate changes the weight by at least \(w_1\), the length of the interval.

By the LYM inequality,

\[
I_1\le M_{n-1}(p),
\]

where \(M_N(p)\) is the largest atom of \(\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
}
\]

For example, when \(n=10\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),

\[
pM_9(p)\le \frac{1792}{19683},
\]

so

\[
\varepsilon<0.0102.
\]

These constraints do not yet contradict existence, but they make any remaining counterexample highly rigid.

---

## 6. Current best honest conclusion

The complete classification would be

\[
\boxed{[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}}
\]

if the tail-ratio lemma, or equivalently the large-support \(p\le1/3\) theorem, were proved.

What is fully proved here is:

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ holds for }p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ fails for }p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1),
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{any remaining strict counterexample for }p\in(0,1/3)
\text{ reduces to }p\in[1/6,1/3),\ n\ge10,
}
\]

with the strong all-pair merge/Fourier constraints above.

So the only missing step is to rule out those large-support, tiny-gap, complete-positive-level-\(2\) threshold counterexamples.

([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables))

## 1. Global skeleton

Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every nonnegative weight vector \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

From the verified endpoint/counterexample result:

- \(B(p)\) holds for
  \[
  p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
  \]
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=3\), \(w_i=1/3\).
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=2\), \(w_i=1/2\).

Thus the only missing interval is

\[
0<p<\frac13.
\]

The verified bootstrap lemma says:

> If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

Consequently, it is enough to prove \(B(p)\) only on the base interval

\[
\boxed{p\in[1/6,1/3].}
\]

Indeed, any \(0<p<1/6\) can be multiplied by a suitable integer \(k\) to land in \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

\[
\left\{v_i=1\right\}\subseteq\left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]

so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Therefore every strict counterexample may be assumed **reduced**:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1.

\[
S=\sum_i w_iv_i,\qquad D=\{S<p\}.
\]

A strict counterexample is exactly

\[
\Pr[D]>q.
\]

Using the verified support-\(9\) strict theorem on \([1/6,1/3)\), plus the elementary splitting argument below, one obtains:

\[
\boxed{\text{Any strict reduced counterexample in }[1/6,1/3)\text{ has at least }10\text{ positive weights.}}
\]

The splitting argument is: if a strict counterexample has fewer than \(9\) positive weights, split one weight \(c\) into \(c-\delta\) and \(\delta\). The new sum differs from the old one by at most \(\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\), for sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) the new bad probability remains \(>q\). Repeating gives a strict reduced counterexample with exactly \(9\) positive weights, contradicting the verified support-\(9\) theorem.

A very clean way to close the whole problem would be the following lemma.

### Tail-ratio lemma needed

Let \(0<a<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), and let

\[
T=\sum_j x_j\eta_j,
\]

where \(\eta_j\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\), \(x_j\ge0\), and

\[
\sum_j x_j=1-a.
\]

If one could prove

\[
\boxed{
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a],
}
\tag{TR}
\]

then \(B(p)\) would follow immediately.

Indeed, condition on one coordinate of weight \(a\):

\[
S=av+T.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]

Subtracting \(p\),

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]

Thus (TR) is exactly the missing inequality after conditioning on one coordinate.

The lemma is known/proved in several cases:

- If \(p=1/k\), a cyclic \(k\)-coloring proof gives (TR).
- If the tail has some weight \(x_j\ge p\), then \(\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\), while \(T<p-a\) forces \(\eta_j=0\), so (TR) follows.
- If the tail has support at most \(3\), (TR) follows by a direct case analysis.

But I do not have a proof of (TR) in arbitrary support for non-reciprocal \(p<1/3\).

# Instructions
Verify the solution against the **Claim Being Verified**, not the Original Problem. The Claim may equal the Original Problem verbatim (a full solution) or be a deliberately weakened, special-case, or partial version (a relaxation). Either is acceptable — judge the solution on the Claim it actually makes.

A **major gap** is a critical error in the argument for the Claim Being Verified that cannot easily be fixed; if there is a major gap, the solver should likely try another approach.

A **minor gap** is a fixable mistake, omission, unclear justification, typo-level mathematical issue, or bibliographic/reference problem that can likely be repaired without changing the main strategy of the solution.

Do not penalise the solution for failing to establish more than the Claim asks for. Classify bibliographic/reference problems as minor if they are easy to correct, and major if the proof relies essentially on an invalid or unavailable reference and the issue cannot easily be repaired.

Major missing theorem needed to solve the original problem: one must prove either \(B(p)\) for all \(0<p\le 1/3\), or at least the stated tail-ratio lemma: for all \(0<a<p\le1/3\), all finite tails \(T=\sum_j x_j\eta_j\) with \(\sum_jx_j=1-a\),  
\[
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]
No such arbitrary-support proof is present.

Major missing theorem needed for the \(n\ge10\) reduction: one must prove the precise support-\(9\) statement: for every \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), no strict reduced counterexample with exactly \(9\) positive weights exists. The current proof supplies neither proof nor citation.

Routine patches:

1. **Bootstrap lemma.** Let \(r=kp\le1\), and assume \(B(r)\). Give each coordinate an independent color in \(\{0,1,\dots,k\}\), with colors \(1,\dots,k\) each having probability \(p\), and color \(0\) probability \(1-r\). Let \(U_i\) be the indicator that coordinate \(i\) has nonzero color. Then \(U_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(r)\), so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_iU_i\ge r\right]\ge r.
\]
On this event, among the \(k\) color-class sums, at least one is \(\ge p\). By symmetry and the union bound,
\[
k\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right]\ge r=kp,
\]
so \(B(p)\) follows.

2. **Failure intervals.** If \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), take three weights \(1/3\). Then \(\sum_iw_iv_i\ge p\) iff at least two successes occur, so
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=3p^2(1-p)+p^3=p^2(3-2p),
\]
and
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)=p(1-p)(1-2p)>0.
\]
If \(p\in(1/2,1)\), take two weights \(1/2\). Then \(S\ge p\) iff both Bernoulli variables equal \(1\), so \(\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p\).

3. **Splitting.** If \(S\) is a strict counterexample and a weight \(c\) is split into \(c-\delta,\delta\), couple the new sum \(S_\delta\) with the old one so that \(|S_\delta-S|\le\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\) and \(\Pr[S<p-\delta]\uparrow\Pr[S<p]\) as \(\delta\downarrow0\), choose small \(\delta>0\) with \(\Pr[S<p-\delta]>q\). Then \(\Pr[S_\delta<p]>q\). Choose \(\delta<c\) and \(\delta<p\) to preserve positivity and reducedness.

4. **Pair merge formula.** For a pair \(i,j\), condition on all other coordinates. Let \(F_{ab}\) be the success indicator when \((v_i,v_j)=(a,b)\). The original conditional success probability is
\[
q^2F_{00}+pqF_{10}+pqF_{01}+p^2F_{11},
\]
while the merged-coordinate success probability is
\[
qF_{00}+pF_{11}.
\]
Their difference is
\[
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}),
\]
which is exactly the conditional contribution to \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\). Hence
\[
P_{\mathrm{merged}}(G)-P(G)=\widehat F(\{i,j\}).
\]
Since every merge has success probability at least \(p\), while \(P(G)=p-\varepsilon\), one gets \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\).

5. **Influence identity.** Let \(T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\), \(A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\}\), and \(I_i=\Pr[A_i]\). Pair positivity gives
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge (1-w_i)\varepsilon.
\]
Using \(S-p=\sqrt{pq}\sum_jw_j\chi_j\) and conditioning on \(T_i\), one obtains
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
p w_i I_i-\mathbb E[(p-T_i)\mathbf 1_{A_i}]
\le p w_i I_i.
\]
Therefore \((1-w_i)\varepsilon\le p w_iI_i\). Since \(A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G\), also \(pI_i\le p-\varepsilon\), giving \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\).

6. **LYM product-measure bound.** If \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[N]}\) is an antichain and \(a_k=|\mathcal A\cap\binom{[N]}k|\), then
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal A)
=\sum_k a_k p^kq^{N-k}
=\sum_k a_k\frac{\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)=k]}{\binom Nk}
\le M_N(p)\sum_k\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\le M_N(p).
\]
For the smallest coordinate \(w_1\le1/n\), this gives
\[
\varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
\]
For \(N=9\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), checking the unimodal functions
\[
p\binom9k p^k(1-p)^{9-k}
\]
over \(k=0,\dots,9\) gives the maximum at \(k=3,p=1/3\), equal to \(1792/19683\).

prompt:
You previously submitted a solution which you will find below. Unfortunately, a referee found some issues with your solution that you will need to address carefully and rigorously.
Use the prior solution and the critique below as inputs to your reasoning, but the artifact you produce must be a brand-new, self-contained solution that stands on its own.

# Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

# Claim You Are Establishing
After routine patches, the proof establishes: \(B(0)\); \(B(1/k)\) for every integer \(k\ge1\); failure of \(B(p)\) for \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\); the stated bootstrap implication; and conditional Fourier/antichain constraints on a minimum-support strict reduced counterexample. It does not establish the full classification, because \(B(p)\) remains unproved for general non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\), and the claimed support-\(9\) elimination is unsupported.

Your refined solution must rigorously establish the **Claim You Are Establishing** above. The Claim may equal the Original Problem verbatim (in which case prove the original) or be a deliberately weakened / partial version (in which case prove that weaker claim — do NOT silently aim at the Original Problem). State the exact claim being proved up front and then prove it.

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
I **do not have a valid proof of the remaining large-support case \(0<p<1/3\)**. So I will not claim the full classification. The strongest assembled, verified result is:

\[
\boxed{\text{The inequality holds at }p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1,\text{ and fails for }p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1).}
\]

Moreover, the only unresolved part is sharply reduced: by the bootstrap lemma it is enough to prove the interval \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), and in that interval any strict reduced counterexample must have at least \(10\) positive weights and satisfy strong pair-merge/Fourier constraints.

A historical note: the reciprocal-coloring proof for \(p=1/k\) and the early counterexamples above \(1/3\) appear in the Math.SE thread where this problem was posed. ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables))

---

## 1. Global skeleton

Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every nonnegative weight vector \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

From the verified endpoint/counterexample result:

- \(B(p)\) holds for
  \[
  p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
  \]
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=3\), \(w_i=1/3\).
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=2\), \(w_i=1/2\).

Thus the only missing interval is

\[
0<p<\frac13.
\]

The verified bootstrap lemma says:

> If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

Consequently, it is enough to prove \(B(p)\) only on the base interval

\[
\boxed{p\in[1/6,1/3].}
\]

Indeed, any \(0<p<1/6\) can be multiplied by a suitable integer \(k\) to land in \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. Standard reductions inside the base interval

Fix \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), put \(q=1-p\), and suppose a strict counterexample exists.

Delete zero weights. If some positive weight satisfies \(w_i\ge p\), then

\[
\left\{v_i=1\right\}\subseteq\left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]

so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Therefore every strict counterexample may be assumed **reduced**:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1.
\]

Let

\[
S=\sum_i w_iv_i,\qquad D=\{S<p\}.
\]

A strict counterexample is exactly

\[
\Pr[D]>q.
\]

Using the verified support-\(9\) strict theorem on \([1/6,1/3)\), plus the elementary splitting argument below, one obtains:

\[
\boxed{\text{Any strict reduced counterexample in }[1/6,1/3)\text{ has at least }10\text{ positive weights.}}
\]

The splitting argument is: if a strict counterexample has fewer than \(9\) positive weights, split one weight \(c\) into \(c-\delta\) and \(\delta\). The new sum differs from the old one by at most \(\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\), for sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) the new bad probability remains \(>q\). Repeating gives a strict reduced counterexample with exactly \(9\) positive weights, contradicting the verified support-\(9\) theorem.

---

## 3. The exact one-coordinate tail-ratio reduction

A very clean way to close the whole problem would be the following lemma.

### Tail-ratio lemma needed

Let \(0<a<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), and let

\[
T=\sum_j x_j\eta_j,
\]

where \(\eta_j\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\), \(x_j\ge0\), and

\[
\sum_j x_j=1-a.
\]

If one could prove

\[
\boxed{
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a],
}
\tag{TR}
\]

then \(B(p)\) would follow immediately.

Indeed, condition on one coordinate of weight \(a\):

\[
S=av+T.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]

Subtracting \(p\),

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]

Thus (TR) is exactly the missing inequality after conditioning on one coordinate.

The lemma is known/proved in several cases:

- If \(p=1/k\), a cyclic \(k\)-coloring proof gives (TR).
- If the tail has some weight \(x_j\ge p\), then \(\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\), while \(T<p-a\) forces \(\eta_j=0\), so (TR) follows.
- If the tail has support at most \(3\), (TR) follows by a direct case analysis.

But I do not have a proof of (TR) in arbitrary support for non-reciprocal \(p<1/3\).

---

## 4. Reciprocal probabilities \(p=1/k\)

For completeness, here is the short verified proof for \(p=1/k\).

Let \(p=1/k\). Color each coordinate independently and uniformly with one of \(k\) colors. For a fixed color \(r\), the indicator that coordinate \(i\) has color \(r\) is Bernoulli\((1/k)\). Let

\[
S_r=\sum_{i:\,C_i=r}w_i.
\]

For every coloring,

\[
S_1+\cdots+S_k=1.
\]

Hence at least one \(S_r\) is at least \(1/k\). By symmetry,

\[
1
\le
\sum_{r=1}^k \Pr[S_r\ge1/k]
=
k\Pr[S_1\ge1/k],
\]

so

\[
\Pr[S_1\ge1/k]\ge\frac1k.
\]

Thus \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\).

---

## 5. Strong constraints on any remaining minimal counterexample

Suppose \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\) and choose a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support \(n\). By the support reduction above,

\[
n\ge10.
\]

Write

\[
\Pr[S<p]=q+\varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon>0,
\]

and let

\[
G=\{S\ge p\},\qquad F=1_G.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]

For \(p\)-biased Fourier characters

\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]

one obtains the following consequences.

### All-pair merge constraint

For any pair \(i<j\), merge \(w_i,w_j\) into one weight \(w_i+w_j\). If \(w_i+w_j<p\), minimality says the merged instance is not a strict counterexample. If \(w_i+w_j\ge p\), the merged coordinate alone gives success probability at least \(p\). Hence in all cases the merged bad probability is at most \(q\).

The exact merge formula gives

\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge \varepsilon
\qquad\text{for every }i<j.
\]

Thus any minimal counterexample has a complete graph of positive level-\(2\) Fourier coefficients.

### Influence lower bounds

Let

\[
A_i=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_i,p)\right\},
\qquad
I_i=\Pr[A_i].
\]

Then

\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]

Also \(A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G\), so

\[
pI_i\le p-\varepsilon.
\]

Combining,

\[
w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p}
\qquad\text{for every }i,
\]

and therefore

\[
\varepsilon\le \frac pn.
\]

### Parseval obstruction

Parseval gives

\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Using the first- and second-level lower bounds,

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon^2
\left[
\frac qp\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2
+
\binom n2
\right]
\le
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
}
\]

Since the convex function \(\bigl((1-x)/x\bigr)^2\) is minimized under \(\sum_iw_i=1\) at equal weights,

\[
\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2\ge n(n-1)^2.
\]

For \(p\le1/3\), this yields the quantitative bound

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon
<
\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
}
\]

In particular, for \(n=10\),

\[
\varepsilon<0.0118.
\]

### Smallest-coordinate antichain bound

Let \(w_1\) be the smallest weight. The pivotal family

\[
A_1
=
\left\{B\subseteq\{2,\dots,n\}:w(B)\in[p-w_1,p)\right\}
\]

is an antichain, because adding any coordinate changes the weight by at least \(w_1\), the length of the interval.

By the LYM inequality,

\[
I_1\le M_{n-1}(p),
\]

where \(M_N(p)\) is the largest atom of \(\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
}
\]

For example, when \(n=10\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),

\[
pM_9(p)\le \frac{1792}{19683},
\]

so

\[
\varepsilon<0.0102.
\]

These constraints do not yet contradict existence, but they make any remaining counterexample highly rigid.

---

## 6. Current best honest conclusion

The complete classification would be

\[
\boxed{[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}}
\]

if the tail-ratio lemma, or equivalently the large-support \(p\le1/3\) theorem, were proved.

What is fully proved here is:

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ holds for }p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ fails for }p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1),
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{any remaining strict counterexample for }p\in(0,1/3)
\text{ reduces to }p\in[1/6,1/3),\ n\ge10,
}
\]

with the strong all-pair merge/Fourier constraints above.

So the only missing step is to rule out those large-support, tiny-gap, complete-positive-level-\(2\) threshold counterexamples.

Routine patches:

1. **Bootstrap lemma.** Let \(r=kp\le1\), and assume \(B(r)\). Give each coordinate an independent color in \(\{0,1,\dots,k\}\), with colors \(1,\dots,k\) each having probability \(p\), and color \(0\) probability \(1-r\). Let \(U_i\) be the indicator that coordinate \(i\) has nonzero color. Then \(U_i\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(r)\), so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_iU_i\ge r\right]\ge r.
\]
On this event, among the \(k\) color-class sums, at least one is \(\ge p\). By symmetry and the union bound,
\[
k\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right]\ge r=kp,
\]
so \(B(p)\) follows.

2. **Failure intervals.** If \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), take three weights \(1/3\). Then \(\sum_iw_iv_i\ge p\) iff at least two successes occur, so
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=3p^2(1-p)+p^3=p^2(3-2p),
\]
and
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)=p(1-p)(1-2p)>0.
\]
If \(p\in(1/2,1)\), take two weights \(1/2\). Then \(S\ge p\) iff both Bernoulli variables equal \(1\), so \(\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p\).

3. **Splitting.** If \(S\) is a strict counterexample and a weight \(c\) is split into \(c-\delta,\delta\), couple the new sum \(S_\delta\) with the old one so that \(|S_\delta-S|\le\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\) and \(\Pr[S<p-\delta]\uparrow\Pr[S<p]\) as \(\delta\downarrow0\), choose small \(\delta>0\) with \(\Pr[S<p-\delta]>q\). Then \(\Pr[S_\delta<p]>q\). Choose \(\delta<c\) and \(\delta<p\) to preserve positivity and reducedness.

4. **Pair merge formula.** For a pair \(i,j\), condition on all other coordinates. Let \(F_{ab}\) be the success indicator when \((v_i,v_j)=(a,b)\). The original conditional success probability is
\[
q^2F_{00}+pqF_{10}+pqF_{01}+p^2F_{11},
\]
while the merged-coordinate success probability is
\[
qF_{00}+pF_{11}.
\]
Their difference is
\[
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}),
\]
which is exactly the conditional contribution to \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\). Hence
\[
P_{\mathrm{merged}}(G)-P(G)=\widehat F(\{i,j\}).
\]
Since every merge has success probability at least \(p\), while \(P(G)=p-\varepsilon\), one gets \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\).

5. **Influence identity.** Let \(T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\), \(A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\}\), and \(I_i=\Pr[A_i]\). Pair positivity gives
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge (1-w_i)\varepsilon.
\]
Using \(S-p=\sqrt{pq}\sum_jw_j\chi_j\) and conditioning on \(T_i\), one obtains
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
p w_i I_i-\mathbb E[(p-T_i)\mathbf 1_{A_i}]
\le p w_i I_i.
\]
Therefore \((1-w_i)\varepsilon\le p w_iI_i\). Since \(A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G\), also \(pI_i\le p-\varepsilon\), giving \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\).

6. **LYM product-measure bound.** If \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[N]}\) is an antichain and \(a_k=|\mathcal A\cap\binom{[N]}k|\), then
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal A)
=\sum_k a_k p^kq^{N-k}
=\sum_k a_k\frac{\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)=k]}{\binom Nk}
\le M_N(p)\sum_k\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\le M_N(p).
\]
For the smallest coordinate \(w_1\le1/n\), this gives
\[
\varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
\]
For \(N=9\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), checking the unimodal functions
\[
p\binom9k p^k(1-p)^{9-k}
\]
over \(k=0,\dots,9\) gives the maximum at \(k=3,p=1/3\), equal to \(1792/19683\).

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Biased Fourier/Parseval patch.** For \(0<p<1\), \(\chi_i=(v_i-p)/\sqrt{pq}\) satisfies \(\mathbb E\chi_i=0\) and \(\mathbb E\chi_i^2=1\). By independence,
\[
\mathbb E[\chi_A\chi_B]=0\quad(A\ne B),\qquad \mathbb E[\chi_A^2]=1.
\]
There are \(2^n\) such functions on a \(2^n\)-point space, so they form an orthonormal basis. Hence
\[
\sum_A \widehat F(A)^2=\mathbb E[F^2],
\]
and since \(\widehat F(\varnothing)=\mathbb EF=p-\varepsilon\) and \(F^2=F\),
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=\mathbb E[F^2]-(\mathbb EF)^2
=(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

2. **Section 5 implication patch.** Given arbitrary weights, if some \(w_i\ge p\), then \(\{v_i=1\}\subseteq\{\sum w_iv_i\ge p\}\), so \(B(p)\) is immediate. Otherwise, after deleting zero weights, choose any coordinate \(a=w_i\in(0,p)\) and write \(S=av+T\). The displayed identity
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p=q\Pr[T\ge p]-p\Pr[T<p-a]
\]
shows that the proposed tail-ratio inequality implies \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

3. **Jensen patch.** For
\[
f(x)=\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)^2=x^{-2}-2x^{-1}+1,
\]
one has
\[
f''(x)=\frac{2(3-2x)}{x^4}>0\qquad(0<x\le1).
\]
Thus \(f\) is convex on \((0,1]\). Since \(w_i>0\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), Jensen gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n f(w_i)\ge n f(1/n)=n(n-1)^2.
\]

4. **\(N=9\) binomial atom patch.** Let
\[
b_k=\binom9k p^k(1-p)^{9-k}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{b_{k+1}}{b_k}=\frac{9-k}{k+1}\frac p{1-p}.
\]
For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(b_1>b_0\), \(b_2>b_1\), and \(b_4<b_3\), with all later ratios even smaller. Hence the maximum is among \(b_2,b_3\). Therefore
\[
pM_9(p)=\max\{36p^3(1-p)^7,\ 84p^4(1-p)^6\}.
\]
The first term is maximized at \(p=3/10\), the second is increasing on \([1/4,1/3]\) and is maximized at \(p=1/3\). Moreover,
\[
84(1/3)^4(2/3)^6=\frac{1792}{19683},
\]
and
\[
36(3/10)^3(7/10)^7<\frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
Thus \(pM_9(p)\le1792/19683\).

5. **Section-reference patch.** Replace “Sections 5–7” by “Sections 5–8” if the antichain constraint is included in the summary.

response:
Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every finite nonnegative weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and for i.i.d. \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p .
\]

We prove the following partial theorem.

**Theorem.**

1. \(B(0)\) holds, and \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\).
2. \(B(p)\) fails for every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\).
3. If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.
4. For \(0<p<1\), a one-coordinate reduction holds: if for every \(0<a<p\) and every weighted Bernoulli tail \(T\) of total weight \(1-a\),
   \[
   (1-p)\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\Pr[T<p-a],
   \]
   then \(B(p)\) holds.
5. Suppose \(0<p<1\) and a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support exists. Namely, suppose \(w_1,\dots,w_n\in(0,p)\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and with
   \[
   S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i,\qquad G=\{S\ge p\},\qquad \Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon
   \]
   for some \(\varepsilon>0\), no strict reduced counterexample has smaller support. Put \(q=1-p\), \(F=\mathbf 1_G\), and define the \(p\)-biased Fourier characters by
   \[
   \chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},\qquad \chi_A=\prod_{i\in A}\chi_i,\qquad \widehat F(A)=\mathbb E[F\chi_A].
   \]
   For each \(i\), define
   \[
   T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i} w_\ell v_\ell,\qquad
   A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\},\qquad
   I_i=\Pr[A_i].
   \]
   Then:
   \[
   \widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\qquad(i\ne j),
   \]
   \[
   \widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i,
   \]
   \[
   \varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i,\qquad
   w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p},\qquad
   \varepsilon\le \frac pn,
   \]
   and
   \[
   \varepsilon^2\left[
   \frac qp\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2+\binom n2
   \right]\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
   \]
   In particular, if \(p\le1/3\), then
   \[
   \varepsilon<\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
   \]
   Finally, after relabeling so that \(w_1=\min_i w_i\), if
   \[
   M_N(p)=\max_{0\le t\le N}\binom Nt p^t(1-p)^{N-t},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
   \]
   For \(n=10\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), this gives
   \[
   \varepsilon\le \frac{1792}{177147}<0.0102.
   \]

---

### 1. Endpoint and reciprocal probabilities

For \(p=0\), all \(v_i=0\), so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]
Thus \(B(0)\) holds.

Now let \(p=1/k\), where \(k\ge1\) is an integer. Color each coordinate independently and uniformly with one of the colors \(1,\dots,k\). Let
\[
S_r=\sum_{i:C_i=r}w_i.
\]
For fixed \(r\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=r\}}\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1/k)\), so \(S_r\) has the same distribution as \(\sum_i w_i v_i\) with \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(1/k)\).

For every coloring,
\[
S_1+\cdots+S_k=1,
\]
so at least one \(S_r\) is at least \(1/k\). Hence
\[
1\le \sum_{r=1}^k \mathbf 1_{\{S_r\ge 1/k\}}.
\]
Taking expectations and using symmetry,
\[
1\le k\Pr[S_1\ge1/k].
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S_1\ge1/k]\ge \frac1k.
\]
Thus \(B(1/k)\) holds.

---

### 2. Failure on \((1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\)

First let \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Take \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=1/3\). Then
\[
\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p
\]
holds exactly when at least two of the three Bernoulli variables equal \(1\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=p^2(3-2p).
\]
But
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)=p(1-p)(1-2p)>0
\]
because \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Hence \(B(p)\) fails on \((1/3,1/2)\).

Now let \(p\in(1/2,1)\). Take \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=1/2\). Then
\[
\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p
\]
holds exactly when both Bernoulli variables equal \(1\), so the probability is \(p^2<p\). Thus \(B(p)\) fails on \((1/2,1)\).

---

### 3. Bootstrap implication

Let \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and assume \(B(r)\). Fix arbitrary weights \(w_i\).

Assign to each coordinate an independent color
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,\dots,k\}
\]
with
\[
\Pr[C_i=j]=p\quad(1\le j\le k),\qquad
\Pr[C_i=0]=1-r.
\]
Let \(U_i=\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\ne0\}}\). Then the \(U_i\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((r)\), so by \(B(r)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r\right]\ge r.
\]
On the event \(\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r=kp\), the total weight among the \(k\) nonzero colors is at least \(kp\), so at least one nonzero color class has weight at least \(p\). Hence
\[
\left\{\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r\right\}
\subseteq
\bigcup_{j=1}^k
\left\{\sum_{i:C_i=j}w_i\ge p\right\}.
\]
By the union bound and symmetry,
\[
r\le \sum_{j=1}^k
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=j}w_i\ge p\right]
=
k\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right].
\]
Since \(r=kp\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
But the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\), so this is precisely \(B(p)\).

---

### 4. One-coordinate reduction

Fix \(0<p<1\), put \(q=1-p\), and let \(0<a<p\). Let
\[
S=av+T,
\]
where \(v\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(T\), and \(T\) is some weighted Bernoulli sum of total weight \(1-a\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]
Since
\[
\Pr[T\ge p-a]=1-\Pr[T<p-a],
\]
we get
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]
Therefore, if
\[
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a]
\]
holds for every such \(a\) and \(T\), then \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

To see that this implies \(B(p)\), take arbitrary weights. If some \(w_i\ge p\), then
\[
\{v_i=1\}\subseteq \left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]
so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Otherwise, after deleting zero weights, choose any coordinate \(a=w_i\in(0,p)\) and write the full sum as \(av+T\). The identity above gives \(B(p)\).

---

### 5. Fourier preliminaries

Assume now \(0<p<1\), \(q=1-p\). For \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\), define
\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbb E[\chi_i]=0,\qquad \mathbb E[\chi_i^2]=1.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), set \(\chi_A=\prod_{i\in A}\chi_i\). By independence,
\[
\mathbb E[\chi_A\chi_B]=0\quad(A\ne B),\qquad
\mathbb E[\chi_A^2]=1.
\]
There are \(2^n\) such functions on a \(2^n\)-point probability space, so they form an orthonormal basis. Therefore, for every function \(F\),
\[
\sum_{A\subseteq[n]}\widehat F(A)^2=\mathbb E[F^2].
\]
If \(F=\mathbf 1_G\) and \(\mathbb E F=p-\varepsilon\), then \(\widehat F(\varnothing)=p-\varepsilon\), and hence
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
\mathbb E[F^2]-(\mathbb E F)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

---

### 6. Pair-merge Fourier bound

Let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support. Thus each \(w_i\in(0,p)\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]

Fix \(i<j\). Merge the two weights \(w_i,w_j\) into one coordinate of weight \(w_i+w_j\). If \(w_i+w_j<p\), the merged vector is still reduced and has smaller support, so by minimality it is not a strict counterexample; its success probability is at least \(p\). If \(w_i+w_j\ge p\), then the merged coordinate alone gives success with probability at least \(p\). Thus in every case the merged instance has success probability at least \(p\).

Condition on all variables except \(v_i,v_j\). Let \(F_{ab}\) denote the value of \(F\) when \(v_i=a\), \(v_j=b\). The original conditional success probability is
\[
q^2F_{00}+pqF_{10}+pqF_{01}+p^2F_{11}.
\]
The merged conditional success probability is
\[
qF_{00}+pF_{11}.
\]
Their difference is
\[
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}).
\]
But direct computation also gives
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\chi_j\mid \text{all other variables}]
=
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}).
\]
Averaging,
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\Pr[\text{merged success}]-\Pr[G].
\]
Since the merged success probability is at least \(p\), while \(\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon\),
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon.
\]

---

### 7. Influence identity and consequences

Fix \(i\). Recall
\[
T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell,\qquad
A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\},\qquad
I_i=\Pr[A_i].
\]
Because \(w_i<p\), changing \(v_i\) from \(0\) to \(1\) changes \(F\) exactly on \(A_i\). Therefore
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\mid T_i]
=
\sqrt{pq}\,\mathbf 1_{A_i}.
\]
Averaging gives
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i.
\]

Next,
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\mathbb E\!\left[
F\chi_i\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\chi_j
\right].
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\chi_j
=
\frac{T_i-p(1-w_i)}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]
and
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\mid T_i]=\sqrt{pq}\,\mathbf 1_{A_i},
\]
we get
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\mathbb E[(T_i-p(1-w_i))\mathbf 1_{A_i}].
\]
On \(A_i\),
\[
T_i-p(1-w_i)=p w_i-(p-T_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
p w_i I_i-\mathbb E[(p-T_i)\mathbf 1_{A_i}]
\le p w_i I_i.
\]
On the other hand, the pair-merge bound gives
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
\ge
\varepsilon\sum_{j\ne i}w_j
=
\varepsilon(1-w_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]

Also,
\[
A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G,
\]
and \(A_i\) is independent of \(v_i\), so
\[
pI_i\le \Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]
Combining,
\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i\le w_i(p-\varepsilon),
\]
which implies
\[
w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p}.
\]
Summing over \(i\),
\[
1=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\ge \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\varepsilon}{p}
=\frac{n\varepsilon}{p},
\]
so
\[
\varepsilon\le \frac pn.
\]

---

### 8. Parseval lower bound

From Parseval,
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]
The level-one coefficients satisfy
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})^2
=
pq I_i^2.
\]
Using
\[
I_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon(1-w_i)}{p w_i},
\]
we obtain
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})^2
\ge
\frac qp\,\varepsilon^2
\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2.
\]
The level-two coefficients satisfy
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})^2\ge\varepsilon^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\varepsilon^2\left[
\frac qp\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2+\binom n2
\right]
\le
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Now define
\[
f(x)=\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)^2=x^{-2}-2x^{-1}+1.
\]
Then
\[
f''(x)=\frac{2(3-2x)}{x^4}>0\qquad(0<x\le1),
\]
so \(f\) is convex on \((0,1]\). Since \(\sum_iw_i=1\), Jensen’s inequality gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2
\ge
n\left(\frac{1-1/n}{1/n}\right)^2
=
n(n-1)^2.
\]

If \(p\le1/3\), then \(q/p\ge2\), and
\[
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)<pq\le\frac29.
\]
Hence
\[
2n(n-1)^2\varepsilon^2<\frac29,
\]
so
\[
\varepsilon<\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
\]

---

### 9. Antichain bound

Relabel so that \(w_1=\min_i w_i\). Consider the family
\[
\mathcal A_1
=
\left\{
B\subseteq\{2,\dots,n\}:
\sum_{j\in B}w_j\in[p-w_1,p)
\right\}.
\]
This family represents the pivotal event \(A_1\).

The family \(\mathcal A_1\) is an antichain. Indeed, if \(B\subsetneq C\) were both in \(\mathcal A_1\), then
\[
\sum_{j\in C}w_j-\sum_{j\in B}w_j\ge w_1,
\]
because every weight is at least \(w_1\). But both sums lie in an interval of length \(w_1\), namely \([p-w_1,p)\), contradiction.

Let \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[N]}\) be any antichain. The LYM inequality says
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal A}\frac1{\binom N{|A|}}\le1.
\]
One proof is by random permutations: for fixed \(A\), the probability that \(A\) is the initial segment of a random permutation is \(1/\binom N{|A|}\), and these events are disjoint for an antichain.

Writing \(a_k=|\mathcal A\cap\binom{[N]}k|\), we get
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal A)
=
\sum_{k=0}^N a_k p^k q^{N-k}
=
\sum_{k=0}^N
\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\binom Nk p^k q^{N-k}
\le
M_N(p)
\sum_{k=0}^N\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\le
M_N(p).
\]
Applying this to \(\mathcal A_1\subseteq2^{\{2,\dots,n\}}\), with \(N=n-1\), gives
\[
I_1\le M_{n-1}(p).
\]
Since \(w_1\le1/n\),
\[
\frac{w_1}{1-w_1}\le\frac1{n-1}.
\]
Using \(\varepsilon(1-w_1)\le p w_1 I_1\), we obtain
\[
\varepsilon
\le
pI_1\frac{w_1}{1-w_1}
\le
\frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
\]

Finally, take \(n=10\), so \(N=9\), and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\). Let
\[
b_k=\binom9k p^k(1-p)^{9-k}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{b_{k+1}}{b_k}
=
\frac{9-k}{k+1}\frac{p}{1-p}.
\]
For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has \(p/(1-p)\in[1/3,1/2]\). Hence
\[
b_1>b_0,\qquad b_2>b_1,\qquad b_4<b_3,
\]
and all later ratios are even smaller. Thus the largest atom is among \(b_2,b_3\), so
\[
pM_9(p)
=
\max\{36p^3(1-p)^7,\;84p^4(1-p)^6\}.
\]
The first term is maximized at \(p=3/10\), and
\[
36\left(\frac3{10}\right)^3\left(\frac7{10}\right)^7
<
\frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
The second term is increasing on \([1/4,1/3]\), and at \(p=1/3\) equals
\[
84\left(\frac13\right)^4\left(\frac23\right)^6
=
\frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
Therefore
\[
pM_9(p)\le \frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
The antichain bound gives
\[
\varepsilon
\le
\frac1{9}\cdot\frac{1792}{19683}
=
\frac{1792}{177147}
<0.0102.
\]

# Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

# Claim Being Verified
After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the advertised partial theorem: \(B(0)\) holds; \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\); \(B(p)\) fails for \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\); and the bootstrap implication holds: if \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

It also proves the stated structural constraints for any strict reduced minimum-support counterexample: the pair-merge Fourier bound \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\), the influence identity \(\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i\), the inequalities \(\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_iI_i\), \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\), \(\varepsilon\le p/n\), the displayed Parseval lower bound, the \(p\le1/3\) consequence \(\varepsilon<1/(3(n-1)\sqrt n)\), and the antichain bound \(\varepsilon\le pM_{n-1}(p)/(n-1)\), including the stated \(n=10\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) estimate. It does not prove \(B(p)\) for general non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\), and it contains no valid support-\(9\) elimination.

# Solution
Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every finite nonnegative weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and for i.i.d. \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p .
\]

We prove the following partial theorem.

**Theorem.**

1. \(B(0)\) holds, and \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\).
2. \(B(p)\) fails for every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\).
3. If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.
4. For \(0<p<1\), a one-coordinate reduction holds: if for every \(0<a<p\) and every weighted Bernoulli tail \(T\) of total weight \(1-a\),
   \[
   (1-p)\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\Pr[T<p-a],
   \]
   then \(B(p)\) holds.
5. Suppose \(0<p<1\) and a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support exists. Namely, suppose \(w_1,\dots,w_n\in(0,p)\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and with
   \[
   S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i,\qquad G=\{S\ge p\},\qquad \Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon
   \]
   for some \(\varepsilon>0\), no strict reduced counterexample has smaller support. Put \(q=1-p\), \(F=\mathbf 1_G\), and define the \(p\)-biased Fourier characters by
   \[
   \chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},\qquad \chi_A=\prod_{i\in A}\chi_i,\qquad \widehat F(A)=\mathbb E[F\chi_A].
   \]
   For each \(i\), define
   \[
   T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i} w_\ell v_\ell,\qquad
   A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\},\qquad
   I_i=\Pr[A_i].
   \]
   Then:
   \[
   \widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\qquad(i\ne j),
   \]
   \[
   \widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i,
   \]
   \[
   \varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i,\qquad
   w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p},\qquad
   \varepsilon\le \frac pn,
   \]
   and
   \[
   \varepsilon^2\left[
   \frac qp\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2+\binom n2
   \right]\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
   \]
   In particular, if \(p\le1/3\), then
   \[
   \varepsilon<\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
   \]
   Finally, after relabeling so that \(w_1=\min_i w_i\), if
   \[
   M_N(p)=\max_{0\le t\le N}\binom Nt p^t(1-p)^{N-t},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
   \]
   For \(n=10\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), this gives
   \[
   \varepsilon\le \frac{1792}{177147}<0.0102.
   \]

---

### 1. Endpoint and reciprocal probabilities

For \(p=0\), all \(v_i=0\), so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]
Thus \(B(0)\) holds.

Now let \(p=1/k\), where \(k\ge1\) is an integer. Color each coordinate independently and uniformly with one of the colors \(1,\dots,k\). Let
\[
S_r=\sum_{i:C_i=r}w_i.
\]
For fixed \(r\), the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=r\}}\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1/k)\), so \(S_r\) has the same distribution as \(\sum_i w_i v_i\) with \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(1/k)\).

For every coloring,
\[
S_1+\cdots+S_k=1,
\]
so at least one \(S_r\) is at least \(1/k\). Hence
\[
1\le \sum_{r=1}^k \mathbf 1_{\{S_r\ge 1/k\}}.
\]
Taking expectations and using symmetry,
\[
1\le k\Pr[S_1\ge1/k].
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S_1\ge1/k]\ge \frac1k.
\]
Thus \(B(1/k)\) holds.

---

### 2. Failure on \((1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\)

First let \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Take \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=1/3\). Then
\[
\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p
\]
holds exactly when at least two of the three Bernoulli variables equal \(1\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=p^2(3-2p).
\]
But
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)=p(1-p)(1-2p)>0
\]
because \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Hence \(B(p)\) fails on \((1/3,1/2)\).

Now let \(p\in(1/2,1)\). Take \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=1/2\). Then
\[
\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p
\]
holds exactly when both Bernoulli variables equal \(1\), so the probability is \(p^2<p\). Thus \(B(p)\) fails on \((1/2,1)\).

---

### 3. Bootstrap implication

Let \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and assume \(B(r)\). Fix arbitrary weights \(w_i\).

Assign to each coordinate an independent color
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,\dots,k\}
\]
with
\[
\Pr[C_i=j]=p\quad(1\le j\le k),\qquad
\Pr[C_i=0]=1-r.
\]
Let \(U_i=\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\ne0\}}\). Then the \(U_i\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((r)\), so by \(B(r)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r\right]\ge r.
\]
On the event \(\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r=kp\), the total weight among the \(k\) nonzero colors is at least \(kp\), so at least one nonzero color class has weight at least \(p\). Hence
\[
\left\{\sum_i w_i U_i\ge r\right\}
\subseteq
\bigcup_{j=1}^k
\left\{\sum_{i:C_i=j}w_i\ge p\right\}.
\]
By the union bound and symmetry,
\[
r\le \sum_{j=1}^k
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=j}w_i\ge p\right]
=
k\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right].
\]
Since \(r=kp\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i:C_i=1}w_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
But the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\) are i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\), so this is precisely \(B(p)\).

---

### 4. One-coordinate reduction

Fix \(0<p<1\), put \(q=1-p\), and let \(0<a<p\). Let
\[
S=av+T,
\]
where \(v\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(T\), and \(T\) is some weighted Bernoulli sum of total weight \(1-a\). Then
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]
Since
\[
\Pr[T\ge p-a]=1-\Pr[T<p-a],
\]
we get
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]
Therefore, if
\[
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a]
\]
holds for every such \(a\) and \(T\), then \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

To see that this implies \(B(p)\), take arbitrary weights. If some \(w_i\ge p\), then
\[
\{v_i=1\}\subseteq \left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]
so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Otherwise, after deleting zero weights, choose any coordinate \(a=w_i\in(0,p)\) and write the full sum as \(av+T\). The identity above gives \(B(p)\).

---

### 5. Fourier preliminaries

Assume now \(0<p<1\), \(q=1-p\). For \(v_i\sim{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\), define
\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbb E[\chi_i]=0,\qquad \mathbb E[\chi_i^2]=1.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), set \(\chi_A=\prod_{i\in A}\chi_i\). By independence,
\[
\mathbb E[\chi_A\chi_B]=0\quad(A\ne B),\qquad
\mathbb E[\chi_A^2]=1.
\]
There are \(2^n\) such functions on a \(2^n\)-point probability space, so they form an orthonormal basis. Therefore, for every function \(F\),
\[
\sum_{A\subseteq[n]}\widehat F(A)^2=\mathbb E[F^2].
\]
If \(F=\mathbf 1_G\) and \(\mathbb E F=p-\varepsilon\), then \(\widehat F(\varnothing)=p-\varepsilon\), and hence
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
\mathbb E[F^2]-(\mathbb E F)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

---

### 6. Pair-merge Fourier bound

Let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support. Thus each \(w_i\in(0,p)\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and
\[
\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]

Fix \(i<j\). Merge the two weights \(w_i,w_j\) into one coordinate of weight \(w_i+w_j\). If \(w_i+w_j<p\), the merged vector is still reduced and has smaller support, so by minimality it is not a strict counterexample; its success probability is at least \(p\). If \(w_i+w_j\ge p\), then the merged coordinate alone gives success with probability at least \(p\). Thus in every case the merged instance has success probability at least \(p\).

Condition on all variables except \(v_i,v_j\). Let \(F_{ab}\) denote the value of \(F\) when \(v_i=a\), \(v_j=b\). The original conditional success probability is
\[
q^2F_{00}+pqF_{10}+pqF_{01}+p^2F_{11}.
\]
The merged conditional success probability is
\[
qF_{00}+pF_{11}.
\]
Their difference is
\[
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}).
\]
But direct computation also gives
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\chi_j\mid \text{all other variables}]
=
pq(F_{00}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{11}).
\]
Averaging,
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\Pr[\text{merged success}]-\Pr[G].
\]
Since the merged success probability is at least \(p\), while \(\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon\),
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon.
\]

---

### 7. Influence identity and consequences

Fix \(i\). Recall
\[
T_i=\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell,\qquad
A_i=\{T_i\in[p-w_i,p)\},\qquad
I_i=\Pr[A_i].
\]
Because \(w_i<p\), changing \(v_i\) from \(0\) to \(1\) changes \(F\) exactly on \(A_i\). Therefore
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\mid T_i]
=
\sqrt{pq}\,\mathbf 1_{A_i}.
\]
Averaging gives
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i.
\]

Next,
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\mathbb E\!\left[
F\chi_i\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\chi_j
\right].
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\chi_j
=
\frac{T_i-p(1-w_i)}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]
and
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\mid T_i]=\sqrt{pq}\,\mathbf 1_{A_i},
\]
we get
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
\mathbb E[(T_i-p(1-w_i))\mathbf 1_{A_i}].
\]
On \(A_i\),
\[
T_i-p(1-w_i)=p w_i-(p-T_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=
p w_i I_i-\mathbb E[(p-T_i)\mathbf 1_{A_i}]
\le p w_i I_i.
\]
On the other hand, the pair-merge bound gives
\[
\sum_{j\ne i}w_j\widehat F(\{i,j\})
\ge
\varepsilon\sum_{j\ne i}w_j
=
\varepsilon(1-w_i).
\]
Therefore
\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]

Also,
\[
A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G,
\]
and \(A_i\) is independent of \(v_i\), so
\[
pI_i\le \Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]
Combining,
\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i\le w_i(p-\varepsilon),
\]
which implies
\[
w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p}.
\]
Summing over \(i\),
\[
1=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\ge \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\varepsilon}{p}
=\frac{n\varepsilon}{p},
\]
so
\[
\varepsilon\le \frac pn.
\]

---

### 8. Parseval lower bound

From Parseval,
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]
The level-one coefficients satisfy
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})^2
=
pq I_i^2.
\]
Using
\[
I_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon(1-w_i)}{p w_i},
\]
we obtain
\[
\widehat F(\{i\})^2
\ge
\frac qp\,\varepsilon^2
\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2.
\]
The level-two coefficients satisfy
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})^2\ge\varepsilon^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\varepsilon^2\left[
\frac qp\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2+\binom n2
\right]
\le
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Now define
\[
f(x)=\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)^2=x^{-2}-2x^{-1}+1.
\]
Then
\[
f''(x)=\frac{2(3-2x)}{x^4}>0\qquad(0<x\le1),
\]
so \(f\) is convex on \((0,1]\). Since \(\sum_iw_i=1\), Jensen’s inequality gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2
\ge
n\left(\frac{1-1/n}{1/n}\right)^2
=
n(n-1)^2.
\]

If \(p\le1/3\), then \(q/p\ge2\), and
\[
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)<pq\le\frac29.
\]
Hence
\[
2n(n-1)^2\varepsilon^2<\frac29,
\]
so
\[
\varepsilon<\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
\]

---

### 9. Antichain bound

Relabel so that \(w_1=\min_i w_i\). Consider the family
\[
\mathcal A_1
=
\left\{
B\subseteq\{2,\dots,n\}:
\sum_{j\in B}w_j\in[p-w_1,p)
\right\}.
\]
This family represents the pivotal event \(A_1\).

The family \(\mathcal A_1\) is an antichain. Indeed, if \(B\subsetneq C\) were both in \(\mathcal A_1\), then
\[
\sum_{j\in C}w_j-\sum_{j\in B}w_j\ge w_1,
\]
because every weight is at least \(w_1\). But both sums lie in an interval of length \(w_1\), namely \([p-w_1,p)\), contradiction.

Let \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[N]}\) be any antichain. The LYM inequality says
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal A}\frac1{\binom N{|A|}}\le1.
\]
One proof is by random permutations: for fixed \(A\), the probability that \(A\) is the initial segment of a random permutation is \(1/\binom N{|A|}\), and these events are disjoint for an antichain.

Writing \(a_k=|\mathcal A\cap\binom{[N]}k|\), we get
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal A)
=
\sum_{k=0}^N a_k p^k q^{N-k}
=
\sum_{k=0}^N
\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\binom Nk p^k q^{N-k}
\le
M_N(p)
\sum_{k=0}^N\frac{a_k}{\binom Nk}
\le
M_N(p).
\]
Applying this to \(\mathcal A_1\subseteq2^{\{2,\dots,n\}}\), with \(N=n-1\), gives
\[
I_1\le M_{n-1}(p).
\]
Since \(w_1\le1/n\),
\[
\frac{w_1}{1-w_1}\le\frac1{n-1}.
\]
Using \(\varepsilon(1-w_1)\le p w_1 I_1\), we obtain
\[
\varepsilon
\le
pI_1\frac{w_1}{1-w_1}
\le
\frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
\]

Finally, take \(n=10\), so \(N=9\), and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\). Let
\[
b_k=\binom9k p^k(1-p)^{9-k}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{b_{k+1}}{b_k}
=
\frac{9-k}{k+1}\frac{p}{1-p}.
\]
For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has \(p/(1-p)\in[1/3,1/2]\). Hence
\[
b_1>b_0,\qquad b_2>b_1,\qquad b_4<b_3,
\]
and all later ratios are even smaller. Thus the largest atom is among \(b_2,b_3\), so
\[
pM_9(p)
=
\max\{36p^3(1-p)^7,\;84p^4(1-p)^6\}.
\]
The first term is maximized at \(p=3/10\), and
\[
36\left(\frac3{10}\right)^3\left(\frac7{10}\right)^7
<
\frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
The second term is increasing on \([1/4,1/3]\), and at \(p=1/3\) equals
\[
84\left(\frac13\right)^4\left(\frac23\right)^6
=
\frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
Therefore
\[
pM_9(p)\le \frac{1792}{19683}.
\]
The antichain bound gives
\[
\varepsilon
\le
\frac1{9}\cdot\frac{1792}{19683}
=
\frac{1792}{177147}
<0.0102.
\]

After adding the integer condition in the bootstrap step, the proof establishes the advertised partial theorem: \(B(0)\) holds; \(B(1/k)\) holds for every positive integer \(k\); \(B(p)\) fails for \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\); and if \(k\in\mathbb N\), \(kp\le1\), and \(B(kp)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

It also proves the one-coordinate reduction and the stated structural constraints for every strict reduced minimum-support counterexample: the pair-merge bound \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\), the influence identity \(\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i\), the inequalities \(\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i\), \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\), \(\varepsilon\le p/n\), the Parseval lower bound, the consequence \(\varepsilon<1/[3(n-1)\sqrt n]\) when \(p\le1/3\), and the antichain bound \(\varepsilon\le pM_{n-1}(p)/(n-1)\), including the \(n=10\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) estimate \(\varepsilon\le1792/177147<0.0102\).

It does not solve the original problem completely: it does not prove \(B(p)\) for all non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\), nor does it give a valid support-\(9\) elimination.
</PROBLEM_SOLVED>

prompt:
You are revising a candidate proof of a mathematical problem.

# Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

# Candidate Proof (on the right track, may have gaps or unnecessary jargon)
I **do not have a valid proof of the remaining large-support case \(0<p<1/3\)**. So I will not claim the full classification. The strongest assembled, verified result is:

\[
\boxed{\text{The inequality holds at }p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1,\text{ and fails for }p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1).}
\]

Moreover, the only unresolved part is sharply reduced: by the bootstrap lemma it is enough to prove the interval \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), and in that interval any strict reduced counterexample must have at least \(10\) positive weights and satisfy strong pair-merge/Fourier constraints.

A historical note: the reciprocal-coloring proof for \(p=1/k\) and the early counterexamples above \(1/3\) appear in the Math.SE thread where this problem was posed. ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables))

---

## 1. Global skeleton

Let \(B(p)\) denote the assertion that for every nonnegative weight vector \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

From the verified endpoint/counterexample result:

- \(B(p)\) holds for
  \[
  p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
  \]
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=3\), \(w_i=1/3\).
- \(B(p)\) fails for every
  \[
  p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
  \]
  by taking \(m=2\), \(w_i=1/2\).

Thus the only missing interval is

\[
0<p<\frac13.
\]

The verified bootstrap lemma says:

> If \(k\ge1\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

Consequently, it is enough to prove \(B(p)\) only on the base interval

\[
\boxed{p\in[1/6,1/3].}
\]

Indeed, any \(0<p<1/6\) can be multiplied by a suitable integer \(k\) to land in \([1/6,1/3]\).

---

## 2. Standard reductions inside the base interval

Fix \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), put \(q=1-p\), and suppose a strict counterexample exists.

Delete zero weights. If some positive weight satisfies \(w_i\ge p\), then

\[
\left\{v_i=1\right\}\subseteq\left\{\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right\},
\]

so the desired probability is at least \(p\). Therefore every strict counterexample may be assumed **reduced**:

\[
0<w_i<p,\qquad \sum_i w_i=1.
\]

Let

\[
S=\sum_i w_iv_i,\qquad D=\{S<p\}.
\]

A strict counterexample is exactly

\[
\Pr[D]>q.
\]

Using the verified support-\(9\) strict theorem on \([1/6,1/3)\), plus the elementary splitting argument below, one obtains:

\[
\boxed{\text{Any strict reduced counterexample in }[1/6,1/3)\text{ has at least }10\text{ positive weights.}}
\]

The splitting argument is: if a strict counterexample has fewer than \(9\) positive weights, split one weight \(c\) into \(c-\delta\) and \(\delta\). The new sum differs from the old one by at most \(\delta\). Since \(\Pr[S<p]>q\), for sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) the new bad probability remains \(>q\). Repeating gives a strict reduced counterexample with exactly \(9\) positive weights, contradicting the verified support-\(9\) theorem.

---

## 3. The exact one-coordinate tail-ratio reduction

A very clean way to close the whole problem would be the following lemma.

### Tail-ratio lemma needed

Let \(0<a<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), and let

\[
T=\sum_j x_j\eta_j,
\]

where \(\eta_j\) are iid Bernoulli\((p)\), \(x_j\ge0\), and

\[
\sum_j x_j=1-a.
\]

If one could prove

\[
\boxed{
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[T<p-a],
}
\tag{TR}
\]

then \(B(p)\) would follow immediately.

Indeed, condition on one coordinate of weight \(a\):

\[
S=av+T.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
p\,\Pr[T\ge p-a]+q\,\Pr[T\ge p].
\]

Subtracting \(p\),

\[
\Pr[S\ge p]-p
=
q\,\Pr[T\ge p]-p\,\Pr[T<p-a].
\]

Thus (TR) is exactly the missing inequality after conditioning on one coordinate.

The lemma is known/proved in several cases:

- If \(p=1/k\), a cyclic \(k\)-coloring proof gives (TR).
- If the tail has some weight \(x_j\ge p\), then \(\Pr[T\ge p]\ge p\), while \(T<p-a\) forces \(\eta_j=0\), so (TR) follows.
- If the tail has support at most \(3\), (TR) follows by a direct case analysis.

But I do not have a proof of (TR) in arbitrary support for non-reciprocal \(p<1/3\).

---

## 4. Reciprocal probabilities \(p=1/k\)

For completeness, here is the short verified proof for \(p=1/k\).

Let \(p=1/k\). Color each coordinate independently and uniformly with one of \(k\) colors. For a fixed color \(r\), the indicator that coordinate \(i\) has color \(r\) is Bernoulli\((1/k)\). Let

\[
S_r=\sum_{i:\,C_i=r}w_i.
\]

For every coloring,

\[
S_1+\cdots+S_k=1.
\]

Hence at least one \(S_r\) is at least \(1/k\). By symmetry,

\[
1
\le
\sum_{r=1}^k \Pr[S_r\ge1/k]
=
k\Pr[S_1\ge1/k],
\]

so

\[
\Pr[S_1\ge1/k]\ge\frac1k.
\]

Thus \(B(1/k)\) holds for every integer \(k\ge1\).

---

## 5. Strong constraints on any remaining minimal counterexample

Suppose \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\) and choose a strict reduced counterexample of minimum support \(n\). By the support reduction above,

\[
n\ge10.
\]

Write

\[
\Pr[S<p]=q+\varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon>0,
\]

and let

\[
G=\{S\ge p\},\qquad F=1_G.
\]

Then

\[
\Pr[G]=p-\varepsilon.
\]

For \(p\)-biased Fourier characters

\[
\chi_i=\frac{v_i-p}{\sqrt{pq}},
\]

one obtains the following consequences.

### All-pair merge constraint

For any pair \(i<j\), merge \(w_i,w_j\) into one weight \(w_i+w_j\). If \(w_i+w_j<p\), minimality says the merged instance is not a strict counterexample. If \(w_i+w_j\ge p\), the merged coordinate alone gives success probability at least \(p\). Hence in all cases the merged bad probability is at most \(q\).

The exact merge formula gives

\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge \varepsilon
\qquad\text{for every }i<j.
\]

Thus any minimal counterexample has a complete graph of positive level-\(2\) Fourier coefficients.

### Influence lower bounds

Let

\[
A_i=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_i,p)\right\},
\qquad
I_i=\Pr[A_i].
\]

Then

\[
\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i.
\]

Also \(A_i\cap\{v_i=1\}\subseteq G\), so

\[
pI_i\le p-\varepsilon.
\]

Combining,

\[
w_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{p}
\qquad\text{for every }i,
\]

and therefore

\[
\varepsilon\le \frac pn.
\]

### Parseval obstruction

Parseval gives

\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Using the first- and second-level lower bounds,

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon^2
\left[
\frac qp\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2
+
\binom n2
\right]
\le
(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
}
\]

Since the convex function \(\bigl((1-x)/x\bigr)^2\) is minimized under \(\sum_iw_i=1\) at equal weights,

\[
\sum_i\left(\frac{1-w_i}{w_i}\right)^2\ge n(n-1)^2.
\]

For \(p\le1/3\), this yields the quantitative bound

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon
<
\frac1{3(n-1)\sqrt n}.
}
\]

In particular, for \(n=10\),

\[
\varepsilon<0.0118.
\]

### Smallest-coordinate antichain bound

Let \(w_1\) be the smallest weight. The pivotal family

\[
A_1
=
\left\{B\subseteq\{2,\dots,n\}:w(B)\in[p-w_1,p)\right\}
\]

is an antichain, because adding any coordinate changes the weight by at least \(w_1\), the length of the interval.

By the LYM inequality,

\[
I_1\le M_{n-1}(p),
\]

where \(M_N(p)\) is the largest atom of \(\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon\le \frac{p\,M_{n-1}(p)}{n-1}.
}
\]

For example, when \(n=10\) and \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\),

\[
pM_9(p)\le \frac{1792}{19683},
\]

so

\[
\varepsilon<0.0102.
\]

These constraints do not yet contradict existence, but they make any remaining counterexample highly rigid.

---

## 6. Current best honest conclusion

The complete classification would be

\[
\boxed{[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}}
\]

if the tail-ratio lemma, or equivalently the large-support \(p\le1/3\) theorem, were proved.

What is fully proved here is:

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ holds for }p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B(p)\text{ fails for }p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1),
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{any remaining strict counterexample for }p\in(0,1/3)
\text{ reduces to }p\in[1/6,1/3),\ n\ge10,
}
\]

with the strong all-pair merge/Fourier constraints above.

So the only missing step is to rule out those large-support, tiny-gap, complete-positive-level-\(2\) threshold counterexamples.

# Other Verified Partial Results From the Same Effort (use as needed)

## writeup_r1_838e9e: The proof establishes the following partial classification.

For every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative weights \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_m\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for
\[
p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
\]

It also proves failure of the universal inequality for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
\]
using \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

It further proves failure for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
\]
using \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=\frac12\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p.
\]

The proof does not address the interval \(0<p<\frac13\).
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

The proof below establishes the following **partial** result.

Let \(m\) be a positive integer, let \([m]=\{1,2,\ldots,m\}\), and let \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) be nonnegative real numbers satisfying
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1.
\]
For \(p\in[0,1]\), let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be independent \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variables such that
\[
\Pr[v_i=1]=p,\qquad \Pr[v_i=0]=1-p
\]
for every \(i\in[m]\). Define
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i.
\]
Then:

1. For every choice of \(m\) and weights \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) as above,
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p
\]
holds for \(p=0\), \(p=\frac13\), \(p=\frac12\), and \(p=1\).

2. For every \(p\in(\frac13,\frac12)\), the inequality is not universally true: taking \(m=3\) and
\[
w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13
\]
gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

3. For every \(p\in(\frac12,1)\), the inequality is not universally true: taking \(m=2\) and
\[
w_1=w_2=\frac12
\]
gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p.
\]

This differs from a complete solution of the original problem because it leaves the interval \(0<p<\frac13\) unresolved.

## Proof

For a subset \(A\subseteq[m]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A} w_i.
\]
Thus \(w([m])=1\), and for every \(A\subseteq[m]\),
\[
w(A)+w(A^c)=1,
\]
where \(A^c=[m]\setminus A\).

### The cases \(p=0\) and \(p=1\)

If \(p=0\), then each \(v_i\) is Bernoulli\((0)\), but in fact no distributional detail is needed: since every \(w_i\ge 0\) and every \(v_i\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge 0.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge 0]=1\ge 0.
\]

If \(p=1\), then each \(v_i=1\) almost surely, so
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1
\]
almost surely. Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge 1]=1\ge 1.
\]

Thus the claimed inequality holds for \(p=0\) and \(p=1\).

### The case \(p=\frac12\)

Assume \(p=\frac12\). Let
\[
A=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}
\]
be the random set of successful coordinates. Since the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((\frac12)\) random variables, the random subset \(A\) is uniformly distributed over all \(2^m\) subsets of \([m]\).

The event \(S\ge\frac12\) is exactly the event
\[
w(A)\ge \frac12.
\]
Pair every subset \(A\subseteq[m]\) with its complement \(A^c\). Since
\[
w(A)+w(A^c)=1,
\]
at least one of \(w(A)\) and \(w(A^c)\) is at least \(\frac12\). Therefore, in each complementary pair \(\{A,A^c\}\), at least one subset contributes to the event \(w(A)\ge\frac12\).

There are \(2^{m-1}\) such complementary pairs, and each subset has probability \(2^{-m}\). Hence
\[
\Pr\left[S\ge \frac12\right]\ge 2^{m-1}\cdot 2^{-m}=\frac12.
\]
So the desired inequality holds for \(p=\frac12\).

### The case \(p=\frac13\)

Assume \(p=\frac13\). We use a three-colour coupling.

For each coordinate \(i\in[m]\), choose a random colour
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,2\}
\]
uniformly and independently. For each colour \(r\in\{0,1,2\}\), define a vector
\[
X^{(r)}=(X^{(r)}_1,\ldots,X^{(r)}_m)
\]
by
\[
X^{(r)}_i=\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=r\}}.
\]
For fixed \(r\), the random variables \(X^{(r)}_1,\ldots,X^{(r)}_m\) are independent Bernoulli\((\frac13)\) random variables. Thus
\[
S_r=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i X^{(r)}_i
\]
has the same distribution as \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\) when the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((\frac13)\) random variables.

For each coordinate \(i\), exactly one of \(X^{(0)}_i,X^{(1)}_i,X^{(2)}_i\) equals \(1\). Therefore
\[
S_0+S_1+S_2
=
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\bigl(X^{(0)}_i+X^{(1)}_i+X^{(2)}_i\bigr)
=
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i
=
1.
\]
Consequently, at least one of \(S_0,S_1,S_2\) is at least \(\frac13\).

Let
\[
E_r=\left\{S_r\ge \frac13\right\}.
\]
We have
\[
\Pr(E_0\cup E_1\cup E_2)=1.
\]
By symmetry among the three colours,
\[
\Pr(E_0)=\Pr(E_1)=\Pr(E_2).
\]
Hence
\[
1
=
\Pr(E_0\cup E_1\cup E_2)
\le
\Pr(E_0)+\Pr(E_1)+\Pr(E_2)
=
3\Pr(E_0).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr(E_0)\ge \frac13.
\]
Since \(S_0\) has the same distribution as \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\) for independent Bernoulli\((\frac13)\) variables \(v_i\), this proves
\[
\Pr\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge \frac13\right]\ge \frac13.
\]

Thus the desired inequality holds for \(p=\frac13\).

### Counterexamples for \(\frac13<p<\frac12\)

Let \(p\in(\frac13,\frac12)\). Take
\[
m=3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13.
\]
Let \(N=v_1+v_2+v_3\), the number of successes. Then
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^3 w_i v_i=\frac{N}{3}.
\]
Because \(p>\frac13\), one success gives \(S=\frac13<p\), so one success is not enough. Because \(p<\frac12<\frac23\), two successes give \(S=\frac23\ge p\). Hence
\[
\{S\ge p\}=\{N\ge 2\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
\Pr[N=2]+\Pr[N=3].
\]
Since \(N\sim \operatorname{Binomial}(3,p)\),
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]
=
\binom32 p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
p^2(3-2p).
\]
Now
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)
=
p(1-3p+2p^2)
=
p(1-p)(1-2p).
\]
For \(p\in(\frac13,\frac12)\), all three factors \(p\), \(1-p\), and \(1-2p\) are positive. Hence
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)>0,
\]
so
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]
Thus the desired inequality fails for every \(p\in(\frac13,\frac12)\).

### Counterexamples for \(\frac12<p<1\)

Let \(p\in(\frac12,1)\). Take
\[
m=2,\qquad w_1=w_2=\frac12.
\]
Let \(N=v_1+v_2\). Then
\[
S=\frac{N}{2}.
\]
Because \(p>\frac12\), one success gives \(S=\frac12<p\), so one success is not enough. The event \(S\ge p\) occurs only when both variables are equal to \(1\). Hence
\[
\{S\ge p\}=\{v_1=1,\ v_2=1\}.
\]
By independence,
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2.
\]
Since \(0<p<1\),
\[
p^2<p.
\]
Thus the desired inequality fails for every \(p\in(\frac12,1)\).

Combining the positive cases and the counterexamples, this partial result proves the inequality for \(p=0,\frac13,\frac12,1\), disproves it for every \(p\in(\frac13,\frac12)\cup(\frac12,1)\), and leaves only the interval \(0<p<\frac13\) to prove for the full classification.

## writeup_r3_3b911c: Let \(B(t)\) denote the assertion that for every \(m\ge 1\), every nonnegative weight vector \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((t)\) variables \(v_i\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge t\right]\ge t.
\]
The proof establishes that for every integer \(k\ge1\) and every \(p\in[0,1]\) with \(r=kp\le1\), the implication
\[
B(r)\implies B(p)
\]
holds. It also establishes that if \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[0,1/3]\). It does not classify all \(p\) in the original problem.
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

For \(t\in[0,1]\), define \(B(t)\) to be the following assertion: for every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative real numbers \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) satisfying \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and every family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) of independent Bernoulli\((t)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=t\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=1-t\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge t\right]\ge t.
\]
This proof establishes the following reduction lemma and consequence: if \(k\) is a positive integer, \(p\in[0,1]\), \(r=kp\le1\), and \(B(r)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds. Consequently, if \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[0,1/3]\). This is not a complete classification of all probabilities \(p\) in the original problem; it is a conditional reduction and interval-extension consequence.

## Proof

### Reduction lemma

Fix a positive integer \(k\), a number \(p\in[0,1]\), and set \(r=kp\). Assume \(r\le1\) and assume \(B(r)\) holds. We prove \(B(p)\).

Let \(m\) be any positive integer, and let \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\). We must prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\).

Construct the following categorical coupling. For each coordinate \(i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}\), choose an independent random variable
\[
C_i\in\{0,1,\ldots,k\}
\]
with
\[
\Pr[C_i=j]=p \quad (1\le j\le k),\qquad \Pr[C_i=0]=1-r.
\]
This is a valid probability distribution because \(p\ge0\), \(1-r\ge0\), and
\[
(1-r)+kp=(1-r)+r=1.
\]

For each nonzero colour \(j\in\{1,\ldots,k\}\), define the total weight of colour \(j\) by
\[
S_j:=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}.
\]
Also define
\[
T:=S_1+\cdots+S_k.
\]
Since each coordinate has exactly one colour, we have
\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq 0\}}.
\]

For each \(i\), the indicator \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i\neq0\}}\) is Bernoulli\((r)\), because
\[
\Pr[C_i\neq0]=\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[C_i=j]=kp=r.
\]
These indicators are independent, since the \(C_i\) are independent. Therefore \(T\) is a weighted sum of independent Bernoulli\((r)\) variables with weights summing to \(1\). By \(B(r)\),
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]\ge r. \tag{1}
\]

Now define events
\[
A_j:=\{S_j\ge p\},\qquad 1\le j\le k.
\]
We claim that
\[
\{T\ge r\}\subseteq A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k.
\]
Indeed, if none of the events \(A_j\) occurs, then \(S_j<p\) for every \(j\), so
\[
T=S_1+\cdots+S_k<kp=r.
\]
Thus \(T\ge r\) is impossible unless at least one \(S_j\ge p\). The non-strict threshold \(\ge\) is handled exactly by passing to the strict complement \(S_j<p\).

Using the inclusion above and the union bound,
\[
\Pr[T\ge r]
\le \Pr[A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_k]
\le \sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j].
\]
For each fixed \(j\), the random variables \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=j\}}\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\), are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Hence all \(S_j\) have the same distribution, and in particular
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k \Pr[A_j]=k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Combining this with (1) gives
\[
r\le k\Pr[S_1\ge p].
\]
Since \(k>0\) and \(r=kp\),
\[
\Pr[S_1\ge p]\ge \frac r k=p.
\]

Finally, \(S_1=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\), and the indicators \(\mathbf 1_{\{C_i=1\}}\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Thus \(S_1\) has exactly the same distribution as \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\) for any independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\). Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Because \(m\) and the weights \(w_i\) were arbitrary, \(B(p)\) holds.

The endpoint cases are included: probabilities equal to \(0\) are allowed in the categorical distribution; if \(p=0\), then the desired lower bound is \(0\); and if \(r=1\), then colour \(0\) simply has probability \(0\).

### Consequence for \([0,1/3]\)

Assume \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\). We prove \(B(p)\) for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

If \(p=0\), then independent Bernoulli\((0)\) variables are all \(0\) almost surely, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]
Thus \(B(0)\) holds.

If \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(p)\) holds by hypothesis.

It remains to consider \(0<p<1/6\). Set
\[
a:=\frac{1}{6p}
\]
and let
\[
k:=\lceil a\rceil,
\]
the smallest integer at least \(a\). Since \(p>0\), \(k\) is a positive integer. Also \(p<1/6\), so \(a>1\). Therefore
\[
a\le k<a+1<2a.
\]
Multiplying by \(p>0\), we get
\[
\frac16=ap\le kp<2ap=\frac13.
\]
Thus \(r:=kp\) lies in \([1/6,1/3]\), so \(B(r)\) holds by hypothesis, and certainly \(r\le1\). By the reduction lemma, \(B(p)\) holds.

Hence \(B(p)\) holds for every \(p\in[0,1/3]\).

The proof establishes: if k is a positive integer, p in [0,1], r=kp<=1, and B(r) holds, then B(p) holds; consequently, if B(t) holds for every t in [1/6,1/3], then B(t) holds for every t in [0,1/3]. This is a conditional reduction and interval consequence, not a complete classification of all p in the original problem.",
