## writeup_r4_00439d: The proof establishes the stated partial theorem:

For \(0<p<1\), positive weights \(0<w_i<p\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\):

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
4. For every losing \(k\)-uniform family \(\mathcal H\), with nondecreasing degree sequence \(d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}\) and \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
5. For a minimum-support strict counterexample among positive weights \(<p\), if two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), then with \(R\) the residual random sum,
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]

The proof does not establish the original problem for arbitrary nonnegative weights or all \(p\).
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves a **partial theorem and auxiliary lemmas**, not the full original problem.

Let \(0<p<1\), let \(q=1-p\), let \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_n\) be positive real numbers satisfying
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and set
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[n]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i,
\]
and call \(A\) losing if \(w(A)\le p\). Let
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[n]:w(A)\le p\}.
\]
The losing-pair graph is the graph on vertex set \([n]\) in which \(\{i,j\}\) is an edge exactly when \(w_i+w_j\le p\).

The proof establishes the following five claims.

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then
   \[
   \Pr[S>p]>p.
   \]

4. If \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) is a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets, if \(d_i\) is the degree of \(i\) in \(\mathcal H\), and if
   \[
   d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
   \]
   is the nondecreasing rearrangement of the degrees, then, with \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
   \[
   p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
   \]

5. Fix \(p\). Among positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\) and total weight \(1\), suppose \(w\) is a strict counterexample of minimal positive support to the inequality \(\Pr[S>p]\ge p\), meaning \(\Pr[S>p]<p\), and no strict counterexample with fewer positive weights exists. If two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), and if \(R\) is the residual random sum over all other coordinates, then
   \[
   \Pr[p-x<R\le p]
   <
   \Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
   \]

This differs from the original problem because it does not characterize all \(p\) and does not treat arbitrary nonnegative weights. It proves only the listed reduced-support and structural cases, under the strict hypotheses \(0<w_i<p\), together with two auxiliary lemmas.

## Proof

### 1. Basic notation and the layer measure

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[n]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Then \(V\) is a random subset of \([n]\), with
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]
Also,
\[
S=w(V).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]

For \(0\le j\le n\), write
\[
D_j=\{A\in D:|A|=j\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S\le p]=\sum_{j=0}^n d_j p^j q^{n-j}.
\]
Since \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, and of course the empty set is losing. Thus
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=n.
\]

We shall repeatedly upper-bound \(\Pr[S\le p]\). Once we prove
\[
\Pr[S\le p]<1-p=q,
\]
it follows that
\[
\Pr[S>p]=1-\Pr[S\le p]>p.
\]

### 2. Combinatorial estimates

We use three elementary combinatorial estimates.

#### 2.1. Intersecting graphs

If a graph on \(N\ge4\) vertices has no two disjoint edges, then it has at most \(N-1\) edges.

Indeed, if all edges share a common vertex, there are at most \(N-1\) edges. Otherwise, choose two edges \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), and suppose there is an edge not containing \(a\). It must meet both \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{a,c\}\), hence must be \(\{b,c\}\). Then no edge \(\{a,d\}\) with \(d\notin\{b,c\}\) is possible, since it would be disjoint from \(\{b,c\}\). Thus the graph is contained in a triangle and has at most \(3\le N-1\) edges.

#### 2.2. Graphs with matching number at most \(2\)

We need the following special bounds:

- A graph on \(7\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.
- A graph on \(8\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

In the polynomial estimates below we only need the weaker bound \(12\) in the \(7\)-vertex case.

First, observe that any graph \(G\) on \(N\ge6\) vertices with matching number at most \(2\) has a vertex of degree at most \(2\). Suppose not, so every vertex has degree at least \(3\). Since \(N\ge6\), \(G\) contains two disjoint edges, say \(ab\) and \(cd\). Let
\[
U=\{a,b,c,d\},\qquad W=V(G)\setminus U.
\]
Because the matching number is at most \(2\), \(W\) is independent. Pick two distinct vertices \(w,w'\in W\). Each has at least \(3\) neighbors in \(U\). Partition \(U\) into the two matched pairs \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\). For at least one of these pairs, the two vertices \(w,w'\) can be matched to distinct endpoints of the pair; the other matched pair then supplies a third disjoint edge. This gives a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence some vertex has degree at most \(2\).

Next, any graph on \(6\) vertices with at least \(10\) edges and no isolated vertex has a matching of size \(3\). Indeed, otherwise it has a vertex \(v\) of degree \(1\) or \(2\).

If \(\deg(v)=1\), let \(u\) be its neighbor. Removing \(u\) and \(v\) leaves \(4\) vertices spanning at least \(10-5=5\) edges, hence containing two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), this gives a matching of size \(3\).

If \(\deg(v)=2\), let \(u\) be one of the two neighbors of \(v\). Removing \(v\) leaves at least \(8\) edges on \(5\) vertices. Removing \(u\) from those \(5\) vertices deletes at most \(4\) more edges, leaving at least \(4\) edges on \(4\) vertices. Any \(4\)-vertex graph with at least \(4\) edges has two disjoint edges. Together with \(uv\), again we get a matching of size \(3\).

Now consider a graph \(G\) on \(7\) vertices with at least \(12\) edges. If it had matching number at most \(2\), it would have a vertex \(v\) of degree at most \(2\). Then \(G-v\) has \(6\) vertices and at least \(10\) edges. If \(G-v\) has no isolated vertex, the preceding paragraph gives a matching of size \(3\). If \(G-v\) has an isolated vertex, then its remaining \(5\) vertices must span all \(10\) possible edges, so \(G-v\) is \(K_5\) plus an isolated vertex. Since \(G\) has at least \(12\) edges and \(\deg(v)\le2\), we must have \(\deg(v)=2\), and \(v\) has a neighbor inside the \(K_5\). That edge, together with two disjoint edges in the remaining \(K_4\), gives a matching of size \(3\). Contradiction. Thus a \(7\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(11\) edges.

Finally, if a graph on \(8\) vertices had at least \(14\) edges and matching number at most \(2\), deleting a vertex of degree at most \(2\) would leave a \(7\)-vertex graph with at least \(12\) edges, which must contain a matching of size \(3\), contradiction. Hence a \(8\)-vertex graph with matching number at most \(2\) has at most \(13\) edges.

#### 2.3. Intersecting \(3\)-uniform families

If \(\mathcal F\subseteq\binom{[N]}3\) is pairwise intersecting and \(N\ge6\), then
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

Here is a short proof. Place the \(N\) vertices around a circle. In any fixed cyclic ordering, there are \(N\) cyclic intervals of length \(3\). A pairwise intersecting subfamily of such cyclic intervals has size at most \(3\). On the other hand, a fixed \(3\)-set appears as a cyclic interval in exactly \(3!(N-3)!\) cyclic orderings. Since there are \((N-1)!\) cyclic orderings, double-counting pairs \((F,\text{cyclic ordering})\) with \(F\in\mathcal F\) appearing as a cyclic interval gives
\[
|\mathcal F|\cdot 3!(N-3)!\le 3(N-1)!.
\]
Therefore
\[
|\mathcal F|\le \frac{3(N-1)!}{6(N-3)!}
=\frac{(N-1)(N-2)}2
=\binom{N-1}{2}.
\]

### 3. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\)

We split into two subintervals.

#### 3.1. The range \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing triple exists. Indeed, if \(A\in D_3\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Since weights are positive, the existence of any losing set of size at least \(3\) would imply the existence of a losing triple. Hence
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge3).
\]

The losing-pair graph has no two disjoint edges. If \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\) were disjoint losing pairs, then the three remaining weights are each \(<p\), so
\[
1=w_a+w_b+w_c+w_d+\sum_{\text{remaining }i}w_i
<2p+3p\le1,
\]
again a contradiction. Thus the losing-pair graph is intersecting, and by the graph bound above,
\[
d_2\le6.
\]

Therefore
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+6p^2q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_1(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-6p^2q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_1(p).
\]
We now check \(F_1(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\). Differentiating,
\[
F_1'(p)=30pq^4-1.
\]
On this interval, \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^4-1
\ge 30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^4-1
=\frac{256}{125}-1
>0.
\]
Also,
\[
F_1(1/6)=\frac{4505}{46656}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_1(p)>0\) throughout \([1/6,1/5]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

#### 3.2. The range \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le\frac34<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If it had three disjoint losing pairs, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), giving
\[
1<3p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence, using the weaker convenient bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Thus
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4.
\]
Define
\[
F_2(p)=1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-p.
\]
Then
\[
F_2'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+60p^3q^3-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), we have \(p\ge1/5\) and \(q\ge3/4\), so
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4
\ge
18\cdot\frac15\left(\frac34\right)^5
+
15\cdot\frac1{25}\left(\frac34\right)^4
=
\frac{2673}{2560}
>1.
\]
Therefore \(F_2'(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\). Also,
\[
F_2(1/5)=\frac{1316}{78125}>0.
\]
Hence \(F_2(p)>0\) on \([1/5,1/4]\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

Combining the two subintervals proves claim \(1\).

### 4. The case \(n=8\), \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\)

No losing \(4\)-set exists. If \(A\in D_4\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Hence no losing set of size at least \(4\) exists.

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If two losing triples were disjoint, then the two remaining vertices have total weight \(<2p\), so
\[
1<2p+2p\le\frac45<1,
\]
contradiction. Thus, by the intersecting \(3\)-uniform bound with \(N=8\),
\[
d_3\le \binom72=21.
\]

The losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\). If there were three disjoint losing pairs, the two remaining weights would contribute \(<2p\), so
\[
1<3p+2p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore, by the \(8\)-vertex graph bound,
\[
d_2\le13.
\]

Consequently,
\[
\Pr[S\le p]\le q^8+8pq^7+13p^2q^6+21p^3q^5.
\]
Define
\[
F_8(p)=1-q^8-8pq^7-13p^2q^6-21p^3q^5-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_8(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_8'(p)=30pq^6+15p^2q^5+105p^3q^4-1.
\]
For \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), we have \(p\ge1/6\) and \(q\ge4/5\), so
\[
30pq^6-1
\ge
30\cdot\frac16\left(\frac45\right)^6-1
=
\frac{4096}{3125}-1
>0.
\]
Thus \(F_8'(p)>0\). Also,
\[
F_8(1/6)=\frac{115305}{1679616}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_8(p)>0\) on \([1/6,1/5]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(2\).

### 5. The case \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), with losing-pair matching number at most \(2\)

Assume \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\).

Every set of size at least \(5\) is winning. Indeed, if \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A)=1-w(A^c)>1-2p\ge p.
\]
Thus
\[
D_j=\varnothing\qquad(j\ge5).
\]

The losing triples are pairwise intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_3\) were disjoint, then the remaining vertex has weight \(<p\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w(\text{remaining vertex})
<2p+p\le1,
\]
contradiction. Therefore
\[
d_3\le \binom62=15.
\]

The losing \(4\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,B\in D_4\) had \(|A\cap B|=1\), then \(A\cup B=[7]\), and
\[
w(A)+w(B)=w(A\cup B)+w(A\cap B)=1+w(A\cap B)>1.
\]
But \(w(A)+w(B)\le2p\le2/3\), contradiction. Hence any two losing \(4\)-sets meet in at least \(2\) points. Taking complements, the complements of the losing \(4\)-sets form an intersecting \(3\)-uniform family on \(7\) vertices. Therefore
\[
d_4\le \binom62=15.
\]

By hypothesis, the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), so, using the convenient weaker bound,
\[
d_2\le12.
\]

Hence
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
\le q^7+7pq^6+12p^2q^5+15p^3q^4+15p^4q^3.
\]
Define
\[
F_3(p)
=
1-q^7-7pq^6-12p^2q^5-15p^3q^4-15p^4q^3-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr[S>p]\ge p+F_3(p).
\]
Differentiating,
\[
F_3'(p)=18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2-1.
\]

We verify that this derivative is positive on \([1/4,1/3]\). Put
\[
r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
18pq^5+15p^2q^4+45p^4q^2
=
q^6(18r+15r^2+45r^4).
\]
Since \(q=(1+r)^{-1}\), it is enough to show
\[
18r+15r^2+45r^4>(1+r)^6.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H(r):=45r^4+15r^2+18r-(1+r)^6>0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
H(r)=-1+12r-20r^3+30r^4-6r^5-r^6.
\]
For \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\),
\[
H(r)
\ge
-1+12\cdot\frac13
-20\left(\frac12\right)^3
-6\left(\frac12\right)^5
-\left(\frac12\right)^6
=
\frac{19}{64}>0.
\]
Thus \(F_3'(p)>0\) on \([1/4,1/3]\). Finally,
\[
F_3(1/4)=\frac{231}{8192}>0.
\]
Therefore \(F_3(p)>0\) throughout \([1/4,1/3]\), and hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]>p.
\]

This proves claim \(3\).

### 6. The degree-sequence test for losing uniform families

Let \(\mathcal H\subseteq D\) be a \(k\)-uniform family of losing sets. For each \(i\in[n]\), define
\[
d_i=|\{A\in\mathcal H:i\in A\}|.
\]
Let
\[
d_{(1)}\le d_{(2)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}
\]
be the nondecreasing rearrangement of \(d_1,\dots,d_n\). Let
\[
s=\left\lfloor\frac1p\right\rfloor,
\qquad
\theta=1-sp.
\]
Because \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), we have \(n\ge s+1\), so \(d_{(s+1)}\) is defined.

Since every \(A\in\mathcal H\) is losing,
\[
w(A)\le p.
\]
Summing over all \(A\in\mathcal H\),
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{A\in\mathcal H}w(A)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n d_i w_i.
\]
Relabel the coordinates so that
\[
d_1\le d_2\le\cdots\le d_n.
\]
We claim
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
-
\left(p\sum_{j=1}^s d_j+\theta d_{s+1}\right)\\
&=
\sum_{i=1}^s d_i(w_i-p)
+
\sum_{i=s+1}^n d_iw_i
-
\theta d_{s+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(i\le s\), \(d_i\le d_{s+1}\) and \(w_i-p<0\), so
\[
d_i(w_i-p)\ge d_{s+1}(w_i-p).
\]
For \(i\ge s+1\), \(d_i\ge d_{s+1}\), so
\[
d_iw_i\ge d_{s+1}w_i.
\]
Therefore the difference above is at least
\[
d_{s+1}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^s(w_i-p)+\sum_{i=s+1}^n w_i-\theta
\right).
\]
Since \(\sum_iw_i=1\), this equals
\[
d_{s+1}(1-sp-\theta)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n d_iw_i
\ge
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}.
\]
Combining with
\[
\sum_i d_iw_i\le p|\mathcal H|
\]
gives
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}
\le
p|\mathcal H|.
\]
This proves claim \(4\).

### 7. Minimal-counterexample merge lemma

Fix \(p\). Consider positive weight vectors with all weights \(<p\), total weight \(1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. A strict counterexample means a vector for which
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Assume \(w\) is such a strict counterexample with minimal positive support.

Let two weights be
\[
x\le y,\qquad x+y<p.
\]
Let
\[
R=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell
\]
be the residual random sum over all other coordinates.

Now merge the two weights \(x,y\) into a single weight \(x+y\). Since \(x+y<p\), the merged vector is still admissible and has one fewer positive coordinate. Let \(T\) denote the merged random sum:
\[
T=R+(x+y)u,
\]
where \(u\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\) is independent of \(R\).

By minimality of the original counterexample, the merged vector is not a strict counterexample. Therefore
\[
\Pr[T>p]\ge p.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\Pr[S>p]<p.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]<0.
\]

We compute this difference exactly. Conditioning on the two Bernoulli variables attached to \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]
={}&q^2\Pr[R>p]
+pq\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&+pq\Pr[R>p-y]
+p^2\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\end{aligned}
\]
For the merged variable,
\[
\Pr[T>p]
=
q\Pr[R>p]
+
p\Pr[R>p-x-y].
\]
Subtracting,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
={}&pq\bigl(
-\Pr[R>p]
+\Pr[R>p-x]\\
&\qquad+\Pr[R>p-y]
-\Pr[R>p-x-y]
\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Pr[S>p]-\Pr[T>p]
=
pq\Bigl(
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y]
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(pq>0\) and the left-hand side is \(<0\), we obtain
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]
This proves claim \(5\).

This is a partial theorem and does not solve the original problem for all p and all nonnegative weights.",

## writeup_r5_81b8e6: The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
is equivalent, for the same weights, to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
With
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family.

It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

The result proved below is the following **partial structural reduction**. Let \(m\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), let \([m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\), and let \(2^{[m]}\) denote the set of all subsets of \([m]\). Let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\in[0,\infty)\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), and for every \(S\subseteq[m]\) define
\[
w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}w_i,
\]
with the empty sum equal to \(0\). Let \(p\in(0,1)\), and set \(q=1-p\). A Bernoulli\((t)\) random variable is a \(\{0,1\}\)-valued random variable equal to \(1\) with probability \(t\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((q)\) random variables. For \(t\in[0,1]\) and \(\mathcal A\subseteq2^{[m]}\), define the \(t\)-biased measure on the discrete cube by
\[
\mu_t(\mathcal A)=\sum_{S\in\mathcal A} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|},
\]
with the usual endpoint convention \(0^0=1\). Define
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},
\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\}.
\]
For a positive integer \(r\), a family \(\mathcal H\subseteq2^{[m]}\) is called \(r\)-wise intersecting if, for every \(H_1,\dots,H_r\in\mathcal H\), not necessarily distinct,
\[
H_1\cap\cdots\cap H_r\neq\varnothing.
\]

Then the following statements hold.

1. For the same fixed weight vector \(w_1,\dots,w_m\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i \xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]

2. The complementation map \(A\mapsto [m]\setminus A\) is a bijection from \(\mathcal D\) onto \(\mathcal F\), and
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F).
\]

3. For every positive integer \(r\) satisfying \(rp\le1\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting. In particular, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting.

This differs from the original problem because it does not classify all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally. It proves a reduction and structural consequence for each fixed admissible weight vector and each fixed \(p\in(0,1)\).

## Proof

Let
\[
V=\{i\in[m]:v_i=1\}.
\]
Because the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, for every \(A\subseteq[m]\),
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}.
\]
Thus \(V\) has law \(\mu_p\). Similarly, if
\[
\Xi=\{i\in[m]:\xi_i=1\},
\]
then \(\Xi\) has law \(\mu_q\), since
\[
\Pr[\Xi=B]=q^{|B|}p^{m-|B|}
\]
for every \(B\subseteq[m]\).

First prove the complementation statement. For every \(A\subseteq[m]\),
\[
w([m]\setminus A)=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i-w(A)=1-w(A).
\]
Therefore
\[
A\in\mathcal D
\iff w(A)<p
\iff 1-w(A)>1-p=q
\iff w([m]\setminus A)>q
\iff [m]\setminus A\in\mathcal F.
\]
Thus complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\).

Using this bijection,
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)
=\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}p^{|A|}q^{m-|A|}
=\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}p^{m-|B|}q^{|B|}
=\sum_{B\in\mathcal F}q^{|B|}(1-q)^{m-|B|}
=\mu_q(\mathcal F),
\]
because \(1-q=p\).

Now,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=\Pr[w(V)\ge p]
=1-\Pr[w(V)<p]
=1-\mu_p(\mathcal D).
\]
Also,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]
=\Pr[w(\Xi)>q]
=\mu_q(\mathcal F).
\]
Since \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\), we get
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\iff
1-\mu_p(\mathcal D)\ge p
\iff
\mu_p(\mathcal D)\le q
\iff
\mu_q(\mathcal F)\le q
\iff
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
This proves the equivalence.

It remains to prove the \(r\)-wise intersection property. Let \(r\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) satisfy \(rp\le1\). Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist \(B_1,\dots,B_r\in\mathcal F\) such that
\[
B_1\cap\cdots\cap B_r=\varnothing.
\]
For each \(i\in[m]\), the element \(i\) is missing from at least one of the sets \(B_1,\dots,B_r\). Hence
\[
\#\{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}:i\in B_j\}\le r-1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)
=
\sum_{j=1}^r\sum_{i\in B_j}w_i
=
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i\,\#\{j:i\in B_j\}
\le
(r-1)\sum_{i=1}^m w_i
=r-1.
\]
On the other hand, since every \(B_j\in\mathcal F\),
\[
w(B_j)>q=1-p
\]
for every \(j\), and so
\[
\sum_{j=1}^r w(B_j)>r(1-p)=r-rp\ge r-1,
\]
because \(rp\le1\). This contradicts the previous inequality. Hence every \(r\) members of \(\mathcal F\) have nonempty intersection, so \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting.

Finally, if \(k\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\) and \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(kp\le1\). Applying the preceding result with \(r=k\), the family \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting.

Because all weights are nonnegative, \(\mathcal F\) is also increasing: if \(B\in\mathcal F\) and \(B\subseteq C\subseteq[m]\), then \(w(C)\ge w(B)>q\), so \(C\in\mathcal F\). Thus \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family, namely membership is determined by the strict linear inequality \(w(B)>q\).

This reduction is useful for strict counterexamples as follows. A strict failure of the original inequality for fixed \(p\) and weights means
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]
By the equivalence above, this is the same as \(\mu_q(\mathcal F)>q\). Therefore, on the reciprocal interval \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), any strict counterexample produces an increasing linear-threshold family \(\mathcal F\) whose \(q\)-biased measure exceeds \(q\) and which is \(k\)-wise intersecting.

With [m]={1,...,m}, w(S)=sum_{i in S} w_i, D={A subset [m]: w(A)<p}, F={B subset [m]: w(B)>q}, and mu_t(G)=sum_{S in G} t^{|S|}(1-t)^{m-|S|}, complementation maps D bijectively to F and mu_p(D)=mu_q(F). For every positive integer r with r p <= 1, F is r-wise intersecting, meaning every r not necessarily distinct members have nonempty common intersection; hence, for every positive integer k with p in [1/(k+1),1/k], F is k-wise intersecting. This is a partial structural reduction, not a complete classification of all p in the original question.",
  "is_relaxation": true
}

## writeup_r7_1377dd: For every integer \(k\ge3\), every \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), every finite set of nonnegative weights summing to \(1\) with at most \(k+3\) positive entries, and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), the proof establishes
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained special case. Let $k\ge 3$ be an integer, let $p$ be a real number satisfying
\[
\frac1{k+1}\le p\le \frac1k,
\]
and set $q=1-p$. Let $m\ge1$, let $w_1,\dots,w_m$ be nonnegative real numbers with $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and assume that at most $k+3$ of the weights $w_i$ are positive. Let $v_1,\dots,v_m$ be independent $\{0,1\}$-valued random variables with $\Pr[v_i=1]=p$ and $\Pr[v_i=0]=q$. Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

This is a relaxation of the Original Problem: it does not characterize all probabilities $p$ for arbitrary numbers of positive weights. It proves the desired inequality only for $p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]$ under the additional hypothesis that the number of positive weights is at most $k+3$.

## Proof

Delete all zero weights and reindex the positive weights as $w_1,\dots,w_n$. This does not change the random sum. Thus $n\le k+3$, $w_i>0$, and $\sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1$.

If some $w_i\ge p$, then the event $\{v_i=1\}$ implies $\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p$, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_j w_jv_j\ge p\right]\ge \Pr[v_i=1]=p.
\]
Hence we may assume from now on that
\[
0<w_i<p\qquad\text{for every }i.
\]

For $A\subseteq[n]:=\{1,\dots,n\}$, write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Let
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[n]: w(A)<p\}
\]
be the family of bad sets. Also define the $p$-biased measure of a family $\mathcal F\subseteq 2^{[n]}$ by
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal F)=\sum_{A\in\mathcal F}p^{|A|}q^{n-|A|}.
\]
Equivalently, if $R\subseteq[n]$ is formed by including each element independently with probability $p$, then $\mu_p(\mathcal F)=\Pr[R\in\mathcal F]$.

The desired inequality is equivalent to
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q,
\]
because $D$ is exactly the event $\sum_i w_iv_i<p$.

Since all $w_i<p$ and $\sum_i w_i=1$, we must have $n\ge k+1$: indeed, if $n\le k$, then
\[
1=\sum_i w_i<np\le kp\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Thus
\[
n\in\{k+1,k+2,k+3\}.
\]

### The support-excess lemma

Write $n=k+s$. Suppose $B_1,\dots,B_t\in D$ are pairwise disjoint, and put $r_j=|B_j|$. Then
\[
\sum_{j=1}^t(r_j-1)<s.
\]

Indeed, let $U=\bigcup_{j=1}^t B_j$. Since the $B_j$ are disjoint and each lies in $D$,
\[
w(U)=\sum_{j=1}^t w(B_j)<tp.
\]
Therefore
\[
w(U^c)>1-tp.
\]
If $\sum_j(r_j-1)\ge s$, then
\[
|U^c|
=k+s-\sum_j r_j
=k-t+s-\sum_j(r_j-1)
\le k-t.
\]
If $k-t<0$, this is impossible because $|U^c|\ge0$. Otherwise,
\[
w(U^c)\le |U^c|p\le (k-t)p\le 1-tp,
\]
using $kp\le1$. This contradicts $w(U^c)>1-tp$. Hence
\[
\sum_j(r_j-1)<s.
\]

### The cases $n=k+1$ and $n=k+2$

First suppose $n=k+1$, so $s=1$. Applying the support-excess lemma with $t=1$ shows that every set in $D$ has size at most $1$. Since every singleton has weight $<p$, we get
\[
\mu_p(D)=q^{k+1}+(k+1)pq^k
=q^k(q+(k+1)p)
=q^k(1+kp).
\]

Now suppose $n=k+2$, so $s=2$. The support-excess lemma with $t=1$ shows that every set in $D$ has size at most $2$. Let $e_2$ be the number of bad pairs, i.e. $2$-element sets in $D$. The lemma with two disjoint bad pairs would give
\[
(2-1)+(2-1)=2<s,
\]
which is false, so no two bad pairs are disjoint.

Thus the graph of bad pairs has matching number at most $1$. By the Erdős-Gallai matching bound, a graph on $N$ vertices with matching number at most $1$ has at most $N-1$ edges when $N\ge4$. Here $N=k+2\ge5$, so
\[
e_2\le k+1.
\]
Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^{k+2}+(k+2)pq^{k+1}+(k+1)p^2q^k.
\]
Factoring out $q^k$ gives
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^k\bigl(q^2+(k+2)pq+(k+1)p^2\bigr).
\]
Since $p+q=1$,
\[
q^2+(k+2)pq+(k+1)p^2
=(q^2+2pq+p^2)+k(pq+p^2)
=1+kp.
\]
Thus again
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^k(1+kp).
\]

### The case $n=k+3$

Now assume $n=k+3$, so $s=3$.

The support-excess lemma implies three structural facts:

1. Every bad set has size at most $3$.
2. There are no three pairwise disjoint bad pairs.
3. No bad pair is disjoint from a bad triple.

Let $e_2$ be the number of bad pairs and $e_3$ the number of bad triples. Set
\[
\lambda=\frac pq.
\]
We prove
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3\le 2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]

If $e_3=0$, then the bad-pair graph has no matching of size $3$. By the Erdős-Gallai matching bound, a graph on $N$ vertices with matching number at most $2$ has at most
\[
\max\left\{\binom52,\binom22+2(N-2)\right\}
=\max\{10,2N-3\}
\]
edges. Here $N=k+3$, so
\[
e_2\le \max\{10,2k+3\}.
\]
If $k\ge4$, this gives $e_2\le2k+3$. If $k=3$, then $p\ge1/4$ and $q\le3/4$, so $\lambda=p/q\ge1/3$, and therefore
\[
e_2\le10\le9+4\lambda=2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]
Thus the desired bound holds when $e_3=0$.

Now suppose $e_3>0$. Consider the $3$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal H$ whose edges are the bad triples. If $T,U\in\mathcal H$ and $|T\cap U|\le1$, then $U$ contains a pair disjoint from $T$. Since $D$ is downward closed, that pair is also bad, contradicting the fact that no bad pair is disjoint from a bad triple. Therefore $\mathcal H$ is pairwise $2$-intersecting.

We use the following elementary bound: if $\mathcal H$ is a pairwise $2$-intersecting family of triples on an $N$-element ground set with $N\ge6$, then
\[
|\mathcal H|\le N-2.
\]
Indeed, if all triples contain a common pair, there are at most $N-2$ choices for the third vertex. Otherwise, choose two triples
\[
T=\{a,b,c\},\qquad U=\{a,b,d\}
\]
with intersection $\{a,b\}$, and choose a triple $V$ not containing both $a,b$. To meet both $T$ and $U$ in at least two points, $V$ must be either $\{a,c,d\}$ or $\{b,c,d\}$. Then every triple containing $\{a,b\}$ must have third vertex $c$ or $d$, and every triple not containing $\{a,b\}$ is contained in $\{a,b,c,d\}$. Hence the whole family is contained in a fixed $4$-set, so it has at most $\binom43=4\le N-2$ triples because $N\ge6$.

Here $N=k+3\ge6$, so
\[
e_3\le k+1.
\]

Fix one bad triple $T$. Every bad pair intersects $T$. Hence bad pairs are either contained inside $T$, contributing at most $3$, or are cross pairs with one vertex in $T$ and one vertex in $T^c$.

The cross pairs form a bipartite graph with parts of sizes $3$ and $k$. This bipartite graph has no matching of size $3$, because such a matching would be three disjoint bad pairs. We claim it has at most $2k$ edges. If every vertex in the $k$-side has degree at most $2$, this is immediate. Otherwise, some vertex on the $k$-side has degree $3$, adjacent to all three vertices of $T$. Removing that vertex, the remaining bipartite graph has no matching of size $2$, for such a matching together with the removed vertex would form a matching of size $3$. A bipartite graph with no matching of size $2$ is a star, so it has at most $\max\{3,k-1\}$ edges. Thus the total number of cross edges is at most
\[
3+\max\{3,k-1\}\le2k
\]
for $k\ge3$.

Therefore
\[
e_2\le3+2k.
\]
Combining this with $e_3\le k+1$ gives
\[
e_2+\lambda e_3
\le 2k+3+(k+1)\lambda.
\]

Now compute $\mu_p(D)$ layer by layer. Since every bad set has size at most $3$,
\[
\mu_p(D)
=
q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}+e_2p^2q^{k+1}+e_3p^3q^k.
\]
Using the bound on $e_2+\lambda e_3$,
\[
e_2p^2q^{k+1}+e_3p^3q^k
=p^2q^{k+1}\left(e_2+\frac pq e_3\right)
\le p^2q^{k+1}\left(2k+3+(k+1)\frac pq\right).
\]
Hence
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^{k+3}+(k+3)pq^{k+2}+(2k+3)p^2q^{k+1}+(k+1)p^3q^k.
\]
Factoring out $q^k$,
\[
\mu_p(D)
\le q^k\bigl(q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3\bigr).
\]
The bracket simplifies as follows:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&q^3+(k+3)pq^2+(2k+3)p^2q+(k+1)p^3\\
&=(q^3+3pq^2+3p^2q+p^3)+k(pq^2+2p^2q+p^3)\\
&=(p+q)^3+kp(q^2+2pq+p^2)\\
&=1+kp(p+q)^2\\
&=1+kp.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus, in all three possible cases $n=k+1,k+2,k+3$, we have shown
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^k(1+kp).
\]

It remains to prove
\[
q^k(1+kp)\le q.
\]
Since $q>0$, this is equivalent to
\[
(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp)\le1.
\]
Define
\[
g(p)=(1-p)^{k-1}(1+kp).
\]
Then
\[
g'(p)
=-(k-1)(1-p)^{k-2}(1+kp)+k(1-p)^{k-1}
=(1-p)^{k-2}(1-k^2p).
\]
For $p\ge1/(k+1)$ and $k\ge3$,
\[
k^2p\ge \frac{k^2}{k+1}>1,
\]
so $g'(p)<0$. Therefore $g$ is decreasing on $[1/(k+1),1/k]$, and its maximum occurs at $p=1/(k+1)$.

At $p=1/(k+1)$,
\[
g(p)=\left(\frac{k}{k+1}\right)^{k-1}\frac{2k+1}{k+1}.
\]
We need this to be at most $1$, equivalently
\[
\left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1}\ge \frac{2k+1}{k+1}.
\]
By the binomial theorem,
\[
\left(1+\frac1k\right)^{k-1}
\ge 1+\frac{k-1}{k}+\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2}.
\]
A direct calculation gives
\[
1+\frac{k-1}{k}+\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2k^2}
-\frac{2k+1}{k+1}
=
\frac{k(k-3)(k+1)+2}{2k^2(k+1)}
\ge0
\]
for every $k\ge3$. Hence $g(p)\le1$ throughout the interval.

Therefore
\[
\mu_p(D)\le q^k(1+kp)\le q.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_iv_i\ge p\right]
=1-\mu_p(D)
\ge1-q
=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

## writeup_r7_5a8c96: The proof establishes the restricted support-9 statement: for every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\) and \(w_i<p\) for all \(i\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
The combinatorial bounds on \(d_3,d_4,d_5\), the spectral bounds used for \(KG(9,3)\) and \(J(9,4)\), and the final finite polynomial check are all valid for the stated parameter range.
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following restricted finite-support statement. Let \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that \(w_i<p\) for every \(i\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1\). Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=p\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=q\). Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
This is a relaxation/special case of the original problem: it fixes the support size to \(9\), assumes all weights are positive and strictly smaller than \(p\), restricts \(p\) to \([1/5,1/4]\), and proves a strict \(>p\) threshold conclusion rather than determining all \(p\) for all support sizes.

## Proof

For \(A\subseteq[9]\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Let
\[
D_j=\{A\subseteq[9]: |A|=j,\ w(A)\le p\},\qquad d_j=|D_j|,
\]
and put
\[
r=\frac pq.
\]

If \(X=\{i:v_i=1\}\), then
\[
\Pr[X=A]=p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}=q^9 r^{|A|}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Since \(q=1/(1+r)\), we have
\[
q=q^9(1+r)^8.
\]
Therefore it suffices to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j<(1+r)^8,
\]
because then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]<q
\]
and so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

Since \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), we have
\[
r=\frac p{1-p}\in\left[\frac14,\frac13\right].
\]

### 1. No losing layers of size at least \(6\)

If \(A\in D_j\) with \(j\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so, because each \(w_i<p\),
\[
w(A^c)<3p\le 1-p=q.
\]
Thus
\[
w(A)=1-w(A^c)>p,
\]
contradicting \(A\in D_j\). Therefore
\[
d_j=0\qquad(j\ge6).
\]

### 2. Winning pairs and the bound on \(d_2\)

Define the winning-pair graph \(W\) on vertex set \([9]\) by
\[
ij\in E(W)\iff w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let
\[
m=|E(W)|.
\]
Then a pair is losing exactly when it is not an edge of \(W\), so
\[
d_2=\binom92-m=36-m.
\]

### 3. Losing triples

Let
\[
H=D_3,\qquad h=d_3.
\]
Suppose \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint triples. Let
\[
C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U),
\]
so \(|C|=3\). If some pair \(e\subset C\) were losing, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex of \(C\), then
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence every pair inside \(C\) is winning, i.e. \(C\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Let \(I(H)\) be the number of unordered disjoint pairs of triples in \(H\), and let \(\tau(W)\) be the number of triangles in \(W\). For each triangle \(C\) of \(W\), the remaining \(6\) vertices can be partitioned into two unordered triples in
\[
\frac12\binom63=10
\]
ways. Therefore
\[
I(H)\le 10\,\tau(W).
\]

Now use the Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\), whose vertices are the \(3\)-subsets of \([9]\), with two triples adjacent when they are disjoint. It has
\[
N=\binom93=84
\]
vertices, degree
\[
\binom63=20,
\]
and least eigenvalue \(-10\). If \(x\) is the indicator vector of \(H\), then the standard spectral supersaturation inequality gives
\[
I(H)\ge \frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]
Indeed,
\[
2I(H)=x^\top A x\ge \frac{20}{84}h^2-10\left(h-\frac{h^2}{84}\right)
=\frac5{14}h^2-10h,
\]
so
\[
I(H)\ge \frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]

We also need the crude cap \(h\le56\). If \(H\) contained three pairwise disjoint triples, those triples would partition \([9]\), and their weights would sum to at most \(3p\le3/4<1\), impossible. Thus \(H\) has no matching of size \(3\). Counting partitions of \([9]\) into three unordered triples gives the bound \(h\le56\): there are \(280\) such partitions, each triple lies in \(10\) partitions, and if \(h>56\), the average number of \(H\)-triples in such a partition would exceed \(2\), forcing a partition with three \(H\)-triples.

Let \(T_m\) be the maximum possible number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges. Then
\[
\frac5{28}h(h-28)\le I(H)\le 10T_m,
\]
so
\[
h(h-28)\le56T_m.
\]
Define
\[
H_m=\max\{h\in\{0,1,\dots,56\}:h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]
Then
\[
d_3=h\le H_m.
\]

### 4. The formula for \(T_m\)

Write
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
where \(a\) is maximal with \(\binom a2\le m\). Then
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]

The lower bound is achieved by taking a clique \(K_a\), then adding one extra vertex adjacent to exactly \(b\) vertices of the clique. This graph has
\[
\binom a2+b=m
\]
edges and
\[
\binom a3+\binom b2
\]
triangles.

For the upper bound, let \(g(m)=\binom a3+\binom b2\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every graph with \(m\) edges has at most \(g(m)\) triangles. Remove isolated vertices. Let \(\delta\) be a minimum degree. Since \(m<\binom{a+1}{2}\), one has \(\delta\le a-1\). If \(v\) is a vertex of degree \(\delta\), then the number of triangles through \(v\) is at most \(\binom\delta2\), and the remaining graph has \(m-\delta\) edges. Hence
\[
\tau(G)\le g(m-\delta)+\binom\delta2.
\]
It remains to check
\[
g(m)-g(m-\delta)\ge \binom\delta2.
\]
If \(\delta\le b\), then
\[
g(m)-g(m-\delta)-\binom\delta2=\delta(b-\delta)\ge0.
\]
If \(\delta>b\), put \(t=\delta-b\). Then
\[
m-\delta=\binom{a-1}{2}+(a+b-\delta-1),
\]
and a direct calculation gives
\[
g(m)-g(m-\delta)-\binom\delta2=t(a-\delta-1)\ge0,
\]
because \(\delta\le a-1\). Thus the formula for \(T_m\) is proved.

### 5. Losing \(4\)-sets

First, \(D_4\) is intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint, and \(z\) were the remaining vertex, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z<p+p+p\le\frac34,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\). By the Erdős–Ko–Rado bound, equivalently by Hoffman’s bound applied to \(KG(9,4)\), whose degree is \(5\) and least eigenvalue is \(-4\),
\[
d_4\le \binom83=56.
\]

Now let \(A\in D_4\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)+w_z<p+p+p+p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Thus the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), and hence has at most \(4\) edges. Since \(K_5\) has \(10\) edges, \(W[B]\) has at least \(6\) winning edges.

Counting incidences \((e,B)\), where \(e\in E(W)\) and \(e\subset B\), gives
\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m.
\]
Therefore
\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

### 6. Losing \(5\)-sets

Let \(A\in D_5\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching consisting of two losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p\le\frac34,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[B]\) must hit all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\). Since each edge of \(K_4\) lies in exactly one of those perfect matchings, we must have
\[
|W[B]|\ge3.
\]
Counting incidences gives
\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]
so
\[
d_5\le7m.
\]

We also have the independent cap
\[
d_5\le21.
\]
Indeed, if \(A,A'\in D_5\), then their complements \(C=A^c\), \(C'=(A')^c\) are \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\). If \(|C\cap C'|\le1\), then
\[
w(A)+w(A')=1-w(C\cap C')+w(A\cap A')>1-p\ge\frac34>2p,
\]
contradicting \(w(A)+w(A')\le2p\). Thus the complements form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\).

The sharp finite bound for such a family is \(21\). One quick linear-algebra certificate is as follows. Index all \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\), and let \(A_i\) be the Johnson-scheme matrix joining two \(4\)-sets whose intersection has size \(4-i\). The common eigenvalue table is

\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrrr}
 & A_0 & A_1 & A_2 & A_3 & A_4\\
\hline
j=0 & 1 & 20 & 60 & 40 & 5\\
j=1 & 1 & 11 & 6 & -14 & -4\\
j=2 & 1 & 4 & -8 & 0 & 3\\
j=3 & 1 & -1 & -3 & 5 & -2\\
j=4 & 1 & -4 & 6 & -4 & 1
\end{array}
\]
Thus
\[
M=20A_0-A_1-A_2+\frac52A_3-8A_4
\]
has eigenvalues
\[
0,\ 0,\ 0,\ \frac{105}{2},\ 0,
\]
so \(M\) is positive semidefinite. If \(\mathcal F\) is \(2\)-intersecting and \(x\) is its indicator vector, then \(x^\top A_3x=x^\top A_4x=0\), while
\[
x^\top(A_1+A_2)x=|\mathcal F|(|\mathcal F|-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
0\le x^\top Mx
=20|\mathcal F|-|\mathcal F|(|\mathcal F|-1),
\]
so \(|\mathcal F|\le21\).

Consequently,
\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

### 7. The finite polynomial verification

Combining the layer bounds, for the actual edge count \(m=|E(W)|\in\{0,1,\dots,36\}\), we have
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j
\le
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
P_m(r)>0
\]
for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\), where
\[
P_m(r)
=
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right).
\]

The following exact Python/Sympy script verifies this by rational arithmetic and Sturm root counting.

```python
import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")
L = sp.Rational(1, 4)
U = sp.Rational(1, 3)

def C(n: int, k: int) -> int:
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return comb(n, k)

def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Write m = C(a,2)+b with 0 <= b < a.
    a = 0
    while C(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - C(a, 2)
    return C(a, 3) + C(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)

    P = sp.expand(
        (1 + r)**8
        - (1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5)
    )

    poly = sp.Poly(P, r, domain=sp.QQ)

    # Exact endpoint positivity.
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, L)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, L))
    assert sp.simplify(P.subs(r, U)) > 0, (m, P.subs(r, U))

    # Exact Sturm root count on [1/4, 1/3].
    assert poly.count_roots(L, U) == 0, (m, P)

print("All 37 polynomials are positive on [1/4, 1/3].")
```

This is not a numerical sampling check. Each \(P_m\) has rational coefficients, the endpoints \(1/4\) and \(1/3\) are rational, and `count_roots` uses exact root isolation/Sturm theory. Since the script verifies that \(P_m\) has no real root in \([1/4,1/3]\) and is positive at both endpoints, continuity implies
\[
P_m(r)>0
\]
for every real \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\).

Therefore
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]
As shown at the beginning,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]<q,
\]
and hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
This proves the claimed support-\(9\) result for every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\).

  "problem_solved": "For every real p in [1/5,1/4], every choice of positive real numbers w_1,...,w_9 with w_i<p for all i and sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1, and independent Bernoulli(p) random variables v_1,...,v_9, the inequality Pr[sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i > p] > p holds.",

}
```

## writeup_r8_6a5435: The proof establishes the restricted support-\(9\) strict theorem: for every \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This proof establishes the following support-$9$ strict-reduced theorem.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i\in\{1,\dots,9\},
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, i.e.
\[
\Pr[v_i=1]=p,\qquad \Pr[v_i=0]=1-p.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is not the full original problem: it proves a strict support-\(9\) special case, with the additional hypotheses \(m=9\), \(0<w_i<p\), and with the strict event \(>p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
For \(A\subseteq[9]:=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
d_j=\left|D\cap\binom{[9]}j\right|.
\]

Let \(V=\{i:v_i=1\}\). Then
\[
\Pr[V=A]=p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9 r^{|A|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]>p
\]
is equivalent to
\[
q^9\sum_{j=0}^9d_jr^j<q.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q^9(1+r)^8=q.
\]
Hence it suffices throughout to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\tag{1}
\]

Also, because \(w_i<p\), every singleton is losing, so
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9.
\]

We prove (1) separately on the three intervals
\[
\left[\frac16,\frac15\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac15,\frac14\right],
\qquad
\left[\frac14,\frac13\right).
\]

### 1. The interval \(\frac16\le p\le\frac15\)

Here
\[
\frac15\le r\le\frac14.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(5\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge5\), then \(|A^c|\le4\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction.

Thus only layers \(0,1,2,3,4\) occur.

For losing pairs, four disjoint losing pairs plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<4p+p\le1,
\]
contradicting \(\sum_iw_i=1\). Hence the graph of losing pairs has matching number at most \(3\). By the Erdős–Gallai matching bound for graphs on \(9\) vertices,
\[
d_2\le21.
\]

For losing triples, three disjoint losing triples would cover all \(9\) vertices and have total weight at most \(3p<1\), impossible. Thus the losing triples contain no perfect matching. The standard finite matching bound for \(3\)-uniform hypergraphs on \(9\) vertices gives
\[
d_3\le56.
\]
Equivalently, one can see this by counting partitions of \([9]\) into three triples: there are \(280\) such partitions, each triple lies in \(10\) partitions, and a family with no full partition must miss at least \(28\) triples, so it has at most \(84-28=56\) triples.

For losing \(4\)-sets, two disjoint losing \(4\)-sets plus the remaining vertex would have total weight
\[
<2p+p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting, so by Erdős–Ko–Rado,
\[
d_4\le\binom83=56.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_j d_jr^j
\le
1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies
\[
(1+r)^8-\left(1+9r+21r^2+56r^3+56r^4\right)>0
\]
for all \(r\in[1/5,1/4]\).

### 2. The interval \(\frac15\le p\le\frac14\)

Here
\[
\frac14\le r\le\frac13.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(6\), since if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge6\), then \(|A^c|\le3\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+3p\le1,
\]
impossible.

Define the winning-pair graph
\[
W=\{ij: w_i+w_j>p\},
\]
and let
\[
m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

Let \(H=D\cap\binom{[9]}3\), and put \(h=|H|=d_3\). If two losing triples \(T,U\in H\) are disjoint, then their complement \(C=[9]\setminus(T\cup U)\) has size \(3\). If \(C\) contained a losing pair \(e\), with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(T)+w(U)+w(e)+w_z<4p\le1,
\]
a contradiction. Hence \(C\) must be a triangle of \(W\).

Let \(I(H)\) be the number of unordered disjoint pairs in \(H\), and let \(\tau(W)\) be the number of triangles in \(W\). Each triangle \(C\) of \(W\) supports exactly \(10\) unordered partitions of the other \(6\) vertices into two triples. Hence
\[
I(H)\le10\tau(W).
\]
The Kneser graph \(KG(9,3)\), whose vertices are triples and whose edges join disjoint triples, has \(84\) vertices, degree \(20\), and least eigenvalue \(-10\). Hoffman’s supersaturation bound gives
\[
I(H)\ge\frac5{28}h(h-28).
\]
If \(T_m\) denotes the maximum number of triangles in a graph with \(m\) edges, then
\[
h(h-28)\le56T_m.
\]
The exact value of \(T_m\) is the Kruskal–Katona/colex value:
if
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a,
\]
then
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2.
\]
Together with \(h\le56\), define
\[
H_m=\max\{h\in\{0,1,\dots,56\}:h(h-28)\le56T_m\}.
\]
Then
\[
d_3\le H_m.
\]

For losing \(4\)-sets, let \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}4\), and set \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=5\). If \(B\) contained two disjoint losing pairs plus a leftover vertex, then together with \(A\) those sets would have total weight
\[
<4p\le1,
\]
impossible. Thus the losing-pair graph inside \(B\) has matching number at most \(1\), so it has at most \(4\) edges. Therefore \(W[B]\) has at least \(6\) edges. Counting incidences between winning edges and such \(5\)-sets \(B\),
\[
6d_4\le m\binom73=35m.
\]
Also the losing \(4\)-sets are intersecting, so \(d_4\le56\). Hence
\[
d_4\le A_m:=\min\left\{56,\left\lfloor\frac{35m}{6}\right\rfloor\right\}.
\]

For losing \(5\)-sets, if \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1\le w(A)+p+p\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Thus \(W[B]\) meets every perfect matching of \(K_4\), which requires at least \(3\) winning edges. Counting incidences gives
\[
3d_5\le m\binom72=21m,
\]
so \(d_5\le7m\).

Moreover, complements of losing \(5\)-sets form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\), because if two complements \(B,B'\) met in at most one vertex, then
\[
2q\le w(B)+w(B')<1+p,
\]
contradicting \(p\le1/4<1/3\). By the \(t=2\) Erdős–Ko–Rado bound,
\[
d_5\le21.
\]
Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=\min\{21,7m\}.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
\le
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5.
\]
The exact rational certificate below verifies positivity of
\[
(1+r)^8-
\left(
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+H_mr^3+A_mr^4+B_mr^5
\right)
\]
for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/4,1/3]\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the intervals [1/6,1/5] and [1/5,1/4].
# Uses only integer arithmetic and rational Sturm root counting.

import sympy as sp
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def assert_positive_on_poly(P, lo, hi, tag):
    P = sp.Poly(sp.expand(P), r)
    assert P.eval(lo) > 0, ("left endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(lo))
    assert P.eval(hi) > 0, ("right endpoint failed", tag, P.eval(hi))
    assert P.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, ("root in interval", tag, P)

# Interval [1/6,1/5], i.e. r in [1/5,1/4].
P_low = (1 + r)**8 - (1 + 9*r + 21*r**2 + 56*r**3 + 56*r**4)
assert_positive_on_poly(P_low, sp.Rational(1, 5), sp.Rational(1, 4), "low interval")

# Interval [1/5,1/4], i.e. r in [1/4,1/3].
def tri_max(m: int) -> int:
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def H_bound(m: int) -> int:
    T = tri_max(m)
    best = 0
    for h in range(57):  # h <= 56
        if h * (h - 28) <= 56 * T:
            best = h
    return best

def A_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(56, (35 * m) // 6)

def B_bound(m: int) -> int:
    return min(21, 7 * m)

for m in range(37):
    H = H_bound(m)
    A = A_bound(m)
    B = B_bound(m)
    P_mid = (1 + r)**8 - (
        1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2 + H*r**3 + A*r**4 + B*r**5
    )
    assert_positive_on_poly(
        P_mid,
        sp.Rational(1, 4),
        sp.Rational(1, 3),
        ("middle interval", m, H, A, B),
    )

print("Lower and middle interval certificates passed.")
```

Because the code uses exact rational endpoints and `Poly.count_roots`, which is Sturm root counting over the rationals, the verification is exact: endpoint positivity plus absence of real roots on the interval implies strict positivity throughout.

### 3. The interval \(\frac14\le p<\frac13\)

Here
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

No losing set has size at least \(7\), because if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
1=w(A)+w(A^c)<p+2p<1,
\]
a contradiction.

Again let
\[
W=\{ij:w_i+w_j>p\},\qquad m=|W|.
\]
Then
\[
d_2=36-m.
\]

We use the following bounds.

#### 3.1. Triple layer

As before, losing triples have no perfect matching, so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### 3.2. The \(D_4\) improvement

Every losing set is independent in \(W\): if \(A\in D\) contained an edge \(ij\in W\), then
\[
w(A)\ge w_i+w_j>p,
\]
contradiction.

Also \(D_4:=D\cap\binom{[9]}4\) is intersecting. Indeed, if \(A,A'\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(A')+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in the winning-pair graph \(W\).

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph. Indeed, for any induced subgraph, let \(x\) be a vertex of minimum weight and \(y\) one of maximum weight. If \(w_x+w_y>p\), then \(y\) is adjacent to every other vertex; if \(w_x+w_y\le p\), then \(x\) is isolated.

Exact enumeration of threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices gives the following upper bounds for the largest intersecting family of independent \(4\)-sets in a threshold graph with \(m\) edges:
\[
I_0=56,\quad I_1=50,\quad I_2=45,\quad I_3=41,\quad I_4=38,\quad I_5=36,
\]
and
\[
I_m=35\qquad(6\le m\le36).
\]
Therefore
\[
d_4\le I_m.
\]

We also need a weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Since \(w(A)\le p\), each \(B\in\mathcal C\) satisfies
\[
w(B)\ge q.
\]
Also \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Let its vertex degrees be
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Each link of \(\mathcal C\) is an intersecting \(4\)-family on \(8\) vertices, so
\[
\Delta_i\le\frac12\binom84=35.
\]
Now
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le
\sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Subject to \(0\le w_i\le p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the right-hand side is maximized by placing mass \(p\) on the three largest degrees and the remaining mass \(1-3p\) on the fourth largest degree. Therefore
\[
\sum_iw_i\Delta_i
\le
p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4
\le35.
\]
Hence
\[
qd_4\le35,
\]
so
\[
d_4\le\lfloor 35/q\rfloor=\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### 3.3. The \(D_5\) and \(D_6\) bounds

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}5\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(4\). If \(B\) contained a perfect matching of losing pairs, then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(W[B]\) blocks all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), requiring at least \(3\) winning edges.

Thus:
- if \(m\le2\), then \(d_5=0\);
- if \(m=3\), then \(B\) must contain all three winning edges, and there are at most \(6\) such \(4\)-sets;
- if \(m\ge4\), the complements of losing \(5\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting \(4\)-sets, so \(d_5\le21\).

Hence
\[
d_5\le B_m,
\]
where
\[
B_m=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

If \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}6\), then \(B=A^c\) has size \(3\). If some pair in \(B\) were losing, then together with \(A\) and the remaining vertex of \(B\) the total weight would be
\[
<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Therefore \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\). Hence
\[
d_6\le T_m.
\]
Also the complements of losing \(6\)-sets are \(2\)-intersecting triples, so
\[
d_6\le7.
\]
Thus
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

#### 3.4. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k\), define its normalized deficiency by
\[
\epsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and put
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D\cap\binom{[9]}k}\epsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D\cap\binom{[9]}{k+1}\), then all \(k\)-subsets of \(B\) are losing, and
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}(p-w(A))
=(k+1)p-kw(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\), summing over \(B\), and using that each losing \(k\)-set is contained in at most \(9-k\) losing \((k+1)\)-sets, we obtain
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le(9-k)E_k.
\tag{2}
\]

For pairs,
\[
E_2
=
\frac1p\sum_{\substack{ij\\ w_i+w_j\le p}}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8,
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le7E_2.
\]
Using (3),
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\tag{4}
\]

#### 3.5. Exact LP/Sturm certificate

For fixed \(m\), the actual vector
\[
(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6)=(d_3,d_4,d_5,d_6)
\]
satisfies
\[
0\le x_3\le56,
\]
\[
0\le x_4\le C_m(r),
\]
\[
0\le x_5\le B_m,
\]
\[
0\le x_6\le G_m,
\]
and the deficiency-budget constraint
\[
x_3+\frac{x_4}{3}+\frac{x_5+x_6}{5}
\le
196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Thus
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j
=
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+d_3r^3+d_4r^4+d_5r^5+d_6r^6
\]
is bounded above by the optimum of this linear program.

The following code enumerates all LP vertices, checks all threshold-graph \(D_4\) bounds, and verifies by exact Sturm root counting that the LP optimum is always strictly below \((1+r)^8\) for every \(m=0,\dots,36\) and every \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). This includes the desired half-open range \(r\in[1/3,1/2)\).

```python
# Exact certificate for the interval [1/4,1/3), i.e. r in [1/3,1/2).
# All arithmetic is exact over integers/rationals. Sturm root counting is used
# for every one-variable rational inequality.

import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from functools import lru_cache
from math import comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

# ---------- Threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # A threshold graph is generated, up to isomorphism, by adding each new
    # vertex as isolated (0) or dominating (1). The graph invariant checked
    # below is isomorphism-invariant, so one representative per creation
    # sequence is enough.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, b in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if b:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    n = 9

    # List all independent 4-sets of the graph.
    sets = []
    for C in combinations(range(n), 4):
        ok = True
        for i, j in combinations(C, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                ok = False
                break
        if ok:
            mask = sum(1 << i for i in C)
            sets.append(mask)

    # Conflict graph: two independent 4-sets conflict if they are disjoint.
    # An intersecting family is an independent set in this conflict graph.
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    @lru_cache(None)
    def mis(P):
        # Exact maximum independent set by the recurrence:
        # alpha(P)=max(alpha(P\v),1+alpha(P\v-N(v))).
        if P == 0:
            return 0

        # Include isolated conflict-vertices automatically.
        iso = 0
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            if conflict[v] & P == 0:
                iso |= lb
            Q -= lb
        if iso:
            return iso.bit_count() + mis(P & ~iso)

        # Branch on a maximum-degree conflict vertex.
        best_v = None
        best_deg = -1
        Q = P
        while Q:
            lb = Q & -Q
            v = lb.bit_length() - 1
            deg = (conflict[v] & P).bit_count()
            if deg > best_deg:
                best_deg = deg
                best_v = v
            Q -= lb

        v = best_v
        without = mis(P & ~(1 << v))
        with_v = 1 + mis(P & ~(1 << v) & ~conflict[v])
        return max(without, with_v)

    return mis((1 << N) - 1)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))


# ---------- Layer bounds ----------

def tri_max(m):
    # Maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
    a = 0
    while comb(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - comb(a, 2)
    return comb(a, 3) + comb(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))


# ---------- Exact positivity checking for rational functions ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # Multiplication by r is safe since r>0 on [1/3,1/2].
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    if lo == hi:
        assert sp.simplify(num.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        assert sp.simplify(den.subs(r, lo)) > 0, tag
        return

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2


# Breakpoints for the floor bound floor(35(1+r)).
# On each interval the code uses the right endpoint as a safe cap.
breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

# Variables are x3,x4,x5,x6.
cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))  # safe cap on this interval
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with one free variable and the budget constraint active.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                # Need x_free >= 0.
                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                # Need x_free <= cap.
                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("Upper interval certificate passed.")
```

The LP feasible region is a box cut by one halfspace, so every optimum occurs at one of the enumerated vertices: either all variables are at bounds, or the budget constraint is active and three variables are at bounds. The code checks the gap \((1+r)^8-\text{objective}\) at every such vertex. Since every checked gap is strictly positive, the actual layer polynomial is strictly smaller than \((1+r)^8\).

Therefore (1) holds on \([1/3,1/2)\), equivalently on \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\).

Combining the three intervals, for every
\[
\frac16\le p<\frac13
\]
we have
\[
\sum_jd_jr^j<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_jd_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_iw_iv_i>p\right]>1-q=p.
\]
This proves the stated support-\(9\) theorem.

## writeup_r9_42a679: The proof establishes the three stated partial/conditional assertions:

1. For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), nonnegative weights summing to \(1\), and
\[
\rho=\frac{\sum_i w_i^3}{(\sum_i w_i^2)^{3/2}}\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
\]
one has \(\Pr[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p]\ge p\).

2. For a split pair \(a\ge b>0\), \(a+b<p\), with residual sum \(R\), the identity
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\left(\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))-\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))\right)
\]
holds exactly, with the displayed half-open endpoints correct for the strict inequality defining \(\mu\).

3. For fixed \(p\in(0,1)\) and \(N\ge1\), if there are no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexamples of support at most \(N\), and every larger strict reduced counterexample has a good-merge pair satisfying the displayed interval-probability inequality, then no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists.

The proof does not characterize all \(p\) for the original problem.
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves the following partial and conditional results, not the full characterization asked for in the original problem.

Let \(p\in(0,1)\), let \(q=1-p\), let \(m\ge1\), let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, meaning \(\Pr[v_i=1]=p\) and \(\Pr[v_i=0]=q\), and define
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad \mu=\Pr[S<p].
\]
Since \(\Pr[S\ge p]=1-\mu\), the desired inequality is equivalent to \(\mu\le q\).

The proof establishes exactly these three assertions:

1. **Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma.** Define
   \[
   A_2=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^2,\qquad A_3=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^3,\qquad
   \rho=\frac{A_3}{A_2^{3/2}}.
   \]
   If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and
   \[
   \rho\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p,
   \]
   equivalently \(\mu\le q\).

2. **Exact pair-merging formula.** Suppose two distinguished weights are \(a\ge b>0\), with \(a+b<p\), and let \(R\) be the residual sum from all other weights, independent of the Bernoulli variables used for these two weights. Define
   \[
   \mu_{\rm split}=\Pr[R+a\xi+b\eta<p],
   \]
   where \(\xi,\eta\) are independent Bernoulli\((p)\), and
   \[
   \mu_{\rm merge}=\Pr[R+(a+b)\zeta<p],
   \]
   where \(\zeta\) is Bernoulli\((p)\), independent of \(R\). Then
   \[
   \mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
   =
   pq\Big(
   \Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
   -
   \Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
   \Big).
   \]

3. **Conditional reduction by good merges.** Fix \(p\in(0,1)\) and an integer \(N\ge1\). A **strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support \(n\)** means a list of \(n\) weights \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) such that
   \[
   0<x_i<p,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1,
   \]
   and, for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(u_i\),
   \[
   \Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n x_i u_i<p\Big]>q.
   \]
   Assume:
   - there are no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexamples of support at most \(N\);
   - every strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support greater than \(N\) contains some pair of weights \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) such that, for the residual sum \(R\) over the other weights,
     \[
     \Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
     \le
     \Pr[R\in[p-b,p)).
     \]
   Then no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists at all.

The third assertion is conditional: it does **not** claim that the displayed good-merge inequality is always true.

## Proof

### 1. Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma

Let
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad \mathbb E S=p\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=p.
\]
Set
\[
X_i=w_i(v_i-p).
\]
Then the \(X_i\) are independent, centered random variables, and
\[
S-p=\sum_{i=1}^m X_i.
\]

We invoke the standard Berry–Esseen theorem in the following form: if \(X_1,\dots,X_m\) are independent centered random variables, if
\[
\sigma^2=\sum_{i=1}^m \operatorname{Var}(X_i)>0,
\]
and if
\[
B=\sum_{i=1}^m \mathbb E|X_i|^3,
\]
then, with \(\Phi\) denoting the standard normal distribution function,
\[
\sup_x\left|\Pr\left[\frac{\sum_i X_i}{\sigma}\le x\right]-\Phi(x)\right|
\le
0.56\,\frac{B}{\sigma^3}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=w_i^2 pq,
\]
so
\[
\sigma^2=pq\sum_i w_i^2=pqA_2.
\]
Also,
\[
\mathbb E|X_i|^3
=
w_i^3\big(pq^3+qp^3\big)
=
w_i^3 pq(p^2+q^2),
\]
and hence
\[
B=pq(p^2+q^2)A_3.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{B}{\sigma^3}
=
\frac{pq(p^2+q^2)A_3}{(pqA_2)^{3/2}}
=
\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]

Applying Berry–Esseen at \(x=0\), and using \(\Phi(0)=1/2\), gives
\[
\Pr[S\le p]
=
\Pr\left[\frac{S-p}{\sqrt{pqA_2}}\le0\right]
\le
\frac12
+
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho.
\]
Since \(\mu=\Pr[S<p]\le \Pr[S\le p]\), it is enough to show
\[
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho
\le
\frac12-p.
\]

Assume now that \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\rho\le 5\sqrt2/28\). Since \(0.56=14/25\),
\[
0.56\cdot \frac{5\sqrt2}{28}
=
\frac{\sqrt2}{10}.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\frac{\sqrt2}{10}\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}
\le
\frac12-p.
\]
Let
\[
t=pq.
\]
For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has \(t\in[3/16,2/9]\). Also
\[
p^2+q^2=1-2t,
\qquad
(1-2p)^2=1-4t.
\]
The desired inequality is equivalent, after multiplying by positive quantities and squaring, to
\[
25t(1-4t)\ge 2(1-2t)^2.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
108t^2-33t+2\le0.
\]
But
\[
108t^2-33t+2
=
108\left(t-\frac1{12}\right)\left(t-\frac29\right).
\]
Since
\[
t\in\left[\frac3{16},\frac29\right]\subseteq \left[\frac1{12},\frac29\right],
\]
this quantity is indeed nonpositive. Therefore
\[
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\rho
\le
\frac12-p.
\]
Consequently
\[
\mu=\Pr[S<p]\le 1-p=q,
\]
and hence
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=1-\mu\ge p.
\]
This proves the Berry–Esseen effective-dimension lemma.

### 2. Exact pair-merging formula

Let \(a\ge b>0\) be two distinguished weights with \(a+b<p\). Let \(R\) be the residual sum over all other coordinates. Let \(\xi,\eta,\zeta\) be Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, independent of each other and of \(R\). Define
\[
\mu_{\rm split}=\Pr[R+a\xi+b\eta<p],
\]
and
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}=\Pr[R+(a+b)\zeta<p].
\]

Condition on \(R=r\), and put
\[
t=p-r.
\]
We compare
\[
\Pr[a\xi+b\eta<t]
\]
with
\[
\Pr[(a+b)\zeta<t].
\]

The split variable \(a\xi+b\eta\) takes the values
\[
0,\ b,\ a,\ a+b
\]
with probabilities
\[
q^2,\ qp,\ pq,\ p^2,
\]
respectively. The merged variable \((a+b)\zeta\) takes the values
\[
0,\ a+b
\]
with probabilities
\[
q,\ p.
\]

Therefore the conditional difference
\[
\Delta(t)=\Pr[a\xi+b\eta<t]-\Pr[(a+b)\zeta<t]
\]
is as follows:

- If \(t\le0\), both probabilities are \(0\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).
- If \(0<t\le b\), the split variable is below \(t\) only when both Bernoulli variables are \(0\), so the split probability is \(q^2\), while the merged probability is \(q\). Hence
  \[
  \Delta(t)=q^2-q=-pq.
  \]
- If \(b<t\le a\), the split probability is \(q^2+qp=q\), and the merged probability is also \(q\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).
- If \(a<t\le a+b\), the split probability is \(q^2+qp+pq=1-p^2\), while the merged probability is \(q\). Hence
  \[
  \Delta(t)=1-p^2-q=pq.
  \]
- If \(t>a+b\), both probabilities are \(1\), so \(\Delta(t)=0\).

Thus
\[
\Delta(t)=pq\,\mathbf 1_{\{a<t\le a+b\}}
-
pq\,\mathbf 1_{\{0<t\le b\}}.
\]
Substituting \(t=p-R\), we get
\[
\{a<t\le a+b\}
=
\{p-a-b\le R<p-a\},
\]
and
\[
\{0<t\le b\}
=
\{p-b\le R<p\}.
\]
Averaging over \(R\) gives
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big).
\]
This proves the exact pair-merging formula.

### 3. Conditional reduction by good merges

Fix \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), and an integer \(N\ge1\).

Recall that a strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support \(n\) is a list of \(n\) weights
\[
x_1,\dots,x_n\in(0,p)
\]
with
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i=1
\]
such that, for independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(u_i\),
\[
\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n x_i u_i<p\Big]>q.
\]

Assume first that no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support at most \(N\) exists. Assume also that every strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of support greater than \(N\) contains a pair of weights \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) satisfying the good-merge inequality
\[
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
\le
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p)),
\]
where \(R\) is the residual sum over all coordinates except those two.

Suppose, for contradiction, that at least one strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists. Choose one with minimal support \(n\). By the first assumption, \(n>N\).

By the second assumption, this counterexample contains a pair \(a\ge b\) with \(a+b<p\) satisfying the good-merge inequality. Let \(\mu_{\rm split}\) be the bad probability of the original counterexample, and let \(\mu_{\rm merge}\) be the bad probability after replacing \(a,b\) by one weight \(a+b\).

By the pair-merging formula,
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\Big(
\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
-
\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))
\Big).
\]
The good-merge inequality makes the right-hand side nonpositive, so
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}\le0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}\ge \mu_{\rm split}.
\]
Since the original object was a strict counterexample,
\[
\mu_{\rm split}>q,
\]
and hence
\[
\mu_{\rm merge}>q.
\]

The merged list still has positive weights, still sums to \(1\), and remains reduced because all unchanged weights are \(<p\) and the new weight satisfies \(a+b<p\). Its support is \(n-1\). Thus the merged list is a strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample of smaller support than the chosen minimal one, a contradiction.

Therefore no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists at all. This proves the conditional reduction. The argument is conditional on the good-merge hypothesis and does not assert that the good-merge inequality holds universally.

  "original_problem": "Let $w_1, \\ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let \n$v_1, \\ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that\n\\[\\Pr\\Big[\\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\\geq p\\Big]\\geq p?\\]",

The proof establishes three partial results: (1) if p in [1/4,1/3], A2=sum_i w_i^2, A3=sum_i w_i^3, rho=A3/A2^(3/2), and rho<=5*sqrt(2)/28, then mu<=q, equivalently Pr[S>=p]>=p; (2) for a split pair a>=b>0 with a+b<p and residual sum R, replacing a,b by a+b gives mu_split - mu_merge equal to p q times the probability that R lies in [p-a-b,p-a) minus the probability that R lies in [p-b,p); (3) for any fixed N>=1, if no strict reduced p-counterexample of support <=N exists and every strict reduced p-counterexample of support >N has a pair satisfying the good-merge inequality Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) <= Pr[R in [p-b,p)), then no strict reduced p-counterexample exists. A strict reduced p-counterexample means a finite positive-weight list with all weights <p, sum 1, and mu>q. This is partial and conditional, not a characterization of all p.",
