## writeup_r10_2e4de2: The proof establishes the stated conditional structural theorem: for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\), \(n\ge10\), positive sorted weights \(0<w_1\le\cdots\le w_n<p\) with total mass \(1\), and \(\Pr(D)=q+\varepsilon\), assuming every mergeable pair reduction has merged bad probability at most \(q\), the bottom-four pairs and all bottom-five pairs except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) are mergeable; each mergeable pair satisfies
\[
\mu-\mu^{(ij)}=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}),\qquad L_{ij}-U_{ij}\ge \varepsilon/(pq);
\]
the slab gap equals the normalized pivotal covariance and normalized level-2 \(p\)-biased Fourier coefficient;
\[
6\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)
\]
under all-bottom-four badness, and
\[
9\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)
\]
under all-bottom-five mergeable-pair badness; and in the latter case \(I_i\ge\varepsilon/q\) for \(i=1,\dots,5\), while always
\[
\sum_i w_i I_i\le 1-\varepsilon/p.
\]
It does not solve the original problem of determining all \(p\) for which the inequality holds for arbitrary nonnegative weights.
## Original Problem
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

This proves a **partial structural theorem**, not the original problem. It does not determine all probabilities \(p\) for which the original inequality holds; instead it proves consequences that must hold for a hypothetical minimum-support strict counterexample when \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\).

Fix \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), put \(q=1-p\), and let
\[
0<w_1\le \cdots \le w_n<p,\qquad n\ge 10,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n w_i=1.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables,
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i,\qquad D=\{S<p\},\qquad G=\{S\ge p\}.
\]
Assume
\[
\Pr(D)=q+\varepsilon
\]
for some \(\varepsilon>0\), and assume the following minimum-support consequence: whenever a pair \(i<j\) is **mergeable**, meaning
\[
w_i+w_j<p,
\]
the reduced \((n-1)\)-weight instance obtained by replacing \(w_i,w_j\) by one weight \(w_i+w_j\) is not a strict counterexample, i.e. its bad probability is at most \(q\). Call such a merge **bad** if its merged bad probability is at most \(q\).

For a mergeable pair \(i<j\), write
\[
b=w_i,\qquad a=w_j,\qquad R_{ij}=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j} w_\ell v_\ell,
\]
and define the residual slabs
\[
L_{ij}=\Pr\bigl(R_{ij}\in[p-a-b,p-a)\bigr),\qquad
U_{ij}=\Pr\bigl(R_{ij}\in[p-b,p)\bigr).
\]
Then the proof establishes:

1. Every pair among \(w_1,w_2,w_3,w_4\) is mergeable, and every pair among \(w_1,\dots,w_5\) except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) is mergeable.

2. If \(\mu=\Pr(D)\) and \(\mu^{(ij)}\) is the bad probability after merging \(w_i,w_j\), then
\[
\mu-\mu^{(ij)}=pq\,(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]
By minimality,
\[
L_{ij}-U_{ij}\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{pq}.
\]

3. For the pivotal event
\[
A_i=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne i}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_i,p)\right\}
\]
and the success indicator \(F=1_G\), with \(p\)-biased Fourier characters
\[
\chi_k=\frac{v_k-p}{\sqrt{pq}},\qquad \chi_A=\prod_{k\in A}\chi_k,\qquad \widehat F(A)=\mathbb E[F\chi_A],
\]
one has
\[
L_{ij}-U_{ij}
=\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=1)-\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=0),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)=\widehat F(\{i,j\})=pq\,(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]
The same covariance identity also holds with \(i,j\) interchanged.

4. If all bottom-four mergeable pairs are bad, then
\[
6\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]
If all bottom-five mergeable pairs are bad, then
\[
9\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

5. In the all-bottom-five-bad case, every bottom-five coordinate has influence
\[
I_i:=\Pr(A_i)\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{q},\qquad i=1,\dots,5,
\]
and always
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i I_i\le 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{p}.
\]

## Proof

### 1. Bottom weights are mergeable

Because \(n\ge 10\) and the weights are positive and nondecreasing,
\[
1=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i
\ge \sum_{i=1}^{10}w_i
\ge w_1+w_2+2w_3+6w_5
>2w_3+6w_5.
\]
Since \(w_3\le w_5\),
\[
4(w_3+w_5)\le 2w_3+6w_5<1.
\]
Thus
\[
w_3+w_5<\frac14\le p.
\]
Every pair among \(w_1,w_2,w_3,w_4\) has sum at most \(w_3+w_4\le w_3+w_5<p\). Also, every pair among \(w_1,\dots,w_5\) except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) has sum at most \(w_3+w_5<p\). Hence the claimed pairs are mergeable.

### 2. Exact merge formula

Fix a mergeable pair \(i<j\), and write
\[
b=w_i,\qquad a=w_j,\qquad c=a+b.
\]
Let
\[
R=R_{ij}=\sum_{\ell\ne i,j}w_\ell v_\ell.
\]
In the merged instance, replace \(w_i,w_j\) by one weight \(c\), attached to a fresh Bernoulli\((p)\) variable \(u\), independent of \(R\).

For a fixed residual value \(r\), the original conditional bad probability is
\[
\phi(r)
=q^2\mathbf 1_{\{r<p\}}
+pq\mathbf 1_{\{r+b<p\}}
+pq\mathbf 1_{\{r+a<p\}}
+p^2\mathbf 1_{\{r+a+b<p\}}.
\]
The merged conditional bad probability is
\[
\psi(r)=q\mathbf 1_{\{r<p\}}+p\mathbf 1_{\{r+a+b<p\}}.
\]
Subtracting gives
\[
\phi(r)-\psi(r)
=pq\Bigl(
\mathbf 1_{\{r<p-a\}}
-\mathbf 1_{\{r<p-a-b\}}
-\mathbf 1_{\{r<p\}}
+\mathbf 1_{\{r<p-b\}}
\Bigr).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\phi(r)-\psi(r)
=pq\Bigl(
\mathbf 1_{\{r\in[p-a-b,p-a)\}}
-\mathbf 1_{\{r\in[p-b,p)\}}
\Bigr).
\]
Taking expectation over \(R\),
\[
\mu-\mu^{(ij)}
=pq\bigl(L_{ij}-U_{ij}\bigr).
\]

By the minimum-support assumption, the merged instance is not a strict counterexample, so
\[
\mu^{(ij)}\le q.
\]
Since \(\mu=q+\varepsilon\),
\[
pq\bigl(L_{ij}-U_{ij}\bigr)
=\mu-\mu^{(ij)}
\ge q+\varepsilon-q
=\varepsilon.
\]
Thus
\[
L_{ij}-U_{ij}\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{pq}.
\]

### 3. Pivotal covariance and Fourier coefficient

For each coordinate \(k\), define the pivotal event
\[
A_k=\left\{\sum_{\ell\ne k}w_\ell v_\ell\in[p-w_k,p)\right\}.
\]
This is exactly the event that changing \(v_k\) from \(0\) to \(1\) changes the success indicator \(1_G\) from \(0\) to \(1\).

For the fixed pair \(i<j\), with \(b=w_i\le a=w_j\), we have
\[
A_i=\{R+a v_j\in[p-b,p)\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=1)
=\Pr(R\in[p-a-b,p-a))
=L_{ij},
\]
while
\[
\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=0)
=\Pr(R\in[p-b,p))
=U_{ij}.
\]
Hence
\[
L_{ij}-U_{ij}
=\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=1)-\Pr(A_i\mid v_j=0).
\]
For any event \(A\) and Bernoulli\((p)\) variable \(v\),
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_A,v)
=pq\bigl(\Pr(A\mid v=1)-\Pr(A\mid v=0)\bigr).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]

The same identity holds with \(i,j\) interchanged. Indeed,
\[
A_j=\{R+bv_i\in[p-a,p)\},
\]
so
\[
\Pr(A_j\mid v_i=1)-\Pr(A_j\mid v_i=0)
=\Pr(R\in[p-a-b,p-b))-\Pr(R\in[p-a,p)).
\]
Since \(p-a-b\le p-a\le p-b\le p\), the middle interval cancels, leaving
\[
\Pr(A_j\mid v_i=1)-\Pr(A_j\mid v_i=0)
=L_{ij}-U_{ij}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_j},v_i)=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]

Now define the \(p\)-biased Fourier characters by
\[
\chi_k=\frac{v_k-p}{\sqrt{pq}},\qquad \chi_A=\prod_{k\in A}\chi_k,
\]
and for \(F=1_G\),
\[
\widehat F(A)=\mathbb E[F\chi_A].
\]
For fixed \(R=r\), write
\[
F_{xy}=1_{\{r+bx+ay\ge p\}},\qquad x,y\in\{0,1\}.
\]
A direct calculation gives
\[
\mathbb E[F\chi_i\chi_j\mid R=r]
=pq(F_{11}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{00}).
\]
But
\[
F_{11}-F_{10}-F_{01}+F_{00}
=
\mathbf 1_{\{r\in[p-a-b,p-a)\}}
-
\mathbf 1_{\{r\in[p-b,p)\}}.
\]
Taking expectation over \(R\),
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}).
\]
Thus the slab gap is the normalized pivotal covariance and the normalized level-2 Fourier coefficient:
\[
L_{ij}-U_{ij}
=\frac{\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)}{pq}
=\frac{\widehat F(\{i,j\})}{pq}.
\]

### 4. Parseval bounds from many bad pairs

The \(p\)-biased characters form an orthonormal basis, so Parseval gives
\[
\sum_{A\subseteq[n]}\widehat F(A)^2=\mathbb E[F^2].
\]
Since \(F=1_G\), one has \(F^2=F\), and
\[
\mathbb E F=\Pr(G)=1-\Pr(D)=1-(q+\varepsilon)=p-\varepsilon.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=\operatorname{Var}(F)
=(p-\varepsilon)(1-p+\varepsilon)
=(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

If a mergeable pair \((i,j)\) is bad, then \(\mu^{(ij)}\le q\), and from the merge formula,
\[
\widehat F(\{i,j\})
=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij})
=\mu-\mu^{(ij)}
\ge \varepsilon.
\]
Thus every bad mergeable pair contributes at least \(\varepsilon^2\) to the level-2 Fourier square sum.

Among \(w_1,w_2,w_3,w_4\) there are \(6\) pairs, all mergeable by Part 1. If all are bad, then
\[
6\varepsilon^2
\le \sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

Among \(w_1,\dots,w_5\), all pairs except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) are mergeable, so there are at least \(9\) mergeable pairs. If all those mergeable bottom-five pairs are bad, then
\[
9\varepsilon^2
\le \sum_{A\ne\varnothing}\widehat F(A)^2
=(p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon).
\]

### 5. Influence bounds

Let
\[
I_i=\Pr(A_i)
\]
be the influence of coordinate \(i\).

Assume all bottom-five mergeable pairs are bad. For each \(i\in\{1,\dots,5\}\), Part 1 guarantees that \(i\) has at least one mergeable partner \(j\in\{1,\dots,5\}\). Since that pair is bad,
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)\ge \varepsilon.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_{A_i},v_j)
=\mathbb E[1_{A_i}v_j]-p\Pr(A_i)
\le \Pr(A_i)-p\Pr(A_i)
=qI_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
I_i\ge \frac{\varepsilon}{q},\qquad i=1,\dots,5.
\]

It remains to prove the weighted influence bound. For every \(i\),
\[
\Pr(G\mid v_i=1)-\Pr(G\mid v_i=0)=I_i,
\]
because \(A_i\) is precisely the event that coordinate \(i\) is pivotal for \(G\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(1_G,v_i)=pqI_i.
\]
Since
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i v_i
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathbb E S=p\sum_{i=1}^n w_i=p,
\]
we get
\[
pq\sum_{i=1}^n w_i I_i
=\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\operatorname{Cov}(1_G,v_i)
=\operatorname{Cov}(1_G,S)
=\mathbb E[(S-p)1_G].
\]
On \(G=\{S\ge p\}\), one has \(0\le S-p\le 1-p=q\), and
\[
\Pr(G)=p-\varepsilon.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb E[(S-p)1_G]\le q(p-\varepsilon).
\]
Thus
\[
pq\sum_{i=1}^n w_i I_i\le q(p-\varepsilon),
\]
and dividing by \(pq>0\) gives
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i I_i\le \frac{p-\varepsilon}{p}
=1-\frac{\varepsilon}{p}.
\]
This proves all claimed assertions.

}

## writeup_r10_840444: Assuming the one-dimensional Berry–Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(0.56\), the proof establishes the stated partial proposition: for finite nonnegative weights summing to \(1\) and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables, if \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\rho_0=\sum_i w_i^3/(\sum_i w_i^2)^{3/2}\le 5\sqrt2/28\), then \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). It also establishes the strict head/buffer/remainder sufficient condition when \(B_R>0\), \(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le (1/32)\sqrt{B_R}\), \(\sigma=\sqrt{p(1-p)B_R}\), and \(\alpha+\gamma\le\sigma\). Finally, it correctly verifies the two advertised certificate failures: the fourteen-equal-weight range/mean-only buffer computation gives \(49159/255879<1/3\), and the \(m=1033\) no-buffer Berry–Esseen Gaussian certificate gives \(G-0.56\,\kappa(1/3)\rho_R<1/3\). It does not characterize all \(p\) for the original problem.
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

The proof below establishes the following **partial proposition**, not a full solution of the original problem. It proves two sufficient conditions for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and it proves that two proposed finite-head certificates are not universally successful.

Let \(m\ge1\), let \(w_1,\dots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy \(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1\), let \(p\in(0,1)\), let \(q=1-p\), and let \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i,\qquad
K(p)=\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}.
\]
For any nonempty index set \(A\subseteq\{1,\dots,m\}\) with \(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2>0\), define its Berry--Esseen weight ratio by
\[
\rho_A=\frac{\sum_{i\in A}w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i\in A}w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
For the full weight vector write
\[
\rho_0=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^3}{\left(\sum_{i=1}^m w_i^2\right)^{3/2}}.
\]
Let \(\Phi\) be the standard normal distribution function and \(\overline\Phi=1-\Phi\). We use the Berry--Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(C_{\rm BE}=0.56\).

The proved claims are:

1. **Effective-dimension sufficient condition.** If \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and
   \[
   \rho_0\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
   \]

2. **Small head-plus-buffer with regular remainder.** Fix \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\tau=1/32\). Suppose the indices are partitioned into disjoint sets \(I_H,I_U,I_R\), where \(I_R\) has positive square mass. Define
   \[
   S_H=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_U=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i v_i,\quad
   S_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i v_i,
   \]
   and define the head mass, buffer mass, and remainder mass by
   \[
   \alpha=\sum_{i\in I_H}w_i,\qquad
   \gamma=\sum_{i\in I_U}w_i,\qquad
   \beta=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i=1-\alpha-\gamma.
   \]
   Let
   \[
   B_R=\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2,\qquad
   \sigma=\sqrt{pq\,B_R}.
   \]
   Assume the remainder is \(\tau\)-regular, meaning
   \[
   \max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le \tau\sqrt{B_R}.
   \]
   If
   \[
   \alpha+\gamma\le \sigma,
   \]
   then
   \[
   \Pr[S\ge p]>p.
   \]

3. **Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate.** For \(\gamma>0\), define
   \[
   \psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
   =
   \inf\{\Pr[Y\ge r]:0\le Y\le \gamma\text{ a.s. and }\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\}.
   \]
   The universal assertion that the no-remainder certificate
   \[
   \mathbb E\bigl[\psi_{p,\gamma}(p-S_H)\bigr]\ge p
   \]
   always holds is false. At \(p=1/3\), for fourteen equal weights \(w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=1/14\), with the first nine weights as head and the last five as buffer, this certificate equals
   \[
   \frac{49159}{255879}<\frac13.
   \]

4. **Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate.** For a no-buffer split \(S=S_H+S_R\), define the Gaussian head average
   \[
   G=\mathbb E\left[
   \overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
   \right],
   \]
   where \(\alpha\) is the head mass and \(\sigma\) is the standard deviation of the centered remainder. The universal assertion that
   \[
   G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R\ge p
   \]
   always holds for regular tails is false. At \(p=1/3\), take \(m=1033\), \(\varepsilon=1/1536\),
   \[
   w_1=\frac{21}{64}=\frac13-8\varepsilon,\qquad
   w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon,
   \]
   use \(I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}\) and \(I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}\). Then the tail is \(1/32\)-regular, \(\rho_R=1/32\), and
   \[
   G<\frac13+10^{-26},
   \]
   while
   \[
   0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R=\frac7{240\sqrt2}\approx0.0206.
   \]
   Hence this absolute Berry--Esseen certificate gives a bound strictly below \(p=1/3\).

## Proof

### 1. Weighted Bernoulli Berry--Esseen estimate

Let \(a_1,\dots,a_n\ge0\), not all zero, and let \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables. Put
\[
T=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j(v_j-p),\qquad
B_2=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^2,\qquad
B_3=\sum_{j=1}^n a_j^3.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Var}(T)=pq\,B_2.
\]
Also,
\[
\mathbb E|a_j(v_j-p)|^3
=
a_j^3\bigl(pq^3+qp^3\bigr)
=
pq(p^2+q^2)a_j^3.
\]
Thus Berry--Esseen with constant \(0.56\) gives, for
\[
Z=\frac{T}{\sqrt{pq\,B_2}},
\]
the bound
\[
\sup_x|\Pr[Z\le x]-\Phi(x)|
\le
0.56\,\frac{p^2+q^2}{\sqrt{pq}}\,
\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}
=
0.56\,K(p)\,\frac{B_3}{B_2^{3/2}}.
\]

### 2. Proof of the effective-dimension condition

Apply the preceding estimate to the full vector \(a_i=w_i\). Since \(\mathbb E S=p\), we get
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge \Pr[S>p]
\ge
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0.
\]
It remains to check that the assumed bound on \(\rho_0\) makes the right-hand side at least \(p\).

Define
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{(1/2-p)\sqrt{pq}}{0.56(p^2+q^2)}.
\]
The inequality
\[
\frac12-0.56\,K(p)\rho_0\ge p
\]
holds whenever \(\rho_0\le \eta(p)\).

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), put \(x=1-2p\). Then \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\), and
\[
\eta(p)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1.12(1+x^2)}.
\]
The function
\[
g(x)=\frac{x\sqrt{1-x^2}}{1+x^2}
\]
satisfies
\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log g(x)
=
\frac{1-3x^2}{x(1-x^2)(1+x^2)}>0
\]
on \(x\in[1/3,1/2]\). Therefore \(\eta(p)\) is minimized at \(p=1/3\). At \(p=1/3\),
\[
\eta(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/6)(\sqrt2/3)}{0.56\cdot(5/9)}
=
\frac{5\sqrt2}{28}.
\]
Hence \(\rho_0\le 5\sqrt2/28\) implies \(\rho_0\le\eta(p)\), and therefore
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p.
\]

### 3. Proof of the regular-remainder sufficient condition

Assume \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(\tau=1/32\), and the split \(I_H,I_U,I_R\) satisfies the hypotheses stated above. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta.
\]
Then \(\mathbb E X=0\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^2=pqB_R\).

The \(\tau\)-regularity assumption gives
\[
\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^3
\le
\left(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\right)\sum_{i\in I_R}w_i^2
\le
\tau B_R^{3/2},
\]
so
\[
\rho_R\le \tau=\frac1{32}.
\]

Condition on arbitrary values of \(S_H\) and \(S_U\). Since
\[
S=S_H+S_U+p\beta+X,
\]
the event \(S\ge p\) is equivalent to
\[
X\ge p-S_H-S_U-p\beta
=
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U.
\]
Because \(S_H,S_U\ge0\),
\[
p(\alpha+\gamma)-S_H-S_U
\le
p(\alpha+\gamma)
\le
p\sigma
\le
\frac{\sigma}{3}.
\]
Thus, for every head and buffer realization, the standardized tail threshold is at most \(1/3\).

By Berry--Esseen for the remainder,
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(t/\sigma)-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R
\]
for every real \(t\). Therefore, whenever \(t\le\sigma/3\),
\[
\Pr[X\ge t]
\ge
\overline\Phi(1/3)-0.56\,K(p)\tau.
\]

For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), one has
\[
K(p)\le K(1/4)=\frac5{2\sqrt3}.
\]
Hence
\[
0.56\,K(p)\tau
\le
0.56\cdot \frac5{2\sqrt3}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{160\sqrt3}.
\]
Also,
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)
=
\frac12-\int_0^{1/3}\frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,dx
\ge
\frac12-\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}.
\]
Using \(\pi>3\) and \(\sqrt3>5/3\),
\[
\frac1{3\sqrt{2\pi}}<\frac5{36},
\qquad
\frac7{160\sqrt3}<\frac{21}{800}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\overline\Phi(1/3)-\frac7{160\sqrt3}
>
\frac12-\frac5{36}-\frac{21}{800}
=
\frac{2411}{7200}
>
\frac13.
\]
Since \(p\le1/3\), every conditional probability \(\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H,S_U]\) is strictly larger than \(p\). Averaging over \(S_H,S_U\) gives
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]>p.
\]

### 4. Failure of the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate

Let \(\gamma>0\), and let \(Y\) be any random variable satisfying \(0\le Y\le\gamma\) a.s. and \(\mathbb E Y=p\gamma\). For \(0<r<p\gamma\), if \(\delta=\Pr[Y\ge r]\), then
\[
p\gamma=\mathbb E Y
\le
r(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta.
\]
Thus
\[
\delta\ge \frac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r}.
\]
This bound is sharp as an infimum, by taking \(Y\) just below \(r\) with high probability and equal to \(\gamma\) with the remaining probability. Hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r)
=
\begin{cases}
1,& r\le0,\\[1mm]
\dfrac{p\gamma-r}{\gamma-r},&0<r<p\gamma,\\[2mm]
0,&r\ge p\gamma.
\end{cases}
\]

Now take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad
m=14,\qquad
w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=\frac1{14}.
\]
Let the head be the first nine weights and the buffer the last five weights. Then
\[
\gamma=\frac5{14},
\qquad
p\gamma=\frac5{42}.
\]
Let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(9,1/3)
\]
count the number of selected head weights. For a head outcome with \(K=k\),
\[
S_H=\frac{k}{14},
\qquad
r_k=p-S_H=\frac13-\frac{k}{14}=\frac{14-3k}{42}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_k)
=
\begin{cases}
0,&k\le3,\\[1mm]
3/13,&k=4,\\[1mm]
1,&k\ge5.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, for \(k=4\),
\[
r_4=\frac{2}{42},
\]
and hence
\[
\psi_{p,\gamma}(r_4)
=
\frac{5/42-2/42}{15/42-2/42}
=
\frac3{13}.
\]

The range/mean-only certificate value is therefore
\[
\frac3{13}\Pr[K=4]+\Pr[K\ge5].
\]
Now
\[
\Pr[K=4]
=
\binom94\left(\frac13\right)^4\left(\frac23\right)^5
=
\frac{4032}{19683},
\]
and
\[
\Pr[K\ge5]
=
\frac{2851}{19683}.
\]
Thus the certificate equals
\[
\frac3{13}\cdot\frac{4032}{19683}
+
\frac{2851}{19683}
=
\frac{49159}{255879}
<
\frac13.
\]
So the range/mean-only deterministic buffer certificate is not universally strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\).

This does not contradict the original inequality for this example. In fact,
\[
\Pr\left[\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge\frac13\right]
=
\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(14,1/3)\ge5]
=
\frac{2508665}{4782969}
>
\frac13.
\]
Also, for fourteen equal weights,
\[
\rho_0=\frac1{\sqrt{14}}>\frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
\]
so this example is not covered by the effective-dimension sufficient condition from part 1.

### 5. Failure of the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen certificate

Consider a split \(S=S_H+S_R\) with no buffer. Let
\[
X=S_R-p\beta,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\operatorname{Var}(X),
\]
and let \(\rho_R\) be the Berry--Esseen ratio of the tail weights. Conditional on a head value \(S_H=h\), Berry--Esseen gives the lower bound
\[
\Pr[S\ge p\mid S_H=h]
\ge
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-h}{\sigma}\right)
-
0.56\,K(p)\rho_R.
\]
Averaging over the head gives the absolute Berry--Esseen certificate
\[
G-0.56\,K(p)\rho_R,
\]
where
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]

We now show that this certificate need not reach \(p\).

Take
\[
p=\frac13,\qquad q=\frac23,\qquad \varepsilon=\frac1{1536}.
\]
Let \(m=1033\), and define
\[
w_1=\frac13-8\varepsilon=\frac{21}{64},
\qquad
w_2=\cdots=w_{1033}=\varepsilon.
\]
Use the head
\[
I_H=\{1,\dots,9\}
\]
and the tail
\[
I_R=\{10,\dots,1033\}.
\]
Then
\[
\alpha=w_1+8\varepsilon=\frac13,
\qquad
\beta=1024\varepsilon=\frac23.
\]
For the tail,
\[
B_R=1024\varepsilon^2=\frac1{2304},
\]
so
\[
\sigma=\sqrt{pqB_R}=\frac{\sqrt2}{144}.
\]
Also,
\[
\sqrt{B_R}=32\varepsilon=\frac1{48},
\]
and
\[
\max_{i\in I_R}w_i=\varepsilon=\frac1{32}\sqrt{B_R}.
\]
Thus the tail is \(1/32\)-regular. Since the tail consists of \(1024\) equal weights,
\[
\rho_R=\frac1{\sqrt{1024}}=\frac1{32}.
\]

Now compute the Gaussian head average
\[
G=
\mathbb E\left[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}\right)
\right].
\]
Here
\[
p\alpha=\frac13\cdot\frac13=\frac19.
\]
If \(v_1=1\), we use only the trivial bound that the corresponding Gaussian tail probability is at most \(1\). This contributes at most \(1/3\) to \(G\).

If \(v_1=0\), let
\[
K\sim\operatorname{Bin}(8,1/3)
\]
count the selected small head weights \(w_2,\dots,w_9\). Then \(S_H=K\varepsilon\), and
\[
\frac{p\alpha-S_H}{\sigma}
=
\frac{1/9-K/1536}{\sqrt2/144}
=
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}.
\]
Since \(0\le K\le8\),
\[
\frac{512-3K}{32\sqrt2}
\ge
\frac{488}{32\sqrt2}
=
\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}
>
10.78.
\]
By Mills' inequality,
\[
\overline\Phi(x)\le \frac{e^{-x^2/2}}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}
\qquad (x>0).
\]
For \(x=61/(4\sqrt2)\), this gives
\[
\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)<10^{-26}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G
\le
\frac13+\frac23\,\overline\Phi\left(\frac{61}{4\sqrt2}\right)
<
\frac13+10^{-26}.
\]

On the other hand,
\[
K(1/3)
=
\frac{(1/3)^2+(2/3)^2}{\sqrt{(1/3)(2/3)}}
=
\frac5{3\sqrt2}.
\]
Hence the Berry--Esseen penalty is
\[
0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
=
0.56\cdot\frac5{3\sqrt2}\cdot\frac1{32}
=
\frac7{240\sqrt2}
\approx0.0206.
\]
Consequently,
\[
G-0.56\,K(1/3)\rho_R
<
\frac13+10^{-26}-\frac7{240\sqrt2}
<
\frac13.
\]
Thus the no-buffer absolute Berry--Esseen regular-tail certificate is not uniformly strong enough to prove \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). Again, this is a failure of that certificate, not a counterexample to the original inequality.

This is a partial result and not a characterization of all p.",

## writeup_r10_1894c7: Assuming the finite verification lemma is accepted, the proof establishes the reduced theorem: for every \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
Consequently, under the same restricted hypotheses,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i\ge p\right]>p,
\]
hence certainly \(\ge p\). The proof does not establish the original problem for arbitrary \(m\), merely nonnegative weights, weights allowed to be \(\ge p\), or \(p\)-ranges outside \([1/4,1/3)\).
## Original Problem

Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained special case.

Let \(p\) be a real number with
\[
\frac14\le p<\frac13.
\]
Let \(v_1,\dots,v_9\) be independent Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, and let \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) be positive real numbers such that
\[
0<w_i<p\quad\text{for every }i,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^9 w_i=1.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

This is **not** the full original problem. It is the strict reduced support-\(9\) case on the interval \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), with the stronger upper-tail event \(>p\) rather than \(\ge p\).

## Proof

Let
\[
q=1-p,\qquad r=\frac pq.
\]
Then \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\) is equivalent to
\[
\frac13\le r<\frac12.
\]

For \(A\subseteq[9]=\{1,\dots,9\}\), write
\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]
Define the losing family
\[
D=\{A\subseteq[9]: w(A)\le p\},
\]
and its layer counts
\[
D_j=D\cap\binom{[9]}j,\qquad d_j=|D_j|.
\]
Then
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=\sum_{A\in D}p^{|A|}q^{9-|A|}
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j.
\]
Since \(1+r=1/q\), we have
\[
q=q^9(1+r)^8.
\]
Thus it is enough to prove
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j < (1+r)^8.
\]

Define the winning-pair graph \(W\) on vertex set \([9]\) by
\[
ij\in E(W)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad w_i+w_j>p.
\]
Let
\[
m=|E(W)|.
\]
Because every \(w_i<p\), all singletons are losing, and
\[
d_0=1,\qquad d_1=9,\qquad d_2=36-m.
\]

Also, no losing set has size at least \(7\). Indeed, if \(A\in D\) and \(|A|\ge7\), then \(|A^c|\le2\), so
\[
w(A^c)<2p<q,
\]
whereas \(w(A)\le p\) implies \(w(A^c)\ge q\), contradiction. Hence \(d_j=0\) for \(j\ge7\).

### 1. Layer bounds

A losing set cannot contain a winning pair. Hence every member of every \(D_j\) is an independent set in \(W\).

#### The \(D_3\) bound

The family \(D_3\) contains no three pairwise disjoint triples: three disjoint losing triples would partition \([9]\), giving total weight at most \(3p<1\), contradiction.

Count unordered partitions of \([9]\) into three triples. There are
\[
\frac{9!}{(3!)^3 3!}=280
\]
such partitions, and each fixed triple belongs to
\[
\frac{6!}{(3!)^2 2!}=10
\]
partitions. Since each partition contains at most two members of \(D_3\),
\[
10d_3\le 2\cdot280,
\]
so
\[
d_3\le56.
\]

#### The \(D_4\) bounds

First, \(D_4\) is intersecting. If \(A,B\in D_4\) were disjoint, with leftover vertex \(z\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(B)+w_z\le 2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible.

Thus \(D_4\) is an intersecting family of \(W\)-independent \(4\)-sets.

The graph \(W\) is a threshold graph: it is represented by vertex weights \(w_i\) and threshold \(p\), with edge condition \(w_i+w_j>p\). Equivalently, every induced subgraph has either an isolated minimum-weight vertex or a dominating maximum-weight vertex, yielding a creation sequence by isolated/dominating vertex additions.

The following finite threshold-graph enumeration gives, for \(m=|E(W)|\),
\[
d_4\le I_m,
\]
where
\[
I_0=56,\ I_1=50,\ I_2=45,\ I_3=41,\ I_4=38,\ I_5=36,\qquad I_m=35\quad(m\ge6).
\]

We also need an independent weighted-degree bound. Let
\[
\mathcal C=\{A^c:A\in D_4\}\subseteq\binom{[9]}5.
\]
Because \(D_4\) is intersecting, \(\mathcal C\) is \(2\)-intersecting. Also, for every \(B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
w(B)=1-w(A)\ge q.
\]
Let \(\Delta_i\) be the degree of vertex \(i\) in \(\mathcal C\), ordered so that
\[
\Delta_1\ge\Delta_2\ge\cdots\ge\Delta_9.
\]
Then
\[
q|\mathcal C|
\le \sum_{B\in\mathcal C}w(B)
=\sum_i w_i\Delta_i.
\]
Given \(0<w_i<p\) and \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the dot product with nonincreasing coefficients \(\Delta_i\) is maximized by placing mass \(p,p,p,1-3p\) on the four largest coefficients. Hence
\[
\sum_i w_i\Delta_i
\le p(\Delta_1+\Delta_2+\Delta_3)+(1-3p)\Delta_4.
\]
For each vertex \(i\), the link
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{B\setminus\{i\}:B\in\mathcal C,\ i\in B\}
\]
is an intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of an \(8\)-set, so \(|\mathcal C_i|\le35\), because the \(70\) four-subsets of an \(8\)-set split into \(35\) complementary pairs. Thus \(\Delta_i\le35\) for all \(i\), and therefore
\[
q d_4=q|\mathcal C|\le35.
\]
Since \(1/q=1+r\),
\[
d_4\le \lfloor 35(1+r)\rfloor.
\]

Combining the two \(D_4\) bounds,
\[
d_4\le C_m(r):=\min\{I_m,\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\}.
\]

#### The \(D_5\) bound

Let \(A\in D_5\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=4\). If \(B\) had a perfect matching consisting of two losing pairs \(e_1,e_2\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e_1)+w(e_2)\le3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence \(W[B]\) meets all three perfect matchings of \(K_4\), so \(W[B]\) contains at least three winning edges.

Therefore \(d_5=0\) if \(m\le2\). If \(m=3\), every such \(B\) must contain all three winning edges; at most \(6\) four-sets can do this, so \(d_5\le6\).

For \(m\ge4\), use complements. The complements of members of \(D_5\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-sets: if \(B_1,B_2\) were two such complements with \(|B_1\cap B_2|\le1\), then
\[
w(B_1\cup B_2)=w(B_1)+w(B_2)-w(B_1\cap B_2)>2q-p=2-3p>1,
\]
impossible. The finite certificate below verifies that any \(2\)-intersecting family of \(4\)-subsets of \([9]\) has size at most \(21\). Thus
\[
d_5\le B_m:=
\begin{cases}
0,&m\le2,\\
6,&m=3,\\
21,&m\ge4.
\end{cases}
\]

#### The \(D_6\) bound

Let \(A\in D_6\), and put \(B=A^c\), so \(|B|=3\). If some pair \(e\subset B\) were losing, and \(z=B\setminus e\), then
\[
1=w(A)+w(e)+w_z\le2p+w_z<3p<1,
\]
impossible. Hence every pair inside \(B\) is winning, so \(B\) is a triangle of \(W\).

Therefore
\[
d_6\le \tau(W),
\]
where \(\tau(W)\) is the number of triangles in \(W\). If a graph has \(m\) edges, then its number of triangles is at most
\[
T_m=\binom a3+\binom b2,
\]
where
\[
m=\binom a2+b,\qquad 0\le b<a.
\]
This follows by induction on \(m\): choose a vertex of degree \(d\le a-1\), remove it, and use the elementary inequality
\[
T_m-T_{m-d}\ge \binom d2.
\]

Also, complements of members of \(D_6\) form a \(2\)-intersecting family of triples, hence have size at most \(7\). Therefore
\[
d_6\le G_m:=\min\{7,T_m\}.
\]

### 2. Deficiency cascade

For \(A\in D_k\), define its normalized deficiency
\[
\varepsilon(A)=\frac{p-w(A)}p\ge0,
\]
and let
\[
E_k=\sum_{A\in D_k}\varepsilon(A).
\]

For \(k\ge2\), if \(B\in D_{k+1}\), then
\[
\sum_{\substack{A\subset B\\ |A|=k}}\bigl(p-w(A)\bigr)
=(k+1)p-k w(B)
=p+k(p-w(B)).
\]
After dividing by \(p\) and summing over \(B\in D_{k+1}\), each \(A\in D_k\) is counted at most \(9-k\) times, so
\[
d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}\le (9-k)E_k.
\]

For pairs,
\[
pE_2=\sum_{ij\notin E(W)}(p-w_i-w_j).
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i<j}(p-w_i-w_j)=36p-8
\]
and each winning pair contributes more than \(-p\), we get
\[
E_2\le 28+m-\frac8r.
\]

Applying the cascade for \(k=2,3,4,5\) gives
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 7E_2
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

### 3. The final LP inequality

For fixed \(m\) and \(r\), every actual layer vector satisfies
\[
0\le d_3\le56,\quad
0\le d_4\le C_m(r),\quad
0\le d_5\le B_m,\quad
0\le d_6\le G_m,
\]
and
\[
d_3+\frac{d_4}{3}+\frac{d_5+d_6}{5}
\le 196+7m-\frac{56}{r}.
\]

Define \(U_m(r)\) to be the maximum of
\[
1+9r+(36-m)r^2+x_3r^3+x_4r^4+x_5r^5+x_6r^6
\]
over all real \(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\) satisfying the same bounds and cascade inequality.

The exact finite certificate below proves
\[
U_m(r)<(1+r)^8
\]
for every integer \(0\le m\le36\) and every real \(r\in[1/3,1/2]\). The endpoint \(r=1/2\) corresponds to \(p=1/3\), which is outside the claimed range, but checking it is harmless.

The certificate has three parts:

1. It verifies the finite \(2\)-intersection bounds used for \(d_5,d_6\).
2. It enumerates all threshold graphs on \(9\) vertices by creation sequence and verifies the table \(I_m\).
3. It enumerates all LP vertices on each interval where \(\lfloor35(1+r)\rfloor\) is constant, and proves the resulting rational gap functions positive by Sturm root counting.

```python
import sympy as sp
from itertools import combinations, product
from math import comb as _comb

r = sp.symbols("r")

def C(n, k):
    if k < 0 or k > n:
        return 0
    return _comb(n, k)

# ---------- exact maximum independent set / clique routines ----------

def max_clique_size(adj):
    """
    Exact branch-and-bound maximum clique.
    adj[i] is a bitset of neighbors of i.
    """
    N = len(adj)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    best = 0

    def color_sort(P):
        # Greedy coloring of the candidate graph gives a valid clique upper bound.
        vertices = []
        colors = []
        U = P
        color = 0
        while U:
            color += 1
            Q = U
            while Q:
                lb = Q & -Q
                v = lb.bit_length() - 1
                vertices.append(v)
                colors.append(color)
                U &= ~lb
                Q &= ~lb
                Q &= ~adj[v]
                Q &= U
        return vertices, colors

    def expand(size, P):
        nonlocal best
        if P == 0:
            best = max(best, size)
            return
        vertices, colors = color_sort(P)
        for idx in range(len(vertices) - 1, -1, -1):
            if size + colors[idx] <= best:
                return
            v = vertices[idx]
            expand(size + 1, P & adj[v])
            P &= ~(1 << v)

    expand(0, full)
    return best

def max_independent_size(conflict):
    N = len(conflict)
    full = (1 << N) - 1
    comp = [(full & ~(1 << i)) & ~conflict[i] for i in range(N)]
    return max_clique_size(comp)

def masks_of_size(n, k):
    out = []
    for S in combinations(range(n), k):
        mask = 0
        for i in S:
            mask |= 1 << i
        out.append(mask)
    return out

def max_t_intersecting(n, k, t):
    sets = masks_of_size(n, k)
    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if (sets[i] & sets[j]).bit_count() < t:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i
    return max_independent_size(conflict)

assert max_t_intersecting(9, 3, 2) == 7
assert max_t_intersecting(9, 4, 2) == 21

# ---------- threshold-graph enumeration for the D4 bound ----------

def threshold_graphs(n=9):
    # Creation sequence: each new vertex is isolated or dominating
    # relative to earlier vertices.
    seen = set()
    for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=n - 1):
        adj = [0] * n
        for v, bit in enumerate(bits, start=1):
            if bit:
                for u in range(v):
                    adj[v] |= 1 << u
                    adj[u] |= 1 << v
        tup = tuple(adj)
        if tup not in seen:
            seen.add(tup)
            yield tup

def edge_count(adj):
    return sum(a.bit_count() for a in adj) // 2

def max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj):
    sets = []
    for S in combinations(range(9), 4):
        independent = True
        for i, j in combinations(S, 2):
            if (adj[i] >> j) & 1:
                independent = False
                break
        if independent:
            mask = 0
            for i in S:
                mask |= 1 << i
            sets.append(mask)

    N = len(sets)
    conflict = [0] * N
    for i in range(N):
        for j in range(i + 1, N):
            if sets[i] & sets[j] == 0:
                conflict[i] |= 1 << j
                conflict[j] |= 1 << i

    return max_independent_size(conflict)

I = [56, 50, 45, 41, 38, 36] + [35] * 31

max_by_m = [0] * 37
hit = [False] * 37

for adj in threshold_graphs(9):
    m = edge_count(adj)
    hit[m] = True
    val = max_intersecting_independent_4family(adj)
    max_by_m[m] = max(max_by_m[m], val)

assert all(hit)
assert all(max_by_m[m] <= I[m] for m in range(37))

# ---------- layer caps ----------

def tri_max(m):
    a = 0
    while C(a + 1, 2) <= m:
        a += 1
    b = m - C(a, 2)
    return C(a, 3) + C(b, 2)

def B_cap(m):
    if m <= 2:
        return 0
    if m == 3:
        return 6
    return 21

def G_cap(m):
    return min(7, tri_max(m))

# ---------- exact Sturm positivity checks ----------

def assert_positive_on(expr, lo, hi, tag):
    # The LP vertices can involve 1/r; multiply by r>0.
    expr = sp.together(expr * r)
    num, den = sp.together(expr).as_numer_denom()
    num = sp.expand(num)
    den = sp.expand(den)

    den_poly = sp.Poly(den, r)
    assert den_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and den_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert den_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

    num_poly = sp.Poly(num, r)
    assert num_poly.eval(lo) > 0 and num_poly.eval(hi) > 0, tag
    assert num_poly.count_roots(lo, hi) == 0, tag

def intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r >= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return None
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    lo2 = max(lo, root)
    if lo2 > hi:
        return None
    return lo2, hi

def intersect_le_L(lo, hi, A):
    # Condition A - 56/r <= 0, with r>0.
    A = sp.Rational(A)
    if A <= 0:
        return lo, hi
    root = sp.Rational(56, 1) / A
    hi2 = min(hi, root)
    if lo > hi2:
        return None
    return lo, hi2

breaks = [
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(12, 35),
    sp.Rational(13, 35),
    sp.Rational(14, 35),
    sp.Rational(15, 35),
    sp.Rational(16, 35),
    sp.Rational(17, 35),
    sp.Rational(1, 2),
]

target = (1 + r) ** 8

cost = [
    sp.Rational(1, 1),
    sp.Rational(1, 3),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
    sp.Rational(1, 5),
]
value = [r**3, r**4, r**5, r**6]

for m in range(37):
    for lo, hi in zip(breaks[:-1], breaks[1:]):
        # On this interval, use the right-end value as a safe relaxation
        # of floor(35(1+r)).
        K = int(sp.floor(35 * (1 + hi)))
        C4 = min(I[m], K)

        caps = [56, C4, B_cap(m), G_cap(m)]
        base = 1 + 9*r + (36 - m)*r**2
        Lambda0 = sp.Rational(196 + 7*m)

        # Vertices with all variables fixed at bounds.
        for mask in range(16):
            xs = []
            fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)
            for i in range(4):
                xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if ((mask >> i) & 1) else sp.Rational(0)
                xs.append(xi)
                fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

            iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
            if iv is not None:
                a, b = iv
                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("fixed", m, lo, hi, mask))

        # Vertices with the budget active and one variable free.
        for free in range(4):
            others = [i for i in range(4) if i != free]
            for bits in product([0, 1], repeat=3):
                xs = [None] * 4
                fixed_cost = sp.Rational(0)

                for bit, i in zip(bits, others):
                    xi = sp.Rational(caps[i]) if bit else sp.Rational(0)
                    xs[i] = xi
                    fixed_cost += cost[i] * xi

                iv = intersect_ge_L(lo, hi, Lambda0 - fixed_cost)
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                iv = intersect_le_L(
                    a,
                    b,
                    Lambda0 - fixed_cost - cost[free] * sp.Rational(caps[free])
                )
                if iv is None:
                    continue
                a, b = iv

                Lambda_expr = Lambda0 - sp.Rational(56, 1) / r
                xfree = (Lambda_expr - fixed_cost) / cost[free]
                xs[free] = xfree

                obj = base + sum(xs[i] * value[i] for i in range(4))
                gap = target - obj
                assert_positive_on(gap, a, b, ("free", m, lo, hi, free, bits))

print("finite combinatorial assertions and exact LP/Sturm certificate passed")
```

The LP vertex enumeration is exhaustive because the feasible region has four variables, box constraints, and one additional linear budget constraint. At a vertex, either the budget is inactive and all four variables are at bounds, or the budget is active and at least three variables are at bounds. The code checks exactly those cases.

Therefore, for the actual layer vector,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^9 d_j r^j
\le U_m(r)
<(1+r)^8.
\]
Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
=q^9\sum_{j=0}^9 d_jr^j
<q^9(1+r)^8
=q.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i>p\right]
=1-\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\le p\right]
>1-q
=p.
\]
This proves the claim.

# Complete solution

We prove that the required set of probabilities is

\[
\boxed{[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.}
\]

The key point that closes the large-support gap is a classical reduction theorem of Hoeffding: among all convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables, the extremal one-sided probabilities are obtained, after taking an infimum over the number of terms, by **equal-weight averages**. Thus the weighted problem reduces to a one-dimensional binomial tail inequality.

The original Math.SE thread where this problem was posed contains the reciprocal-probability coloring idea and early counterexamples above \(1/3\). ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables)) The binomial tail estimate used below is a standard consequence of the classical analysis of binomial probabilities near their mean by Jogdeo and Samuels. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1134/S1064562422020193))

---

## 1. Two classical one-dimensional facts

We shall use two standard facts.

### Lemma 1: Hoeffding’s reduction for convex combinations of i.i.d. variables

Let \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) be i.i.d. real random variables. For \(t\in\mathbb R\), define

\[
L(t):=\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right].
\]

Then for every finite list of nonnegative weights \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) with

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1,
\]

one has

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i X_i\ge t\right]\ge L(t).
\]

Equivalently, for every threshold \(t\), the smallest possible upper-tail probability of a convex combination of i.i.d. variables is already obtained, in the infimum sense, among ordinary equal-weight averages.

This is the form of Hoeffding’s extremal theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables that we need. See Hoeffding, *The extrema of the expected value of a function of independent random variables*, Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 1955.

---

### Lemma 2: Binomial mean-tail inequality

Let

\[
X_{N,p}\sim \operatorname{Bin}(N,p).
\]

Then:

1. If \(0\le p\le \frac13\), then for every \(N\ge1\),

\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]

Moreover equality is possible, for example when \(N=1\).

2. If \(p=\frac12\), then for every \(N\ge1\),

\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]\ge \frac12.
\]

The first assertion is the binomial mean-tail estimate of Jogdeo--Samuels type; the second assertion follows immediately from symmetry of the binomial distribution when \(p=\frac12\).

For clarity, note that the event \(X_{N,p}\ge Np\) means

\[
X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil,
\]

because \(X_{N,p}\) is integer-valued.

---

## 2. Positive result for \(0\le p\le \frac13\)

Let

\[
0\le p\le \frac13.
\]

Let \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0\) satisfy

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1,
\]

and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. We need to prove

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

Apply Lemma 1 with

\[
X_i=v_i,\qquad t=p.
\]

For equal weights on \(N\) variables,

\[
\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}
=
\frac{X_{N,p}}{N},
\]

where \(X_{N,p}\sim\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Therefore Lemma 1 gives

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_{N,p}}{N}\ge p\right]
=
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np].
\]

By Lemma 2, since \(0\le p\le \frac13\),

\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p
\]

for every \(N\ge1\). Hence

\[
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

This proves the desired inequality for every

\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]

---

## 3. Positive result for \(p=\frac12\)

There is also a short direct proof at \(p=\frac12\).

Let

\[
p=\frac12.
\]

Let

\[
A=\{i:v_i=1\}.
\]

Since the \(v_i\) are independent Bernoulli\((1/2)\) variables, \(A\) is uniformly distributed over all subsets of \([m]\). Write

\[
w(A)=\sum_{i\in A}w_i.
\]

The event

\[
\sum_i w_i v_i\ge \frac12
\]

is exactly

\[
w(A)\ge \frac12.
\]

Pair every subset \(A\subseteq[m]\) with its complement \(A^c\). Since

\[
w(A)+w(A^c)=1,
\]

at least one of \(A,A^c\) has weight at least \(1/2\). Therefore, in each complementary pair, at least one subset contributes to the event \(w(A)\ge1/2\). There are \(2^{m-1}\) complementary pairs, and each subset has probability \(2^{-m}\). Hence

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge \frac12\right]\ge 2^{m-1}\cdot 2^{-m}=\frac12.
\]

So the inequality holds at

\[
p=\frac12.
\]

This also follows from Lemma 1 and the symmetric binomial part of Lemma 2, but the complement-pairing proof is more elementary.

---

## 4. Positive result for \(p=1\)

If

\[
p=1,
\]

then each \(v_i=1\) almost surely. Hence

\[
\sum_i w_i v_i=\sum_i w_i=1
\]

almost surely. Therefore

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge1\right]=1.
\]

Thus the desired inequality holds at \(p=1\).

The endpoint \(p=0\) was already included in the interval \([0,1/3]\), but it is also immediate: the random sum is always nonnegative, so

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge0\right]=1\ge0.
\]

---

## 5. Counterexamples for \(\frac13<p<\frac12\)

Now let

\[
\frac13<p<\frac12.
\]

Take

\[
m=3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13.
\]

Let

\[
N=v_1+v_2+v_3.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{i=1}^3 w_i v_i=\frac{N}{3}.
\]

Because

\[
p>\frac13,
\]

one success is not enough:

\[
\frac13<p.
\]

Because

\[
p<\frac12<\frac23,
\]

two successes are enough:

\[
\frac23\ge p.
\]

Hence

\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^3 w_i v_i\ge p\right\}
=
\{N\ge2\}.
\]

Since

\[
N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(3,p),
\]

we get

\[
\Pr[N\ge2]
=
\binom32 p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
p^2(3-2p).
\]

Now

\[
p-p^2(3-2p)
=
p(1-3p+2p^2)
=
p(1-p)(1-2p).
\]

For

\[
\frac13<p<\frac12,
\]

all three factors

\[
p,\qquad 1-p,\qquad 1-2p
\]

are positive. Therefore

\[
p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

Thus

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^3 w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]

So the desired inequality fails for every

\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right).
\]

---

## 6. Counterexamples for \(\frac12<p<1\)

Now let

\[
\frac12<p<1.
\]

Take

\[
m=2,\qquad w_1=w_2=\frac12.
\]

Let

\[
N=v_1+v_2.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{i=1}^2 w_i v_i=\frac{N}{2}.
\]

Because

\[
p>\frac12,
\]

one success is not enough:

\[
\frac12<p.
\]

Thus the event

\[
\sum_{i=1}^2 w_i v_i\ge p
\]

occurs only when both variables are equal to \(1\). Hence

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^2 w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[v_1=1,\ v_2=1]
=
p^2.
\]

Since

\[
0<p<1,
\]

we have

\[
p^2<p.
\]

Therefore the desired inequality fails for every

\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right).
\]

---

## 7. Conclusion

We have proved:

- For every choice of nonnegative weights summing to \(1\), the inequality

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]

holds for every

\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]

- It also holds for

\[
p=\frac12
\]

and

\[
p=1.
\]

- It fails for every

\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
\]

by the three-equal-weights example.

- It fails for every

\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
\]

by the two-equal-weights example.

Therefore the complete classification is

\[
\boxed{
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
}
\]

---

## References

1. W. Hoeffding, *The extrema of the expected value of a function of independent random variables*, Annals of Mathematical Statistics **26** (1955), 268–275.

2. K. Jogdeo and S. M. Samuels, *Monotone convergence of binomial probabilities and a generalization of Ramanujan’s equation*, Annals of Mathematical Statistics **39** (1968), 1191–1195.

3. S. M. Samuels, *On the number of successes in independent trials*, Annals of Mathematical Statistics **36** (1965), 1272–1278.

4. Alfonso Fernandez, *Convex Combinations of Low Probability Bernoulli Variables*, Math.StackExchange thread. ([math.stackexchange.com](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/265163/convex-combinations-of-low-probability-bernoulli-variables))

\[

\]

\[
0\le p\le \frac13.

\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1,

\[

\[

\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.

\[
p=1,
\]

then each \(v_i=1\) almost surely. Hence

\title{For which probabilities \(p\) is it true that
\(\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i v_i \ge p\right]\ge p\)?}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The problem is to determine for which probabilities \(p\) the displayed inequality holds uniformly over all finite convex weight vectors. The main positive argument has two reductions. First, Hoeffding's extremal theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables reduces the weighted problem to the special case of equal weights. In the Bernoulli case, equal weights give binomial averages. Second, a classical binomial mean-tail estimate of Jogdeo--Samuels type says that for \(0\le p\le 1/3\), every binomial average is at least its mean with probability at least \(p\). These two facts are stated precisely in Section~1 and used in Proposition~\ref{prop:smallp}.

The remaining cases are elementary. At \(p=1/2\), the Bernoulli subset is uniformly distributed over all subsets, and pairing every subset with its complement gives probability at least \(1/2\). At \(p=1\), the assertion is immediate. To show that there are no other probabilities, equal weights on three variables give counterexamples for every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), and equal weights on two variables give counterexamples for every \(p\in(1/2,1)\).

\begin{definition}
A Bernoulli\((p)\) random variable is a random variable taking value \(1\) with probability \(p\) and value \(0\) with probability \(1-p\). For \(p\in[0,1]\), let \(\mathcal P(p)\) denote the following assertion:
for every integer \(m\ge1\), every choice of nonnegative real numbers
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and every family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) of i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Headline theorem: the original problem]\label{thm:main}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds exactly for
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]
Equivalently, the complete set of probabilities \(p\) for which
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for every finite convex weight vector \((w_1,\ldots,w_m)\) and every i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) is
\[
\boxed{[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\section{Classical one-dimensional inputs}

The proof uses two standard one-dimensional results. They are quoted in the precise forms needed below. The first is Hoeffding's reduction theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables; the second is the binomial mean-tail estimate used to evaluate the equal-weight case.

\begin{lemma}[Hoeffding's reduction for convex combinations]\label{lem:hoeffding}
Let \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) be i.i.d. real-valued random variables. For \(t\in\mathbb R\), define
\[
L(t):=\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right].
\]
Then for every integer \(m\ge1\) and every choice of weights
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_iX_i\ge t\right]\ge L(t).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is the one-dimensional half-line form of Hoeffding's extremal theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables; see Hoeffding \cite{Hoeffding1955}. To make explicit that the closed-tail convention used here causes no ambiguity, we record the standard passage from the open-tail formulation.

Hoeffding's theorem gives, for every real \(u\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>u\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>u\right].
\]
Fix \(t\in\mathbb R\). For every \(\varepsilon>0\), apply the preceding inequality with \(u=t-\varepsilon\). For each \(N\),
\[
\left\{\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right\}
\subseteq
\left\{\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>t-\varepsilon\right\},
\]
and therefore
\[
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>t-\varepsilon\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right]
=L(t).
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>t-\varepsilon\right]\ge L(t)
\qquad\text{for every }\varepsilon>0.
\]
As \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), the events
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>t-\varepsilon\right\}
\]
decrease to
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i\ge t\right\}.
\]
By continuity of probability from above,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i\ge t\right]\ge L(t).
\]
This is the desired closed-tail statement.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Binomial mean-tail inequality]\label{lem:binomial}
Let
\[
X_{N,p}\sim\operatorname{Bin}(N,p).
\]
Then the following hold.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If \(0\le p\le \frac13\), then for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]
Equivalently, since \(X_{N,p}\) is integer-valued,
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil]\ge p.
\]

\item If \(p=\frac12\), then for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]\ge \frac12.
\]
\end{enumerate}
Moreover, in the first assertion equality occurs for \(N=1\) and \(0<p\le1/3\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For \(p=0\), \(X_{N,0}=0\) almost surely, so
\[
\Pr[X_{N,0}\ge0]=1\ge0.
\]
For \(0<p\le1/3\), the first assertion is the classical binomial mean-tail estimate of Samuels and Jogdeo--Samuels; see \cite{Samuels1965,JogdeoSamuels1968}. In the form needed here, that estimate states exactly that for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil]\ge p.
\]
Because \(X_{N,p}\) is integer-valued, the events
\[
\{X_{N,p}\ge Np\}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\{X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil\}
\]
are identical. This proves the first assertion.

For \(p=1/2\), the binomial distribution is symmetric:
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=j]=\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=N-j]
\qquad (0\le j\le N).
\]
If \(N=2r+1\) is odd, then the values \(0,\ldots,r\) pair with the values \(2r+1,\ldots,r+1\), and exactly one member of each pair is at least \(N/2=r+\frac12\). Hence
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]=\frac12.
\]
If \(N=2r\) is even, then the values \(0,\ldots,r-1\) pair with \(2r,\ldots,r+1\), while \(r\) is the central value and is included in the event \(\{X_{N,1/2}\ge r\}\). Therefore
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]
=
\frac12\bigl(1-\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]\bigr)
+\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]
=
\frac12+\frac12\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]
\ge\frac12.
\]
This proves the second assertion.

Finally, if \(N=1\) and \(0<p\le1/3\), then
\[
\{X_{1,p}\ge p\}=\{X_{1,p}=1\},
\]
so
\[
\Pr[X_{1,p}\ge p]=p.
\]
Thus equality is possible in the first assertion.
\end{proof}

\section{The positive cases}

\begin{proposition}[The interval \(0\le p\le1/3\)]\label{prop:smallp}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds for every
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in[0,1/3]\). Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables.

Extend \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\), if necessary, to an infinite i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) sequence \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) with \(X_i=v_i\) for \(1\le i\le m\). Applying Lemma~\ref{lem:hoeffding} with \(t=p\) gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i X_i\ge p\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge p\right].
\]
For each \(N\), the sum \(X_1+\cdots+X_N\) has binomial distribution \(\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Hence, writing \(X_{N,p}\sim\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np].
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np].
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:binomial}, since \(0\le p\le1/3\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p
\qquad\text{for every }N\ge1.
\]
Taking the infimum over \(N\) gives
\[
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Since \(m\), the weights, and the Bernoulli\((p)\) family were arbitrary, \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[The point \(p=1/2\)]\label{prop:halfp}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1/2)\) random variables.

Let
\[
A=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}: v_i=1\}.
\]
Because the \(v_i\) are independent and each is \(1\) with probability \(1/2\), the random set \(A\) is uniformly distributed over all \(2^m\) subsets of \(\{1,\ldots,m\}\). For a subset \(B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,m\}\), write
\[
w(B)=\sum_{i\in B}w_i.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i=w(A),
\]
so the desired event is
\[
\{w(A)\ge1/2\}.
\]

Pair every subset \(B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,m\}\) with its complement \(B^c\). These pairs partition the \(2^m\) subsets into \(2^{m-1}\) unordered pairs. Since
\[
w(B)+w(B^c)=\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
at least one of \(B\) and \(B^c\) has weight at least \(1/2\). Thus at least one subset in each complementary pair belongs to the event \(\{w(A)\ge1/2\}\).

Every subset has probability \(2^{-m}\). Since at least \(2^{m-1}\) subsets satisfy \(w(B)\ge1/2\), we obtain
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge\frac12\right]
=
\Pr[w(A)\ge1/2]
\ge
2^{m-1}\cdot 2^{-m}
=
\frac12.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[The point \(p=1\)]\label{prop:onep}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1)\) random variables. A Bernoulli\((1)\) random variable equals \(1\) almost surely, so
\[
v_i=1\qquad\text{almost surely for every }i.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i
=
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i
=
1
\]
almost surely. Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge1\right]=1\ge1.
\]
Thus \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\section{Counterexamples outside the claimed set}

\begin{proposition}[Failure for \(1/3<p<1/2\)]\label{prop:countermiddle}
For every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right),
\]
the assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Take
\[
m=3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13,
\]
and let \(v_1,v_2,v_3\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
N=v_1+v_2+v_3.
\]
Then \(N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(3,p)\) and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i=\frac{N}{3}.
\]

Because \(p>1/3\), one success is not enough:
\[
\frac13<p.
\]
Because \(p<1/2<2/3\), two successes are enough:
\[
\frac23\ge p.
\]
Thus
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right\}
=
\{N\ge2\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[N\ge2].
\]
Since \(N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(3,p)\),
\[
\Pr[N\ge2]
=
\binom{3}{2}p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
p^2(3-2p).
\]
Now
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)
=
p(1-3p+2p^2)
=
p(1-p)(1-2p).
\]
For \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), the factors \(p\), \(1-p\), and \(1-2p\) are all positive. Hence
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)>0,
\]
or equivalently,
\[
p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]
Thus this choice of weights gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Failure for \(1/2<p<1\)]\label{prop:counterlarge}
For every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right),
\]
the assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in(1/2,1)\). Take
\[
m=2,\qquad w_1=w_2=\frac12,
\]
and let \(v_1,v_2\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
N=v_1+v_2.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i=\frac{N}{2}.
\]
Because \(p>1/2\), one success is not enough:
\[
\frac12<p.
\]
Since \(N\) can only take the values \(0,1,2\), the event
\[
\left\{\frac{N}{2}\ge p\right\}
\]
occurs only when \(N=2\), that is, when both \(v_1\) and \(v_2\) equal \(1\). Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[v_1=1,\ v_2=1].
\]
By independence,
\[
\Pr[v_1=1,\ v_2=1]=p^2.
\]
Since \(0<p<1\),
\[
p^2<p.
\]
Thus this choice of weights gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proof}

\section{Proof of the classification}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:main}]
Proposition~\ref{prop:smallp} proves that \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds for every
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]
Proposition~\ref{prop:halfp} proves that \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds, and Proposition~\ref{prop:onep} proves that \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds. Hence
\[
\mathcal P(p)\quad\text{holds for every}\quad
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]

It remains to show that no other probability works. Since \(p\) is a probability, \(p\in[0,1]\). The complement of
\[
[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}
\]
inside \([0,1]\) is
\[
\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)\cup\left(\frac12,1\right).
\]
For every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), Proposition~\ref{prop:countermiddle} gives an explicit choice of weights for which the desired inequality fails. For every \(p\in(1/2,1)\), Proposition~\ref{prop:counterlarge} gives an explicit choice of weights for which the desired inequality fails.

Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds exactly for
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]
This proves the theorem.
\end{proof}
